title:
Low-Energy Room-Temperature Ni-Functionalized ZnO–TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Ternary Nanocomposite Thin-Film Transistor Formaldehyde Sensor
authors: Suman Kumar, Subindu Kumar, Basanta Bhowmik
abstract: Fabrication of low-cost Ni–ZnO–TiO2 ternary nanocomposite thin-film transistor for selective formaldehyde detection at room temperature is investigated in the present paper. Dip coating of the zinc oxide solution on top of the hydrothermally synthesized titania nanostructure, followed by Ni functionalization was characterized by X-ray diffraction analysis and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). Room-temperature formaldehyde response of Ni-functionalized ZnO–TiO2 ternary composite in thin-film transistor configuration shows a maximum threshold voltage shift of 0.65 V compared to 0.45 V for ZnO–TiO2 sensor and 0.2 V for pristine TiO2 sensor, respectively. Drain current of the ternary nanocomposite sensor was found to be 2.52 times higher than that of the ZnO–TiO2 sensor and 4.32 times more compared to the pristine TiO2 sensor counterpart, respectively. The optimum HCHO response of ~96.43% was measured in the Ni-functionalized ZnO–TiO2 sensor compared to ZnO–TiO2 sensor (51%) and TiO2-based sensor (38%). The sensor exhibited a fast response time of 67 s and recovery time of 66 s for −100 ppm at room temperature operation, demonstrating its suitability for low-power sensing applications. Spillover mechanism of functionalized Ni nanoparticles with the trap-assisted synergistic effect of ZnO–TiO2 interfaces, possibly attributed to maximum accumulation of carriers at the sensing channel leading to higher threshold voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\textit {th}}\text {)}$ </tex-math></inline-formula> shifting and highest drain-current (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${I}_{d}\text {)}$ </tex-math></inline-formula> variation in the ternary composite sensor. Moreover, the optimal selectivity window (41%) with neighboring interfering species, as well as the highly stable response, make such a ternary sensor a suitable candidate for real-time monitoring applications.

title: Plasmonic Optical DAC for Coherent PAM and IM/DD ASK Transmission
authors: Samuel Hess, Daniel Rieben, Konstantinos Moschopoulos, Chenrui Xu, Marcel Destraz, Wolfgang Heni, Moshe Nazarathy, Ioannis Tomkos, David Moor, Juerg Leuthold
abstract: Optical digital-to-analog converters (DAC) offer power-efficient amplitude multiplexing in the optical domain. They allow simplification to complex electronic circuits as the binary signals are directly fed to the Mach-Zehnder modulators (MZM). Drive circuits with a large effective number of bits (ENOB) and complex power-hungry electronic predistortion of the MZMs nonlinear transfer characteristic are eliminated. This results in a power advantage and smaller electronics, ultimately delivering an improved signal-to-noise and distortion ratio (SNDR). As oDACs can be operated by means of direct digital drives (DDD) with minimal signal processing, they are ideal for highest speed operation. When implemented with terahertz-bandwidth plasmonic modulators, they may provide highest symbol rates on a small footprint with the least power consumption. We demonstrate the first plasmonic oDAC implementation for IM/DD and coherent transmission, operating at up to 420 Gb/s line rate without SNR penalty. We show energy-efficient amplitude multiplexing in the optical domain at symbol rates up to 180 GBd.

title: A Multi-Voltage Method for Extracting Multi-Chips Junction Temperature Distributions in Power Electronics
authors: Lixin Wu, Erping Deng, Maoyang Pan, Wenbo Hua, Luhong Xie, Shengqian Xu, Yanhao Wang, Haotian Zhang, Lijian Ding
abstract: As the core of energy conversion, power modules play a critical role in determining the reliability and efficiency of energy systems, thereby exerting a significant impact on the broader energy economy. To address the current limitations wherein existing junction temperature measurement methods fail to obtain the internal junction temperature distribution of multi-chip modules both non-destructively and accurately, our research proposes a Multi-Voltage Method, based on the principle that the voltage across chips connected in parallel remains constant. During the junction temperature measurement process, a set of voltage with different amplitudes—equal in number to the parallel-connected chips—is applied independently. The junction temperature distribution of the module is then obtained by solving a system of nonlinear equations. In comparison with the conventional small-current method, tests were conducted on both single-chip and multi-chip configurations, and the resulting junction temperature deviations were found to be within 3 K and 5 K, respectively. Moreover, since a single chip is composed of numerous unit cells, the proposed method was further extended to extract the temperature distribution within a single chip. When compared with infrared thermographic measurements, the relative error was within 5.8%, and the maximum temperature error was further reduced as the division granularity increased.

title: A Reconfigurable eHISL Phase Shifter With Transmission and Dual-Reflection Modes
authors: Jin Wu, Yuefeng Hou, Tingxue Yu, Chenlin Guo, Yang Yuan, Tao Chen, Daodan Deng, Kaixue Ma
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents a novel reconfigurable phase shifter with transmission and dual-reflection modes based on enhanced hybrid integrated suspended line (eHISL) technology. The proposed phase shifter comprises a reconfigurable coupler with loads connected to its through and coupled ports. First, a novel reconfigurable phase shifter topology is proposed with detailed analysis, enabling electronic switching between transmission mode and dual-reflection mode. In dual-reflection mode, high isolation between the two ports enables independent control of their reflection phases. Second, to reduce insertion loss (IL), an air-dielectric-based eHISL structure is adopted in the design. Third, the parallel <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$L$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dual-resonant loads are employed to achieve 7-bit 360° phase shift in both transmission and dual-reflection modes. Finally, the proposed phase shifter operating at 2.45 GHz is designed, fabricated, and measured. In transmission mode, it achieves a full phase shift range (PSR) with an IL of 1.42–2.20 dB. In dual-reflection mode, both the two ports achieve PSRs of 360° and return losses of 0.73–1.36 dB and 0.77–1.31dB, respectively, while maintaining an isolation better than 19dB between the two ports. The proposed reconfigurable phase shifter offers a solution for constructing full-coverage, high-gain, and high-precision reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs).

title: A Topography-Constrained Marine Gravity Field Modeling Method for Gravity Matching-Aided Navigation
authors: Yu Han, Fangjun Qin, Kailong Li, Yun Xiao, Tiangao Zhu
abstract: Gravity matching-aided navigation (GMAN) has become a critical technology for autonomous underwater navigation due to its high precision, strong autonomy, and long endurance. Significant short-wavelength signal attenuation in existing global marine gravity models leads to a fundamental mismatch with high-sensitivity gravity sensors, severely limiting high-precision GMAN applications. This paper proposes a topography-constrained multi-quadric (TMQ) optimization method for high-precision gravity field reference map construction. By exploiting the physical correlation between seafloor topography and gravity anomalies, the method recovers short-wavelength signals through topographic forward modeling in the frequency domain, and integrates topographic complexity as a physical constraint into multi-quadric functions for fusing sparse shipborne gravity data in the spatial domain. Experimental results show an overall accuracy improvement of 44.33% compared with EGM2008, reaching 51.84% in topographically complex regions. Gravity matching navigation experiments demonstrate that the gravity field reference map enhances system observability. The RMSE of the horizontal position error for GMAN-TMQ is reduced by approximately 45.42% to 65.60% compared to GMAN-EGM2008. The incorporation of physical constraints effectively improves both the accuracy of gravity field modeling and the performance of gravity matching-aided navigation for autonomous underwater navigation.

title: Iterative Reconstruction Performance on the NeuroEXPLORER: Evaluation with Static and Dynamic Human Imaging
authors: Yifan Hu, Jiazhen Zhang, Jean-Dominique Gallezot, Tommaso Volpi, Tianyi Zeng, Takuya Toyonaga, Richard E. Carson
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: The NeuroEXPLORER (NX) is a next-generation, brain-dedicated PET scanner offering 10× higher sensitivity compared to the HRRT and < 2 mm spatial resolution. This work evaluated the impact of iterative reconstruction on the quantitative performance of NX using human <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F-SynVesT-1 and <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C-PHNO data with an injection dose of 167.2 ± 13.6 MBq and 275.7 ± 64.8 MBq, respectively. Both 100% count level and 20% down-sampled count level were evaluated. Images were reconstructed with ordered-subsets expectation maximization incorporated PSF and TOF (PSF-TOF-OSEM) using 5 subsets and up to 20 iterations. Quantitative performance of static imaging was evaluated by computing bias and noise from statistically independent list-mode replicates. Simulations incorporating iteration-dependent bias and noise of each dynamic frame were used to evaluate effects on kinetic parameters. In static imaging, 5 subsets × 10 iterations achieved ROI bias < 10% and noise < 1% for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">18</sup>F-SynVesT-1, and bias < 10% with noise < 10% for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>C-PHNO. In dynamic imaging simulation, with 5 subsets and 15 iterations, kinetic parameter bias remained < 20% and noise < 5%. The results demonstrate quantitative stability and precision of NX across tracers and count levels.

title: Spatially Consistent RIS-Aided Multi-User Communications: Channel Modeling and Analysis
authors: Yuan Yuan, Ruisi He, Bo Ai, Ruifeng Chen, Zhengyu Zhang, Zhuoyin Li, Zhicheng Qiu
abstract: In this paper, a 3D geometrical cluster-based channel model is developed for reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted multi-user communication systems. Herein, spatial consistency of multi-user channels is considered to guarantee smooth evolution of large-scale (i.e. Ricean K-factor, shadow fading, and number of clusters) and small-scale (i.e. distance and angle of multipath components) channel parameters for base station (BS)-receiver (Rx) and BS-RIS-Rx links of different users. Two different optimization objectives for multi-user channels are presented, namely, maximizing sum of channel gains (O1) and maximizing channel capacity (O2). Then, projected gradient ascent algorithm is applied to solve RIS phase shift matrix under both optimization objectives. Furthermore, some channel statistical characteristics, namely, correlation coefficient, singular value spread, channel capacity, and root mean square (RMS) delay spread are derived and analyzed. At the same time, impacts of solved RIS phase matrices under different optimization objectives, as well as locations of RIS and users, on channel characteristics are compared and investigated. Simulation results demonstrate that the role of RIS under O1 is to align path phases of BS-RIS-Rx link with those of BS-Rx link as much as possible, whereas under O2, the RIS aims to enhance power while reducing user channel correlation. In addition, solved RIS phase under O2 can increase channel capacity and reduce channel correlation between different BS antennas, whereas O1 leads to a reduction in channel capacity. Location of RIS is also found to affect channel response impulse amplitude of different users. Furthermore, accuracy of the proposed model is verified by comparing RMS delay spread with measured data. These observations will provide a foundation for developing reconstructed wireless propagation environment of future multi-user communication systems.

title: A Tutorial on Cognitive Biases in Agentic AI-Driven 6G Autonomous Networks
authors: Hatim Chergui, Farhad Rezazadeh, Merouane Debbah, Christos Verikoukis
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: The path to higher network autonomy in 6G lies beyond the mere optimization of key performance indicators (KPIs), requiring systems that perceive and reason over the network environment as it is. This can be achieved through agentic AI, where large language model (LLM)-powered agents utilize multimodal telemetry, memory, and cross-domain negotiation to achieve multi-objective goals. However, deploying such agents introduces cognitive biases inherited from human design, which can severely distort reasoning and actuation. This paper provides a comprehensive tutorial on well-known cognitive biases, detailing their taxonomy, mathematical formulation, emergence in telecom systems, and tailored mitigation strategies. We validate these concepts through two distinct use-cases in 6G management1. First, we tackle anchoring bias in inter-slice resource negotiation. To overcome the prohibitive execution delays of cloud-based LLMs, this use-case deploys a locally hosted 1B-parameter model on an RTX A4000 GPU, successfully achieving sub-second inference latencies compatible with near-real-time (near-RT) operations. By replacing fixed heuristic anchors with a Truncated Weibull randomized anchor strategy, the agents dismantle rigid biases, intelligently consume SLA slack, and dynamically double the system-wide energy savings (peaking at 25%) without violating strict latency limits. Second, we mitigate temporal and confirmation biases in RAN-Edge cross-domain negotiation by designing an unbiased collective memory. By integrating semantic/temporal decay and an inflection bonus that actively highlights past negotiation failures, agents are prevented from over-relying on recent data or repeating past mistakes. Grounding decisions in this richer, debiased historical context yields highly robust agreements, achieving a ×5 latency reduction and roughly 40% higher energy savings compared to memoryless baselines.

title: EAGLE: A Flexible Heterogeneous Analog Compute-In-Memory Architecture With RISC-V Programmable Multi-Core Accelerators
authors: Elena Ferro, Alessandro Ottaviano, Corey Lammie, Gamze İslamoğlu, Angelo Garofalo, Francesco Conti, Thomas Boesch, Luca Benini, Abu Sebastian, Irem Boybat
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: In today’s AI-driven landscape, designing hardware to accelerate deep learning inference faces challenges in meeting growing demands for speed, computational efficiency, and flexibility. Emerging technologies like Analog Compute-In-Memory (ACIM) hold significant promise for enhancing computational efficiency in these workloads, particularly for matrix-vector multiplications. However, architectural constraints hinder ACIM’s ability to support end-to-end workloads, necessitating complementary specialized digital units for essential operations like activation functions, pooling, and attention. These units are typically hardwired, lacking the adaptability needed in a rapidly evolving AI landscape. To address this challenge, we propose EAGLE, an ACIM-based architecture that integrates general-purpose Programmable Multi-Core Accelerators (PMCAs) as the sole digital accelerator, offering unprecedented flexibility and enabling seamless end-to-end execution across diverse workloads while maintaining competitive throughput. The architecture integrates state-of-the-art RISC-V Snitch clusters as PMCAs, extended with specialized instructions to enhance performance and energy efficiency. We introduce novel lightweight LUT-based ISA extensions to approximate commonly used transcendental functions and perform HW/SW co-optimization of key AI kernels, leveraging hardware extensions to eliminate control and memory instruction overhead. The flexibility of EAGLE is demonstrated across a range of model architectures, including encoder- and decoder-based transformers, a convolutional neural network, and a recurrent model (LSTM). Evaluated in 28 nm FD-SOI, EAGLE sustains 61.1/97.96 Inf/s on end-to-end MobileBERT/BERT-Large inference, achieving up to $3\times $ energy efficiency improvements over state-of-the-art 8 nm GPUs.

title: Computational Intelligence for Social Good: Advancing Cybernetics and Medical Systems
authors: Amir H Gandomi
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Computational Intelligence (CI) plays an increasingly important role in addressing complex societal challenges, particularly in cybernetic and medical systems where adaptability and robustness are essential. This plenary lecture, Computational Intelligence for Social Good: Advancing Cybernetics and Medical Systems, introduces Evolved Machine Intelligence (EMI), a class of biologically inspired CI methods based on evolutionary principles such as variation, selection, and adaptive learning. The talk outlines recent advances in evolutionary intelligence, with emphasis on genetic programming, automated learning, and optimisation in complex, nonlinear environments. Through representative case studies, including many-objective and large-scale optimisation problems, the lecture demonstrates how EMI-based approaches can outperform classical techniques in real-world applications. The session concludes by discussing hybrid CI frameworks that integrate evolutionary methods with complementary techniques, highlighting their potential to deliver robust and socially impactful solutions for cybernetic and medical systems.

title: Dot-Shaped Range-Angle Beampattern Synthesis for Frequency Diverse Array with Linear Frequency Offset_supp1-3692774.xlsx
authors: Xiaolong Xie
abstract: Frequency Diverse Array (FDA) suffers from inherent range–angle coupling, which causes ambiguous target localization and dispersed far-field energy. Existing decoupling approaches rely on nonuniform frequency offsets that are difficult to implement in hardware. In contrast, this letter introduces a feed-to-element random remapping architecture. The method randomly routes readily available linearly incremental frequency offset signals to individual array elements. This random routing strategy shifts the key implementation challenge from signal generation to signal routing. Because the routing function can be realized with mature, low-cost switch matrix technology, the overall system complexity is substantially reduced. The architecture is validated by applying it to measured five-channel data originally acquired from a photonics-based FDA system, confirming dot-shaped range–angle beampattern synthesis under realistic hardware conditions. The approach requires only the addition of a switch matrix to existing linearly incremental frequency offset sources, offering a practical pathway toward engineering implementation of dot-shaped range–angle beampattern synthesis.

title: Automatic Cattle BCS Estimation Using Point Cloud Surface Features from a Single Top-View ToF RGB-D Camera
authors: Pyae Phyo Kyaw, Thi Thi Zin, Masaru Aikawa, Ikuo Kobayashi, Pyke Tin
abstract: Body Condition Score (BCS) is a key indicator of nutritional status, productivity, and reproductive performance in dairy cows; however, manual assessment is subjective, labor-intensive, and unsuitable for continuous monitoring. This study proposes an automated and contactless BCS estimation framework using a single top-view time-of-flight (ToF) RGB-D camera and 3D dorsal region point cloud analysis. The cow back surface is reconstructed into a point cloud, and both edge-based geometric features and Fast Point Feature Histograms (FPFH) are integrated into a modified Dynamic Graph Convolutional Neural Network (DGCNN) for robust learning of fat-related surface patterns. By combining handcrafted 3D descriptors with graph-based deep feature learning, the proposed method significantly improves strict BCS matching performance. Experiments conducted on 95 Holstein cows across two commercial farms achieve accuracies of 69.2% at tolerance 0.0 and 94.85% at tolerance 0.25, outperforming existing 2D and depth-based approaches. The system enables scalable, objective, and non-invasive BCS monitoring, supporting proactive health interventions, optimizing feed efficiency, and enhancing transition-cow management in precision dairy farming.

title: Multi-UAV Collaborative ISAC with Dynamic Resource Allocation: A Hierarchical Graph Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Jinyu Wang, Xianchao Zhang, Zhiqing Wei, Fengsong Sun, Yiheng Li, Zhiyong Feng, Jun Lu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) enables low-altitude wireless networks (LAWN) to operate in complex 3-D environments. However, single unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) architectures suffer from limited sensing coverage and communication throughput, and multi-UAV collaborative systems face substantial challenges in resource coordination. To address these challenges, we propose a multi-UAV collaborative ISAC framework for dynamic LAWNs. First, synthetic aperture processing enhances range resolution via bandwidth utilization on dedicated sensing subcarriers. A multi-objective optimization model is then formulated to jointly minimize the Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) for positioning and maximize communication throughput. To efficiently evaluate collaborative positioning performance, the equivalent Fisher information matrix (EFIM) is derived, enabling dimensionality reduction by eliminating per-UAV Fisher information matrix (FIM) computations. Furthermore, a hierarchical graph-based multi-agent reinforcement learning (HGMRL) approach is developed using graph neural networks (GNNs). In this framework, a coordinating agent manages global bandwidth allocation and user association, while distributed UAV agents collaboratively optimize subcarrier selection, power control, and trajectory planning. Simulations demonstrate that HGMRL adapts effectively to LAWN topological evolution, achieving dynamic trade-offs between sensing accuracy and communication performance.

title: Whisper in Focus: Parameter-Efficient Stuttering Disfluency Classification
authors: Huma Ameer, Mehwish Fatima, Esam Mohammed Asem Othman, Sana Mukhtar, Nor Shahida Mohd Jamail, Rabia Latif, Seemab Latif
abstract: Stuttering disfluency classification remains a complex challenge in speech processing due to the high variability of disfluency patterns. Transformer-based models such as Wav2Vec2.0 have demonstrated promising results but struggle with generalization across diverse speakers and incur substantial computational costs. In this study, we present a parameter-efficient adaptation of Whisper, an encoder-decoder transformer trained on 680,000 hours of labeled speech-text pairs, for automatic classification of stuttering disfluencies. We introduce three key contributions: (1) a refined SEP-28k dataset with improved quality and speaker diversity, (2) novel layer-freezing strategies within Whisper’s encoder to balance task-specific learning and computational efficiency, and (3) extensive evaluation on the FluencyBank dataset to assess model generalizability. Our experimental results show that Whisper achieves a weighted F1-score of 0.812, outperforming Wav2Vec2.0 and HuBERT while reducing trainable parameters by 46% and training time by 37%. Analysis reveals that deeper encoder layers encode critical task-specific representations necessary for robust disfluency detection.

title: A Modified Gilbert-Cell Down-Conversion Mixer in 0.13 μm SiGe BiCMOS
authors: Rahul Maithani, Vadim Issakov
abstract: This paper presents a modified down-conversion Gilbert cell mixer designed in a 130nm BiCMOS technology. The mixer is designed with a Gilbert cell architecture with a passive transconductance stage. The mixer achieves a conversion gain of 7.2 dB and a 1 dB compression point of around −13 dBm, consuming a current close to 5.3mA from a 1.8V supply. The presented mixer favors compared to state-of-the-art in terms of power consumption and conversion gain, while preserving a competitive linearity and noise figure. The total area of the chip is 0.51mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> which makes it a viable solution for radar applications.

title: ULL-RA: Unsupervised Learning-Based Location-Aware Random Access for Massive Machine-Type Communications
authors: Lan Lu, Wei Chen, Bo Ai, Yuxuan Sun, Guowei Shi
abstract: Grant-free (GF) random access has emerged as a promising solution for massive machine-type communications (mMTC). However, the non-uniform distribution of user equipment (UE) and real-world limitations on base station (BS) placement lead to random access imbalance among BSs and heavy access collisions in some cells. To this end, an unsupervised learning-based location-aware random access (ULL-RA) scheme is proposed in this paper to select the accessing BSs and channels simultaneously. Specifically, ULL-RA adopts a deep learning model named ULL-RA-Net, comprising parameter-shared feedforward neural network (FFNN) layers that enable each active UE to select a BS and an access channel to maximize the achievable rate. The model is trained in an unsupervised manner to maximize a designed differentiable objective, mapping UE locations to channel access probabilities. Notably, we propose a rate-collision loss tailored to the model architecture, which combines a collision-free channel capacity term and a sparsity-inducing collision penalty term to reduce access collisions and enhance the achievable rate. Experimental results using the Deep-MIMO dataset indicate that ULL-RA outperforms conventional GF access in both the average achievable rate and the access success rate.

title: Data-Driven Prediction of Electric Vehicle Charging Behavior
authors: Liwei Zhu, Yucheng Du, Dong Cheng
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: The rapid growth rate of electric vehicle (EV) ownership has, to some extent, led to issues such as supplydemand mismatch of charging facilities and charging load, as well as grid impacts. There is an urgent need to investigate EV charging behavior to better predict charging loads and optimize the layout of charging infrastructure. This paper proposes a spatiotemporal prediction model for EV charging behavior based on GAT-BiGRU. The model treats actual distributed charging stations as nodes in a graph structure and employs a multi-head graph attention mechanism (GAT) to dynamically quantify the importance of neighboring nodes, thereby accurately capturing complex spatial dependencies between charging stations. This enables the model to focus on the interactive influences of key nodes. Furthermore, the model utilizes a bidirectional gated recurrent unit (BiGRU) network to mine the temporal evolution patterns of multi-feature charging behavior data and integrate multi-dimensional features, adapting to dependencies at different time scales. Case validation conducted on a specific demographic groups within a city demonstrates that, compared to existing spatiotemporal prediction models, the proposed model exhibits superior performance in both prediction efficiency and accuracy.

title: Control Co-Design Framework for Joint Optimization of PID Governor and Hydraulic Dynamics for a Hydropower Plant
authors: Bidur Poudel, Sang Won Seo, Daniel R. Herber, Sanjeev Pannala
publish: 2026 IEEE Power and Energy Conference at Illinois (PECI)
abstract: This paper presents a control co-design (CCD) framework tailored to the hydraulic and mechanical subsystems of hydropower systems, aimed at enhancing their dynamic performance and responsiveness to grid demands. Conventional sequential design approaches often fall short in capturing the coupled interactions between plant dynamics and control objectives. The proposed CCD methodology enables simultaneous optimization of key hydro plant parameters—such as governor settings, penstock characteristics, and valve control—with the control system architecture. Results indicate that both CCD and sequential design approaches achieve comparable transient and steady-state performance, with minor discrepancies arising from differences in their optimized parameters. However, CCD demonstrates a distinct advantage by tripling the water time constant (T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">w</inf>) while maintaining similar dynamic performance to the sequential approach. This increase in T<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">w</inf> enables optimization of penstock dimensions, as it is directly influenced by penstock design, thereby contributing to overall cost optimization.

title: Enhancing Oxide Film Thickness Measurement Accuracy via Temperature Drift Compensation With GAN-Augmented Data
authors: Jiarui Feng, Chaofeng Ye, Ming Li, Guozheng Zhou, Jingqi Jiao
abstract: Accurate measurement of oxide film thickness on nuclear fuel cladding is critical for ensuring reactor safety, as excessive oxidation can degrade thermal conductivity and increase the risk of structural failure. Eddy current testing (ECT) is the predominant technology for measuring oxide film thickness due to its high sensitivity and suitability for in-situ inspections. However, the presence of temperature fluctuations during measurement introduces drift in the ECT signal, compromising its accuracy. This article proposes a deep learning-based framework for temperature drift compensation. A generative adversarial network is used to augment limited experimental datasets by synthesizing physically consistent temperature drift profiles. These augmented datasets are then used to train a random forest (RF) model that performs nonlinear compensation of the ECT signal. A correction approach is developed to mitigate the effects of temperature measurement delay, enhancing compensation performance. Experimental validation demonstrates that the proposed method reduces the maximum measurement error from 28.38 μm to 0.98 μm and the average error from 26.76 μm to 0.62 μm. Repeated trials confirm the robustness and reliability of the method. The study provides an innovative technique for accurate measurement of oxide film thickness on nuclear fuel cladding, which can also be extended to other small-sample sensing tasks.

title: Spray-Coated Silver as Backside Metal for III–V Photovoltaic Devices on GaAs and Ge Substrates
authors: Thales Borrely, Thien Truong, Theresa E. Saenz, Sarah A. Collins, Julian Wagner, Jennifer G. Selvidge, William E. McMahon, Sneh Sinha, Mitchell Smith, Melbs LeMieux, Brett Walker, Myles A. Steiner
abstract: The accelerated increase in demand for III–V space photovoltaics on GaAs and Ge substrates, as well as growing interests in terrestrial applications, motivate the development of cost-effective, high-throughput processing routes of these materials. Here, we assess spray-coated silver (Ag) back contact metallization as a substitute for electron-beam-evaporated metals currently used in industry. We find that the spray-coated Ag films are dense and continuous. By means of quantum efficiency, dark current–voltage, and illuminated current–voltage characterizations, we show that spray-coated GaAs and Ge solar cells perform similarly to baseline devices with electroplated Au, including under high current densities. We estimate that the thresholds for specific contact resistance below which back contacts do not significantly contribute to resistive loss are <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{2.1\times 10^{-1}\Omega }\cdot$</tex-math></inline-formula>cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for GaAs and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{4.7\times 10^{-2}\Omega }\cdot$</tex-math></inline-formula>cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> for Ge. We experimentally confirm that our spray-coated samples meet these requirements. Peel tests show that the adhesion of plain spray-coated Ag films to the back of p-type Ge substrates used in III–V solar cells is currently insufficient, whereas adhesion to p-type GaAs substrates is outstanding and requires no further optimization.

title: A Semantic Digital Twin Framework for Predictive Failure Analytics in IoT
authors: Prem Kumar Ramesh, Koundinya Koorapati, Sairam Veeraswamy, Usha Narasappa
abstract: The proliferation of IoT devices in Software-Defined Data Center (SDDC) environments has made large-scale, real-time failure prediction both critical and complex. Traditional monitoring systems remain reactive, generating disconnected alerts with limited context. In this paper, we present a novel, digital twin-driven framework that unifies semantic modeling, machine learning, and real-time telemetry to deliver intelligent, predictive maintenance for IoT infrastructures. Built upon an extensible ontology derived from the Semantic Sensor Network (SSN) standard and enriched with dynamic battery metadata, our system integrates classification and regression models, such as Random Forest and XGBoost, to forecast device failures and estimate Remaining Useful Life (RUL). These insights are contextually embedded into a live semantic layer, enabling expressive SPARQL queries for impact analysis and proactive fault management. To further democratize access, we integrate a Large Language Model (LLM) interface that translates natural language queries into SPARQL, bridging the gap between technical complexity and user accessibility. Validated on NASA’s battery datasets, our approach demonstrates superior explainability, operational relevance, and scalability—pushing the boundaries of predictive analytics in IoT-SDDC ecosystems. We also showcase case studies with five practical scenarios where our framework can be readily integrated and used.

title: Feasibility and Efficiency of Subliminal Warnings Against Phishing Attacks
authors: Henry Schulz, Feng Zhu

title: Design and Development of an OTA Remote Upgrade System: A Case Study on Fully Electric Autonomous Buses
authors: Li Li
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 3rd International Conference on Autonomous Driving and Intelligent Sensing Technology
abstract: None

title: Compensation of Bending due to Gravity for MetOp-SG Scatterometer Antenna On-Ground Calibration at the DTU-ESA Antenna Test Facility
authors: Javier Fernández Álvarez, Jeppe M. Bjørstorp, Samel Arslanagić, Michael Mattes
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: The on-ground calibration of the MetOp-SG Scatterometer Antenna Subsystem (SAS) requires spherical near-field measurements of large and heavy antennas mounted on a roll-over-azimuth positioner. Due to the significant mass of the antenna under test, the supporting structure experiences gravity-induced deformation; this results in the near-field signal of the antenna under test to be measured in points that are non-uniformly distributed on the measurement sphere. This paper proposes a compensation technique based on a non-uniform 2D Fast Fourier Transform to compute the spectral representation of the irregularly sampled near-field signal. The near-field values at the nominal sampling locations are subsequently reconstructed from this spectrum by interpolation, enabling recovery of the field corresponding to the ideal measurement geometry. The method was validated using measured and simulated data from an antenna with characteristics comparable to those of the MetOp-SG instruments, demonstrating negligible reconstruction error, and robustness with respect to uncertainty of the bending angle. Then, the technique was successfully applied to real data from the MetOp-SG SAS FM2 near-field measurement campaign conducted at the Technical University of Denmark in 2022, confirming its effectiveness in practical measurement scenarios. Additionally, it is proposed that the method can be adapted to compensate for a variety of systematic measurement setup errors; this is demonstrated by applying the method to real measured data of a standard gain horn affected by an axis-intersection error.

title: Continual Test-Time Adaptation via Low-Frequency Modulation
authors: Boyuan Zhang, Jie Pan, Shuai Yang, Lichuan Gu
abstract: Continuous Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to adapt a pre-trained model to continuously evolving unlabeled target domain. Recent advances in this field predominantly adopt pseudo-label–guided self-supervised learning under teacher–student paradigms. However, under dynamic distribution shifts, such methods often suffer from noisy pseudo-labels, which leads to error accumulation and catastrophic forgetting. To alleviate this issue, we propose a novel Low-Frequency Modulation (LFM) algorithm for CTTA. Specifically, we introduce a low-frequency-guided sample selection strategy that filters out samples dominated by high-frequency components, guided by posterior prediction discrepancy. To further enrich the target distribution and reduce the model’s reliance on high-frequency cues, we employ a cross-class mixed-frequency augmentation method that selectively injects high-frequency components from other classes while preserving low-frequency content. In addition, we introduce a frequency-aware domain knowledge allocation strategy, which dynamically distributes domain-specific and domain-shared knowledge to maintain model stability in continuously evolving environments. Extensive experiments on four widely used benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of LFM in both classification and segmentation CTTA tasks.

title: SkyWin-SLAM: Robust Visual-LiDAR Fusion Integrating Swin Transformer Depth Completion and Semantic Sky Masking
authors: Jin Sun, Xue Shen, Jinwu Tong, Haitao Zhao
abstract: Visual-LiDAR fusion offers a promising solution to the limitations of pure visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) in challenging outdoor environments. However, existing Red, Green, Blue, Lime (RGB-L) systems often suffer from poor localization accuracy due to two primary issues: the inability of CNN-based depth completion networks to capture global geometry from sparse LiDAR data, and the “virtual wall” bias where sky regions are incorrectly predicted as finite depths. This paper proposes a robust RGB-L SLAM framework to address these challenges. First, we introduce Swin-PENet, a depth completion network incorporating Swin Transformer blocks to capture long-range dependencies and generate high-fidelity depth maps. Second, we propose a semantic decoupling strategy that explicitly filters out sky regions, preventing erroneous depth biases from corrupting the SLAM backend optimization. Experimental results on the KITTI Odometry Benchmark demonstrate that our system effectively eliminates scale contraction and rotational drift. The proposed framework achieves a 15.2% improvement in localization accuracy compared to the ORB-SLAM3 stereo mode.

title: In-Band Network Telemetry for End-to-End Service Performance Monitoring in Broadband Networks: A Survey and Deployment Feasibility Analysis
authors: Tanzeela Altaf, Mehran Abolhasan, Raymond Owen, Ingrid Barton, Thedin Guruge, Daniel R. Franklin
abstract: End-to-end performance monitoring is essential for ensuring service reliability and operational visibility in modern telecommunications networks. Traditional monitoring techniques often rely on out-of-band measurements, which provide limited visibility into real-time packet behavior across complex network infrastructures. In-band telemetry has emerged as a promising approach that enables performance information to be collected directly from packets as they traverse the network. This article presents a comprehensive survey of in-band telemetry techniques and evaluates their applicability to end-to-end performance monitoring in broadband networks. The survey first examines header stamping approaches, including mechanisms such as In-band Network Telemetry (INT), In-situ Operations, Administration, and Maintenance (IOAM), and related implementations. It then reviews packet marking and selective sampling techniques that aim to reduce measurement overhead while maintaining monitoring accuracy. Hybrid approaches that combine multiple telemetry mechanisms are also discussed. Building on this taxonomy, the paper analyzes the feasibility of deploying in-band telemetry across multi-segment telecommunications environments using broadband services as a representative use case. The analysis highlights several limitations in current mechanisms, including the absence of standardized Layer-2 telemetry support, the assumption of single-domain deployment, and the lack of mechanisms for sharing telemetry information across adjacent network segments. Finally, the paper identifies key open challenges and research directions required to enable scalable, interoperable, and operationally viable end-to-end telemetry solutions for future broadband networks.

title: Universal Graph Convolutional Network: Zero-Shot Transfer Across Power Grid Topology Reconfigurations
authors: Tong Wu, Anna Scaglione, Sandy Miguel, Daniel Arnold
abstract: This work addresses a fundamental challenge in applying deep learning to network topology-dependent power system applications: developing neural network models that transfer across significant system changes, including networks with entirely different topologies and dimensionalities, without requiring training data from unseen reconfigurations. We focus on situational awareness tasks where predictions inherently depend on network-wide spatial relationships through electrical connectivity. Despite extensive research, most ML-based approaches remain system-specific, limiting real-world deployment. This limitation stems from a dual barrier. First, topology changes shift feature distributions and alter input dimensions due to power flow physics. Second, reconfigurations redefine output semantics and dimensionality, requiring models to handle configuration-specific outputs while maintaining transferable feature extraction. Our core insight is treating topology as a learnable dimension through training on diverse reconfigurations, enabling the model to learn universal power flow physics rather than memorizing fixed networks. We achieve this through three synergistic mechanisms: scalar-weight graph convolutions with physics-aware operators handle variable input dimensions and distribution shifts, adaptive pooling maps topology-specific features to unified latent representations, and parallel transformer generates configuration-specific outputs using positional encoding. Our Universal Graph Convolutional Network (UGCN) achieves transferability to any reconfiguration or variation of existing power systems without any prior knowledge of new grid topologies or retraining during implementation. Experimental results on state forecasting and false data injection detection demonstrate that UGCN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in cross-system zero-shot transferability across both transmission and distribution networks.

title: Cavity-Independent Time-Frequency Interleaved Photonic ADC Enabled by Monolithic TFLN Electro-Optic Comb for 40-GSa/s Sampling
authors: Fujian Yu, Ke Yin, Tao Zhang, Wei Dong, Chao Zhou, Pan Han, Xiangai Cheng, Tian Jiang
abstract: Time-frequency interleaved photonic analog-to-digital convertor (TIPADC) is a promising architecture for scaling analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) bandwidth and sample rate, but constraining by cavity-dependent limitations intrinsic to the mode-locked lasers (MLLs). This work demonstrates a four-channel TIPADC using on-chip electro-optic comb pulses from a monolithic thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) device to solve these problems. The on-chip source generates a 10 GHz repetition rate comb with >100 lines spanning >9 nm (within 10 dB). Achieving 40 GSa/s sampling rate, 40 GHz bandwidth (modulator limited) with effective number of bit (ENOB) of 4.53 bits (17.49 GHz) and 3.52 bits (38.49 GHz). The cavity-independent source integration strategy overcomes a key barrier in TIPADC scaling, paving the way for fully monolithic PADCs deployable in 6G mmWave and electronic sensing systems.

title: Phase Shifting Transformer Protection Overview and Impact of ARS on Protection Scheme
authors: Glenn Callaghan, Jacob Billings, Jason Campisi
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: As the modern power system continues to evolve with high load centers supplied with power from remote locations, there will be an increase in use of Phase Shifting Transformers to transfer real power to the appropriate locations of the power system. The Phase Shifting Transformer has unique attributes that need to be evaluated to develop a robust protection scheme. This paper focuses on high level evaluation of Phase Shifting Transformer protection and how a protection engineer can utilize familiar protection schemes for this device. Additionally, lessons learned from the integration of Advance-Retard-Switch and the impact on the amp-turn balance protection is shared.

title: A Calculus for Web Services Choreography: Formal Semantics, Projection, and Correctness
authors: Chang Yang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Existing choreography languages often lack a uniform formal semantics that enables provably correct projection from global specifications to local behaviours. To address this, we propose <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Orc}_{\pi}$</tex>, a minimal calculus for web services choreography that integrates service coordination primitives with name-passing mobility within a strictly confined syntax of only six core primitives. Unlike approaches relying on complex global types or coherence proofs, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Orc}_{\pi}$</tex> employs a lightweight linear typing disciplinedeliberately avoiding the overhead of complex multiparty session type subtyping and merging mechanisms-to enforce protocol correctness. We define operational and trace-based semantics to facilitate rigorous equivalence reasoning. The main results establish that for every closed, well-typed choreography, the projected endpoint processes are deadlock-free and trace-equivalent to the global specification. We evaluate the framework's formal guarantees and illustrate its applicability through canonical interaction patterns typical of Internet of Things (IoT) environments. The proposed calculus deliberately trades expressive power for simplicity, enabling concise correctness arguments and yielding a foundational model for reliable distributed execution.

title: Modeling, Design and Measurement of a Compact mmWave Antenna Array with Radome for Biomedical Radar Sensing
authors: Thi Huyen Le, Ivan Ndip, Uwe Maass, Jens Schneider, Ulrike Ganesh
abstract: In this work, the design, performance analysis, and experimental validation of a compact mmWave 1×8 series-fed antenna array with radome effects for medical radar sensing operating in the 60 GHz ISM band are presented. The Dolph–Chebyshev synthesis method is employed in the array design to determine the amplitude coefficients for each element, thereby effectively suppressing side lobes. Furthermore, the impacts of various radome parameters such as material properties, thickness, and spacing between the array and radome on impedance matching, radiation characteristics, and overall efficiency are thoroughly investigated and analyzed. Finally, the fabricated antenna array was experimentally characterized under the examined radome conditions, demonstrating excellent agreement between simulated and measured results.

title: Large AI Models for the Internet of Everything
authors: Feibo Jiang, Kezhi Wang, Xingqin Lin, Merouane Debbah, Zhu Han

title: Evaluating Transfer Learning Architectures for Robust Arecanut Grading Under Capture-Induced Image Variations: Impact on Model Performance
authors: Jyothi V Prasad, Roopa B Hegde
abstract: Arecanuts hold significant cultural, economic, and therapeutic value in Southeast Asia, where they are regarded as symbols of goodwill and wealth and serve as a principal economic crop. Ensuring consistent grading is essential for maintaining quality and market value. Automated grading methods using machine learning and deep learning provide scalable alternatives to manual inspection, reducing human error and labour costs. However, image classification accuracy is often influenced by background variability. This study evaluates the robustness of four transfer-learning architectures, such as InceptionNet, ResNet, VGG16, and EfficientNet, for binary arecanut quality classification using a smartphone-acquired dataset captured under white and black backgrounds. Models were assessed using five-fold stratified cross-validation, with results reported as mean accuracy and standard deviation to ensure reliability. Cross and mixed-background experiments were conducted to examine background dependence, while non-uniform brightness augmentation was applied to training data to simulate realistic capture variability which is done dynamically during training, giving natural effects on the images. Results reveal that while all models achieved strong accuracy under controlled conditions, robustness varied significantly across architectures. InceptionNet demonstrated superior generalization, achieving a mean accuracy of 90.98 ± 2.54 with low variance under heterogeneous backgrounds. In contrast, EfficientNet and ResNet showed notable performance degradation when background conditions changed. This highlights that high accuracy under uniform conditions does not, in itself, guarantee practical grading applicability. Whereas InceptionNet consistently outperforms other architectures in terms of stability and generalization, and looks like a critical factor in designing reliable, low-cost smartphone-based arecanut grading systems.

title: A Realistic Control Approach for Set Stabilization of Complex Biological Networks With Logical Models
authors: Jung-Min Yang, Namhee Kim, Kwang-Hyun Cho
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: Driving cellular biological systems to one of desired long-term behaviors, an important subject in systems biology, can be interpreted as the set stabilization problem. This article presents a realistic control approach to set stabilization of complex biological networks modeled by Boolean networks (BNs) wherein only open-loop control is applied. First, the considered BN is transformed into an auxiliary BN having redundant state variables. Based on the similarity between desired attractors and Boolean algebra, a number of reduced BNs are constructed from the auxiliary BN in favor of set stabilization. Next, the feedback vertex set (FVS) control law is applied to reduced BNs for obtaining open-loop control inputs that take partial state variables toward desired values. This procedure continues in a recursive way until ensuring that the controlled BN converges to an unspecified desired attractor. The proposed scheme has the superiority that both fixed-point and cyclic attractors can be included in the problem setting, and that it can tackle complex BNs with moderate computational burdens. Extensive numerical experiments on random BNs and real biological systems are provided to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed scheme.

title: Switchable nonreciprocity of spin-wave interference patterns in in-plane/perpendicular-magnetic-anisotropy waveguide system
authors: Khalid A. Omari, Xingtai Chen, Matthew T. Bryan, Phil Meeson, Wenqing Liu
abstract: Wave-based computing paradigms exploit interference and nonreciprocal propagation as essential ingredients for non-Boolean information processing. In magnetic systems, spin-wave dynamics naturally provide access to such functionalities. Previously reported nonreciprocal spin-wave devices generally lack reconfigurability. Here, we demonstrate through micromagnetic simulations that exchange-coupled bilayers combining in-plane anisotropy and perpendicular magnetic anisotropy (PMA) form a reconfigurable waveguide enabling switchable nonreciprocal SW interference patterns. The nonreciprocal SW interference patterns arise from quasi-domain-wall nanochannels due to competing anisotropies, and the nonreciprocity depends on the switchable magnetization polarity of the PMA layer. This provides additional degrees of freedom in the design of magnonic computing devices.

title: Automotive MIMO OFDM Radar Demonstrator with 3D-Printed Waveguide Antennas
authors: Benjamin Nuss, Christian Karle, Tobias T. Braun, Jan Schoepfel, Florian Vogelsang, Farhan Bin Khalid, Eva Waldner, Nils Pohl, Thomas Zwick
abstract: Digital radar sensors are becoming increasingly popular for various applications. On the one hand, they offer a wide range of possibilities for generating uncorrelated signals for massive multiple-input multiple-output systems as well as for the synchronization of radar networks. On the other hand, the high sampling rates and memory sizes still pose a major challenge for implementation. This paper describes an OFDM radar demonstrator for automotive applications at 79 GHz, which enables wide signal bandwidths of up to 2 GHz. In addition, an exchangeable 3D-printed waveguide antenna array with four transmit and receive channels is used. Exemplary radar measurements prove the functionality of the demonstrator and show its range and angle separation capabilities using real objects.

title: Sem-Wavemamba: Disentangling Noise and Texture in Low-Light Image Enhancement Via Semantic-Guided Texture Dialysis
authors: Chengxin Xu, Tieru Wang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Low-light image enhancement (LLIE) remains a challenging task, particularly in balancing noise removal and texture preservation under high-ISO conditions. While recent Transformer-based methods achieve impressive results, they often incur prohibitive computational costs, limiting their practical deployment. Furthermore, lightweight CNNs struggle to distinguish between high-frequency structural details and severe sensor noise, leading to grid artifacts, ringing, or color shifts. To address these issues, we propose Sem-WaveMamba (SWM), an efficient architecture that integrates a Wavelet-Mamba encoder with a novel Semantic-Guided Texture Dialysis module. Specifically, SWM leverages the frequency-aware nature of wavelet transforms and the global modeling capabilities of Mamba to eliminate color casts and structural artifacts. Meanwhile, the semanticguided module acts as a filter, explicitly disentangling noise from texture based on semantic priors. Extensive experiments on realworld benchmarks demonstrate that SWM achieves state-of-theart robustness against severe noise. Notably, on 4 K resolution data, SWM outperforms Restormer while requiring <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0} \times$</tex> fewer MACs.

title: Pathogenic Gene Prediction Model Based on L3-Enhanced Diffusion Networks
authors: Haoyu Wu, Chun Wang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Effectively identifying disease-gene associations can reveal disease pathogenesis at the microscopic level and provide strategies for precision medicine. With the deepening exploration of biological network medicine, pathogenic gene prediction methods based on heterogeneous graph learning have been widely proposed and applied. Specially, enhanced graph representation methods based on the L3 principle have been proven to effectively improve prediction performance. However, existing research overlooks the interference of noise data on embedding quality, which further limits the learning performance of models. To address this shortcoming, we propose L3DiffNet, a diffusion-based heterogeneous graph learning framework. By incorporating an adaptive denoising mechanism tailored to biological network characteristics, it enables the accurate extraction of information from protein enrichment networks, thereby significantly improving pathogenic gene association prediction. Extensive crossvalidation experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our model outperforms all baselines.

title: A Novel Two-Stage Optimization Strategy for Solving Electric Field Inverse Problems in Multi-Circuit Overhead Transmission Lines
authors: Bangjie Xia, Yalong Mai, Jun Chen, Libing Tao, Yankai Xing, Daotian Pang, Haopeng An, Jian Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Non-contact electric field sensing has emerged as a key research area due to its inherent advantages of safety, flexibility, and real-time capability. However, utilizing electric field data to inversely reconstruct conductor voltages in Multi-Circuit systems presents significant challenges, including high dimensionality, parameter coupling, and numerical instability. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a two-stage optimization method that decomposes the inverse problem into two sub-problems: voltage estimation and position estimation. The approach enhances solution accuracy and stability through the application of constraint conditions and exploitation of system structural characteristics. Additionally, a sensor array is employed for spatially distributed measurements to reduce parameter coupling and improve observability. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated using double-circuit transmission lines as a demonstration case. Simulation and scaled experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves voltage estimation errors within ±5% and position estimation errors within ±3% under various operating conditions, confirming its effectiveness for non-contact measurement applications.

title: WEmarker: breast cancer-specific prognostic analysis with weighted multiplex network embedding
authors: Xingyi Li, Junming Li, Zhelin Zhao, Huihui Kong, Min Li, Xuequn Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
abstract: In clinical trials, prognostic biomarkers have become essential for guiding treatment decisions after breast cancer surgery. Network-based methods have gained notable attention to reveal marker genes, but many existing methods only focus on a single network, which inevitably neglects the incompleteness of interaction relationships within the network. Even when based upon the multiplex network, most of methods directly integrate the multiplex network into an aggregated network and do not take into account the inherent noise in the biological networks, which can not preserve the topological structure of each original network very well. In this study, we propose a novel method, WEmarker, for breast cancer-specific prognostic analysis. WEmarker reduces the noise level of biological networks and quantifies the probability of interactions between genes, and represents the nodes in the weighted multiplex network as vectors while efficiently retaining the structure information of these networks for identifying prognostic biomarkers. The results show that WEmarker outperforms comparative methods and the case study also demonstrates that biomarkers identified by WEmarker have reliable biological interpretability for breast cancer prognosis.

title: Joint Terminal Identification and Channel Estimation of Asynchronous Grant-Free Access for LEO Satellite-IoT Network
authors: Jiaqi Huang, Lixia Xiao, Wenhao Zheng, Jiaxi Zhou, Chengwen Xing, Tao Jiang
abstract: In this paper, the joint active terminal identification (ATI) and channel estimation (CE) problem is investigated for the asynchronous grant-free random access system in the context of low-earth orbit (LEO) satellite Internet of Things (IoT) network. An asynchronous grant-free model is established to characterize the implications of delay and Doppler caused by LEO satellites. Concurrently, a generalized approximate message passing-aided structured joint detection (SJD) scheme with Rician parameters learning (RPL) is proposed for joint ATI and CE. The prior mean and variance of the Rician channel are treated as hyperparameters and updated via the expectation-maximization algorithm. Furthermore, to alleviate the modeling mismatch, we develop an off-grid model following Taylor expansion, accompanied by a mismatch error parameter learning (MPL) framework to boost the accuracy of joint detection. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed RPL-SJD scheme outperforms existing approaches in terms of normalized mean square error with 4dB and activity detection error rate with 7dB.

title: IB2MC: Information Bottleneck Inspired Balanced Multiview Clustering
authors: Shengzhao Guo, Jingyu Wang, Letian Zhang, Feiping Nie, Xuelong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Information bottleneck (IB) theory aims to compact coding while retaining task-relevant information, but the lack of prior knowledge in clustering poses challenges to its application. Besides, since most methods fail to emphasize clustering security, empty clusters caused by excessive preference can lead to significant performance degradation. Motivated by these, a scalable framework is proposed, named Information Bottleneck Inspired Balanced Multiview Clustering (IB2MC), which boasts clustering security, optimization synergy, and tuning simplicity. To begin with, the in-depth discussion on unsupervised IB theory reveals the balance principle via maximizing label entropy, which preserves the uncertainty of cluster distribution to overcome bias or neglect for clusters. On this basis, compressed descriptors are expected to evolve toward discriminability, thus facilitating label inference from them at the same time. Inspired by this, our method proposes dynamic cosine graph learning for label transmission, and achieves seamless label extraction to strengthen dual-level consistency. Moreover, the representation alignment under independent constraint can enhance cluster separability, while the trace ratio for self-balance clustering can further avoid extra hyperparameter tuning. In this way, the time complexity of our method is linear with sample size, while no pre- or post-processing is required for joint optimization. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, seventeen state-of-the-art methods are chosen as baselines and our method ranks first in average performance across all twelve real-world data sets.

title: Microseis-WCDD:Wavelet-Conditioned Diffusion Denoising for Microseismic Data via Adversarial Guidance
authors: Liangliang Yao, Ying Shi, Yan Zhang, Wei Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Microseismic monitoring is a key technology in hydraulic fracturing of unconventional oil and gas reservoirs, with high-quality data essential for accurate detection and effective evaluation. To improve microseismic signals reliability and enhance event recognition accuracy, efficient denoising is crucial. Although recent deep learning approaches have improved microseismic denoising through powerful feature learning, diffusion-based methods still face several limitations, including high computational cost during sampling, limited ability to model non-Gaussian noise, and insufficient conditional constraints that may cause discrepancies between generated and real data distributions. To address these issues, this study proposes the Microseismic Wavelet-Conditioned Diffusion Denoising (Microseis-WCDD), a diffusion-based framework integrating wavelet conditioning and adversarial guidance. It systematically improves inference efficiency, modeling capability, while retaining the generative advantages of diffusion models: 1) Constructing an end-to-end signal prediction mechanism, combined with a wavelet domain multi-subband parallelization strategy, to significantly reduce sampling steps and improve inference efficiency; 2) Improving the forward diffusion process by incorporating mixed noise features to enhance the model’s adaptability in handling non-Gaussian noise; 3) Introducing a conditional diffusion mechanism and distribution consistency constraints to guide the model to focus on the key microseismic phase and achieve high-fidelity denoising. Experimental results on synthetic and field datasets demonstrate that the method achieves superior inference efficiency and denoising performance over existing methods, especially under low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions.
