title: A 6–85 GHz Non-Uniform Distributed Variable Gain Amplifier Achieving High Output Power and Efficiency in SiGe HBT Technology
authors: Burak G. Özat, Mathias P. Scharpf, Marc Günter, Maximilian Heckele, Benjamin Schoch, Ingmar Kallfass
abstract: This paper presents a non-uniform variable gain distributed amplifier implemented in 130-nm SiGe HBT BiCMOS technology. The design incorporates five cascode gain cells (GCs) with transistor sizes systematically scaled down along the signal path. Initial GCs utilize larger devices to extend the linear operation range and improve power efficiency. Further linearity enhancement and a reduction in effective input capacitance are achieved by introducing 15Ω resistive emitter degeneration in each GC, which also mitigates the need for highly inductive transmission lines, especially for the larger GCs. The design was validated by on-wafer measurements up to 110 GHz. The distributed amplifier demonstrates an average gain of 13 dB with a 79 GHz 3-dB bandwidth. Large-signal continuous wave measurements between 10 – 47 GHz yield a peak output-related 1-dB compression point (OP1dB) of 13.3 dBm and a power-added efficiency (PAE) of 11.62% at 17 GHz. The chip occupies only 0.62 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and consumes 176.4 mW power.

title: Acoustic Sensor-Based Azimuthal Far-Detection with Shear Waves: A Review
authors: Gao X H, Wen Y Y, Zhang M S, Zhang W H, Li Y H, Zhang Z H, Hei C, Liu P
abstract: Dipole shear wave far-detection technology can effectively identify the azimuth of geological bodies outside the wellbore, which is a core technology for fracture characterization and reservoir evaluation in oil and gas exploration and geothermal engineering. However, the inherent structural symmetry of traditional dipole sensors leads to a 180° azimuthal ambiguity in the reflected wavefield, which has long been a fundamental hardware bottleneck restricting the precise imaging of fractures and formation interfaces. To address this limitation, this review systematically examines the innovative acoustic sensor designs and systems developed to resolve azimuthal ambiguity from a sensor-centric perspective, which is fundamentally different from traditional algorithm-focused reviews. The reviewed technologies are classified into four categories: phase difference decoupling through multi-azimuth receiver sensor array innovation, asymmetric acoustic systems via source-receiver spatial layout optimization, beamforming focusing using phased array sensors, and multi-mode excitation sensor systems integrating monopole and dipole sources. For each technology, we systematically analyze its core symmetry-breaking mechanism, hardware-algorithm dependence, quantitative performance, and inherent limitations. On this basis, we establish a unified comparative framework for mainstream technologies, and clarify the evolution law of azimuth identification sensor technology. Finally, we anticipate the future development path of next generation sensor systems towards wideband, intelligent, and multi-physical field fusion, emphasizing that the next-generation sensor system is the core driver for quantitative reservoir evaluation

title: Integrated Mechanical–Electromagnetic Design and Experimental Validation of a High-Speed Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor Considering Sleeve Interference Fit and Harmonic Losses
authors: Sang-Hyeop Kim, Kyeong-Won Kwak, Jun-Ho Jang, Yong-Joo Kim, Kyung-Hun Shin, Jang-Young Choi
abstract: High-speed permanent magnet synchronous motors (HSPMSMs) are widely used in compact, high-performance applications, but their design is challenged by rotor mechanical integrity and accurate electromagnetic performance prediction under inverter-fed operation. Conventional approaches often assume ideal sinusoidal excitation, leading to inaccurate performance prediction. This paper proposes a harmonic-current-informed multiphysics design methodology for a surface-mounted HSPMSM with a retaining sleeve. An analytical rotor stress model considering interference fit, centrifugal force, and thermal expansion is validated using finite element method (FEM) simulations. Inverter-driven harmonic current spectra are predicted through circuit simulation and incorporated into electromagnetic analysis. The results show that harmonic currents significantly increase losses and reduce efficiency. Conventional sinusoidal-based analysis results in noticeable prediction errors, whereas the proposed approach achieves good agreement with experimental results. The proposed methodology improves prediction accuracy under realistic operating conditions and provides a reliable design framework for high-speed PMSMs.

title: Incipient Inter-Turn Short-Circuit Fault Diagnosis Based on Spatiotemporal Multibranch Graph Convolutional Network_supp2-3684175.mp4
authors: Qian Chen
abstract: Permanent magnet synchronous motors are widely used in various industrial drive systems. Accurately diagnosing inter-turn short-circuit (ITSC) faults in windings is a crucial prerequisite for ensuring the safe and stable operation of the drive system. Hence, this article proposes a spatiotemporal multibranch graph convolutional network (ST-MGCN) for the diagnosis of incipient ITSC faults. To address the challenge of weak fault features under complex conditions, ST-MGCN employs a three-branch architecture to simultaneously extract spatial, temporal, and global features from multichannel current data, thereby enriching the representation of fault features. A novel causality-driven fusion module adaptively reweights branch contributions and feature significances, preserving subtle incipient fault representations. Experimental validations on two distinct datasets exhibit the superior diagnostic accuracy and noise robustness of the proposed ST-MGCN. It demonstrates exceptional sensitivity to the weak features of the incipient ITSC fault. It maintains high performance under complex conditions, with varying speeds and load levels, outperforming the state-of-the-art methods.

title: CSI Feedback Based on Bi-Directional Channel Reciprocity Using Magnitude and Phase Separation
authors: Dan Liu, Jie Wu, Jindan Xu, Wei Xu, Derrick Wing Kwan Ng
abstract: In frequency division duplex (FDD) massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) systems, the uplink and downlink channel state information (CSI) exhibits an implicit reciprocal relationship, which can be leveraged for effective CSI compression and feedback. However, directly learning the reciprocity from complex-valued CSI matrices is prone to introducing irrelevant information or noise into the reconstructed downlink CSI, due to the high sensitivity of real and imaginary CSI components to frequency variations across the uplink and downlink. As the CSI magnitude is influenced by the propagation path common to both uplink and downlink, it demonstrates a strong degree of reciprocity than the real and imaginary parts of the CSI. To efficiently extract the reciprocal information, we propose a magnitude and phase separated CSI feedback neural network, named MPSCsiNet. Specifically, the proposed MPSCsiNet adopts a disentangled representation learning (DRL) network to accurately capture the reciprocal relationship between uplink and downlink CSI magnitudes, while applying a filtering approach to compress the essential information in the downlink CSI phases. Numerical results demonstrate that compared with the existing state-of-the-art AI compression feedback methods, MPSCsiNet can reduce the computational complexity by more than 80% while improving the accuracy of CSI recovery by approximately 4 dB, which verifies the advantages of integrating domain knowledge into deep learning (DL).

title: MDP3: A Multimodal 3D Diffusion Policy for Visuomotor Control
authors: Daifeng Li, Junwei Liu, Wei Zhang
abstract: Diffusion-based imitation learning has emerged as a dominant paradigm for robotic manipulation, offering stable training and multimodal action modeling. However, state-of-the-art diffusion policies predominantly rely on isolated sensory modalities—either 2D RGB images or 3D point clouds—limiting their robustness in unstructured environments where visual semantics and spatial geometry are jointly required. To bridge this gap, we propose a Multimodal 3D Diffusion Policy (MDP3), a unified framework that actively synthesizes heterogeneous sensory inputs for robust visuomotor control. MDP3 introduces two core architectural innovations: (1) a Gated Multimodal Unit (GMU) that acts as a context-aware arbitrator, dynamically modulating the fusion of RGB and point cloud features based on their real-time reliability; and (2) a Simplified PointNet++ (SPN2) encoder designed to inject local geometric inductive biases while maintaining the optimization stability required for diffusion training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MDP3 significantly outperforms existing single-modality baselines. Specifically, it improves the average success rate by 10.9% over the leading 3D diffusion policy, exhibiting superior generalization under diverse lighting and geometric variations.

title: SiGe HBT Optimizations for Power Amplifiers in G-band
authors: Nicolas Eyer, Sébastien Sadlo, Philippe Ferrari, Sylvain Bourdel, Didier Belot, Alexandre Siligaris
abstract: In this paper, a methodology is given to design the optimal SiGe heterojunction bipolar transistor (HBT) for a power amplifier (PA) in the G-band around 300 GHz. The methodology is split into two main parts. In the first part, transistor’s topology and emitter’s width are addressed with a small signal study. It is done on common-emitter (CE) and common-base (CB) architectures with theoretical equations and simulations. For both cases, the "Nx(CBEB)-C" topology with a minimal emitter’s width is shown optimal, leading to a higher gain. In the second part, trade-off between emitter’s length and number of emitters is addressed through large signal simulations on a cascode architecture. These simulations show that increasing the total emitter’s area increases the P1dB at the expense of gain. In addition, it is shown that for a same emitter’s area, a transistor with 2 emitters delivers the best performances. All simulations use a "npnxhs" HBT fabricated by STMicroelectronics in 55 nm "BiCMOS55X" technology (F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">t</inf> / F<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</inf> = 350 GHz / 500 GHz) with V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BE</inf> = 0.88 V and V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CE</inf> = 1.4 V.

title: Power-Constrained and Quantized MIMO-RSMA Systems with Imperfect CSIT: Joint Precoding, Antenna Selection, and Power Control
authors: Jiwon Sung, Seokjun Park, Jinseok Choi
abstract: To utilize the full potential of the available power at a base station (BS), we propose a joint precoding, antenna selection, and transmit power control algorithm for a total power budget at the BS. We formulate a sum spectral efficiency (SE) maximization problem for downlink multi-user multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) systems with arbitrary-resolution digital-to-analog converters (DACs). We reformulate the problem by defining the ergodic sum SE using the conditional average rate approach to handle imperfect channel state information at the transmitter (CSIT), and by using approximation techniques to make the problem more tractable. Then, we decompose the problem into precoding direction and power control subproblems. We solve the precoding direction subproblem by identifying a superior Lagrangian stationary point, and the power control subproblem using gradient descent. We also propose a complexity-reduction approach that is more suitable for massive MIMO systems. Simulation results not only validate the proposed algorithm but also reveal that when utilizing the full potential of the power budget at the BS, medium-resolution DACs with 8 ∼ 11 bits may actually be more power-efficient than low-resolution DACs.

title: Root-Protograph-based Anytime Codes for Non-Ergodic Block Fading Channels
authors: Xiaoxi Yu, Zhaojie Yang, Yong Liang Guan
abstract: The study of anytime codes over non-ergodic block-fading (BF) channels remains largely unexplored. To address this gap, we propose a novel root-protograph-based anytime (RP-anytime) coding framework and develop modified density evolution (DE) equations. Leveraging these equations, we introduce two performance metrics to evaluate the asymptotic behavior of RP-anytime codes under the assumption of infinite frame length: the average asymptotic bit error rate (BER), which characterizes anytime reliability, and the anytime outage region, which reflects the code’s ability to achieve full diversity. Using these metrics, we analyze the performance of the proposed RP-anytime codes and compare them with both root-protograph LDPC codes and conventional anytime codes. The results demonstrate the superiority of the RP-anytime structure in terms of reliability and diversity over non-ergodic BF channels. Furthermore, we employ a multi-objective differential evolution (MODE) algorithm to design optimized RP-anytime codes. Simulation results confirm that the newly designed codes achieve excellent anytime reliability, full diversity, and near-outage-limit error performance—significantly outperforming prior-art root-protograph LDPC codes and anytime codes.

title: An on-chip Split Ring Resonator for Graphene Dielectric Permittivity Characterization
authors: Zhichu Cao, Naveen Kolluru, Marcel Andree, Arjith Chandra Prabhu, Xinpeng Du, Zhuang Miao, Jonas Woeste, Michael Gensch, Daniel Neumaier, Ullrich R. Pfeiffer
abstract: This paper presents an on-chip split ring resonator (SRR) for graphene dielectric permittivity characterization. SRR shows a permittivity based frequency shift. The SRR is comprised of a LC-tank for resonant frequency tuning and a sensing strip for permittivity measurement. The SRR operates at a resonance frequency of 372.2 GHz, with a transmission loss of -10 dB, a Q factor of 9.8 and an area of 140μm × 250μm. When graphene is placed on the top of SRR, 39.6 GHz resonant frequency shift is measured. By correlating the simulation result from a 3-D full-wave EM solver (HFSS) and the measured frequency shift, the relative permittivity of graphene is characterized. The relative permittivity of graphene at 372.2 GHz is estimated to be ϵ′ = 2.95×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup>, while the theoretical absolute permittivity of CVD graphene at 372.2 GHz is in a range of 1.88 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup> and 5.61 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup>.

title: An Energy-Efficient Edge-Assisted Monitoring System for Youth Plyometric Training
authors: Qi Niu, Tao Li
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Sustainable youth sports call for data-driven, low-overhead training schemes. This study presents a 12 -week edge-intelligent plyometric training system integrating wearable inertial sensors (MPU-9250 IMU) with Raspberry Pi edge nodes. The system enables real-time load monitoring and localized data processing, significantly improving both change-of-direction (COD) and jumping performance in youth athletes. Sixty male adolescent athletes (14-16 y) were randomly assigned to an experimental group (three 45-min plyometric sessions per week) or a control group (standard PE). Illinois Agility, 505 COD, counter-movement jump and standing long jump were measured pre- and post-intervention. The experimental group significantly outperformed the control group in all four tests <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(p<0.01$</tex>, Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{d} \geqslant 0.8)$</tex>, with energy consumption reduced by 64% compared to traditional weight-training facilities. Pearson analysis revealed strong correlations between jumping and COD improvements <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(r =0.65-0.68)$</tex>, indicating that enhanced lower-limb explosiveness simultaneously benefits both skills. Edge-localized processing minimized data transmission overhead, demonstrating a scalable, resource-efficient approach for school-based athletic development. The results provide sustainable, green-computing evidence for integrating short-duration, high-efficiency plyometrics into youth athlete development programs.

title: IoT-Integrated WCIP-Based Modeling and Machine Learning Optimization of a THz Hydrogen Sensor for Green Hydrogen Monitoring
authors: V. Kalaipoonguzhali, Azath Mubarakali, Abdulrahman Saad Alqahtani, U. Arun Kumar, Aymen Hlali
abstract: This article presents a high-sensitivity terahertz (THz) hydrogen smart sensor based on three laterally cascaded spiral resonators functionalized with graphene, black phosphorus (BP), and vanadium dioxide (VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>). The electromagnetic behavior is rigorously modeled using an extended wave concept iterative process (WCIP), enabling efficient full-wave characterization of tunable 2-D materials without volumetric meshing. Hydrogen detection is achieved through resonance frequency shifts induced by refractive-index perturbations over concentrations spanning trace ppm levels to sub-lower-explosive-limit (LEL) regimes. The optimized architecture achieves sensitivities ranging from 8.4 to 9.03 THz/RIU while preserving high quality factors and sharp spectral features. To enable rapid parametric exploration, a gradient boosting regressor (GBR) surrogate model is developed to capture the nonlinear coupling between material conductivities and sensing performance, demonstrating strong predictive accuracy (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R^{2} \gt 0.996$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). The proposed smart sensor is designed for seamless integration with Internet-of-Things (IoT) frameworks, enabling real-time data acquisition, intelligent processing, and scalable deployment in advanced industrial environments. This smart sensing platform supports real-time hydrogen monitoring, hydrogen storage safety, and intelligent inspection of hydrogen infrastructure. Furthermore, integration within a 6G-enabled IoT ecosystem facilitates predictive analytics, automated monitoring, and efficient infrastructure management. The proposed system contributes to sustainable infrastructure development by enabling reliable hydrogen monitoring, predictive maintenance, and enhanced industrial safety. Overall, the proposed platform establishes a scalable pathway toward next-generation THz smart sensor technologies by synergistically combining physics-based electromagnetic modeling and data-driven optimization for industrial innovation and smart infrastructure applications.

title: A 129.9-141.1 GHz VCO Quadrature Frequency Generator Using Frequency Division and Mixing
authors: Manuel Koch, Jens Löffler, Chiara Kohl, Andre Engelmann, Sascha Breun, Robert Weigel, Norman Franchi
abstract: This paper presents a local oscillator signal generation scheme at D-band frequencies for homodyne transmitters and transceivers that require quadrature phases and reduced susceptibility to oscillator pulling by the integrated power amplifiers. A voltage-controlled oscillator at two-thirds of the output frequency is used to drive a static frequency divider that generates quadrature phases. Based on the divider outputs, subsequent mixing stages generate quadrature D-band signals. Individual characterization of the W-band oscillator core shows a tuning range from 86.7 to 94.3 GHz while the divider-based quadrature generator’s input frequency range covers the complete W-band and achieves < 2° divider output phase error at most frequencies. The oscillator-enabled quadrature generator operates over a 11.2 GHz (8.2 %) tuning range from 129.9 to 141.1 GHz, achieving a phase noise of -78 dBc/Hz at 1 MHz offset and a quadrature phase error of 6.5°. The circuits are fabricated using 130 nm BiCMOS technology.

title: Low Operation Point In-loop Filter for VVC Based on Over-parameterized Training and Variable Channel Configurations
authors: Jiang Han, Cheolkon Jung, Qipu Qin, Yi Liu
abstract: The low operation point (LOP) in-loop filter, which has been integrated into the neural network-based video coding (NNVC) reference software in VVC, uses two types of backbone blocks (i.e., BBB-A and BBB-B) to reduce network complexity and ensure coding efficiency. In this paper, we propose an LOP in-loop filter for VVC based on over-parameterized training and variable channel configurations. We replace the 1×3 and 3×1 separable convolutions in the LOP BBB with the 1×5 and 5×1 separable convolutions to increase the receptive field and enhance the prediction accuracy of the LOP network. For over-parameterized training, we extend the 1×5 and 5×1 separable convolutions into a multi-branch structure to enhance the multi-scale feature extraction capability of the LOP BBB. Moreover, we adopt variable channel configurations in the LOP BBB to enhance the richness of its input information while reducing network complexity. Therefore, the proposed method enhances the Bjøntegaard Delta rate (BD-rate) performance while reducing the complexity of LOP network from 16.83k multiply-accumulate (kMAC)/pixel to 16.81 kMAC/pixel. Experimental results show the proposed method achieves average BD-rate gains of {0.15% (Y), 1.59% (U), 1.65% (V)} in all intra (AI) configuration and {0.04% (Y), 1.75% (U), 1.75% (V)} in random access (RA) configuration over the VTM-11.0_NNVC-11.0 anchor.

title: 1 Transistor-Dynamic Random Access Memory as Reservoir for Temporal Signal Processing_supp1-3690766.docx
authors: Yasir Bashir MD

title: APaC: a Context-aware and Availability Policy as Code Framework for Elastic Object Storage
authors: Ignacio Castillo-Barrios, J. L. Gonzalez-Compean, Ivan Lopez-Arevalo, Jose Juan Garcia-Hernandez
abstract: Replication strategies provide availability for cloud storage, yet existing policies typically rely on static popularity metrics. These works struggle to react dynamically to contextual workload variations, specifically the temporal increments and decays in user concurrency and data consumption. This paper introduces APaC, a framework that combines a context-reactive replication method with an Availability Policy-as-Code programming model for storage systems to autonomously update availability policies and adapt at runtime to workload fluctuations. The APaC model defines replication policies through primitives that specifies who, what, where, how, and when should replicate data, which is interpreted and executed by a configurable and reactive replication system without modifying the storage system basis code core. Under scenarios of read/write intensity and concurrency variation, dynamic adjustments to availability policies enabled effective responsiveness of storage to workload changes, which yielded high throughput rates, and lower capacity consumption. APaC outperformed state-of-the-art policies, achieving gains of 16.6% over time-distribution-based, 26% over object-lifetime-based, 34.6% over popularity-based strategies, and more than 70% over the baseline configuration that produce three replicas for each five nodes. It was also observed that performance gains increased with higher levels of concurrency, underscoring the effectiveness of dynamically managing availability policies.

title: The Synergy of Giants and Scouts—Edge-Enabled Collaboration Between Large-Scale and Lightweight Models in Sensor-Cloud Networks
authors: Tian Wang, Yao Liu, Geyong Min, Nektarios Georgalas, Yan Zhang

title: 3D thermal-hydraulic modeling an in-cryostat Loss-of-Coolant Accident from a superconducting magnet
authors: Mauro Sprò, Antonio Froio, Andrea Zappatore
abstract: Accidental transients in superconducting magnet systems for tokamak fusion reactors require thorough analysis, as they can jeopardize the integrity of critical, expensive, and often irreparable components like Toroidal Field (TF) coils. The in-cryostat Loss-of-Coolant Accident (LOCA) is particularly critical among these scenarios. This event is triggered by the release of pressurized Supercritical Helium (SHe), a phenomenon observed in experimental facilities, including ITER Central Solenoid Module (CSM) testing and JT-60SA commissioning. The SHe is discharged into the vacuum cryostat, forming an underexpanded supersonic jet; this rapid expansion results in a multiphase jet comprising supercritical, vapor, and liquid phases. Assessing potential magnet damage and developing diagnostics requires detailed knowledge of the 3D evolution of pressure and temperature distributions. Therefore, a 3D Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) model is essential for capturing the complex spatial and temporal dynamics of cryostat pressurization and jet propagation. In this work, a 3D transient analysis of the in-cryostat LOCA is performed, using a simplified ITER CSM geometry. The model simulates the SHe jet propagation from the moment of rupture for several minutes, allowing sufficient time for the module to heat-up. The physical models, especially the multiphase model, are validated against a 2D benchmark problem before application to the 3D scenario. In addition, an Adaptive Mesh Refinement algorithm dynamically tracks the expected shock fronts. Particular attention is paid to the temperature distribution in the CSM, with its evolution monitored from the onset of the accident to the end of the transient.

title: Enhancing Automatic Berthing of Unmanned Surface Vessels: A Finite-Time Observer Approach with Flapped Rudder Actuation
authors: Zaiji Piao, Shuang Sun, Yingying Yu
abstract: The increasing frequency of coastal accidents has drawn growing attention to vessel maneuvering in near-shore areas. Consequently, autonomous berthing of unmanned surface vessels (USVs) has emerged as a prominent research focus, though it faces significant challenges due to low-speed navigation, shallow water, and bank effects. This paper presents an automatic berthing control method for USVs equipped with flapped rudders, utilizing a finite-time observer-based backstepping controller. The proposed approach integrates a finite-time disturbance observer to estimate and compensate for external disturbances, such as wind and waves, in real time, while the backstepping control framework ensures precise trajectory tracking and stability throughout the berthing process. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves rapidity and disturbance rejection compared with conventional control strategies. The finite-time observer enables faster convergence of estimation errors, and the backstepping controller guarantees robustness against system uncertainties. Numerical simulations under various sea states validate the effectiveness of the control scheme, confirming its superiority in achieving accurate and stable autonomous berthing for USVs. The proposed combination of a finite-time observer and backstepping sliding-mode control, together with the high maneuverability provided by the flapped rudder, effectively addresses complex environmental disturbances and system nonlinearities during automatic berthing, enabling high-precision and highly stable autonomous docking.

title: Plug-and-Play Distributed Fencing of Multi-Vehicle Systems for Multiple Uncooperative Targets With Dynamic Radius
authors: Song Jiang, Jiang Zhao, Luning Yang, Pei Chi, Yingxun Wang
abstract: This paper investigates the multiple target fencing problem of multi-vehicle systems (MVSs), addressing a group of uncooperative targets (GUT) with an unknown dynamic target radius. A plug-and-play distributed multiple target fencing strategy is proposed that operates independently of global information, comprising a distributed fencing center observer, a distributed fencing radius estimator, and a distributed balanced fencing controller. Under this strategy, all vehicles can observe a fencing center within the GUT's convex hull and a dynamic fencing radius in finite time. Moreover, dynamic balanced fencing for the GUT is achieved, ensuring the convergence of all vehicle-center distances to the dynamic fencing radius and an equal angle distribution among vehicles. Furthermore, the plug-and-play feature of this strategy enables vehicles to join or leave the MVS and new targets to join the GUT during the fencing process. In contrast, the remaining vehicles can still achieve the multiple target fencing without requiring parameter adjustments for activated vehicles. The strategy's efficacy is validated through rigorous theoretical analysis and corroborated by numerical experiments.

title: A 3D-Printed GRIN Lens Array for Hybrid Beamforming: Design and Experimental Validation
authors: Yubo Wen, Shiyao Zhu, Zhaoqi Cui, Lizhao Song, Hao Zhang, Peiyuan Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper presents a 13 GHz hybrid beamforming system integrating a gradient-index (GRIN) lens array, radio-frequency (RF) switches, and a digital beamforming processor. Compared with conventional phased arrays, the proposed system significantly reduces the number of phase shifters required. For the lens array design, a beam-interleaved architecture is proposed to effectively suppress grating lobes in both linear and planar array configurations, thereby enabling robust scanning performance with low sidelobe levels (SLLs) and high aperture efficiency. To validate the proposed architecture, a hybrid beamforming system based on a 4-lens linear array was fabricated and measured. The measurement results show that the lens array achieves a realized gain of approximately 22 dBi and a 5° beamwidth. The overall system demonstrates a scanning range of ±36° with a scanning loss below 1 dB. The aperture efficiency of the scanning system is over 48%.

title: Design and Development of an End-Effector for Fabric Edge Folding Along a Convex Curve With Multiple Pleats
authors: Akira Seino, Akinari Kobayashi, Fuyuki Tokuda, Kai Tang, Kazuhiro Kosuge
abstract: In this article, we propose a novel end-effector designed for folding fabric edges along convex curves. Edge folding is essential in garment manufacturing for fabric parts, such as pockets, front panel hems, and cuffs before sewing. Existing automated edge folding systems rely on templates or fixtures designed for specific fabric parts. These systems must be reconfigured to perform edge folding of different fabric parts. In order to solve this problem, we developed a robot fabric edge folding system along straight lines using the end-effector for free-form folding (F-FOLD) in our previous study. In this article, we propose a new end-effector designed to fold fabric edges along convex curves and straight lines. When the fabric edge is folded along convex curves, generating multiple pleats is necessary to form a smooth folded edge. In this article, we first analyze the pleats generation mechanism and design a new robot end-effector, F-FOLD2, that actively generates pleats during the folding process along convex curves. Experiments show that fold width, rather than curvature, affects the number of pleats, and the average deviation from the desired folding line is within 1.0 mm. These results demonstrate that the proposed F-FOLD2 enables accurate, template-less folding along convex curves and straight lines, offering an alternative solution for flexible, automated garment manufacturing.

title: A Triangular Pyramid Geometry-Guided Hand-Eye Calibration Framework for Line Laser Cameras With Constrained Optimization_supp1-3693129.mp4
authors: Jianliang Mao
abstract: Hand-eye calibration of line laser cameras (LLCs) is a prerequisite for accurate 3D measurement and reliable welding path execution in robotic welding systems. However, calibration methods based on planar or spherical targets often exhibit fitting ambiguity, sensitivity to illumination variations, and limited robustness under industrial operating conditions. To address these limitations, this paper presents a novel hand–eye calibration framework guided by the geometry of a triangular pyramid target for LLCs. By exploiting the surface normal discontinuities of the triangular pyramid, the proposed approach induces stable and unique laser extremum features during scanning, enabling consistent feature extraction and well-posed geometric calibration constraints. Under these constraints, the hand-eye transformation is formulated as a constrained optimization problem and solved using sequential least squares quadratic programming (SLSQP), which enforces rotational orthogonality and bounded translation. Experiments on a real robotic welding system show that the proposed method achieves a calibration error of 0.648 mm based on the bootstrap 95% confidence analysis, and maintains reliable performance across both comparative and field evaluations.

title: Unilateral Upper Limb Fine Motor Imagery Recognition via Symmetric Cross-Modal Attention EEG-EMG Fusion_supp1-3680124.docx
authors: Xueliang Bao
abstract: Precise sensing and decoding of unilateral upper limb fine motor intentions are critical for developing robust brain–computer interfaces (BCIs). However, noninvasive electroencephalography (EEG) faces inherent limitations, including low spatial resolution and significant signal overlap. These constraints hinder unimodal methods from effectively decoupling complex motor features. To address these challenges, this article proposes a cross-attentional temporal convolutional network (CATA-Net), a multimodal fusion architecture combining EEG and electromyography (EMG). The model operates on an intention–execution synergistic mechanism to exploit the complementarity between central neural and peripheral muscle signals. The framework constructs modality-specific feature extractors. The EEG branch employs depthwise separable convolution with atrous spatial pyramid pooling to capture multiscale spatiotemporal dependencies. The EMG branch utilizes a dilated residual temporal convolutional network to extract dynamic muscle activation patterns. Crucially, unlike standard cross-attention frameworks that merely perform generic semantic feature exchange, the proposed symmetric cross-modal attention (SCMA) module explicitly addresses the inherent spatiotemporal misalignment caused by variables. It establishes a bidirectional channel between cortical intention and muscle execution to actively anchor EEG spatial representations to EMG temporal dynamics for mutual feature calibration. Experiments with 25 subjects covered three real movement (RM) tasks and two motor imagery (MI) tasks. CATA-Net achieved 98.3% accuracy in RM scenarios. Furthermore, in challenging MI grasping and twisting tasks, it outperformed the unimodal ATCNet baseline by 7.7% and 14.7%. Analysis confirms the effective integration of EEG spatial coding with EMG fine force control. This study provides a high-precision decoding solution offering novel perspectives on multimodal neural synergies.

title: A novel Physically Enhanced Real-Time Visual Navigation method for UAVs
authors: Zhijie Chen, Hongbo Li, Long Wang, Yong Chen, Yuxuan Yuan, Tianxing Li
publish: IEEE Journal on Miniaturization for Air and Space Systems
abstract: To address the degradation of visual navigation and positioning accuracy in UAVs under hazy weather conditions, this paper proposes a TANet dehazing model as the baseline framework. By introducing a dark channel attention mask and a dark channel consistency loss, a deep fusion framework integrating physical guidance and data-driven image restoration is constructed. Firstly, during the feature extraction stage of TANet, a dark channel attention mask is applied, transforming the traditional dark channel prior into a differentiable spatial weighting scheme. This explicitly enhances the network’s perceptual capability for regions with dense haze through feature modulation. Secondly, in the training optimization stage, a dark channel consistency loss is introduced, which imposes physical-statistical constraints to ensure the output according to the physical characteristics of haze-free images. Experimental results demonstrate that the enhanced TANet model not only preserves its original computational speed and performance but also significantly improves the quality of image restoration across varying haze scenarios. The restored images provide more reliable inputs for UAV visual navigation systems, which improves the accuracy of feature matching and pose estimation, and offers technical support for autonomous navigation in complex meteorological conditions.

title: Heat Treatment for Bi-2212 CICC-type Insert Coil
authors: Meng-Liang Zhou, Min Yu, Ming Deng, Dong-sheng Yang, Hua-Jin Zhu, Zhen-Chuang Zhang, Huan Jin, Chao Zhou, Jing-Gang Qin
abstract: A heat treatment system, which is used in combination with a stepwise heat treatment method, has been constructed at the Institute of Plasma Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (ASIPP) for the primary Bi-2212 (Bi2Sr2CaCu2Ox) CICC (cable-in-conduit conductors)-type high-field insert coil. The design and manufacturing of the first Bi-2212 CICC-type insert coil has been completed. Prior to the final heat treatment for the CICC-type coil, a validation experiment is required to demonstrate the effectiveness and feasibility of the system and method. The validation experiment was concluded with the implementation of a heat treatment process on the model coil and the witness samples. In the course of the validation experiment, the various parameters of the heat treatment process – including temperature, pressure and oxygen content – were subjected to meticulous monitoring by a range of measuring instruments. The temperature evolution and uniformity of the process meet the technical requirements of the heat treatment process for the Bi-2212 CICC-type coil. It is evident that the critical performance of the witness samples meets the requisite value. The results of the study demonstrate the efficacy and feasibility of the system and method of heat treatment employed in the final heat-treatment process of the first Bi-2212 CICC-type coil. Consequently, the concluding phase of the heat treatment process was initiated on the inaugural Bi-2212 CICC-type insert coil. It is evident from the findings of the experiment that the coil displays a critical current of 5.4 kA at 4.2 K and 20 T background field, thus indicating its superior capacity. The success of the heat treatment for the first Bi-2212 CICC-type coil establishes a solid foundation for the heat treatment of the future large-scale and full-size Bi-2212 cable-in-conduit conductor.

title: A Rescheduling Mechanism for Cloud Data Centers Leveraging Dynamic Fault-Tolerant Classification
authors: Bin Liang, Shaohui Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: The proliferation of cloud computing has prompted leading technology providers to establish extensive cloud data center (CDC) infrastructures. Balancing service quality and profitability is a crucial goal due to the intense competition brought about by constantly upgrading user demands. As a result, suppliers are increasingly relying on complex fault-tolerant mechanisms to ensure the reliability of CDC. The traditional rescheduling method is a commonly used approach. However, this often leads to longer completion times or increased compensation, thereby damaging reputation. We investigates operational inefficiencies attributable to virtual machine (VM) failures and failed cloud task (FCT) rescheduling in CDC. Our contribution includes three aspects. First, we present a dynamic classification rule of FCT according to the attribute for the FCT. Second, building upon this rule, we propose a rescheduling mechanism for CDC leveraging dynamic fault-tolerant classification (RMDFC) algorithm to concurrently enhance failure repair rate and minimize compensation overhead. Finally, perform performance tests on the RMDFC algorithm. The failure repair rate of RMDFC algorithm has increased by an average of 3.8 times compared to the comparative algorithms. At the same time, compensation decreased by an average of 58% and profit increased by an average of 9% for cloud service providers in CDCs. The test results show that the RMDFC algorithm exhibits excellent performance.

title: Citizen Sentiment and Public Value in AI-Mediated Public Services: A Case Study of a Ministry Chatbot
authors: Tehila Tigist Neguse, Hagar Gabay, Iris Reychav, Roger McHaney, Gideon Mekonnen Jonathan
publish: Proceedings of the 63rd ACM Conference on Computers and People Research
abstract: None

title: MeanCut: Greedy graph clustering by fast maximum spanning tree and degree descent criterion_supp1-3692651.pdf
authors: Zhipeng Gui
abstract: As the most typical graph clustering method, spectral clustering is popular and attractive due to its remarkable performance, easy implementation, and strong adaptability. Classical spectral clustering measures the edge weights using pairwise Euclidean similarity and resolves the optimal graph partitioning by relaxing the constraints of indicator matrix and decomposing the Laplacian matrix. However, Euclidean similarity might cause skew graph cuts when handling non-spherical clusters, and the relaxation strategy introduces information loss. Meanwhile, spectral clustering requires specifying the number of clusters, which is difficult to determine without enough prior knowledge. In this work, we propose a greedy-optimized scheme for resolving the indicator matrix using path-based similarity and degree descent criterion. It yields an indicator matrix with strictly binary entries without destructive relaxation and discretization steps. Path-based similarity can enhance the intra-cluster associations of arbitrary-shaped clusters, while degree descending is theoretically proven to be the best order to minimize our proposed objective function MeanCut. Moreover, we define a density gradient factor to separate clusters with fuzzy boundaries, and develop a fast maximum spanning tree algorithm to improve the scalability of similarity calculation. The effectiveness of MeanCut has been demonstrated on synthetic datasets and real-world benchmarks. By fusing multi-view image features, MeanCut outperforms cutting-edge subspace clustering methods in face recognition. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/ZPGuiGroupWhu/MeanCut</uri>.
