title: UVC: A Large Scale Dataset and Physical Priors-based Network Towards Underwater Video Compression
authors: Mengyao Li, Liquan Shen, Haiyan Zhang, Feifeng Wang, Zhaoyi Tian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The booming underwater applications require efficient underwater video compression technology to transmit numerous videos capturing ocean scenes through the narrow acoustic channel. Recently, based on a large amount of training data, learned video compression methods have achieved better performance than traditional methods on general terrestrial scenes. However, there is no large-scale underwater video dataset sufficient to train the underwater video compression network. To this end, this paper first constructs a Large Scale Underwater Video Compression Dataset (LS-UVCD) for the underwater video compression society. Specifically, to construct the LS-UVCD, we manually select a large amount of underwater videos based on scene similarity, clarity, and duration validity of original videos and filter 8426 high-quality video sequences. Through extensive statistical analysis, it is verified that the LS-UVCD covers rich underwater spatial texture types and diverse temporal motion patterns, making LS-UVCD adequate for training great underwater video compression performance. After that, we further propose an efficient Underwater Video Compression Network (UVC-Net), which combines the characteristics of underwater videos with the motion compensation mechanism. Specifically, based on the underwater imaging physical model, it can be analyzed that the details magnitudes of underwater videos are severely heterogeneous and are related to the imaging physical priors, including the attenuation coefficient and imaging distance. Hence, UVC-Net includes an adaptive quantization based on underwater imaging physical priors, which utilizes the priors estimated from the decoded frame and the motion optical flow to predict the global (video-wise) and local (spatial-wise) quantization step map for scenes and regions with varying details magnitudes. Finally, comprehensive experiments are conducted to verify that the proposed UVC-Net trained on LS-UVCD<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup> consistently outperforms existing methods.

title: Large-Signal Stability Modeling for Grid-Forming Inverters Capturing Inner-Loop Dynamics and Considering Anti-Windup
authors: Yi Xiao, Yang Shao, Guang Hu, Hao Ruan, Yongheng Yang, Marta Molinas
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy sources, non-rotational power electronics are widely adopted as grid interfaces, leading to low-inertia power systems. In this context, grid-forming (GFM) control becomes essential for grid stability. However, as GFM inverters have limited overloading capability, current limiters (CL) are usually employed to restrain fault currents under grid disturbances. Once being activated, the CL forces the GFM inverter to operate in the current-limiting mode (CLM). Then, the interaction of the CL with anti-windup schemes and inner control loops exhibits high-order large-signal dynamics. This makes the stability analysis more challenging. To address this issue, this paper develops an accurate high-order large-signal model for the GFM inverters with a virtual-impedance-based back-calculation method (VIBCM). The developed model captures the nonlinear inner-loop behaviors under the CLM and enables accurate large-signal stability analysis. With the proposed model, the stability boundaries with respect to the magnitude and phase angle of the VIBCM are derived, and accordingly, the instability mechanisms are explored. It has been revealed that an excessively small virtual impedance magnitude degrades the anti-windup performance and traps the inverter in the CLM. By contrast, an overly large virtual impedance magnitude drives the inverter toward instability due to the inner-loop dynamics, and potentially, triggers large-signal instability of the overall system. In addition, design guidelines are provided to assist the selection of parameters for the VIBCM with robust large-signal stability. Experimental tests validate the correctness of the proposed model and the theoretical analysis

title: Light First, RF Assist: Spatial Optimisation for Minimum Energy Guarantees in Battery-less IoT Network
authors: Khodr Hammoud, Hubregt J. Visser, Hazem Sallouha, Dominique Schreurs, Sofie Pollin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: The rapid expansion of battery-less Internet of Things (IoT) deployments demands energy delivery strategies that minimise maintenance while ensuring reliable operation. This paper studies the placement optimisation of photovoltaic (PV)-equipped donor IoT nodes that harvest dedicated/ambient light power and redistribute surplus energy via radiofrequency (RF) transmissions, together with targeted ceiling-mounted RF transmitters.We formulate an illuminance-informed donor placement problem across heterogeneous light zones and extend it to a co-design framework that integrates dedicated RF support with donor placement. To address these problems, we develop a greedy illuminance-aware algorithm and a joint greedy–genetic algorithm for co-design, supported by a complexity analysis. Numerical results in a 5×5 m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> indoor simulation testbed demonstrate that the co-design approach reduces donor counts by up to 33% while maintaining the required energy thresholds. These results indicate that deployment-aware optimisation can effectively sustain dense IoT networks under practical illumination and RF constraints.

title: Nonparametric Control Koopman Operators
authors: Petar Bevanda, Bas Driessen, Lucian Cristian Iacob, Stefan Sosnowski, Roland Tóth, Sandra Hirche
abstract: This paper presents a novel Koopman composition operator representation framework for control systems in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHSs) that is free of explicit dictionary or input parametrizations. By establishing fundamental equivalences between different model representations, we are able to close the gap of control system operator learning and infinite-dimensional regression, enabling various empirical estimators and the connection to the well-understood learning theory in RKHSs under one unified framework. Consequently, our proposed framework allows for arbitrarily accurate finite-rank approximations in infinite-dimensional spaces and leads to finite-dimensional predictors without a priori restrictions to a finite span of functions or inputs. To enable applications to high-dimensional control systems, we improve the scalability of our proposed control Koopman operator estimates by utilizing sketching techniques. Numerical experiments demonstrate superior prediction accuracy compared to bilinear EDMD, especially in high dimensions. Finally, we show that our learned models are readily interfaced with linear-parameter-varying techniques for model predictive control.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Time-To-Digital Converters: Architectures, Applications, and Trends
authors: Md Anas Abdullah, Mohamed Elamien, M. Jamal Deen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Precise time measurement is a fundamental pillar of modern electronics, enabling applications that range from biomedical imaging and autonomous driving to quantum computing. Key to this capability is the Time-to-Digital Converter (TDC), a mixed-signal circuit that functions as an ultrafast stopwatch, resolving time intervals finer than a single logic gate delay. The integration of TDCs into standard CMOS technology, particularly alongside single-photon avalanche diodes, has been transformative. This synergy creates the “digital eyes” for Time-of-Flight (ToF) based 3D ranging, Light Detection and Ranging, and fluorescence lifetime imaging. Further, driven by industrial demands for higher performance and lower power, TDC architectures have evolved into highly efficient digital building blocks. To enable fair benchmarking across fifteen years of diverse design progress, this review introduces four application-aware figures of merit (FoMs). Our analysis, supported by a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art implementations, reveals that no single architecture simultaneously optimizes all metrics. Instead, each architecture aligns with specific application needs: ring-oscillator (RO)-based TDCs offer the best overall balance of resolution, dynamic range, power, and area for practical, power-constrained systems. Stochastic and hybrid TDCs excel in resolution and sampling rate, albeit with reduced dynamic range, making them attractive for all-digital timing systems such as All-Digital Phase-Locked Loops. In contrast, counter-based (Nutt-method) TDCs provide wide dynamic range and high effective resolution, positioning them as key enablers for long-range ToF sensing. The paper concludes with a forward-looking discussion of emerging applications, persistent challenges, and future research directions in TDC development.

title: On Design of Tomlinson-Harashima Precoded OTFS System
authors: Yonghua Liu, Tao Yang, Rongke Liu, Ling Zhao
abstract: An orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) scheme that operates in the delay–Doppler (DD) domain has competitive performance in high-mobility wireless environments. However, its complexity for tackling the interferences among the DD grids remains too high for many practical applications. In this paper, we study a DD-domain Tomlinson-Harashima precoding (THP) approach for OTFS system. First, a new DD-domain selective zero-padding (SZP) scheme is developed, which addresses the non-causality of the time–frequency doubly-selective channel (DSC) matrix is introduced. Based on that, THP that pre-cancels the DD domain interference on a symbol-by-symbol basis is carried out, where the requirement of large matrix inversion is avoided. Further, we derive the exact a posteriori probability (APP) for optimal soft detection in the THP-OTFS system with state-of-the art channel codes. Numerical results demonstrate that our proposed THP-OTFS scheme brings up to 5 dB improvement in complex multipath scenario over the benchmark system operated with linear precoding. For the channel-coded THP-OTFS system, our developed optimal soft detection brings about 2 dB improvement over existing THP method. Also, the complexity is significantly reduced relative to the linear precoding scheme.

title: Sum Rate Maximization for Distortion-Aware RSMA System via Accelerated Gradient-Based Precoding With Near-Optimal Power Scaling
authors: Saehan Song, Jeongju Jee, Hyesang Cho, Junil Choi
abstract: This paper investigates a practical precoding scheme for rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) in multi-user multiple-input single-output (MU-MISO) systems where the nonlinear characteristics of power amplifiers are explicitly taken into account. To enhance performance in the presence of distortion caused by nonlinear power amplifiers, we propose an iterative accelerated gradient-based precoding scheme. The proposed precoding scheme is further integrated with a novel power scaling function that dynamically adjusts transmit power to satisfy practical power consumption constraints while maximizing an achievable sum rate. The gradient of the achievable sum rate for the considered system is derived to support efficient gradient-based optimization. We also prove that the proposed iterative precoding scheme converges to a critical point. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed precoding scheme consistently outperforms modified conventional schemes including distortion-aware and weighted minimum mean square error beamforming, across both overloaded and underloaded scenarios. In particular, the proposed scheme demonstrates consistent performance in high-power regimes, where conventional schemes exhibit severe degradation due to distortion. Furthermore, the proposed precoding scheme exhibits faster convergence compared to the conventional benchmark.

title: Nonlinear precise tracking and regulation for unmanned aircraft flight control
authors: Zhang Hongyan, Guo Xiaoyong
abstract: Flight controller is a brain for unmanned aircraft, being responsible for processing flight data from different sources, but classical control strategies often firstly linearize the original nonlinear unmanned aircraft model into one simplified linear model and then secondly apply the existing linear control methods. Due to inaccuracy of the simplified linear model, the consequent linear controller cannot guarantee unmanned aircraft fly with safety, perfect tracking and satisfied requirements, so this paper proposes the nonlinear controller based on differential geometry for the goal of precise tracking and regulation, i.e. our named nonlinear precise tracking and regulation. More specifically, after reviewed the typical flight control loop for unmanned aircraft, the entire flight control loop means a closed loop with both nonlinear controller and nonlinear model. Consider our precise tracking and regulation, speed gradient algorithm is applied to design one nonlinear controller for nonlinear model without that linearization process. Furthermore, one additional unknown parametric term is added in nonlinear model, thus leading to both nonlinear controller design and parameter estimation, speed gradient algorithm is also proposed to design nonlinear controller and identify unknown parameters simultaneously, and its convergence analysis is given to prove our goal, i.e. precise tracking and regulation. Latter a more general case with one unknown parametric term is considered. After constructing one parameter adjustment law, one explicit nonlinear adaptive controller is yielded to satisfy closed loop stability and precise tracking and regulation, then the corresponding robustness analysis is dealt with. Finally, to prove the efficiency of our theoretical results about nonlinear precise tracking and regulation for nonlinear unmanned aircraft model, a practical platform is established to do some interesting simulation.

title: Experience with Assistive and Service Robots in the General Population of Hungary
authors: Anikó Vágvölgyi, Levente Kovács, László Gulácsi, Márta Péntek
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Societal readiness for social robots is an underexplored area despite its decisive role in their implementation. This study aimed to investigate the geographical and demographic patterns of assistive or service robot encounters in Hungary, supplemented with regional Robot Anxiety Scale (RAS) analyses. A cross-sectional survey among a representative sample of the Hungarian adult population (N = 1000) was conducted. Overall, 15.50% of the participants reported having met an assistive or service robot in person ever, with significant demographic variations. Higher education levels, full-time employment, and younger age groups were significantly associated with increased robot encounters. Geographical analysis revealed substantial variations across the counties both in frequency of robot encounters and anxiety levels towards robots. Findings highlight significant sociodemographic and geographic stratification in assistive or social robot exposure and anxiety towards robots across Hungary, with important implications for future implementation, particularly in healthcare settings. Results can be used for developing targeted strategies to bridge exposure gaps and foster more consistent acceptance of robotic technologies.

title: Service-Oriented Orchestration and Scheduling in Agentic Computing Power Networks
authors: Jingzhao Xie, Gang Sun, Boquan Feng, Long Luo, Shuai Gao, Hongfang Yu, Dusit Niyato, Hongke Zhang
abstract: Smart devices are performing complex, context- aware tasks that far exceed traditional human-oriented application capabilities. This evolution calls for a new service paradigm - generalized services - able to accommodate diverse, device-specific requirements. However, current service provisioning remains resource-centric and template-driven, lacking flexibility to meet emerging and heterogeneous demands. This creates a significant gap between advanced resource management models, such as Computing Power Networks (CPNs), and static provisioning approaches, hindering on-demand service delivery. To address this, we propose a service-oriented declarative provisioning (SoDP) approach that leverages agentic autonomy to shift focus from static resource allocation to dynamic, intent-driven management. Crucially, enabling this declarative paradigm requires agentic orchestration and scheduling capabilities: the system must autonomously perceive user declarations and, through service abstraction as a bridge between user intent and system execution, reason about what services to compose, where to locate them, and how to instantiate them. We present the SoDP controller - an agentic orchestration and scheduling framework that incorporates large language model (LLM)-in-the-loop composition, hierarchical scheduling, and synergy-aware mapping. Simulation-based case studies demonstrate the effectiveness of SoDP for next-generation digital infrastructures.

title: MW-class Microwave Pulse Compressors operating with a Low-Pressure Gas Discharge Tube at self-breakdown mode
authors: Vasiliki A. Katsara, Konstantinos E. Orfanidis, Zisis C. Ioannidis, Stylianos P. Savaidis
abstract: Microwave Pulse Compressors (MPC) based on a waveguide cavity with a Gas Discharge Tube (GDT) acting as a switching unit to extract the stored energy can produce compressed pulses at excessively high peak amplitudes. This work aims to present an overview of the theoretical models that simulate both charging and discharging phases of such MPCs when operate with a low-pressure GDT in self-breakdown mode. The utilization of the theoretical models in design and optimization studies is also discussed. The key findings from experiments with L and S-Band MPCs validate the theoretical models and the feasibility to produce MW-class pulses using a 10-kW source. Furthermore, an MPC development roadmap towards 10 and next 100-MW class MPCs is proposed.

title: DRL-Based Noncoherent PPM Detection in Time-Varying Wireless Channels
authors: Joo-Young Choi, Hyeonsu Kim, Younghwan Song, Jaesoo Park, Chan-Sun Park
abstract: Pulse position modulation (PPM) is an energy-efficient modulation technique widely used in low-complexity wireless communication systems. However, conventional noncoherent PPM receivers, which rely on simple slot-wise envelope comparison, suffer significant performance degradation in time-varying Rayleigh fading channels. To address this limitation, we propose a deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based detection framework for Q-ary noncoherent PPM. The proposed detector observes enriched envelope-domain features extracted from the received waveform and learns a slot-selection policy during training, while performing inference using only received observations without requiring channel state information. Simulation results show that the DRL-based receiver improves detection accuracy compared with conventional noncoherent detectors under time-varying fading conditions.

title: Correcting Sea Surface Wind Model Outputs in the Western Tropical and Southwestern Atlantic Oceans using Scatterometer Data
authors: Bruna Larissa R. dos Reis, Natalia Rudorff, Rogério Batista, Cláudia Klose Parise, Ronald Buss de Souza, Antonio Marcos V. Campos
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The surface wind speed and direction are essential climate variables (ECVs). The measurement or estimation of sea surface wind speed and direction is critical to understand the ocean-atmosphere interactions in all spatial and temporal scales, being determinant to the ocean-atmosphere fluxes of heat, momentum and mass. These ECVs are also critical for any numerical modeling effort aiming to forecast in all temporal scales from the weather to the climate one. It is known, however, that wind speed and direction outputs in numerical weather prediction modeling mostly fail to represent some processes at the spatial small scale. Scatterometers, which are orbital sensors designed to estimate the sea surface wind speed and direction, are useful tools for evaluating and correcting numerical model outputs. In the present study, we estimated and corrected the bias of the wind speed and direction outputs of the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model in the Western Tropical and Southwestern Atlantic oceans using a scatterometer correction method. Satellite data were obtained for the same study region and period of the WRF data from the ASCAT and HSCAT sensors onboard four satellites. Quantitative and qualitative analyses demonstrated that the corrected WRF outputs offered a better representation of atmospheric mesoscale processes such as front formation and convection, as well as the synoptic and large-scale circulation. The WRF wind fields, once corrected with the scatterometer data, are useful for further applications including the definition of better initial and boundary conditions for regional weather and ocean-atmosphere numerical models.

title: Facial-Based Heterogeneous Graph Representation Learning for Autism Spectrum Disorder Detection
authors: Yongfeng Tao, Tongxu Su, Minqiang Yang, Minghui Ni, Yingying She, Weihao Zheng, Bin Hu
abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder whose early manifestations may include atypical facial behaviors. Prior facial-based ASD studies often focus on localized static cues or short-range temporal variations, while under-exploring the joint modeling of temporal dynamics and non-local semantic consistency across facial states. In this work, we propose a graph representation learning framework consisting of a Graph Construction module (GC) and a heterogeneous graph neural network, HGMoE. The GC module builds a temporal–semantic heterogeneous graph from facial videos, where video frames are treated as nodes and heterogeneous edges encode both local temporal continuity and semantic similarity across frames. To better model heterogeneous relations and capture diverse graph signals, HGMoE integrates a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with relation-aware edge attention. Specifically, the MoE dynamically routes messages to specialized experts, while the edge attention mechanism explicitly leverages edge types and initial semantic weights to modulate message passing across relations. Experiments on our recruited dataset achieve a classification accuracy of 97.4%, demonstrating the potential of the proposed framework for supporting facial-based ASD screening.

title: Dynamic Trend Modeling With Joint Interval Fuzzy Information Granulation: A Framework for Long-Term Forecasting of Interval-Valued Time Series
authors: Yanan Jiang, Fusheng Yu, Wenyi Zeng, Chenxi Ouyang, Fangyi Li, Jiayin Wang
abstract: The long-term forecasting of interval-valued time series (ITS) is vital for applications such as financial risk warning and energy planning. Modeling ITS requires capturing not only long-term temporal dependencies but also the coevolution between interval bounds as well as between centers and radii, which together characterize the intrinsic structure of interval data. Recently, trend fuzzy information granulation methods have been introduced to ITS forecasting, enhancing the uncertainty modeling capabilities. However, these methods impose predefined assumptions on the trend-patterns described by Trend Fuzzy Information Granules (TFIGs), limiting their adaptability to depict nonlinear dynamic trends and interval structural interactions within an ITS. To address these issues, we propose a new kind of TFIGs, that is, the Joint Interval Trend-Pattern Unlimited Fuzzy Information Granules (JITPUFIGs), as well as their construction method, which leverages the AutoEncoder neural network based on Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU-AE) to jointly learn the coevolution patterns of interval centers and radii in a data-driven manner, avoiding predefined trend-pattern assumptions and incorporating an interval structure constraint to ensure logical consistency in JITPUFIGs. Building on this, the JITPUFIG-based Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks are developed for long-term ITS forecasting. Experimental results on multiple benchmark ITS datasets show that our proposed model achieves superior forecasting accuracy while ensuring interval logical consistency—effectively preventing counterintuitive outputs such as predicted interval lower bounds exceeding upper bounds. This establishes a theoretically rigorous and effective paradigm for long-term ITS forecasting within the fuzzy system framework.

title: Multiscale Spatial Frequency-Aware Transformer and Saturation Analysis for Universal Deepfake Detection
authors: Kaiwen Xu, Xiyuan Hu, Chen Chen, Yichao Zhou, Yang Xu, Hadeel Alsolai, Shahid Mumtaz
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative artificial intelligence technologies has introduced new security challenges, raising significant concerns. As deepfake technology becomes more sophisticated, it might be exploited by malicious actors to generate highly realistic fake images, thereby compromising the authenticity and reliability of the original content. Driven by this concern, this article introduces a universal deepfake detection model, multiscale spatial frequency-aware transformer and saturation analysis (MSFTSA), based on a multiscale, spatial-frequency-aware Transformer and saturation analysis. Unlike existing methods, MSFTSA reexamines fundamental differences between real and fake images across the frequency, spatial, and saturation domains. For this purpose, an efficient multiscale frequency-domain decoupling module has been designed to capture image features from different frequency bands, assisting in identifying inherent fake characteristics across multiple frequency scales. In addition, a spatial scattering module (SSM) is introduced to model global relationships between multiscale frequency features, achieving full-frequency interactive learning in the spatial domain. Furthermore, image saturation is used as a critical indicator to distinguish between real and fake images. Extensive experiments across multiple deepfake image datasets generated by generative adversarial networks (GANs) and diffusion models (DMs) demonstrate MSFTSA’s performance, significantly outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods and showcasing exceptional generalization capability and robustness.

title: Hybrid Quantum Machine Learning for Intrusion Detection: A Comparative Study of QNN and QSVM Models
authors: Raid Anis Kerkatou, Hacene Belhadef, Aicha Eutamene, Svetlana Petrova Stefanova
abstract: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) are a cornerstone of modern cybersecurity; however, their performance is increasingly constrained by high-dimensional network traffic and the scalability limitations of classical machine learning approaches. Quantum Machine Learning (QML) has recently emerged as a promising paradigm by leveraging quantum feature spaces and hybrid quantum–classical optimization. In this work, we present a unified and reproducible benchmarking framework for QML-based intrusion detection under realistic Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) constraints. The proposed framework integrates hybrid Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) and Quantum Support Vector Machines (QSVMs) within a consistent experimental pipeline, enabling a systematic comparison of quantum models, embedding strategies, and dimensionality reduction techniques. Experiments are conducted on the NSL-KDD benchmark dataset for both binary (normal versus attack) and multiclass intrusion detection tasks. Multiple quantum embedding strategies, including angle, amplitude, and Instantaneous Quantum Polynomial (IQP) embeddings, are evaluated in conjunction with PCA-based dimensionality reduction to analyze trade-offs between expressivity, circuit complexity, and computational cost. The proposed models are benchmarked against strong classical baselines under identical preprocessing and evaluation conditions. The results demonstrate that QNN- and QSVM-based IDS models achieve competitive and, in several scenarios, superior performance compared to classical approaches, particularly in multiclass settings characterized by nonlinear and imbalanced data distributions. In addition, we provide an in-depth analysis of scalability limitations, training complexity, and NISQ feasibility, highlighting practical considerations for real-world deployment. To ensure full reproducibility, all implementation details, datasets, and experimental configurations are publicly available at: https://github.com/raidanis/QML-IDS-NSLKDD. These findings position hybrid QML as a promising complementary paradigm for next-generation intrusion detection systems, particularly in low-dimensional and high-complexity feature spaces.

title: Improved Layered Yield Network Efficient Hierarchical Encryption Algorithm for Secure Communication in IoT and IoE
authors: Abhiram Sharma, Rudra Raj, P B Krishna, Ashwin Singh Slathia, M Afzal Khan, R Suganya, Nabin Sharma
abstract: Advanced NEHA is a three-tier symmetric cipher developed to meet the high-security and performance demands of mission-critical IoT environments, including military applications. It generates dynamic session keys using device-specific identifiers, then performs lightweight character substitution and circular bit rotation across fixed 512-bit blocks. A CRC32 checksum is appended to detect accidental transmission errors. The algorithm’s efficiency is evaluated using a unified, multi-criteria framework that standardizes five key metrics: encryption/decryption throughput, latency, memory footprint, computational complexity, and security strength. These metrics are normalized on a 0–10 scale and visualized through radar charts. A polygonal area derived from the chart quantifies overall efficiency. Compared to DES, 3DES, AES, RSA, and the original NEHA, Advanced NEHA achieves sub-millisecond encryption (0.4 ms) and decryption (0.2 ms) with low memory usage (4 KB and 2 KB respectively). Its aggregate efficiency score of 131.4 ranks second only to RSA, demonstrating strong practical performance. A controlled decryption challenge involving 237 cybersecurity professionals yielded a success rate below 1%, indicating strong resistance to brute-force and known-plaintext attacks. While the algorithm is lightweight and highly efficient, further analysis is needed to assess its scalability, entropy strength at large scales, and resistance under side-channel attack scenarios. These aspects are recommended for future exploration. Overall, Advanced NEHA offers a secure, fast, and resource-efficient solution for encrypted communication in environments where both speed and robustness are paramount.

title: Plenary Talks
authors: N/A

title: Diffusion Versus GAN for Subject-Specific Gaze Synthesis
authors: Kamrul Hasan, Oleg V. Komogortsev
abstract: Generating realistic, subject-specific eye movement signals is crucial for data augmentation, gaze-based authentication, privacy-preserving analytics, and robust gaze-based interfaces. Recent advances in deep learning have enabled the generation of synthetic gaze data, but individualized gaze sequence generation has been less explored, with most approaches relying on random noise distributions or predefined latent embeddings. In this work, our main contribution is the introduction of explicit subject-aware modifications to both diffusion and generative adversarial networks (GANs)-based methods for generating realistic, individualized synthetic gaze data. In particular, we equip the diffusion-based method with compact user embeddings to capture per-subject traits and enhance the GAN-based generator with a subject-specific synthesis module to better retain idiosyncratic gaze information. We comprehensively assess these subject-aware methods with standard eye-tracking signal quality metrics, including spatial accuracy and precision. This work defines synthetic signal quality, realism, and subject specificity, and advances the potential of subject-aware gaze-based applications.

title: Vision-Based Distress and Anomaly Detection for Unpaved Roads: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Rafael De S. Toledo, Cristiano S. De Oliveira, Aldo Von Wangenheim
abstract: Autonomous-driving perception is typically developed and evaluated on paved roads, despite unpaved surfaces representing a large share of road networks (e.g., 78.5% in Brazil). We present a systematic literature review of computer-vision methods for detecting unpaved road distress published from January 2020 to May 2025. Following a Population–Intervention–Outcomes framework, we searched seven databases (ACM DL, arXiv, ASCE Library, IEEE Xplore, ScienceDirect, SpringerNature, and Wiley OL) using prespecified eligibility criteria. Screening yielded 1,400 records, 39 full texts, and 11 eligible studies. Deep learning is the preferred computer vision technique today, while classical methods (e.g., morphological operations) are occasionally used as support. Semantic segmentation is the most common task, followed by object detection and classification; depth and size estimation are rare. Most systems use RGB cameras, typically vehicle-mounted (roof or dashboard), with fewer studies incorporating stereo cameras, LiDAR, or low-atitude UAV setups. Pothole detection dominates the literature, whereas rutting and washboarding are seldom addressed. Overall, the reviewed works indicate that low-cost visual pipelines can support unpaved-road distress perception, but broader progress is limited by restricted data diversity, narrow task coverage, inconsistent evaluation, and incomplete reporting of methodological details.

title: CAFE: Condition-Attention Feature Extractor for Multi-Condition Fault Diagnosis of Rotating Machinery
authors: Hongkai Xue, Baokang Zhang, Qi Wu, Caoyuan Gu, Hangfeng Guo, Wen-An Zhang
abstract: Variations in rotational speed and load cause significant changes in the statistical distribution of machinery signals, presenting a major challenge for multi-condition fault diagnosis (MCFD). A common strategy is to align condition-invariant features to mitigate cross-condition variability. However, the coupling of condition and fault representations persists, limiting generalization across conditions. To address these challenges, a condition-attention feature extractor is proposed for MCFD of rotating machinery, namely CAFE. In this work, stator current signals are utilized for MCFD instead of vibration signals. A learnable notch module (LNM) is employed to decompose the three-phase current into a fundamental component and a residual component, thereby decoupling condition and fault representations. A conditionguided representation module (CGRM) is developed to improve the robustness and adaptability of fault diagnosis under diverse conditions. In addition, an adaptive weighting strategy (AWS) is introduced to dynamically balance condition recognition and fault diagnosis. Experimental results on three datasets show that the proposed method achieves diagnostic performance comparable to vibration-based approaches, reaching a peak fault diagnosis accuracy of 99.33%, surpassing most existing state-of-the-art MCFD approaches.

title: Metallization Techniques for Additive Manufacturing of Antennas and RF Systems: A Survey
authors: Ulan Myrzakhan, Hafsa Talpur, Juan Andres Vasquez-Peralvo, Shuai Zhang, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Additive manufacturing has recently become a manufacturing method of choice of the antennas and RF components, gaining traction not only in research but also in industrial applications. This fabrication approach offers unique advantages, including the ability to create complex, lightweight antenna structures monolithically, all within a low-cost and rapid fabrication process, using a wide variety of materials. While direct metal printing is possible, it typically yields heavy structures with rough surface finishes. The more widely used method involves high resolution dielectric printing followed by metallization. Given the increasing interest in this fabrication approach, several review papers have been published to highlight different additive manufacturing techniques. However, they mostly focus on the dielectric printing, offering limited insights into the various metallization strategies. The goal of this paper is to bridge that gap by compiling, categorizing and comparing the latest metallization techniques used in the literature to implement antennas and RF components in a single resource. The survey covers both full-surface metallization techniques (electroless plating, dip coating, spray plating), as well as more advanced methods (hydro/water transfer printing, screen printing, inkjet printing, fused deposition modeling, aerosol jet printing, ultra-precise dispensing) that enable high resolution patterned metallization. For each technique, the paper details the required equipment and conductive materials, and highlights notable antenna designs realized through these approaches. The authors believe this work will serve as a valuable reference for the antenna and RF research community involved in additive manufacturing.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Operators for Signal Modeling in Particle-based Communications
authors: O. Tansel Baydas, Ozgur B. Akan
abstract: Data-driven channel models often require extensive simulation data and capture only limited statistics of the underlying physical process. To address these limitations in diffusion-based particle signaling, we build upon physics-informed machine learning to develop a framework that fuses sparse channel measurements with governing diffusion–reaction laws. This work explores physics-informed operator learning for particle-based communication channels, aiming to bridge mechanistic PDE modeling and data-driven surrogates in this domain. Our method employs a Physics-Informed Neural Operator (PINO) to predict the spatiotemporal particle concentration field and generalizes across channel configurations with reduced dependence on explicit geometric parameterization. We further extend the framework to model quorum sensing between bacterial colonies and capture autoinducer dynamics. Compared with Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and Deep Operator Networks (DeepONets), PINO achieves high accuracy and significant computational efficiency: on the nanomachine channel, PINO reduces the relative ℓ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> error from 99.3% to 9.2%; on the quorum sensing model, PINO improves R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> from 0.808 (DeepONet) to 0.999, while multi-resolution inference yields 4–5× speed-ups on coarse grids. These results highlight physics-informed operator learning as a promising method for particle-based communication networks.

title: Feasibility Study on Low-Cost 3D Printed Waveguide Components with Simple Metallization
authors: Michael Neubauer, Saeid Karamzadeh, Christoph Wagner
abstract: This paper acts as a feasibility study on the topic of using low-cost 3D printing and metallization techniques for manufacturing waveguide-based microwave components. Several basic components are manufactured using simple fused deposition modeling (FDM) printing and generic metal paints. The performance is then evaluated for different types of paints and the results compared to each other. This work shows, that although this simple approach isn’t as good as commercially available parts, the performance is acceptable enough for a lot of applications.

title: Investigation on FMCW-Waveforms in Vector Network Analysis
authors: Ali Al-Tayar, Justin Romstadt, Vincent Lange-Nowak, Nils Pohl, Ilona Rolfes, Jan Barowski
abstract: This work investigates the use of heterodyne FMCW excitation for accurate one-port measurements in combination with a W-band frequency extender module. Instead of the stepped frequency continuous wave signal typically used in VNAs, a broadband FMCW chirp is generated using an arbitrary waveform generator and downconverted inside the extender to a fixed intermediate frequency. This enables separate acquisition of incident and reflected waves while benefiting from the phase-noise advantages of FMCW signals. An one-port error model similar to the VNA error model is applied to calibrate the measurement setup. The method is evaluated using both waveguide and free-space calibration standards, and its accuracy is verified by extracting the complex permittivity of a PVC sample using the Baker–Jarvis algorithm.

title: An Off-The-Shelf Modulation-Based Enhancement for Automotive Radar Target Emulators
authors: A. Sims, B. Holzinger, T. Vandeplas, T. Dallmann, D. Heberling
abstract: Radar sensors in Advanced Driving Assistance Systems (ADAS) allow sensing of the driving environment and identification of vulnerable traffic participants. Measured data includes detected reflections coming not only from targets like pedestrians, but also from e.g. the road, infrastructure and guardrail which can be referred to clutter in automotive scenarios. Recreating these environments for the sensor in a laboratory requires a radar target simulator (RTS). Especially for the emulation of traffic scenarios including clutter, a multitude of targets is required. However, most RTS are limited in the amount of targets. In this paper, an accessible approach for modulation-based target emulation shall be presented. An analog RTS consisting of off-the-shelf components (OTSC) is built and complemented with a modulation-based architecture to emulate a multitude of targets for chirp-sequence frequency-modulated continuous waveform (CS-FMCW) sensors. The applied frequency shifts, resulting range-velocity (RV) maps and target emulation updates are analyzed. Benefits and limitations of the system are discussed.

title: Model-Driven Fault and Disturbance Validation in Complex Industrial Power Systems
authors: Oveis Asgari, Mohammad Zadeh, Derek Dean
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Industrial electrical networks often include hundreds of protection relays, metering devices, and control interlocks that operate under tightly coupled logic. Post-disturbance analysis in such environments is time-consuming when event interpretation relies solely on raw disturbance files and manual verification. This paper presents a model-driven fault and disturbance validation framework deployed in a large industrial system to automate verification of protection and control behavior under real operating events. The framework performs automated event identification of diverse system conditions such as voltage dips, short circuits, and motor re-acceleration to establish event context before model validation. It then integrates the facility’s single-line model with actual relay measurements and device logic to reconstruct event sequences and evaluate interlocks in real time. By correlating simulated responses with measured quantities, the framework identifies inconsistencies such as incorrect CT polarity, deviations in modeled line impedances, and discrepancies between relay settings in the model and their actual field behavior. Field implementation results demonstrate how this model-driven approach reduces troubleshooting time, ensures settings consistency across protection zones, and improves confidence in protection coordination. The system provides a foundation for automated disturbance validation, enabling industrial grids to transition from event reporting to model-verified protection intelligence.

title: A Causal Balance Sampling Framework for Few-Shot Cross-Component Fault Diagnosis
authors: Jiahan Zhu, Juan Xu, Xu Ding, Xiaochuan Li
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Few-shot cross-component learning often suffers from imbalanced class and environment distributions, which weakens model generalization across different components and operating conditions. To address this issue, we propose a causal balance sampling mechanism that reduces unstable correlations between class labels and environment-related features. We model latent-variable distributions in each environment with a variational autoencoder (VAE) and learn the generative process by maximizing the evidence lower bound (ELBO). By introducing balance-score matching, we construct more balanced mini-batch episodes to approximate a balanced training distribution in metalearning. Experimental results show that the proposed method consistently improves cross-component few-shot generalization and achieves stronger robustness under varying environments. Both theoretical analysis and empirical validation demonstrate that causal balance sampling can effectively enhance the generalization ability of meta-learning models in nonstationary few-shot fault-diagnosis scenarios.

title: Characterization of Low-Temperature Intrinsic Field-Effect Mobility With Contact Resistances in Amorphous InGaZnO Thin Film Transistors
authors: Haesung Kim, Ju Young Park, Seung Hyeop Han, Hyojin Yang, Yoon Jung Lee, Sung-Jin Choi, Dae Hwan Kim, Jong-Ho Bae, Dong Myong Kim
abstract: Amorphous indium-gallium-zinc-oxide (a-IGZO) thin-film transistors (TFTs) are characterized for their potential in cryogenic memory applications, focusing on the intrinsic field-effect mobility <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(\mu _{\text {FEo}})$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and parasitic source/drain resistances <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(R_{\text {S}\vert \text {D}})$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Over the temperature (T) range of 90–300K, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu _{\text {FEo}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> is observed to increase significantly from 0.20 to 5.90 cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{\mathbf {{2}}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/V <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot $</tex-math> </inline-formula> s, exhibiting a strong T-dependence with an exponent <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n{\,}={\,}2.97$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. This behavior is attributed to the trap-assisted Coulombic scattering (TAC) caused by the high-subgap density of states (DoS) in a-IGZO which overshadows the phonon scattering. The extracted Urbach energy (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E_{\mathbf {U}}\approx ~4$</tex-math> </inline-formula> meV) from the mobility exponent was notably smaller than the conventionally obtained <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$E_{\mathbf {U}}^{\boldsymbol {}}~(\approx 25$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–48meV) from the subgap DoS. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(R_{\text {S}\vert \text {D}})$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, extracted by the thermionic emission (TE) model, revealed an effective barrier height of 0.018eV and increased ~ fivefold as T decreased from 300 to 90K. These findings highlight the importance of understanding the T-dependence of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu _{\text {FEo}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(R_{\text {S}\vert \text {D}})$</tex-math> </inline-formula> for optimizing a-IGZO TFTs, paving the way for energy-efficient and scalable memory solutions in quantum computing systems operating under cryogenic conditions.

title: Corruption-Resilient Combinatorial Bandit Learning for Heterogeneous Network Systems_supp1-3691423.pdf
authors: Junkai Wang
abstract: We study online decision-making problems in network applications using the framework of contextual combinatorial multi-armed bandits (C2MAB). Although bandit methods provide a natural solution, two key challenges arise in practice: corruption in feedback and heterogeneity across clients. Corruption may stem from adversarial behaviors or system anomalies, leading to biased reward estimations, while heterogeneity reflects differences in clients’ environments such as computation capabilities or network conditions. To address these challenges, we formulate the C2MAB under corruption (C2MAB-C) problem and extend it to the setting with heterogeneous environmental parameters. We then propose two novel algorithms, namely CW-C2UCB, which leverages confidence-weighted estimations to mitigate corruption effects, and CW-C2CLUB, which further integrates an online clustering mechanism to adaptively group tasks based on shared structure. We derive tight theoretical regret bounds for both algorithms under various corruption models showing that our upper bounds match the established lower bounds up to logarithmic factors. Empirical results on three real-world applications, content delivery networks, client selection in federated learning, and VR video streaming, demonstrate that our proposed algorithms significantly outperform existing baselines, achieving lower regret and stronger resilience in adversarial environments.
