title: In-Situ Electric Field-Assisted Preparation of SiC/Epoxy Composite Coatings with Oriented Filler Structure_supp1-3691698.mp4
authors: Cheng Pan
abstract: The SiC/epoxy nonlinear conductive coating has shown great potential in DC GIS/GIL for its ability to suppress surface charge accumulation and homogenize the electric field. However, a high doping amount severely restricts its further application. In this paper, an in-situ electric field-assisted technology is introduced to fabricate epoxy-based composite coatings with aligned SiC fillers. In-situ microscopic observation and micro computed tomography (Micro-CT) characterizations reveal the 3D orientation of the coatings. A comparison with uniformly doped samples at a higher concentration shows that the oriented coatings at a lower concentration have more continuous conductive paths, facilitating carrier migration. As a result, compared with uniformly doped coatings, the oriented coatings exhibit significantly lower trap levels and higher shallow trap densities. Moreover, combining experimental and simulation results reveals that increasing the in-situ electric field amplitude can significantly enhance the permittivity and DC nonlinear conductivity in the orientation direction. This increase alleviates local electric field distortion and improves the DC flashover voltage of the samples. This technology is more effective than simply increasing the doping amount, showing great potential in the insulation design of high-voltage DC devices.

title: Analysis of Shuffling Beyond Pure Local Differential Privacy
authors: Shun Takagi, Seng Pei Liew
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: Power Analysis attack resilient Novel 9T & 10T NVSRAM for Emerging Memory
authors: Sandeep Tripathi, Pritesh Kumar Yadav, Sudhanshu Choudhary, Prasanna Kumar Misra
abstract: A novel power analysis (PA) attack resilient 9T & 10T Non-Volatile Static Random Access Memory (NVSRAM) bit cells have been proposed in this article. The write operation, store mode and restore mode operations of NVSRAM are vulnerable due to its single stage operation. In single stage write and restore operations, if the stored data at the charging nodes is same as the processed data, no bit-flip is required at the charging nodes. Otherwise, a bit-flip is required. The power consumption difference between bit-flip and no bit-flip forms a strong correlation. Similarly, in the store operation different critical current is needed for storing logic ‘0’ and logic ‘1’, which forms a correlation. Attackers exploit these correlations to obtain the stored information. The proposed NVSRAM bit cell with two-stage write, store and restore operations offers PA attack resilience by reducing the power consumption correlation between processed data and stored data. The two-stage operation consists of equalization and flip process. The two-stage operation offers a weak energy correlation (75x/120x less compared to single-stage operation) between stored data and processed data, resulting in PA attack resilience. Additionally, two different types of store operations for the proposed cell have been designed: one using word lines and bit lines, and the other one without using the same.

title: Modeling, Control, and Stiffness Regulation of Layer Jamming-Based Continuum Robots
authors: Yeman Fan, Bowen Yi, Dikai Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Continuum robots with variable compliance have gained significant attention due to their adaptability in unstructured environments. Among various stiffness modulation techniques, layer jamming (LJ) provides a simple yet effective approach for achieving tunable stiffness. However, most existing LJ-based continuum robot models rely on static or quasi-static approximations, lacking a rigorous control-oriented dynamical formulation. Consequently, they are unsuitable for real-time control tasks requiring simultaneous regulation of configuration and stiffness and fail to capture the full dynamic behavior of LJ-based continuum robots. To address this gap, this article proposes a port-Hamiltonian formulation for LJ-based continuum robots, formally characterizing the two key phenomena—shape locking and tunable stiffness—within a unified energy-based framework. Based on this model, we develop a passivity-based control (PBC) approach that enables decoupled regulation of stiffness and configuration with provable stability guarantees. We validate the proposed framework through comprehensive experiments on the OctRobot-I continuum robotic platform. The results demonstrate consistency between theoretical predictions and empirical data, highlighting the feasibility of our approach for real-world implementation.

title: Knowledge-Guided Multi-Task Video Classification for Industrial Process via Fuzzy Rule Graph Embedding
authors: Xuhanyu Zheng, Yongfang Xie, Shiwen Xie, Hao Ying
abstract: Multi-task video classification is a challenging task in video understanding, which is particularly evident in industrial applications. Existing multi-task learning methods enhance task performance by sharing representations of different tasks, which lacks systematic guidance with prior knowledge. In industrial process, abundant prior knowledge is underutilized for video understanding, because the prior knowledge is usually unstructured text which is hard to be embedded for video understanding. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel fuzzy rule graph representation that systematically encodes expert knowledge derived from manual video observations, providing a highly generalized and structured knowledge representation. Correspondingly, a novel multi-channel fuzzy rule graph embedding (MC-FRGE) method is proposed to model relationships among fuzzy rules. Then, a self-supervised two-stream attention autoencoder is constructed for visual feature extraction. Combining the MC-FRGE and the two-stream attention autoencoder, a novel knowledge guided multi-task video classification (KG-MTVC) framework is developed. Finally, experimental results on both the general video understanding dataset HMDB51 and a specialized zinc flotation process video dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/Galazxhy/KG-MTVC.git

title: Inverse-designed ultra-compact on-chip broadband bandpass filter with high extinction ratio
authors: Jie Huang, Yutai Chen, Chuanyi Lu, Yang Wang, Junbo Yang, Xiaogang Qiang
abstract: An ultra-compact on-chip broadband bandpass optical filter, characterized by a wide working bandwidth, broad passband, and high sidelobe suppression ratio, is presented. By employing an inverse design method combined with a step-by-step and combinatorial optimization strategy, we effectively overcome the challenge of optimization stagnation caused by complex optimization targets. This filter achieves C-band passfiltering (1.52 μm - 1.58 μm) within an ultra-compact footprint of 9.6 × 3.6 μm², with insertion loss <2.07 dB and passband transmittance fluctuation <0.43 dB. Additionally, it maintains sidelobe suppression >23.15 dB over the full operational spectrum ranging from 1.2 μm to 1.9 μm. The proposed step-by-step and combinatorial optimization framework offers a generalized approach for designing photonic devices with very complex optimization objectives, such as broadband bandstop filters and multi-channel bandpass filters. This methodology significantly extends the applicability of the inverse design method while advancing the integration density of photonic integrated circuits.

title: Joint Beamforming Design and Trajectory Optimization for STAR-RIS Assisted UAV-enabled ISAC System
authors: Xingyuan Lv, Zhaolin Zhang, Qian Xu, Wen-Bin Sun, Xin Yang, Ling Wang
abstract: Recognizing the coverage limitations of traditional reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS), the simultaneously transmitting and reflecting RIS (STAR-RIS) with the ability to provide coverage on both sides of the surface, has been regarded as a promising technology especially for the integrated indooroutdoor communication environment. In this paper, we investigate a STAR-RIS assisted unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-enabled integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system in the presence of imperfect channel state information (CSI). Specifically, the UAV serves as an aerial base station to provide communication services to outdoor users, as well as positioning for indoor users. To maximize the communication sum-rate while satisfying the sensing beampattern gain requirement, the UAV’s trajectory, beamforming, and the STAR-RIS transmission/reflection coefficients are jointly optimized. Meanwhile, the UAV’s flight safety, the maximum flight duration, and the minimum communication rate for each user are also considered. An online decision-making framework that utilizes deep reinforcement learning (DRL) is proposed to address the sum-rate maximization problem with imperfect CSI. Furthermore, in order to decouple the continuous optimization variables and improve the applicability of the system, we introduce a twin-twin-delayed deep deterministic policy gradient algorithm based on self-attention mechanism (SA-TTD3), which utilizes dual agents to decouple and optimize variables, and incorporates attention mechanisms to improve applicability. The numerical results indicate that the proposed SA-TTD3 algorithm significantly improves the performance of the ISAC system compared with the baseline schemes.

title: Design and evaluation of a bicycle-integrated antenna for prospective V2X connectivity of vulnerable road users
authors: Y. Gillani, M. E. Asghar, M. A. Hein
abstract: Integrating cyclists into V2X communication networks is a crucial step towards enhancing road safety and enabling intelligent, connected mobility solutions. This paper presents a slot antenna integrated into a bicycle handlebar for 5.9 GHz V2X communication of vulnerable road users. The compact design achieves a realized gain of 5 dBi and an impedance bandwidth of 200 MHz. A cylindrical metallic rod was initially employed as a handlebar, with the hollow structure acting as a waveguide. The comparison of measurements with simulations shows good agreement. The radiation patterns reveal an average deviation of approximately 5 dB from the omnidirectional response typically expected for a dipole-like slot antenna. This deviation is attributed to the closed-back configuration of the handlebar structure. The main lobe remains aligned along the forward driving direction, ensuring optimal angular coverage for V2X communication. The results demonstrate the feasibility of practical handlebar-integrated antennas that are easily adaptable to any type of bicycle.

title: Copula-Based Analysis of Fluid Antenna-Assisted Over-the-Air Computation
authors: Saeid Pakravan, Mohsen Ahmadzadeh, Wessam Ajib, Ghosheh Abed Hodtani, Ming Zeng
abstract: This letter studies an uplink over-the-air computation (AirComp) framework in which multiple user equipments are equipped with fluid-antenna (FA) arrays and operate over spatially correlated fading channels. By explicitly modeling channel dependence using the Gumbel copula, closed-form analytical expressions are derived for the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the mean-squared error (MSE) of the aggregated function. The proposed analysis provides a quantitative performance characterization of AirComp under spatial correlation and provides analytical insights into the role of FA-assisted transmission in correlated wireless environments. Numerical results validate the derived expressions and show that FA deployment can substantially reduce the MSE compared with conventional fixed-antenna systems, although the achievable gain decreases as spatial correlation becomes stronger.

title: Morphology-Aware Graph Reinforcement Learning for Tensegrity Robot Locomotion
authors: Chi Zhang, Mingrui Li, Wenzhe Tong, Xiaonan Huang
abstract: Tensegrity robots combine rigid rods and elastic cables, offering high resilience and deployability but at the same time posing major challenges for locomotion control due to their underactuated and highly coupled dynamics. This paper intro duces a morphology-aware reinforcement learning framework that integrates a graph neural network (GNN) into the Soft Actor Critic (SAC) algorithm. By representing the robot's physical topology as a graph, the proposed GNN-based policy captures coupling among components, enabling faster and more stable learning than conventional multilayer perceptron (MLP) policies. The method is validated on a physical 3-bar tensegrity robot across three locomotion primitives, including straight-line track ing and bidirectional turning. It shows superior sample efficiency, robustness to noise and stiffness variations, and improved trajec tory accuracy. Additionally, the learned policies transfer directly from simulation to hardware without fine-tuning, achieving stable real-world locomotion. These results demonstrate the advantages of incorporating structural priors into reinforcement learning for tensegrity robot control. https://tensegrity-graph-rl.github.io/.

title: Wafer-scale estimation of thermal stability with magneto-optical measurement system for STT-MRAM
authors: Kenji Suzuki, Ingi Kim, Shinji Ueyama, Junho Shin, Je Ung Song, Wookrae Kim, Taehyun Kim, Junho Jeong, Sangwoo Bae, Sungho Jang, Jeong-heon Park, Myungjun Lee
abstract: The magnetic anisotropy field of perpendicular magnetic anisotropy films is an important magnetic characteristic directly linked to data retention for Spin Transfer Torque Magnetoresistive Random Access Memory (STT-MRAM). To measure the full-wafer characterization, we develop a polar magneto-optical Kerr effect measurement system incorporating a unique electromagnet with a temperature-controlled chuck and stage. The validity of this system is confirmed by correlation with the conventional technique. The spatial resolution of the system is demonstrated to 20μm, which is on the order of millimeters with conventional in-line MOKE tool. We also measured the uniformity of magnetic anisotropy across the full wafer at 150°C and estimated the temperature stability of patterned MTJ. The magneto-optical measurement system is expected to be promising for in-line inspection of device characteristics.

title: LightGBM-SHAP-Based Clinical ML Feasibility Screening: Experimental Validation via Controlled Cross-Domain Data*
authors: Liting Xiang, Qian Zhou, Saijun Shi, Linfang Ding
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Objective: To validate LightGBM-SHAP as a diagnostic tool for clinical ML feasibility via controlled cross-domain experiments. Methods: Identical pipelines applied to TCM rehabilitation (n = 57, positive control) and Parkinson's disease (n=42, negative control). The TCM target was deterministically constructed from predictors <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\Delta$</tex> VAS and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta$</tex> Lysholm) to validate framework functionality under known signal strength, not generalizable prediction. PD represented real-world small-sample challenges. Performance evaluated via AUC-ROC and TreeSHAP, with XGBoost/CatBoost benchmarking. Results: Under deterministic conditions, TCM achieved perfect discrimination (AUC =1.000) with SHAP correctly identifying predefined predictors <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\Delta$</tex> Lysholm 45.7%, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta$</tex> VAS 43.8%), validating explanatory power rather than clinical prediction. PD showed no capability ({AUC=0.500) with uniformly zero SHAP values, indicating feature-outcome dissociation. Algorithmic consensus was perfect in TCM <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\rho=1.00)$</tex> but near-random in PD <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\rho=0.12)$</tex>. Conclusions: SHAP serves dual functions: explaining successful models and diagnosing infeasible tasks via zero-value detection. We propose a preliminary threshold — <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n<50$</tex> with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p>10$</tex> risks failure-for clinical AI feasibility screening.

title: A Multistatic Doppler Radar Measurement of Particulate Matter with a J-Band Radar Sensor
authors: Kennet Braasch, Alexander Teplyuk, Stephan Hauptmeier, Nils Pohl, Michael Höft
abstract: This work presents a novel multistatic measurement approach for monitoring particle streams using a radar sensor with a transmit frequency of 265.6 GHz (J-Band). The system utilizes a single transmitter and two receivers positioned at different angles to exploit the angular dependence of the scattering patterns in the Rayleigh to Mie transition region to obtain further information. A suitable measurement setup and corresponding results with glass spheres with a radius of 0.25mm are shown as a proof-of-concept measurement.

title: Emerging Electric Vehicle Charging Market: Analyzing the Impact of Firm Pricing, Charging Speed, Consumer Preferences, and Power Tariff Subsidy
authors: Nandan Kumar Singh, Milan Kumar, Nishant Kumar Verma
abstract: As the electric vehicle (EV) market expands, Charging Service Providers (CSPs) must decide whether to offer Alternating Current (AC) charging, which is slower and less costly, and/or Direct Current (DC) charging, which is faster but more expensive. Consumers differ in their valuation of charging speed, while AC and DC systems face distinct electricity tariffs and efficiency levels, resulting in different operating costs. Governments increasingly provide power-tariff subsidies to lower these costs and promote charging infrastructure. This study develops a Stackelberg framework in which the government acts as the leader maximizing social welfare, and the CSP acts as the follower maximizing profit underpricing and service constraints. We analytically characterize equilibrium outcomes for optimal pricing and service configurations under varying subsidy levels. The analysis identifies four market regimes: joint AC–DC offerings, exclusive AC or DC markets, and market exit. When no subsidy is provided, CSPs optimally withhold service; full support induces exclusive DC service, while intermediate support yields mixed outcomes depending on base tariffs and efficiency. Under certain parameter ranges, subsidies can induce a CSP to narrow rather than broaden its offerings. Extending the analysis to a duopoly shows that charging prices become inversely related to the efficiency of the competing technology, and demand responses to subsidies become non monotonic. The results provide actionable insights for designing coordinated subsidy and pricing strategies for sustainable EV charging ecosystems

title: From Blackboard to Algorithm: AI's Impact on Teaching and Learning Design
authors: György Molnár, Levente Juhász, Gyula Gubán, László Kadocsa, Andrea Tick, Eniko Nagy
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: The teaching of professional methodology occupies a central place in the basic curricula for engineering teacher training and vocational teacher training. In the new, changed training structure, there is less time for the transfer, acquisition, and application of new knowledge, and this new situation is transforming the content of the curriculum and the system of consultation tasks, as well as expanding training opportunities with the advent of AI. We are at the beginning of the transformation process, so every opportunity and form of development must be analyzed. At the University of Dunaújváros, the previous academic year's vocational teacher methodology course provided an opportunity to experiment with AI and to collect students' opinions on this topic using a questionnaire. During the experimental semester, we were interested in how AI affects student practice and how familiar our students are with the new opportunities. We have summarized the details of this study in our paper.

title: A Compact Four-Way Waveguide Power Divider Optimized for Additive Manufacturing
authors: Frederike Bartels, Dominik Langer, Nico Weiss, Alexander Koelpin
abstract: This work presents a design scheme for a very compact in-phase elliptical waveguide divider specifically optimized for 3D printing. An E- and H-plane divider and a combination EH-plane divider based on this design scheme are presented with 64.1 %, 17.6 %, and 27.6 % simulated relative bandwidth in V-Band. The divider can be constructed using only CST Studio with no need for additional software. The EH-plane divider was manufactured and measured. The measurements show good agreement with the simulation results.

title: Reflection Reduction Effects in Multi Probe Near Field Scanner Systems
authors: Martin Obermaier, Thomas Deckert, Dirk Plettemeier
abstract: Planar near field (NF) scanners are an attractive and versatile tool to measure and characterize large antennas in a relatively compact setup. NF Scanners are able to utilize multiple probes to increase measurement speed and scan area. In this work we investigate the effects of using multiple distinct probes to suppress reflections in the measurement setup.

title: Cross-Frequency Feature Learning for Dual-Band Radar HRRP Target Recognition_supp1-3692758.pdf
authors: Yan-hua Wang
abstract: Radar ground target recognition using high-resolution range profile (HRRP) has attracted considerable attention recently. Existing methods primarily focus on single-band data, yet the HRRP of targets with similar shape structures are highly similar, making this a challenging task in complex scenarios. To address this, we use dual-band HRRPs to exploit target's scattering variations across frequencies, thereby improving recognition. In the paper, a cross-frequency feature learning (CFFL) method is proposed to extract inter-frequency discriminative features via both handcrafted and deep-learning perspectives. The handcrafted features are extracted at raw HRRP levels and their single-band feature level. The resulting heterogeneous feature sets are optimized using sparse coding and coding rate divergence maximization. For deep features, wavelet packet decomposition is integrated into a neural network to enable multi-scale feature extraction. Attention mechanism is employed to characterize the wavelet domain differences between the dual-band data, with training guided by a difference search loss function. Frequency interaction further enhances the deep feature representations. Finally, deep discriminant correlation analysis is applied to jointly optimize both feature types, preserving optimal discriminative information. Extensive experiments on three datasets were conducted to verify the proposed method's effectiveness.

title: Impact of Ethernet Network Conditions on Protective Relaying using Sampled Values
authors: Carlos Pimentel, Alexandre R. Aoki
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Electricity sector is experiencing deep changes since renewable energy technology started being adopted, and technology involved in these new energy resources had challenged engineers to adapt Protection, Automation and Control systems, running for over decades, to this new paradigm. In this scenario, IEC 61850 standard has been the basis for digitizing these systems and addressing these new needs, and this led to the widespread utilization of Ethernet communication within these time-sensitive systems. As growth of using communication networks for critical infrastructure has increased, these systems have become a target for cyber-attacks. This study presents the results of assessing a Process bus-based protection system under DoS flooding cyber-attack while executing its expected protection functions, in situations where the process bus network does not control and do filter this flood of messages to the IEDs. The assessment took place by simulating an attack directed at different components of the process bus-based protection system. Results have shown that uncontrolled DoS attack may be an effective way to prevent a Process bus-based protection system operating as expected and include non-deterministic delays on its operating time, even preventing it to operate at all. On the other hand, simple filtering capabilities embedded on IEC 61850 managed switches proved to be effective as countermeasures to be used by protection engineers on real world systems.

title: Research and Application of a Data-Driven Intelligent Construction System for Mountain Photovoltaic Power Stations in Complex Terrains
authors: Ruiqi Ning, Meili Ning, Meijun Ning
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Driven by China's national strategic goals of achieving carbon peak and carbon neutrality, mountain photovoltaic (PV) power generation has become a significant component of the country's renewable energy portfolio. However, construction in complex mountainous terrains presents severe challenges, including topographical constraints, foundation construction difficulties, complex cable routing, logistical complications, and safety and environmental concerns. Acknowledging that existing research predominantly focuses on isolated technological aspects and lacks integrated systemic solutions, this paper proposes an intelligent construction system for mountain PV that deeply integrates data-driven and intelligent systems methodologies. The system synthesizes laser point cloud surveying, 3D intelligent layout, structural simulation analysis, wind tunnel testing, precise prestress control, modular construction, and dynamic logistics scheduling, thereby establishing a complete “problem-technology-data-verification” closed loop. Validated through typical projects in the Shaanxi Loess Plateau, Ningxia sandy-gobi-deserts (SGD), Yunnan mountainous areas, and Western Zhejiang, this technical framework indicates a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 \%}-\mathbf{2 0 \%}$</tex> improvement in land use efficiency, an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 \%} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{1 8 \%}$</tex> reduction in construction costs, and a project duration shortened by 20-30 days in these specific projects. It also achieves electrical safety targets, including a grounding resistance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 4 \Omega$</tex> and a lightningcaused failure rate of zero. This work provides a systematic, data-driven solution for the high-quality and efficient construction of mountain PV power stations under complex topographical conditions, offering substantial value for engineering application and academic reference.

title: Fast World-Model Decoding for Lightweight 3D Autonomous Vehicular Scenes
authors: Charles Louie Siplao, Johnson Agbinya, Mohammad Belayet Hossain, Ajit Jaiswal
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: We address a practical failure mode in cameraLiDAR fusion for urban driving: extremely small image disparities that, when converted directly to metric depth, yield unstable estimates and derail the front end before LiDAR cues can anchor the pose. We propose a minimal, intrinsics-aware conditioning step prior to fusion that rescales disparity using the true focal length and baseline and enforces a small minimumdisparity floor to bound depth, complemented by conservative dynamic-object masking and a LiDAR-anchored back end. On representative sequences, we verify that the data operate in a “small-disparity” regime and show that conditioning restores usable depth maps and allows the fusion pipeline to proceed. We further report preliminary trajectory metrics on KITTI Odometry Sequence 00 using standard absolute and relative pose errors: a LiDAR ICP baseline (filtered point-to-plane) achieves APE RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$28.14 \mathbf{m}$</tex> and RPE (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta=10$</tex>) translation/rotation RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17.37 \mathbf{m} / 23.58^{\circ}$</tex>. A disparity-conditioned point-selection variant yields comparable <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\operatorname{RPE}(\Delta=10)\left(17.04 \mathbf{~ m} / 23.42^{\circ}\right)$</tex> but higher APE RMSE (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$46.67 \mathbf{m}$</tex>), suggesting that disparity-based gating alone can reduce long-range geometric constraints needed for global consistency. These results quantify end-to-end operability and highlight where conditioning must be integrated as an explicit residual/prior (rather than selection-only) to improve trajectory accuracy.

title: Preclinical Assessment of an Image-Guided Histotripsy System for Renal Ablation
authors: Muskan Singh, Erik Saucedo, George R. Schade, Vishwas Trivedi, Abhinav Kumar Singh, Himanshu Shekhar, Adam D. Maxwell, Sonia L. Hernandez, Abdul Khan, Kenneth B. Bader
abstract: Objective: This work presents the development and evaluation of a preclinical ultrasound imaging-guided histotripsy system for non-invasive renal ablation. A primary design consideration for the system was to integrate the therapy source with imaging sequences that provide effective visualization of bubble activity. Methods: A custom focused transducer was manufactured to enabled targeting of the lower and upper poles of the kidney in pig, a common pre-clinical model for renal applications. The transducer included a coaxial opening to integrate a curvilinear imaging probe. An ultrafast imaging sequence that combined subharmonic chirp-coded excitation and Volterra filtering was used for active bubble detection. Passive images of bubble activity were also formed with a pth root algorithm. Bubble detection with these sequences was assessed in a tissue-mimicking phantom and in vivo porcine kidney. Results: The ultrafast sequence provided improved active bubble detection compared to conventional B mode imaging, with increased bubble-to-tissue and bubble-to interference ratios. Trends captured with passive imaging differed from those collected with ultrafast imaging, providing complementary information on bubble activity. In vivo experiments confirmed these sequences provide improved bubble visualization relative to conventional B-mode imaging. Further, the successful detection of bubble activity with ultrafast imaging was concurrent with visible ablation observed in gross examination and histology. Conclusion: The system provides safe and effective image-guided histotripsy in the kidney, addressing prior limitations with bubble cloud detection. Significance: This work demonstrates the feasibility of integrating advanced ultrasound imaging onto a histotripsy platform to monitor renal ablation and support accurate and effective treatment.

title: Enhancing SAM2 for Industrial Defect Detection via Dual-Adapter Fine-Tuning
authors: Yu Zhao, Jing Sun, Guohui Zhang, Fuming Sun, Haojie Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Surface defect detection plays an essential role in modern industrial quality control. Adapting vision foundation models for this task aims to deliver detection capabilities characterized by high accuracy and strong generalization. While models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) exhibit great potential in generic segmentation, their effectiveness diminishes in industrial scenarios involving complex backgrounds and low-contrast defects due to insufficient feature perception and imprecise localization. To overcome these challenges, we introduce SDDNet, an efficient framework specifically tailored for industrial defect detection. By incorporating a functionally decoupled dual-adapter strategy, our method adapts the foundation model’s representation capabilities while fine-tuning only 1.8% of the parameters. The core components include a multi-scale perceiver integrating a Shared Transformer Block (STB) and an adaptive decoder dedicated to precise boundary recovery. This approach achieves competitive performance across multiple benchmarks with minimal computational overhead. Experimental results demonstrate that SDDNet compares favorably with the state-of-the-art method SAM2-UNet on the SD-SALIENCY-900 and NEU-RSDDS-AUG datasets. Code and results will be available at https://github.com/wellMachine/SDDNet/.

title: Synthetic Priors for Real-World Detection: a Label-Free Framework for Identifying Ultra-Rare Objects
authors: Di Wang, Tongning Wu, Lei Yang, Xianjun Fan, Xin Ye, Renbing Huang, Xing Lu, Xu Xu, Congsheng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Detecting ultra-rare objects (frequency < 10-6) without positive annotations remains a critical challenge in computer vision. We propose a Synthesis-Prior Driven paradigm that eliminates the need for real positive samples. Our core insight is that models trained on statistically and perceptually indistinguishable synthetic data can generalize effectively to real-world environments. To achieve this, we introduce a Differentiable Morphological Modeling framework, which embeds explicit physical and geometric priors into a differentiable synthesis engine. Unlike black-box generative models, this approach ensures high-fidelity imagery with perfectly accurate, objective annotations. We validated this paradigm on the safety-critical task of detecting Circulating Genetically Abnormal Cells (CACs). Perceptual assessments confirmed that synthetic data is indistinguishable from clinical imagery (expert accuracy ≈ 50%). Consequently, our model, trained exclusively on synthetic data, achieved state-of-the-art performance on real patient samples (98.43% accuracy, 97.40% sensitivity), outperforming both human experts and existing automated methods. Furthermore, the model demonstrated exceptional robustness across a multi-center cohort (CV < 0.8%) and achieved high-precision zero-shot transfer in industrial defect inspection (DAGM 2007). This study establishes a principled methodology for ultra-rare object detection, offering broad applicability across domains with definable geometric priors.

title: CoreScaler: A Resource-Efficient Hybrid Scaling Framework for Dynamic Workloads in Cloud
authors: Dinghao Zeng, Fagui Liu, Runbin Chen, Jingwei Tan, Dishi Xu, Qingbo Wu, C.L. Philip Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Containerized microservices face significant challenges in balancing service quality and resource efficiency under dynamic workloads. Existing approaches suffer from horizontal scaling’s cold start latency, vertical scaling’s resource ceilings, and hybrid methods’ limited adaptability. We present CoreScaler, a resource-efficient hybrid scaling framework based on analysis of CPU usage patterns revealing substantial consumption differences between working mode and waiting mode instances. This insight drives our dual-mode instance management model that distinguishes between working instances actively handling requests and waiting instances maintaining hot standby with minimal resource allocation. CoreScaler employs a master-subordinate distributed architecture where the master node performs capacity planning using multi-confidence interval predictions and contextual multi-armed bandit optimization, while subordinate nodes execute mode-aware CPU quota adjustments. Comprehensive evaluation on a Kubernetes cluster with a typical microservice system under four representative production work-loads demonstrates that CoreScaler maintains SLO compliance while reducing CPU and memory allocation by 22.53% and 30.83% respectively compared to state-of-the-art solutions. The framework achieves substantially higher resource utilization than single-dimension scaling approaches, validating the effectiveness of coordinated hybrid scaling for dynamic cloud environments.

title: A Coarse-to-Fine Metric Learning Framework for Robust Identification of Overlapping Surgical Instruments
authors: Haichuan Li, Jingbo Wang, Wenxi Gan, Chen Jiang, Haocong Zhang, Chi Wang, Hewei Yu
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Automated surgical instrument identification is crucial for patient safety but inherently challenged by severe overlapping, specular reflections, and data scarcity. Traditional closed-set detectors struggle with feature entanglement and lack extensibility for novel instruments. To address these issues, we propose a lightweight, coarse-to-fine metric learning framework that decouples the task into class-agnostic localization and openset identification. To support this paradigm, we develop a scalable synthetic data engine to augment detector training and introduce a Distractor Category regularization to autonomously filter background noise. Furthermore, through feature-aligned knowledge distillation, the framework is compressed into a highly efficient architecture. Our approach mitigates spatial ambiguity in overlapping scenes and supports flexible extension to new categories without retraining. It further enables efficient inference suitable for resource-constrained deployment settings.

title: Self-Aligned, High-Aspect Ratio Electrodes for Fused Silica Resonators Digitally Defined by Laser Modification of Material
authors: Austin R. Parrish, Esther Wong, Mohammad H. Asadian, Lois Meira-Lopez, Andrei M. Shkel
abstract: This paper presents a fabrication approach that uses femtosecond laser–induced chemical etching (FLICE) to digitally define high-aspect-ratio capacitive electrodes directly within fused silica substrates. Demonstrated on dual-shell hemispherical resonator gyroscopes (HRG), the method achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m capacitive gaps with aspect ratios up to 150:1 on flat test samples and 64:1 on 3-D shell micro-HRGs. Conventional HRG designs often use separately fabricated and assembled electrodes, whereas the approach presented here yields self-aligned electrodes without an assembly step. Fabricated micro-HRGs exhibit quality factors exceeding 4.5 million. With 10 nm Pt atomic layer deposition, total capacitance reached 4.68 pF. Open-loop gyroscope operation was demonstrated with 0.07 mV/(°/s) scale factor, establishing this approach as viable for assembly-free fused silica vibratory sensors. [2026-0028]

title: Comprehensive Electricity Demand Forecasting with a Custom Multi-Dimensional Dataset with Model Analysis and Mobile Visualization
authors: Md. Nur-E Ferdaus, Md. Shahriar Hossain Apu, Rakib Hossen, Anichur Rahman, Mst. Deloara Khushi, Bicrom Adhikari Utsha, Fahmid Al Farid, Hezerul Abdul Karim, Abu Saleh Musa Miah
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Accurate electricity demand forecasting is critical for efficient energy management in regions affected by rapid urbanization, grid instability, and load shedding. The traditional forecasting methods are inadequate when it comes to the short-term variation due to weather conditions, socio-economic activities, and unforeseen events. This paper introduces a deployable, statistically grounded electricity demand forecasting model that incorporates meteorological, calendar-based, and grid operational information, such as temperature, humidity, rainfall, daylight hours, festive days, past consumption, and load shedding data. To learn about the complex non-linear and temporal relationships in daily electricity demand, Random Forest and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) frameworks are used and to facilitate the implementation of the model in near real-time, the framework uses a FastAPI-based backend and a Flutter-based mobile app to visualize the predictions. Resistance to sensor noise and non-observable types of events is improved by integrating contextual features and outlier-aware data preprocessing. The experimental results on a dataset with a time span of 7 years (2017–2024) indicate that the LSTM model is superior to the traditional models, where the RMSE is reduced from 7.43 to 4.84 and the MAPE from 14.76% percent to 8.2%. The proposed system provides a useful and scalable solution in the dynamic power grid environment for real-time electricity demand forecasting.

title: A Diffusion-Model Based Approach for Low-Overhead and Accurate Channel Estimation in LEO-MIMO Systems
authors: Boliang Han, Lei Lei, Shenghua Zhai
abstract: This letter investigates the synergy of diffusion models (DMs) and correlation-sparsity features for channel estimation in low earth orbit (LEO) satellite multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. We exploit the received signal as the guidance of DM to learn the structured features of LEO satellite channels. By extracting the high spatial correlation in the space-frequency domain and sparsity in the angle-delay domain, the DM approach exhibits potential in enabling accurate channel estimation and pilot overhead reduction. Simulation results show that the proposed approach achieves approximately 36.93% higher estimation accuracy than three advanced deep learning-based estimators and 79.39% greater pilot overhead reduction than the compressed sensing-based approach. In addition, model robustness to scenario dynamics, orbital dynamics, and frequency offset is analyzed.

title: Spatial Affordance-Aware Affine Transformation Between Heterogeneous Spaces for Mixed Reality Remote Collaboration
authors: Seonji Kim, Dooyoung Kim, Selin Choi, Woontack Woo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: We propose a spatial affordance-aware affine transformation method between heterogeneous spaces for continuous multi-object matching in shared Mixed Reality (MR) spaces. While previous redirection and spatial mapping approaches utilize physical objects and walkable areas, a critical gap remains in enabling continuous mapping between dissimilar physical environments that supports both precise object alignment and seamless locomotion in a shared space. Our method structurally segments heterogeneous spaces into interaction zones and constructs affine patches based on object adjacency and facing configuration, enabling continuous correspondence. We evaluate our method using a dataset of paired dissimilar spaces and demonstrate that, unlike conventional grid-based methods, our approach achieves broader spatial alignment and richer object matching. The results show that our method can serve as an effective mapping framework for shared environments requiring semantic continuity and structural coherence across diverse real-world spaces.

title: A Surrogate-Assisted Multi-Objective Framework for Thermal–Hydraulic Optimization of Serpentine Liquid Cooling in Lithium-Ion Battery Modules
authors: Nguyen Minh Chau, Le Van Quynh, Duong Ngoc Khanh, Le Van Nghia, Nguyen Trung Kien, Ta Tuan Hung, Nguyen Thi Hong Ngoc, Nguyen Trong Hieu
abstract: Optimizing liquid-cooled battery modules using high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) remains computationally demanding, particularly when multiple design variables and competing performance objectives must be considered simultaneously. To address this issue, this study develops a surrogate-assisted multi-objective framework for the thermal–hydraulic optimization of a serpentine liquid-cooling system for cylindrical lithium-ion battery modules. First, a three-dimensional CFD model is established and benchmarked against published results. Latin hypercube sampling is then used to generate representative design points, and Gaussian process regression (GPR) models are constructed to relate three design variables, namely channel wall thickness, coolant inlet velocity, and inlet temperature, to three performance metrics: maximum temperature, maximum temperature difference, and pressure drop. Morris global sensitivity analysis is further employed to quantify the relative importance of the design variables. The surrogate models are subsequently coupled with the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to obtain Pareto-optimal solutions. The results indicate that inlet temperature has the strongest influence on the maximum temperature, inlet velocity predominantly affects temperature uniformity, and channel wall thickness mainly governs pressure drop. Compared with the baseline design, the selected Pareto solution reduces the pressure drop from 539.0 Pa to 208.8 Pa, corresponding to a reduction of 61.26%, while increasing the maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference by only 0.21% and 1.00%, respectively. A full CFD re-evaluation of the selected optimum yields relative errors below 2.5%, supporting the reliability of the proposed framework. The proposed approach provides a practical and computationally efficient tool for balancing thermal and hydraulic performance in liquid-cooled battery modules.

title: Energy-Efficient Trajectory Planning for Collision-Free UAVs Communication in Hybrid Low-Altitude Airspace
authors: Simeng Feng, Yunyi Zhang, Shujun Zhao, Jingxiang Yuan, Kefeng Guo, Chao Dong, Qihui Wu
abstract: With the rapid development of low-altitude intelligent networks (LAINs), the growing demand for data services poses significant challenges for the existing networks. At the same time, the airspace becomes increasingly complex due to the escalating count of low-altitude users. Although unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) carrying mobile base stations to provide communication services can effectively alleviate pressure on existing network infrastructure, they unfortunately face the dual challenge of sustaining reliable data transmission and guaranteeing UAV flight safety. Therefore, in this paper, we propose a collision-free UAVs communication model specifically designed for the hybrid low-altitude environment, incorporating both static and dynamic, as well as known and unknown obstacles. To efficiently support safe flight operations of UAVs, an artificial potential field (APF)-based collision probability map is constructed, enabling the UAVs to dynamically evaluate and avoid obstacles while maintaining high communication performance constrained by limited energy resources. To maximize energy efficiency in low-altitude environments with hybrid obstacles, an adaptive association multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (AA-MADDPG) algorithm is proposed to enable collaborative trajectory planning among multiple UAVs. Simulation results confirm that the proposed strategy enhances energy efficiency by 58.06% and reduces collision probability by 86.18%, achieving significant improvements in both communication performance and flight safety.

title: Utility-Scale Battery Energy Storage Systems - Field Pilot Unit Experience, Analysis, and Review of Technological Advancements
authors: Kwabena A. Kyeremeh, Shaun E. Lavin, Grant M. Fischer, Dan M. Ionel, Aron Patrick
abstract: Utility-scale Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) are becoming increasingly important in modern power systems, providing services ranging from backup support to load balancing and peak shaving. The long-term operational performance and reliability of these systems with expanding deployment remain insufficiently documented. This paper provides an operational assessment of a 1MW/2MWh utility-scale BESS alongside a systematic review of technology developments across major subsystems. Operational metrics including round-trip efficiency (RTE), outage characteristics, and degradation patterns are examined to identify performance trends and the underlying causes of outages. In addition, testing and operational principles aligned with utility practice and standards are presented to ensure the safety and reliability of batteries. These field experiences are compared with technological advancements in BESS subsystems like thermal management systems, power conversion systems (PCS), and battery management systems (BMS). The analysis shows that early challenges such as cooling inefficiencies, limitations in power electronics, and frequent unplanned outages correspond with subsequent improvements in thermal performance, diagnostics, modularity, and control strategies. By comparing operational performance with technological enhancements, this study demonstrates how field experiences have influenced research directions and design improvements. The results provide important insights to utilities, researchers, and policy makers seeking to enhance the reliability and life-cycle performance of next-generation grid-scale BESS deployments.

title: Decoding Dyslexia, One Emotion at a Time: An AI-Powered Generative Framework
authors: Vibha Tiwari, Rohit Gupta, Rebakah Geddam, Muhammad Awais, Muhammad Ahmed Khan, Thippa Reddy Gadekallu, Hemant Ghayvat
abstract: Conventional dyslexia interventions, limited by their static, one-size-fits-all nature, fail to address learner heterogeneity, thus reducing efficacy and engagement. We introduce the Emotion-based Generative Cognitive Imprinting (EGCI) framework, a personalized, emotionally adaptive therapeutic paradigm. EGCI's closed-loop architecture uses WebSockets to connect a Python-based Emotion Sensor (DeepFace, MediaPipe) for realtime affective analysis, a core logic server, and a web interface. Its foundation is a Bayesian Dynamic Affective-Cognitive Learner Model (DACLM) that fuses a learner's cognitive profile with real-time emotion data. The DACLM's state functions as a highdimensional prompt for a Generative Content Engine (GCE) that leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to dynamically synthesize therapeutic exercises. An optimal intervention policy is derived by a Q-learning agent, which solves a state-aware reinforcement learning problem to co-optimize for skill acquisition and learner well-being. This integration of probabilistic modeling, affective sensing, generative AI, and reinforcement learning yields a scalable, dynamic, and deeply personalized solution poised to advance assistive educational technology.

title: RLSR: A Reinforcement Learning-Based Segment Routing for QoS-Aware Software Defined Network
authors: Wang Wumian, Sajal Saha, Anwar Haque, Greg Sidebottom
publish: IEEE Transactions on Machine Learning in Communications and Networking
abstract: Traffic routing is crucial for the efficient operation of the Internet. As network traffic grows, adaptive and intelligent routing algorithms are needed to meet various Quality of Service (QoS) requirements. Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based routing approaches have demonstrated superior performance over traditional methods. In this study, we propose RL-based Segment Routing (SR), called RLSR, a QoS-aware routing algorithm for Software-Defined Networking (SDN). Our approach ensures loop-free path exploration and leverages learned network QoS states to accelerate convergence when optimizing paths for multiple traffic demands. By integrating SR, RLSR enables flow-based source packet routing while reducing communication overhead between the SDN controller and network switches. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that RLSR improves load balancing by reducing the maximum link utilization from 71.67% to 50% compared with a non–RL baseline. In addition, the proposed knowledge-reuse mechanism accelerates learning, reducing the total number of training episodes by up to 27% compared with a baseline RL configuration without knowledge reuse. Further analysis shows that a higher discount factor (γ = 0.9) leads to the fastest convergence among the tested settings. These findings highlight the effectiveness of RLSR in optimizing traffic engineering and network performance in dynamic environments.
