title: List Recoverable Codes: The Good, the Bad, and the Unknown (hopefully not Ugly)
authors: Nicolas Resch, S. Venkitesh
publish: IEEE BITS the Information Theory Magazine
abstract: List recovery is a fundamental task for error-correcting codes, vastly generalizing unique decoding from worst-case errors and list decoding. Briefly, one is given “soft information” in the form of input lists $S_{1},\ldots ,S_{n}$ of bounded size, and one argues that there are not too many codewords that agree a lot with this soft information. This general problem appears in many guises, both within coding theory and in theoretical computer science more broadly. In this article we survey recent results on list recovery codes, introducing both the good (i.e., possibility results, showing that codes with certain list recoverability exist), the bad (impossibility results), and the unknown. We additionally demonstrate that, while list recoverable codes were initially introduced as a component in list decoding concatenated codes, they have since found myriad applications to and connections with other topics in theoretical computer science.

title: EMSTrans: An Efficient Hardware Accelerator for Transformer With Multi-Level Sparsity Awareness
authors: Dingyang Zou, Baichen Chen, Qiye Ding, Meiqi Wang, Zhongfeng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: Transformer-based models achieve remarkable performance but incur substantial computational overhead and data movement, especially in the attention layer that inherently exhibits multi-level sparsity (bit-level and element-level). Efficiently exploiting this sparsity to jointly optimize matrix multiplications (MMs) and softmax operations remains challenging due to the lack of rigorous theoretical support for balancing sparsity utilization, hardware complexity, and precision preservation. This paper presents EMSTrans, a sparsity-aware Transformer accelerator with two theory-driven innovations. First, through rigorous mathematical analysis, we verify that any 16 consecutive effective bits formed from INT8 elements formatted to Power-Of-2 (PO2) effective bit-widths contain an 8-bit segment that can be exactly composed from complete element fields, enabling the Sparsity-Aware Bit-Element Fusion (SABEF) systolic array for in-place fusion without reordering, achieving up to 78% energy reduction and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.99\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> speedup for sparse MMs. Second, we rigorously derive softmax numerical error bounds and propose Subtraction-Based Quantization-Pruning (SBQP) with negligible accuracy loss bounded by 0.58%, integrated with a Two-Stage Operation Fusion (TSOF) scheme to compress the sparse score matrix and reduce memory access by at least 79%. Leveraging these innovations, EMSTrans achieves a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.78\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> overall energy efficiency improvement over conventional accelerators (peak performance of 43.13 TOPS/W) without model retraining, providing a provably efficient and hardware-friendly solution for Transformer inference.

title: Self-learning PI-iTransformer: Adaptive physics modeling for forecasting real world soil temperature sensor data
authors: Shuwang Rui, Zhangbo Qiao, Yating Wang, Shaofeng Xu, Yixuan Wang, Liping Wang, Yan Shi
abstract: Temperature monitoring and forecasting are of great significance for accurately evaluating soil thermal remediation processes. However, existing data-draven forecasting models simultaneously learn from noise, which limits the accuracy of soil temperature forecasting. If a physical model is to be introduced, it is necessary to first overcome the additional errors caused by the high uncertainty of soil internal structure and local physicochemical parameters. To address these challenges, this study proposes a self-learning physics constrained iTransformer prediction framework. This model transforms multi-scale physical constraints into regularization mechanisms embedded in the training process through autonomous learning of dynamic control equations, avoiding the rigid introduction of physical models that may cause additional errors. At the same time, it captures to some extent the transient characteristics of sensor time series and the periodic patterns of heat conduction. The experimental results show that the physical constraints reduces the average MAE and MSE of real prediction scenarios by 5%, effectively overcoming the inherent bottleneck of high noise data. This method has also achieved optimal performance on multiple industrial time series benchmarks, confirming its generalizability for predicting complex physical systems and establishing a new paradigm for temporal prediction of noisy sensor data in industrial control.

title: Endless Runner F0: Loudness-Based and Pitch-Based Voice Control for VR Arcade Games
authors: Kacper Godula, Sławomir Tadeja, Daniela Hekiert, Magdalena Igras-Cybulska
abstract: When present, the voice control in virtual reality (VR) gaming is usually speech-based, while non-verbal voice features (e.g. loudness and pitch) remain underexplored. Consequently, we developed Endless Runner F0, a VR-based endless runner for Meta Quest 2 that integrates non-verbal voice mechanics as core interactions via loudness- and pitch-controlled obstacles. In a counter-balanced within-subjects study ($N{=}16$), participants played five variants (no voice control as a baseline; loudness with/without visual indicators; pitch with/without visual indicators). We collected performance scores and questionnaire data, namely state anxiety (STAI-1) and presence (IPQ) after each variant, and pre- and post-simulator sickness (SSQ). Practical comparability across these variants was evaluated using paired Two One-Sided Tests (t-TOST). Results showed practical equivalence for the baseline and voice-enabled variants for comfort, presence, and overall performance within a priori margins; however, pitch control yielded higher voice-obstacle collision rates than loudness. The findings highlight loudness-based mechanics as easier to learn and pitch-based mechanics as viable with appropriate feedback and calibration. We discuss design implications and outline opportunities for applying voice-controlled VR in playful vocal training contexts.

title: Generative Agents for Learning Behavioral Simulation: An Interview-Driven and Multi-Expert Reflection Framework
authors: Cunling Bian, Zimeng Zhai, Saisai Ye, Weigang Lu
abstract: Accurately modeling and predicting student learning behaviors remains a central challenge in educational research, as traditional approaches based on surveys or learning-process traces often struggle to capture the multidimensional and latent nature of learners' motivational and strategic states. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) and generative agents offer new opportunities to simulate learning behaviors by integrating memory construction, contextual reasoning, and expert-informed reflection. In this study, we propose a theory-grounded framework for Learning Behavioral Simulation that leverages structured interview transcripts and multi-expert reflective modeling to instantiate generative student agents. We conducted interviews with 46 preparatory mathematics students and generated domain-specific reflections, spanning educational psychology, learning sciences, educational technology, assessment, and sociocultural education, using LLM-instantiated educational expert agents. The instantiated generative student agents, informed by these expert reflections, were tasked with predicting responses on the Motivated Strategies for Learning Questionnaire, enabling construct-level evaluation of motivational and learning strategy behaviors. Experimental results show that interview-driven agents augmented with expert reflections consistently outperform persona- and demographic-based baselines. Further analysis reveals that reflections from different expert domains capture complementary dimensions of learning behavior, improving both predictive robustness and interpretability. Overall, this work demonstrates how interview-grounded memory construction and expert-guided reflection can support interpretable, scalable, and model-agnostic generative simulation of student learning behaviors, with implications for personalized and explainable educational applications.

title: Adaptive finite-gain prescribed-time attitude tracking control of RLV: a super-twisting based approach
authors: Ruifeng Li, Liqian Dou, Jianuo Sun, Zhiyu Li, Bailing Tian, Qun Zong, Hao Cheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper proposes an adaptive finite-gain prescribed-time tracking control framework for reusable launch vehicle attitude systems subject to unknown external disturbances. A nominal prescribed-time controller is first developed for disturbance-free dynamics. Subsequently, to address unknown disturbances, a prescribed-time integral sliding manifold integrating the nominal controller is constructed. Then, an adaptive prescribed time super-twisting algorithm is developed without the need for disturbances' prior upper bound information while suppressing the chattering phenomenon. The proposed scheme ensures global prescribed-time stability with uniformly bounded control gains, whose bounds are independent of initial states, and offers continuous control input. In addition, closed-loop stability is rigorously proven via the Lyapunov analysis along with the time trans formation and state transformation technique. Finally, numerical simulations are provided to validate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme.

title: SAHG-WMRNet: A Structure-Aware Hierarchical Graph Network for Multifunction Radar Work Mode Recognition
authors: Zixiang Zhou, Chuyi Liu, Ping Lang, Jian Dong, Meijing GaoMember, Xiongjun Fu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The pulse sequence-based multifunction radar (MFR) work mode recognition (WMR) is crucial for cognitive electronic warfare. However, the missing and spurious pulses, measurement errors, and limited labeled samples pose severe challenges to the WMR task. SAHG-WMRNet is proposed based on self-supervised pulse group segmentation and semi-supervised adaptive hierarchical graph nodes recognition. Firstly, pulse sequence parameters are encoded with multiple feature maps in the high inter-class separable latent space through self-supervised multi-scale encoder, and the differences among feature maps are utilized to automatically segment the pulse sequence into multiple pulse groups with their individual modulation types via segmentation manner. Then, the initial pulse-level graph is constructed based on feature maps via K-nearest neighbor algorithm. Finally, a pulse and pulse group-level hierarchical graph is constructed through adaptive coarsening and refining based on the pulse group segmentation results. By using hierarchical graph, the WMR task can be implemented in a semi-supervised manner, utilizing a small amount of labeled data and explicit geometric relationships. The simulation results in 1-shot and 5-shot scenarios show that the proposed method can achieve better recognition accuracy with limited labeled samples under non-ideal scenarios, compared to some existing methods.

title: Toward Emotion-Preserving Speech Semantic Communication in Affective Social Systems
authors: Taojie Zhu, Mingkai Chen, Lei Wang, M. Shamim Hossain
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: With the rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) and ubiquitous connectivity, speech communication is becoming increasingly important in achieving intelligent interactions between humans, machines, and objects in computational social systems. However, current neural network-based semantic communication frameworks primarily focus on transmitting semantic information while largely overlooking the emotion features in speech communication, which is vital for naturalness and effective interaction in computational social systems. In this article, we propose an emotion-enhanced speech semantic communication system, which effectively enhances the expressiveness and robustness of emotion AI for speech communication. First, we propose an emotion fusion encoding module at the transmitter, where features are dynamically fused via attention mechanisms and subsequently encoded through a channel encoder. Then, we introduce an emotion orthogonal decoding module at the receiver, which reconstructs the fused features via a channel decoder followed by an orthogonally constrained disentanglement network. In addition, a conditional diffusion model guided by emotion features reconstructs high-fidelity speech with enriched emotional expressiveness. Finally, experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves both the bit error rate and the mean opinion score over state-of-the-art models. Furthermore, the system achieves notable reductions in transmission dimensionality.

title: CWS-YOLO : An Optical Remote Sensing Ship Detection Model with Cauchy-Based Attention and Sparse Shared Head
authors: Yinfei Geng, Ningning Qi
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Ship detection using optical remote sensing plays a critical role in maritime monitoring. However, existing methods face significant challenges under complex sea conditions, such as high background noise interference, instability caused by large variations in target scale, and real-time processing bottlenecks when dealing with high-resolution imagery. To address these issues, this paper proposes an optical remote sensing ship detection model with Cauchy-based attention and a sparse shared head(CWS-YOLO). The model enhances performance through two key modules. First, the Pyramid Cauchy Attention (PCA) module leverages the heavy-tailed nature of the Cauchy distribution to effectively suppress background interference from waves and clouds while enhancing focus on critical ship features. Second, the Cauchy Shared Detection Head (CSD) employs parameter sharing and sparsity constraints to significantly reduce model complexity while maintaining high detection accuracy across multiple scales. Experimental results demonstrate that CWS-YOLO achieves 85.2% mAP@0.5 and 60.3% mAP@[0.5:0.95] on datasets such as HRSC2016, with model parameters and computational cost reduced by approximately 20% and 7.9%, respectively, compared to the baseline. The inference speed reaches 150 FPS, showing strong potential for deployment on edge devices. These results validate the effectiveness of CWS-YOLO in achieving a favorable balance between accuracy and efficiency, offering a practical solution for real-time ship detection under complex maritime conditions.

title: SNR Comparison Between Anomalous Hall Effect and Tunneling Magneto Resistance HDD Readers for 4 Tbpsi Using a Simulated Read Head Equivalent Circuit
authors: Kazuaki Okamoto, Satoshi Shirotori, Akira Kikitsu, Kenichiro Yamada, Yoshihiro Higashi
abstract: Hard disk drive (HDD) areal density (AD) has increased remarkably in recent years. This increase in AD has caused shrinkage of magnetic recording patterns, thus requiring a reduction of magnetic sensor size in HDD readers. This paper focuses on HDD readers based on the anomalous Hall effect (AHE) magnetic sensor (AHE reader) and tunneling magnetic resistance (TMR) magnetic sensor (TMR reader). To estimate the potential of these readers for 4 Tbpsi ultra-high AD, we calculate the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the reader using a read head equivalent circuit including the input impedance of the preamp (Zin). As a result, we find that the AHE reader is superior to the TMR reader in terms of the balance between magnetic sensor resistance and Zin, and the suppression of the magnetic noise effect in the high-SNR region.

title: DBEDML: Dual Branch Energy-Driven Meta-Learning for Hyperspectral Imagery Cross-Domain Few-Shot Open-set Recognition
authors: Qixing Yu, Zhongwei Li, Fangming Guo, Ziqi Xin, Guangbo Ren, Jianbu Wang, Zhenggang Bi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: In the true hyperspectral scenes, open-set recognition (OSR) is inevitable, yet large scale label acquisition is challenging due to environmental complexity. An intriguing approach is to transfer knowledge from scenes with large-scale labeled samples (source domain, SD) to target scenes (target domain, TD), then fine-tune using a few labeled TD samples to recognize known classes and reject unknown ones. However, three issues arise, namely insufficient class samples, asymmetrical inter-domain label spaces, and the open-set learning problem. To address the aforementioned problems, we define this as the cross-domain few-shot open-set recognition (CDFSOSR) task and propose a dual branch energy-driven meta-learning (DBEDML) algorithm. For problems 1 and 3, we design a dual branch architecture incorporating contrastive learning and distance metrics, driven by an energy function, to effectively solve OSR in few-shot learning (FSL). For problem 2, we introduce fake prototypes as unknown classes in SD for domain alignment (DA), and design a Decoupled Adversarial Domain Alignment Module to align known class distributions between SD and TD while distinguishing unknown classes. Extensive experiments on three self-constructed coastal wetland datasets and two public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of DBEDML. Code will be available at https://github.com/Yqx-ACE/TGRS_2026_DBEDML.

title: Transient Voltage and Current Calculations Based on Formulation of Modified Baum-Liu-Tesche Equation in Phase Domain
authors: Zhaoyang Wang, Yihui Zuo, Siying Liu, Reza Razzaghi
abstract: This study presents a modified Baum-Liu-Tesche (BLT) equation, derived in a compact matrix-algebraic form, for the frequency-domain analysis of transient voltages and currents in multi-conductor transmission lines (MTLs). The proposed formulation addresses two broad categories of transient events, including faults and lightning strikes, while accommodating diverse event types and occurrence scenarios. The modified BLT equation is further extended into an explicit form, enabling its application to power networks with inhomogeneous parameters and complex topologies. This extension is supported by a systematic derivation of the structural features and element characteristics of the involved matrices and vectors. Numerical validation using the benchmark IEEE 34-bus test distribution network confirms strong agreement between the results obtained from the modified BLT equation and those generated by electromagnetic-transient-type simulations. Overall, the proposed formulation provides an effective and robust analytical framework for transient analysis in power systems.

title: Auto-Tuning of Droop Controllers for Grid-Forming Inverters
authors: Xiangchen Zeng, Tommaso Caldognetto, Diego Rigato, Andrea Lauri, Davide Biadene, Qing Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This article proposes an auto-tuning control strategy for the power loop of grid-forming inverters to compensate the dynamic performance degradation caused by the variation of unknown variables (e.g., grid impedance, inverter parameter values) under grid-connected operating conditions. The autotuning is achieved by online monitoring the crossover frequency and phase margin of the power control loop, and adjusting key parameters of the droop controller according to these performance indices. It is shown that the crossover frequency and phase margin can be maintained at desired values, thus the expected dynamic control performance can be achieved and maintained. Simulation and experimental results are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: VCF-Pose: Velocity-Guided Coarse-to-Fine Pose Estimation from mmWave Point Clouds
authors: Xiaokai Liu, Lihao Qiao, Zhiyong Ji, Yangyang Wang, Jie Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Human pose estimation with millimeter-wave radar has become a promising alternative sensing method due to its robustness against varying lighting conditions and occlusions. However, existing methods often suffer from temporal inconsistencies and spatial inaccuracies, primarily due to the sparse nature of point clouds and the underutilization of valuable Doppler velocity information. To address this challenge, we propose VCF-Pose, a novel Velocity-guided Coarse-to-Fine Pose estimation framework that creatively transforms unstructured Doppler data into a structured motion representation to dynamically guide the refinement of an initial coarse pose, ensuring it aligns with temporal dynamics and anatomical priors. Specifically, VCF-Pose first generates an initial coarse pose estimate using a GRU-based sequential model to capture temporal context from voxelized radar data. Then we design a velocity space projection module that creatively transforms unstructured raw Doppler data into a structured 3D velocity feature volume. This novel representation enables a 3D relational convolution network to effectively model the fine-grained correlations between motion dynamics and spatial geometry. Finally, a structural prior-guided feature fusion module leverages anatomical constraints to perform global alignment and refinement, ensuring the final pose is anatomically plausible. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VCF-Pose significantly improves inter-frame continuity and reduces estimation error, achieving an average error of 4.03 cm and outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods.

title: Evaluating Multi-Chiplet Integration of Automotive Radar System-In-Packages in eWLB and RDL-first
authors: Pascal Stadler, Christian Geissler, Philipp Fromme, Yasin Comak, Nils Pohl, Tobias T. Braun
abstract: This work demonstrates heterogeneous integration of a 76–77 GHz 4x4 radar system in package using two advanced packaging technologies, Infineon’s embedded wafer-level ball grid array (eWLB) and Fraunhofer IZM’s redistribution line (RDL)-first. Both package designs employ identical designs aside from PCB and chiplet interfaces, enabling a direct performance comparison between their thermomechanical reliability and electromagnetic performance. The analysis further highlights the importance of chiplet-package co-design by addressing potential package degradation mechanisms. Both 7.8 x 8.8 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> packages were validated with MIMO measurements in the same measurement environment and under similar conditions. Within the 37 m measurement environment, a successful target detection with both SiPs was achieved unambiguously with at least ±16° azimuth resolution.

title: A Miniaturized and Flexible Ultrasound-driven Intracranial Brain Stimulator for Neuromodulation Applications
authors: Yulong Chen, Yaqi Huang, Qin Zeng, Zhen Wang, Zeqing Liu, Guoyi Yu, Jun Ou-Yang, Xiaofei Yang, Furong Wang, Tao Zhang, Benpeng Zhu
abstract: Objective: Conventional brain stimulators primarily rely on implantable batteries, necessitating repeated replacement surgeries. Ultrasound-driven stimulators offer a promising wireless alternative, yet existing systems are predominantly extracranial and face limitations in stability and efficiency. Here, we fabricated a miniaturized, implantable ultrasound-driven intracranial brain stimulator (UIBS), achieving stable and efficient neuromodulation. Methods: The UIBS was developed by integrating a flexible composite structure consisting of PVDF-TrFE and a flexible acoustic matching layer with a rectifier circuit embedded in a PEEK structure. Additionally, transcranial ultrasound transmission was optimized through numerical simulations and experimental validation. Electrical output performance, the electrolysis-defined safety window, and neuromodulation efficacy as well as biocompatibility following UIBS implantation into the rat primary somatosensory cortex were systematically assessed. Results: The optimal transcranial ultrasound frequency was determined to be 1.5 MHz. Driven by transcranial ultrasound at 2 MPa, the UIBS generated a rectified output voltage exceeding 1.3 V, with a safe electrolysis duration exceeding 10 seconds at 100 Hz. Furthermore, in vivo experiments demonstrated that under ultrasound driving, the device can be stably implanted and reliably evoke neural activity in the primary somatosensory cortex, while maintaining good biosafety. Conclusion: This work presents a novel and miniaturized UIBS, enabling effective intracranial energy harvesting and precise neuromodulation, addressing key constraints of battery-dependent and extracranial devices.

title: Hybrid DNN-ELM design surrogate for Pierce Gun of HPM sources
authors: Mita Jana, MV Kartikeyan, Abhisekh Kumar Jha, Raja Ramana Rao P, S K Datta
abstract: Accurate prediction of electron-beam parameters in Pierce gun design requires efficient surrogate models capable of learning highly nonlinear geometric–beam interactions from limited datasets. Conventional deep neural networks (DNNs) often suffer from overfitting when the training data is sparse. To address this, a hybrid Deep Neural Network–Extreme Learning Machine (DNN–ELM) model is proposed. The hybrid architecture extracts nonlinear latent features using a compact DNN and performs fast generalized regression with an ELM output layer, demonstrating superior generalization and reduced error compared to a standalone DNN.

title: Research on Physics-Constrained Low-Rank Tensor Decomposition for Undersampled Reconstruction in Modulated Active Infrared Thermography for GFRP Defect Detection
authors: Feng Yang, Fei Wang, Rongcheng Li, Jianghao Zhao, Zhuoyan Yue, Jiaye Chen, Honghao Yue, Junyan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Modulated excitation active infrared thermography (AIT) often requires recording sufficiently long infrared image sequences over one or multiple excitation periods for reliable defect characterization. This leads to a large data volume that burdens transmission, storage, and latency in online inspection. This work proposes a physics constrained low rank reconstruction framework, Tucker Decomposition constrained by the Lattice Boltzmann Method (TDLBM), to recover complete infrared image sequences (IIS) from sparsely sampled frames. The IIS is modeled by Tucker decomposition to exploit spatiotemporal low rank structure, while heat diffusion consistency is enforced through a differentiable LBM-based physical evolution step constructed from a multi relaxation time thermal lattice Boltzmann operator in the Tucker core tensor space. This design couples measurement consistency with the acquired frames and diffusion consistent temporal evolution within a unified optimization. Experiments on glass fiber reinforced polymer specimens under sinusoidal excitation and chirp excitation evaluate random temporal sampling rates (SR) from 1% to 30%. The results show that almost lossless reconstruction with peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR) above 40 dB and structure similarity index measure (SSIM) above 0.99 can be achieved at SR between 5% and 10% (30 fps camera used in this work). At SR of 5%, the reconstructed data preserve the thermal field characteristics and support subsequent defect characterization with signal to noise ratio (SNR) above 80. The proposed method provides an effective route to reduce acquisition and transmission requirements while maintaining reconstruction quality and defect detectability for practical online infrared nondestructive testing.

title: Floating Photovoltaic Systems in Urban Areas: Insights on the Thermoelectric Behavior and Water Evaporation Reduction
authors: Breno Bezerra Freitas, Paulo Cesar Marques de Carvalho, José Carlos de Araújo, Leticia de Oliveira Santos
abstract: Floating photovoltaic (FPV) systems have gained attention worldwide due to their potential gains in generation, land saving, and water evaporation reduction. However, experimental data from installed FPV plants operating under high irradiance and ambient temperature remains scarce. Our study presents an experimental assessment comparing FPV and ground-mounted photovoltaic (GPV) systems installed side by side in an urban area under semiarid climate. The analysis considers operating weather conditions, and focuses on photovoltaic (PV) modules’ thermal and electrical behavior, as well as on the impact of FPV on the water evaporation rates. The FPV system covers 50% of the surface of a water tank; an adjacent uncovered tank is used as a reference for evaporation data. Overall, FPV and GPV modules show a similar electrical performance, with equivalence tests indicating that differences remain practically negligible at the operational scale (between <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-$</tex-math></inline-formula>1.08% and 0.06%). FPV modules often operate at similar or slightly higher temperatures than GPV modules, consequence of low wind speed values, usually found in urban environments. In contrast, FPV modules consistently reduce water evaporation, with an average reduction of 40%, saving about 8.4 m<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula> during the period.

title: Parametric Modeling and GPR Imaging of Planetary Lava Tubes Constrained by Terrestrial Analogs_supp1-3692190.pdf
authors: Ling Zhang
abstract: Lava tubes are priority subsurface targets on basaltic planetary bodies, and Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) is a primary detection method. However, interpretation is often limited by forward models relying on idealized cavities, which underestimate scattering effects caused by geometric asymmetry and boundary roughness. We introduce a parametric modeling and imaging workflow constrained by terrestrial analogs. Using measured cross-sections from the Pyroduct database, we embedded realistic cavity geometries into a lunar stratigraphic model defined by correlated random permittivity. Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) simulations were performed with a 60 MHz source, followed by Kirchhoff pre-stack time migration. Unlike ideal cavities, analog-constrained models produce asymmetric diffractions and local phase discontinuities. Quantitative analysis shows that realistic morphological complexity severely degrades the signal-to-clutter ratio (SCR) of ceiling reflections, which drops from 80.67 dB in the idealized model to 25.19 dB in the analog-constrained model. Migration analysis suggests that while ceiling location and lateral extent are reliably constrained, the tube floor remains difficult to resolve. Field GPR data from a terrestrial lava tube show similar ceiling-dominant responses, validating this geometric prior model for evaluating performance in future planetary missions.

title: Double ended travelling wave fault location using unsynchronized data
authors: J. Blumschein
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Travelling Wave Fault Location becomes more and more popular. Users worldwide praise the great accuracy and reliability of travelling wave fault location. Due to the principle of travelling wave fault location the accuracy of the fault location is based on an accurate time measurement of the travelling wave wavefronts. For travelling wave fault location, the time accuracy should be in the range of nanoseconds. A time inaccuracy of one microsecond could cause an error of up to 300 m for the fault location. For double ended travelling wave fault location this time accuracy needs to be maintained for both devices which can be placed several hundreds of kilometers away from each other.The paper starts with a short introduction of travelling wave fault location, followed by a discussion of possible sources of inaccuracy for single ended and double ended travelling wave fault location.The main part of the paper is related to a new method of double ended travelling wave fault location which does not need synchronized data. The method is explained in detail evaluating its advantages and disadvantages compared to the well-known double ended and single ended methods for travelling wave fault location.Finally, examples based on real fault records are used to demonstrate the performance of the new method compared to the well-known single ended and double ended travelling wave fault location.

title: Measurement of the Effective Input Noise Temperature of a Cryogenic Amplifier at 4 K using the Cold Attenuator Method
authors: M Salter, M Stanley, A Elarabi, D Singh, M Celep, N Ridler
abstract: Techniques are described for measuring the noise properties (effective input noise temperature and available gain) of a cryogenic amplifier at 4 K based on the cold attenuator method and results obtained using a vector network analyzer (VNA) fitted with a low noise receiver are presented.

title: The 105/140 GHz Megawatt Waveguide Transmission Line at ASDEX Upgrade
authors: D. Wagner, J. Stober, F. Leuterer, H. Schütz, G. Schrepfer, M. Schubert, M. Thumm
abstract: The Electron Cyclotron Resonance Heating (ECRH) system at the ASDEX Upgrade tokamak employs 8 gyrotrons, each delivering 1 MW at 140 GHz and about 800 kW at 105 GHz with a maximum pulse length of 10s. The gyrotron output beams are coupled to 8 waveguide transmission lines via quasi-optical Matching Optics Units (MOUs). The oversized corrugated HE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf> waveguides with an inner diameter of 87 mm are operated at atmospheric pressure with overall lengths between 65 and 103 meters. The number of quasi-optical miter bends per line is between 6 and 8. High mode purity in the transmission lines is critical with respect to both, losses and atmospheric breakdowns in the waveguides.

title: A Review of Lunar Rover Wheels
authors: Sandor Burian, Dmitri Garin, Miklos Kozlovszky
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review and comparative analysis of lunar rover wheel designs that have been successfully deployed and tested on the Moon. Unlike conceptual models or prototypes that failed during terrestrial testing, this study focuses exclusively on wheels that achieved operational performance in real lunar missions. The evaluation emphasizes the physical characteristics and engineering principles that govern wheel functionality under the Moon's unique environmental conditions. To establish a foundation for comparison, the initial section examines the lunar terrain and regolith properties, as these factors critically influence wheel design requirements such as traction, load distribution, and abrasion resistance. The subsequent sections analyze both historical and modern wheel architecture, highlighting its structural features, material choices, and performance.

title: Table Tennis Scoring System: Single-Camera, Real-Time Scoring System with Hybrid Image Processing
authors: Márk Hanyvári, Ádám Pintér
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Automated scoring of table tennis matches presents a significant technological challenge due to the high speed of the ball, its small size, and variable environmental conditions. This paper presents a cost-effective, single-camera system that performs real-time ball detection and scoring locally. The system employs a hybrid approach, combining traditional OpenCV-based geometry detection with a YOLOv8 object detection architecture optimized for ONNX. A Finite State Machine (FSM) interprets the ball's trajectory and bounce locations to award points automatically. Results demonstrate that the system maintains a stable processing pipeline on consumer-grade hardware while providing robust scoring accuracy across diverse lighting conditions.

title: Fluid Antenna Enabled Direction-of-Arrival Estimation Under Time-Constrained Mobility
authors: He Xu, Tuo Wu, Ye Tian, Kangda Zhi, Wei Liu, Baiyang Liu, Hing Cheung So, Naofal Al-Dhahir, Kin-Fai Tong, Chan-Byoung Chae, Kai-Kit Wong
abstract: Fluid antenna (FA) technology has emerged as a promising approach in wireless communications due to its capability of providing increased degrees of freedom (DoFs) and exceptional design flexibility. This paper addresses the challenge of direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation for aligned received signals (ARS) and non-aligned received signals (NARS) by designing two specialized uniform FA structures under time-constrained mobility. For ARS scenarios, we propose a fully movable antenna configuration that maximizes the virtual array aperture, whereas for NARS scenarios, we design a structure incorporating a fixed reference antenna to reliably extract phase information from the signal covariance. To overcome the limitations of large virtual arrays and limited sample data inherent in time-varying channels (TVC), we introduce two novel DOA estimation methods: TMRLS-MUSIC for ARS, combining Toeplitz matrix reconstruction (TMR) with linear shrinkage (LS) estimation, and TMR-MUSIC for NARS, utilizing sub-covariance matrices to construct virtual array responses. Both methods employ Nyström approximation to significantly reduce computational complexity while maintaining estimation accuracy. Theoretical analyses and extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed methods achieve underdetermined DOA estimation using minimal FA elements, outperform conventional methods in estimation accuracy, and substantially reduce computational complexity.

title: A Multi-Scale Network with Cross-View Gated Attention Enhancement for Human Activity Recognition
authors: Hao Wang, Fangyu Liu, Xiang Li, Yiqian Zheng, Ye Li, Fangmin Sun
abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) based on inertial sensors plays a pivotal role in consumer electronics applications such as health monitoring, fitness tracking, and human-computer interaction. However, achieving robust HAR performance remains challenging due to significant variations in activity duration and rhythms, restricted views in attention modeling, and information loss caused by excessive dimensionality compression. In this paper, we propose MSNCVGA, a multi-scale network enhanced by cross-view gated attention for inertial sensor-based HAR tasks. This method explicitly models sequence temporal scale diversity through multi-scale convolutions and a cross-scale feature fusion branch, while utilizing the cross-view gated attention mechanism to synergistically focus on complementary and dependent cross-view global key features among channel, sequence, and sample dimensions, effectively avoiding excessive information loss. Finally, a recurrent neural network is employed to integrate contextual features from different scale branches. Experiments on five public datasets demonstrate that MSNCVGA achieves better performance than the baseline methods, reaching F1-scores of 0.848, 0.989, 0.985, 0.642, and 0.979 on PAMAP2, UCI HAR, mHealth, RealWorld, and KU-HAR, respectively. Notably, the proposed model has only 0.54M parameters, providing a high-accuracy, low-latency solution suitable for deployment on resource-constrained consumer electronic devices.

title: Uncertainty-Driven Generative Prior Learning for Sparse Model-Guided Hyperspectral Image Fusion
authors: Junwei Xu, Teng Feng, Zhenxuan Fang, Fangfang Wu, Le Dong, Tao Huang, Zhou Yang, Weisheng Dong, Xin Li
abstract: s an alternative to acquiring high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI), Hyperspectral Image Fusion (HIF) aims to recover clean HR-HSIs by fusing degraded low spatial resolution hyperspectral images and high spatial resolution multispectral images. Existing model-guided HIF methods elegantly integrate physical degradation constraints with the powerful learning capabilities of data-driven networks.s an alternative to acquiring high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI), Hyperspectral Image Fusion (HIF) aims to recover clean HR-HSIs by fusing degraded low spatial resolution hyperspectral images and high spatial resolution multispectral images. Existing model-guided HIF methods elegantly integrate physical degradation constraints with the powerful learning capabilities of data-driven networks.A However, these methods struggle with severe, unseen degradations, as their deep priors are trained on degraded-clean pairs and thus lack knowledge of clean HSI characteristics. To address these issues, we introduce a Vector-Quantized Prior-Guided Network (VPG-Net), which incorporates a novel uncertainty-driven generative model prior alongside a traditional sparse representation prior. Specifically, VPG-Net unfolds the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation with a sparse representation model into an uncertainty-aware generative prior-guided network implementation. The sparse representation prior is integrated into the MAP framework to improve noise resistance, whose solution is implemented as a trainable neural network. As the core of our contribution, we leverage a high-quality vector-quantized (VQ) prior, which serves as a powerful degradation-free generative prior for the HIF process. Specifically, we pre-train a discrete codebook and encoder from clean HR-HSIs to generate a VQ-prior representation (VQPR), which preserves complete spatial-spectral information. To effectively bridge the gap between degraded inputs and the learned degradation-free codebook, we incorporate a novel uncertainty-driven probabilistic matching strategy that robustly aligns features and prevents artifacts. The learned VQPR is then incorporated into the reconstruction model as dynamic modulation parameters to enhance the fidelity and realism of the reconstructed results, particularly for severely degraded inputs. Extensive experiments on clean and degraded synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of- the-art HIF methods in both quantitative metrics and visual quality.

title: Edge Feature Enhancement and Multi-scale Convolutional Kernel Selection Aggregation for Fine-grained Ship Detection
authors: Yaxiong Chen, Shuo Gao, Qi Wu, Zhong Sai, GuiRong Feng
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: In recent years, with the continuous advancement of high-resolution remote sensing imaging technology, the capability of perceiving maritime ship targets under complex sea conditions has been significantly improved. This holds substantial theoretical value and practical significance for enhancing maritime traffic situational awareness, strengthening the supervision of fishery activities, and safeguarding the marine environment as well as maritime security. However, due to the single imaging perspective of remote sensing images, fine-grained ship detection still faces challenges. such as large intra-class scale variations caused by varying imaging heights and ambiguous inter-class boundaries for visually similar ship types.To address this issue, we propose the Edge-Aware Feature Enhancement Module (EFEM) to strengthen the fusion of edge details and spatial contextual information of ships in remote sensing images, and design the Multi-Scale Kernel Aggregation Module (MSKAM) to improve the model’s ability to perceive ships of varying sizes. These modules effectively solve the problem of extracting discriminative features of ships. Finally, experiments conducted on the third hierarchy of the ShipRSImageNet dataset and the FAIR1M dataset demonstrate that, compared with the benchmark model, the proposed fine-grained ship detection model achieves improvements of 1.87 % and 1.72 % in the mAP50 metric, respectively. Moreover, comparative validations on the two datasets further confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in enhancing the accuracy of fine-grained ship detection.

title: A Two-Stage Decision-Making Method for Team Member Replenishment Problem
authors: Jiting Li, Yu Cheng
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Team formation is a complex human resource allocation activity that need consider not only the match between individuals and positions within the team but also the collaboration among team members. This paper focuses on the team member replenishment (TMR) scenario in team formation problem, and proposes a two-stage decision-making method, comprising person-position matching degree calculation and team formation optimization. The person-position matching degree calculation stage integrates human resource data and group decision-making preference information, effectively addressing the evaluation of person-position matching for different types of positions within a team. In the team formation optimization stage, an improved NSGA-II algorithm (NSGA-II algorithm for Team Member Replenishment, TMR-NSGA-II) is proposed, which designs an individual fitness calculation mechanism based on an accelerated computation strategy. The effectiveness of the proposed method is first demonstrated through a detailed case study. Furthermore, a large-scale comparative experiment demonstrates that the proposed TMR-NSGA-II algorithm improves solution efficiency compared to the standard NSGA-II by 13.02 %.

title: Prediction of Nanobody-Antigen Interaction Based on Epitope Aware with Crossmodal Attention
authors: Pengyan Zhang, Juntao Deng, Miao Gu, Yunfan Zhang, Yuanhang Mao, Tao Liu, Mingyu Dong, Min Liu
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Accurately predicting nanobody-antigen interactions is essential for nanobody discovery and design. Recently, a number of computational approaches have been proposed to identify antibody-antigen interactions using sequence or structural information. However, effectively capturing the cross-molecular interaction patterns between nanobody paratopes and antigen epitopes remains a challenging problem. In this study, we propose CrossNano, a deep learning framework for predicting nanobodyantigen interactions based on epitope-aware feature learning and crossmodal attention. The model first extracts sequence and structural representations of nanobody and antigen molecules, and then employs a cross-attention mechanism to model the interactions between nanobody sequence features and antigen structure features. Furthermore, an antigen epitope prediction task is incorporated to guide the interaction representation learning. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves improved performance compared with other methods, indicating its effectiveness for nanobody-antigen interaction prediction.

title: A Dual-Stator Synchronous-Vernier Permanent Magnet Machine: Comprehensive Design-Space Study for Power Factor Enhancement
authors: Litao Dai, Shuangxia Niu, Xiaohui Huang, Zekai Lyu, Zhenyao Sun, K. T. Chau
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive study of a new double‑stator synchronous‑Vernier permanent‑magnet (PM) machine topology aimed at maximizing both power factor and torque density. The proposed machine employs two stators sharing the same rotor pole count: one stator adopts a low‑spatial‑pole PM Vernier machine (PMVM) winding, while the other uses a high‑spatial‑pole PM synchronous machine (PMSM) winding. This configuration combines the high torque density characteristic of PMVMs with the superior power factor of PMSMs. Crucially, the double‑stator configuration offers substantial design flexibility, including the selection of pole-slot combinations, winding connection schemes, inter‑winding phase shifts, and multiple geometric parameters, thereby enabling a continuous trade-off between power factor and torque. These design degrees of freedom are systematically analyzed in this work to fully exploit the performance potential of the proposed topology.

title: Simulating Cracking and Breaking of Glass and Other Objects
authors: Krisztián László, Sándor Szénási
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: The aim of this paper is to develop a three-dimensional fracture and crack generation system capable of producing realistic crack patterns and separating 3D objects based on the resulting fracture surfaces. The model applies a discrete grid-based approach, where crack propagation is driven by energy-based rules and local material resistance defined by an alpha texture. The generated 2D crack pattern is extended into a 3D fracture plane, which is then used by a voxelbased separation algorithm to divide the object into individual pieces. The program was implemented in the. NET environment using C# programming language, while visualization is performed in the Unity game engine. The paper presents the physical background of fracture mechanics, an overview of existing methods, the design and implementation of the developed algorithm, and the evaluation of test results. The program is capable of generating diverse, reproducible crack structures and performing stable, real-time separation of 3D objects.

title: Extended Accelerated Stress Testing of Lamination-Free Edge-Sealed Photovoltaic Minimodules
authors: Steve Johnston, Ryan Ruhle, Dana B. Kern, Kent Terwilliger, David Durney, Walajabad Sampath
abstract: Lamination-free minimodules are constructed with silicon solar cells between glass sheets and an edge seal of polyisobutylene and silicone. These samples are stressed using a repeated sequential testing sequence including ultraviolet-containing simulated solar spectrum light exposure at elevated temperature, damp heat, humidity freeze, and thermal cycling adapted from the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) TS 63209-2:2022. The minimodule performance throughout the stressing is characterized by flash testing and electroluminescence imaging. Results are compared to conventional laminated minimodules using the same type of solar cells. The lamination-free minimodules experience up to 8% power loss compared to roughly 3% for the laminated versions. However, those losses, dominated by current and fill factor, are caused by glass soiling and busbar ribbon separation on the cells. When glass is replaced and the stressed cells are contacted with probes bypassing the delaminated ribbons, the initial performance is recovered, and overall losses of the lamination-free samples become negligible.

title: Soft-Aided Hard-Decision Decoding and Efficient Stall Pattern Search for Higher-Order Staircase Codes
authors: Junfeng Pan, Shancheng Zhao
abstract: Higher-order staircase codes form a class of generalized staircase codes in which each bit is protected by more than two component codes. Among them, the extended-Hamming-based higher-order staircase codes are promising candidates for applications that require high coding rate, low error floor, and low decoding complexity. Despite their potential, their performance is still limited by conventional decoding methods, and the structural properties of their minimum stall patterns are not fully understood. To address these issues, we first introduce a novel soft-aided bounded distance decoder (SA-BDD) based on constrained bit flipping (CBF), denoted as SA-BDD-CBF, which demonstrates an effective performance–complexity–latency tradeoff of the extended-Hamming-based higher-order staircase codes. With appropriate parameter, the complexity of SA-BDDCBF is at most 3.4 times that of BDD. This is significantly lower than those of the multiply-chained zipper code and the traditional staircase code, which require approximately 31 and 39 times the complexity of BDD, respectively. Meanwhile, its performance gaps to the comparable multiply-chained zipper code and the traditional staircase code is only 0.10–0.15 dB, demonstrating an effective trade-off between decoding complexity and performance. We then establish several properties of the stall patterns and leverage these properties to develop efficient algorithms to search for both intra- and inter-block stall patterns. These algorithms provide exact values or upper bounds on the minimum sizes of the stall patterns. Extensive simulation results confirm the advantages of the proposed SA-BDD-CBF. In particular, it achieves a gain of 0.4 dB, while the associated increase in complexity remains acceptable for practical deployment. Moreover, the searching algorithms determine the exact minimum sizes of the stall patterns for block sizes up to 10 and obtain upper bounds for block sizes larger than 10. These findings advance the practical deployment of higher-order staircase codes in future high-throughput communication systems.

title: Simulative performance assessment of D band planar antenna arrays with circular arrangement
authors: Andreas Maaßen, Youssef Amr, Robert Elschner, Colja Schubert, Ronald Freund
abstract: In this paper, we analyze in simulations the performance of antenna arrays with circular arrangement for D-Band (152 GHz carrier frequency) wireless data transmission in point-to-point (P2P) and point-to-multi-point (P2MP) scenarios, and compare them to traditional rectangular arrangements. Circular antenna arrangements are of high interest for compact packaging of highly integrated D band transceivers, as the small required element spacing at 152 GHz results in reduced space for phase shifter, power amplifier and mixer components. At first, we compare different array configurations assuming beam steering using ideal phase shifters, and evaluate them in terms of directivity, beam width and side lobe attenuation at different beam steering angles from 0 up to 60 degrees and frequencies between 110 and 170 GHz. Second, reduced phase shifter resolutions between 1 bit and 4 bits are taken into account for the different array configurations, and the impact on the directivity and the steering accuracy is analyzed. Finally, the achievable channel capacity is evaluated in a typical use case scenario as a system-level metric. The results show a high performance similarity between the two array arrangements at D-Band frequencies, therefore underlining the applicability of circularly arranged phased array antennas for the implementation of highly integrated transceiver packages.

title: Ion Depletion Defects in RRAMs: Characterization, Modeling and Test
authors: Hanzhi Xun, Moritz Fieback, Sicong Yuan, Hassen Aziza, Mottaqiallah Taouil, Said Hamdioui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: The commercialization of Resistive Random Access Memories (RRAMs) has been invested by many companies as an alternative to conventional memories. This necessitates a thorough comprehension of manufacturing defects to create high-quality, efficient test and diagnosis solutions that will facilitate high-volume production. This work presents the identification and characterization of a novel defect, called Ion Depletion (ID), utilising silicon measurements. In the selected defective device, after excluding the first 100 unstable cycles, 14% of the remaining 836 cycles exhibit ID-defective High Resistance State (HRS) degradation, while the low resistance state remains unaffected. Furthermore, this unique defect is non-linear and inside the device itself; hence, the conventional defect and fault modeling, which relies on linear resistors beside the device, cannot be applied to model the ID defect. To tackle this issue, the Device-Aware (DA) defect modeling approach is used. A calibrated RRAM model representing the defective device is developed using measurement data to precisely characterize how the defect influences the electrical properties of the memory hardware. Subsequently, fault analysis is conducted based on the DA defect model, leading to the introduction of suitable fault models. These models demonstrate that the ID defect can trigger undefined state faults. Finally, dedicated test and diagnosis solutions specifically designed for the ID defect are proposed.

title: Evaluating Facial Expression Recognition Datasets for Deep Learning: a Benchmark Study with Novel Similarity Metrics
authors: F. Xavier Gaya-Morey, Cristina Manresa-Yee, C´elia Martinie, Jose M. Buades-Rubio
abstract: This study investigates the key characteristics and suitability of widely used Facial Expression Recognition (FER) datasets for training deep learning models. In the field of affective computing, FER is essential for interpreting human emotions, yet the performance of FER systems is highly contingent on the quality and diversity of the underlying datasets. To address this issue, we compiled and analyzed 24 FER datasets–including those targeting specific age groups such as children, adults, and older adults–and processed them through a comprehensive normalization pipeline. In addition, we enriched the datasets with automatic annotations for age, gender, and race, enabling a more nuanced evaluation of their demographic properties. To further assess dataset efficacy, we introduce three novel metrics–Local, Global, and Paired Similarity–which quantitatively measure dataset difficulty, generalization capability, and cross-dataset transferability. Benchmark experiments using state-of-the-art neural networks reveal that large-scale, automatically collected datasets (e.g., AffectNet, FER2013) tend to generalize better, despite issues with labeling noise and demographic biases, whereas controlled datasets offer higher annotation quality but limited variability. Our findings provide actionable recommendations for dataset selection and design, advancing the development of more robust, fair, and effective FER systems.

title: Joint Optical Switching Reconfiguration and Spatial-temporal Traffic Optimization for Optical Machine Learning Clusters
authors: Qiaojun Hu, Wei Wang, Xiaoyu Wang, Yajie Li, Yongli Zhao, Jie Zhang
abstract: AI training, e.g., large language models (LLMs), has emerged as one of the most critical workloads in modern data centers. The rapid growth of model sizes and training datasets has driven the need to scale up training clusters. While optical circuit switches (OCSs) offer cost and latency advantages for building large-scale machine learning (ML) clusters, their physical layer limitations, such as non-negligible reconfiguration latency, hinder their ability to handle the dynamic traffic patterns of ML workloads, leading to degraded network throughput and prolonged training time. In this paper, we propose Op tiML to optimize the interaction between ML workloads and reconfigurable optical topologies. OptiML integrates three key functionalities: (1) cluster partition, (2) spatial-temporal traffic optimization, and (3) on-demand OCS reconfiguration, which collectively optimize GPU allocation, traffic scheduling, and topology adaptation to maximize OCS resource utilization. We demonstrate OptiML's efficacy on a 6-GPU testbed, achieving iteration time improvements ranging from 1.12× to 1.19×. Large scale simulations further validate its performance superiority, reducing iteration time by up to 31% compared to state-of-the art ML training schedulers.

title: Pioneering AI Acceleration on European Space Qualified Hardware: Implementation and Characterization of a DNN on NanoXplore FPGA
authors: Tommaso Bocchi, Tommaso Pacini, Pietro Nannipieri, Luca Zulberti, Alp Kilic, Silvia Moranti, Luca Fanucci
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: This article presents a practical design case study describing the first successful implementation of an AI algorithm on a NanoXplore radiation-hardened FPGA, the first fully European solution for space. Recently, the space industry started looking at the growing interest in Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms, driven by advancements in system miniaturisation and growing commercial competition. Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) have emerged as efficient platforms for the hardware acceleration of AI algorithms, together with automated design methodologies. Among these, the FPG-AI framework represents a valuable solution for deploying AI on satellites. The framework exploits a fully handcrafted, human-readable Hardware Description Language (HDL), avoiding third-party Intellectual Property (IP), thereby enhancing the explainability and reliability of the architecture. We re-engineered the FPG-AI flow to ensure compatibility with NanoXplore technology. Then we successfully deployed the LeNet-5 neural network on the NG-ULTRA FPGA, demonstrat ing its capability to support AI accelerators. The implemented prototype achieved an inference time ranging from 0.720 to 0.393 ms, with a clock frequency between 34.4 and 38.2 MHz, and a maximum hardware complexity of 7% of the Functional Elements. The solution's performance was compared with devices of different vendors (AMD Xilinx, Microchip) and the state-of-the-art High Level Synthesis (HLS) frameworks.

title: A porous core-shell fibrous membrane for high-performance soft strain sensors: post-functionalization strategy and multimodal motion monitoring
authors: Weiru Li, Guannan Si, Hanqi Wang, Zhiyong Zhao, Jiaolong You, Yun Hao, Yanjiao Chang, Qian Zhao, Shengzhu Zhou, Mingzhuo Guo, Siyang Wu, Jiale Zhao
abstract: This study presented a high-performance flexible strain sensor fabricated through a strategy combining a porous core-shell fiber structure with post-functionalization. Coaxial electrospinning was employed to fabricate core-shell fibers consisting of a thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) core and a polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) shell. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was incorporated into the shell solution as a pore-forming agent. Washing with water subsequently generated a uniform porous structure within the shell layer. A conductive layer of silver nanoparticles was then firmly constructed on fiber surfaces via sequential immersion and in-situ reduction of silver ions, yielding Ag-TPU/pPVDF porous core-shell fibrous membranes. This method overcame the common limitation in conventional blending approaches where achieving high conductivity often compromised mechanical properties. It successfully maintained the high elasticity and tensile strength of membranes while establishing a stable conductive network. Experimental results indicated that with a PVP content of 10 wt.%, the fibrous membrane exhibited optimal mechanical performance and uniform silver particle distribution. The derived sensor demonstrated high sensitivity with a gauge factor of 45.45, a broad strain detection range, and excellent cycling stability. After encapsulation with a pure PVDF layer, the sensor reliably monitored signals from various human motions such as swallowing, finger bending, knee flexion, and ankle dorsiflexion. This work provides an effective pathway for the synergistic enhancement of mechanical and electrical properties in flexible sensing materials.

title: DepthGeoLoc: Learning Structure-Aware Depth Priors for Robust UAV-Satellite Cross-View Geo-Localization
authors: Jian Sun, Kangdao Liu, Chen-Bin Feng, Jin Wu, Weiping Ding, Chi Man Vong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Recent advances in cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) serve as a critical foundation for UAVs to deliver reliable remote sensing applications in GNSS-denied complex environments. However, current CVGL methods primarily focus on RGB images, neglecting the potential advantages of incorporating additional modalities. In this paper, we propose DepthGeoLoc, a novel multimodal training CVGL framework that integrates depth-aware structural features to address the significant cross-view disparity challenges. Concretely, we propose two key innovations: i) a Depth-aware Feature Fusion (DAFF) module to enable joint geometry-semantics learning of target landmarks, effectively bridging geometric depth cues with high-level semantic representations. ii) Moreover, inspired by the depth distribution patterns of salient regions in real-world urban scenarios captured from UAV and satellite perspectives, we propose two novel components: Regional Depth Distribution Masking (RDDM) and Target Region Structural Alignment (TRSA). These mechanisms synergistically address the cross-view alignment challenges by leveraging depth correlation priors. Benefiting from our proposed coarse-to-fine knowledge distillation paradigm, we achieves a favorable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our multimodal training framework significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art models. The depth-distilled version of our DepthGeoLoc achieves 1.5× higher throughput (vs. SDPL) and a 52.68% reduction in FLOPs (vs. Safe-Net), while maintaining competitive performance.

title: DC-link Voltage Control for a Hybrid Generator-Based Wind Turbine System
authors: Mahnoor Fatima, Omid Beik
abstract: This paper proposes a control scheme for hybrid generator (HG)-based wind turbine (WT) conversion system where DC-link voltage regulation is achieved by the HG while maximum power point tracking (MPPT) is realized by a grid side converter (GSC). Further, in this paper, a synchronization control loop is also proposed that generates the required phase angle for the GSC controller, thus eliminating the phase lock loop (PLL). The HG features a dual-rotor configuration comprising a wound-field (WF) rotor and a permanent-magnet (PM) rotor. For each wind velocity, the PM rotor induces a fixed stator voltage, while the WF rotor induced voltage is controlled by adjusting a DC current into the WF rotor winding such that the total phase voltage of HG is constant between defined limits of wind speeds. The HG-based WT output is rectified by a passive rectifier before connecting to the wind farm’s collector grid with a GSC. This paper provides a detailed analytical modelling of the HG-based WT system, along with the details of proposed DC-link, MPPT and synchronization control. The analytical models are verified by simulation results under variable wind velocity and grid conditions, and in different regions of WT operation.

title: Inferring the Chemotaxis Distortion Function from Cellular Decision Strategies
authors: Fardad Vakilipoor, Johannes Konrad, Maximilian Schäfer
publish: IEEE Transactions on Molecular, Biological, and Multi-Scale Communications
abstract: Cellular intelligence allows cells to process environmental signals and make context-dependent decisions. This is clearly seen in chemotaxis, where cells navigate chemical gradients despite noisy signaling pathways. To investigate how cells deal with uncertainty, an information-theoretic framework based on rate-distortion theory (RDT) is applied. The Blahut-Arimoto algorithm (BAA) computes optimal decision strategies that minimize mutual information while satisfying distortion constraints, balancing sensing accuracy with distortion constraint equivalent to resource cost. The inverse Blahut-Arimoto algorithm (IBAA) is proposed to compute the distortion function, which quantifies the system’s decision-making criteria for realizing a decision strategy to map input signals to outputs. This general framework extends beyond chemotaxis to biological and engineered systems requiring efficient information processing under uncertainty. The proposed IBAA is validated by estimating theoretical distortion functions in a binary decision scenario, which is similar to apoptosis. Additionally, using the local excitation global inhibition (LEGI) model to simulate chemotactic responses, the distortion function from the cell’s perspective is computed. The finding reveals that the decision criterion, i.e., the distortion function, of the cell in chemotaxis processes is fixed regardless of the cell’s state. This observation is analogous to any system view where the decision criterion is fixed while the optimal decision strategy can vary based on the environmental conditions.

title: Weighted Online Koopman Learning for Model Predictive Control of Thrust-Vectored Underwater Vehicles
authors: Yizong Chen, Zhiqiang Miao, Jun Wei, Yaonan Wang
abstract: Underwater vehicles face control challenges including hydrodynamic parameter uncertainties and environmental disturbances. While Koopman-based model predictive control (Koopman-MPC) offers computational efficiency through linear lifted representations, existing methods rely on offline-collected datasets and thus struggle to adapt to the aforementioned challenges. This letter proposes a weighted online Koopman-MPC framework that achieves trajectory tracking through online learning. First, we construct physical information basis functions, explicitly embedding the Coriolis, damping, and restoring force terms into the Koopman lifted space. Second, a weighted incremental Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) scheme fuses offline data with real-time measurements via a tunable parameter. Finally, an incremental MPC scheme is designed to optimize the control increments to satisfy the actuator rate constraints. Experimental validation on a thrust-vectored underwater vehicle demonstrates that the proposed framework effectively adapts to changing dynamics during operation, improving trajectory tracking accuracy over both the offline model and uniform online updates.

title: Data-Efficient Modeling of Hysteresis and Crosstalk for Inverse Kinematics of Soft Manipulators
authors: Korn Borvorntanajanya, Fung Flora Leung, Jialei Shi, Enrico Franco, Philip Wai Yan Chiu, Yeung Yam, Ferdinando Rodriguez y Baena
abstract: Nonlinearities in soft continuum manipulators, aris ing from material hysteresis and intersegmental coupling in multi-segment robots, present significant challenges for accurate open-loop inverse kinematics (IK) control. In particular, morphable pneumatic chambers adjust their shape and stiffness with internal pressure, increasing force output but also introducing nonlinearities that complicate control. This paper introduces a sequence-based machine learning approach that is data-efficient, modeling and compensating for both hysteresis and crosstalk in systems with morphable chambers. Through systematic comparison of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Transformer architectures under data-limited conditions, we demonstrate the effectiveness of sequence-based models in capturing temporal de pendencies. We then propose a Recursive Segment-wise Crosstalk Compensation (RSCC) pipeline that decomposes control of multi segment robots into independent single-segment subproblems, with each constituent model trained using 500 samples. Ap plied to a two-segment morphable-chamber manipulator, RSCC achieves approximately 11% normalized positional error and outperforms a monolithic multi-segment LSTM baseline trained on 2000 samples within the same workspace, highlighting its potential for precise open-loop control in minimally invasive surgical applications.

title: Distributed Topology Reconfiguration for Open Multiagent Systems via Algebraic Connectivity and Kirchhoff Index
authors: Yanping Peng, Jun Cheng, Bin Zhang, Mei Yan, Michael V. Basin, Leszek Rutkowski
abstract: This study proposes a distributed topology reconfiguration protocol for open multiagent systems (MASs) subject to dynamic agent entries and exits. Traditional greedy strategies that maximize algebraic connectivity via the Fiedler vector are limited by their myopic nature and tendency to trap the network in structurally imbalanced local optima. To address these shortcomings, the Kirchhoff index is introduced as a complementary global metric, and a unified network improvement index (UNII) is formulated to balance local connectivity gains with global structural robustness. Equipped with distributed spectral estimation algorithms, the UNII protocol enables fully decentralized decision-making for both agent entry and network repair. Simulation results confirm that the proposed method consistently constructs topologies with higher algebraic connectivity and improved robustness compared to benchmark strategies across diverse dynamic scenarios. Furthermore, application of the proposed protocol to the cooperative control of heterogeneous Euler–Lagrange systems shows that it reduces the steady-state mean squared error by several orders of magnitude and accelerates recovery from structural faults, thereby validating its feasibility as an effective and scalable solution for dynamic open-topology environments.

title: Generalizable Data Routing for UWSNs Based on Fuzzy State Representation in Asynchronous MARL
authors: Yuan Liu, Guangjie Han, Chuan Lin, Shengchao Zhu, Fan Yang
abstract: In recent years, the development of Underwater Wireless Sensor Networks (UWSNs) has advanced the progress of marine environmental monitoring, offshore resource exploration, and underwater military surveillance. The performance of routing algorithms is crucial to ensuring the efficiency of UWSN-based marine data transmission. A critical challenge of routing algorithms in UWSNs is the lack of generalization, where algorithms trained for a specific network topology fail in new or larger-scale deployments. To overcome the challenge, this paper combines Software-Defined Networking (SDN) with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) and proposes a Generalizable Software-Defined UWSN (GSD-UWSN). This framework enables intelligent routing decision-making for nodes and provides a generalizable management platform. Based on GSD-UWSN, this paper models routing decision-making as a Generalizable Markov Decision Process (G-MDP), realizing routing decisions with variable action spaces. To prevent the routing algorithm from overfitting to specific scenarios, this paper proposes an Asynchronous Fuzzy Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (AF MARL) algorithm, which fuzzifies the scenario information and inter-node collaboration information during the training process to enhance the generalization ability of the routing algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that even when trained in a very simple scenario, the proposed method can be well transferred to unseen, more complex scenarios with a larger number of nodes

title: GODEFITI: Enhancing Healthcare Data Forecasting in 6G Networks
authors: Le Sun, Jie Zhang, Yue Zou, Zhiguo Qu, Jinliang Liu, Yanchun Zhang
abstract: The convergence of 6G networks and edge computing is set to revolutionize healthcare through applications such as remote surgery, real-time monitoring, and personalized treatment. These systems generate vast amounts of Irregularly sampled Time Series with Missing values (IMTS) from physiological and behavioral sensors, posing significant challenges for real-time processing and forecasting. Existing methods struggle to balance structural modeling with dynamic evolution, relying on biased discrete approximations and complex self attention that limit deployment in real time healthcare. To address these gaps, we introduce Graph neural Ordinary Differential Equation for Forecasting IMTS (GODEFITi), a Graph neural Ordinary Differential Equation model designed to transform IMTS into sparse bipartite graphs. By unifying structural sparse modeling with continuous time Neural ODEs, GODEFITi bypasses imputation biases and captures correlations between channels across uneven intervals. Within an edge enhanced 6G framework, the model enables minimal latency, interpretable, and resilient forecasting. Experiments demonstrate superior accuracy in critical use cases like remote patient monitoring, bridging the gap between complex physiological sensing and deterministic 6G requirements.
