title: A Scalable Cloud–Edge Digital Twin Framework for Urban Healthcare Analytics
authors: Mahtab Shahin, Riina Palu, Ralf Martin Soe, Mohammad H. Nadimi-Shahraki, Amir.H Gandomi
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Healthcare systems in smart cities increasingly rely on fine-grained sensing and analytics to support continuous and preventive care. At an urban scale, centralized cloud-only approaches are limited by scalability, latency, and privacy concerns. Using Parkinson's disease handwriting analysis as a representative workload, this paper presents a cloud-edge digital twin architecture for scalable healthcare analytics. Data transmission overhead is reduced, and privacy is preserved by analyzing handwriting signals at the edge, while cloud-based clusters perform distributed analytics and synchronize digital twins. Based on the PaHaW handwriting dataset, the proposed system is validated and evaluated using large-scale simulations simulating smart city deployments with thousands of users. The results demonstrate that digital twin healthcare systems in smart cities are feasible due to significant reductions in end-to-end latency and improved scalability when compared with cloud-only processing.

title: 1 kW RF Power System for Possible Endoscopy Sterilization, Surface Treatment and Remote Plasma Source Applications
authors: Tsai-Fu Wu, Xi-Ming Duan, Che-Yu Chen, Han-Chen Hsu
abstract: The paper presents design and implementation of a class-D LCC resonant converter operating at a frequency of 13.56 MHz. This design utilizes enhanced-mode gallium nitride high electron mobility transistors (E-Mode GaN HEMTs) as the power switches, thereby enabling an output power of 1 kW. The present study addresses the resonant circuit employing an LCC series-parallel resonant tank and designs its parameters to achieve zero-voltage-switching (ZVS) soft-switching characteristics in the radio frequency (RF) power system. Through testing and adjustment of parameters, a class-D LCC converter architecture with an output power of 1 kW, efficiency of approximately 95.1%, and the switching frequency of 13.56 MHz is implemented. The designed RF power system is intended for endoscopy sterilization, surface treatment, and remote plasma source applications. Simulated and experimental results are used to verify the analysis and discussion.

title: Towards EnergyGPT: A Large Language Model Specialized for the Energy Sector
authors: Amal Chebbi, Babajide Kolade
abstract: Large language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities across various domains. However, their general-purpose nature often limits their effectiveness in specialized fields such as energy, where deep technical expertise and precise domain knowledge are essential. In this paper, we introduce EnergyGPT, a domain-specialized language model tailored for the energy sector, developed by fine-tuning the LLaMA 3.1-8B model on a high-quality, curated corpus of energy-related texts. We consider two adaptation strategies: a full-parameter Supervised Fine-Tuning variant and a parameter-efficient LoRA-based variant that updates only a small fraction of the model parameters. We present a complete development pipeline, including data collection and curation, model fine-tuning, benchmark design, LLM-judge choice and calibration, evaluation, and deployment. Through this work, we demonstrate that our training strategy enables improvements in domain relevance and performance without the need for large-scale infrastructure. By evaluating the performance of both EnergyGPT variants using domain-specific question-answering benchmarks, our results show that the adapted models consistently outperform the base model in most energy-related language understanding and generation tasks, with the LoRA variant achieving competitive gains at significantly reduced training cost.

title: TEBScore: Trustworthy and Explainable AI May Be Within Reach?
authors: Saad M. Siddiqui, Vijay K. Madisetti
abstract: The evaluation of large language model (LLM) outputs remains a central challenge in their safe and effective deployment across domains that require transparency, reliability, and interpretability. Existing reference-based LLM response evaluation methods require a ground truth for evaluation, limiting their utility in real-world contexts. Reference-free systems often reduce multiple aspects of model response evaluation to a single metric, offering limited insights into the nature, severity, and uncertainty associated with errors identified in LLM responses. To address this gap, we propose TEBScore (Multi-axis Diagnostic Framework for Trustworthy and Explainable LLM Evaluation): a configurable, context-aware, model-agnostic framework for reference-free evaluation of LLM responses across the dimensions of Trustworthiness, Explainability, and Bias. In this work, we adopt a design-oriented perspective in evaluating TEBScore and explicitly articulate the architectural and design choices underlying the framework and its functionality, including dimensional decomposition, span-level error localization, ensemble-based severity estimation, additive aggregation, and uncertainty quantification.We empirically analyze how these design decisions influence diagnostic specificity, sensitivity to error magnitude, confidence interval calibration, as well as computation costs.We further validate TEBScore’s automated human alignment calibration module across subsets of four benchmark datasets and six LLMs. In doing so, we demonstrate TEBScore’s compatibility with diverse natural language tasks and judge models, and the ability of its automated calibration module to identify task-specific configurations that better reflect human quality judgments, with alignment gains observed in 17 of 24 model × task combinations. With its support for tunable weighting, bring-your-own-model extensibility, and an interactive graphical interface for span-level auditing and oversight, we propose TEBScore as a proof-of-concept for transparent, uncertainty-aware, and human-in-the-loop evaluation of LLM responses in real-world applications.

title: Static Output-Feedback LQR Control for Predecessor-Leader Following Vehicle Platooning with Energy Efficiency Analysis via Aerodynamic Drag Reduction
authors: Fernando Viadero-Monasterio, Miguel Meléndez-Useros, Beatriz López Boada, María Jesús López Boada
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Vehicle platooning represents a significant advancement in intelligent transportation systems, with the potential to optimize traffic flow, enhance road safety, and reduce energy consumption. By enabling vehicles to travel in close formation, platooning reduces aerodynamic drag, which translates into significant energy savings. This paper proposes a static output-feedback linear quadratic regulator (LQR) control strategy for vehicle platooning under the predecessor-leader following (PLF) topology. The controller is designed in a decentralized manner using only local output measurements, ensuring scalability and robustness while eliminating the need for full-state information on the platoon status. The controller design is formulated as a linear matrix inequality (LMI) optimization problem that ensures closed-loop stability and robustness in the vehicle platoon under Lyapunov and H∞ criteria. The simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach enhances traffic flow and achieves up to 25% energy savings in a platoon composed of five follower vehicles, in comparison with adaptive cruise control (ACC). It matches the performance of model predictive control (MPC)-based cooperative ACC (CACC) solutions, but with considerably lower computational and communication demands, as the proposed controller is designed offline. The results demonstrate the efficacy of static output feedback control approaches as a practical and energy-aware solution for real-time vehicle platoon control.

title: Dual-Parameter Measurement of Temperature and Refractive Index Based on Polynomial Regression Using a TFBG-FBG Cascaded Oceanic Optical Fiber Sensor
authors: Hao Huang, Guangyao Wang, Jianping Sun, Ting Li, Yingfan Xiong, Ziang Wu, Songkui Chen
abstract: Temperature and refractive index (RI) are two core parameters in ocean hydrological monitoring. However, their inherent cross-sensitivity has long been a bottleneck restricting the measurement accuracy of fiber optic sensors. To address the issue, a cascaded fiber-optic sensor based on a Tilted Fiber Bragg Grating (TFBG) and a Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) is proposed and demonstrated in the paper, leveraging structural optimization and advanced demodulation technology. By utilizing an optimized encapsulation design based on a precision fluid-control stainless steel tube, the sensing unit achieves miniaturization and rigidification while ensuring effective interaction between the grating region and the measured seawater. Leveraging the high-sensitivity response of FBG to temperature and the differential response characteristics of TFBG cladding modes to both temperature and RI, the sensor enables simultaneous measurement of temperature and RI. Experimental results show that within the RI range of 1.3348–1.3770 RIU and temperature range of 5–35°C, the sensor exhibits a linear RI sensitivity of 486.05 nm/RIU and a maximum linear temperature sensitivity of 11.69 pm/°C. Furthermore, to resolve the cross-sensitivity issue between temperature and RI, the study realizes high-precision decoupling and prediction of temperature and RI by constructing a quadratic polynomial regression model of these two parameters. The system provides an innovative solution with high reliability and easy integration for addressing cross-sensitivity problems, and holds broad application prospects in fields such as multi-parameter measurement in ocean environments.

title: Mutual Coupling Reduction in Closely Spaced Antennas using Complementary Omega-Shaped Single Negative Metamaterial
authors: Shriyansh Sharma, Ramesh Patel, M. V. Kartikeyan, Peter Aaen, Abhishek Sahu
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a complementary omega-shaped metamaterial (COSM) for reducing mutual coupling between extremely closely spaced antennas. The proposed methodology is based on creating the band-reject characteristic at the resonant frequency of patch antennas using single negative (SNG) metamaterials. Initially, the COSM with a reduced footprint was designed to operate at the resonating frequency of the antennas. Afterwards, to introduce the band-reject behavior, these COSM structures were incorporated at both sides of the non-radiating edges of the antenna array in the ground plane. The final design achieves at least 20 dB of improvement in isolation at the resonant frequency, even with a gap of merely 3 mm (0.055λ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf>) between the antennas. In addition to high decoupling efficiency, the proposed solution offers a compact and planar design, making it easy to implement in practical applications.

title: A Physics-Inspired Neural Network Framework for Nanoscale Biological Slice Thickness Measurement
authors: Zhuangzhuang Zhao, Hongtu Ma, Xiaohui Dong, Bohao Chen, MingGang Wang, Qiwei Xie, Xi Chen, Hua Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate measurement of biological slice thickness is essential for the precise reconstruction of neuronal connections in microscale connectomics. Conventional metrology often faces performance bottlenecks, struggling to balance measurement accuracy with system complexity and operational efficiency. This study proposes a novel, low-cost, and high-precision method for biological slice thickness measurement. The proposed data-driven approach eliminates the need for high-precision physical modeling and complex instrumentation. By utilizing a simple optical setup based on a standard RGB camera, the proposed method achieves a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.77 nm over a wide measurement range of 30–1000 nm. To address the limited generalization capability of conventional deep learning (Conv. DL) methods under sparse training data conditions, a physics-inspired three-wavelength interferometric neural network (TWI-NN) framework is proposed. The architecture incorporates physics-based structural constraints, including task decoupling and input–output inversion, enabling the network to learn accurate mappings from limited data. Experimental results indicate that, under sparse training data conditions, the prediction accuracy of the TWI-NN is significantly better than that of Conv. DL models. Uncertainty analysis confirms that the total expanded uncertainty (k = 2) is less than 0.947 nm. Furthermore, compared with existing interferometric color measurement methods, the proposed method eliminates the need for repeated calibration and features a simpler implementation. Thickness distribution measurement at an effective resolution of 200 × 200 pixels can be completed in 0.73 s. Overall, the proposed method provides a scalable, cost-effective, and high-precision solution, demonstrating strong potential for high-throughput thickness measurement.

title: Maximum-length sequence encoded Brillouin optical time-domain analysis_supp1-3693071.pdf
authors: Yang Zhang
abstract: pulse coding is a key technique in distributed fiber-optic sensing (DFOS) to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio and spatial resolution. The maximum-length sequence (m-sequence), widely known for its optimal autocorrelation properties among pseudo-random binary sequences, has not yet been applied to DFOS to the best of our knowledge. We propose a Brillouin optical time-domain analysis (BOTDA) based on the m-sequence. The pump wave carries a periodically extended msequence pattern whose period is larger than the fiber round-trip time, which brings about long code length, fully-occupied injection and simple modulation/demodulation at the same time. In the demonstration, a 101.2 km BOTDA with 2047-bit m-sequence is realized, achieving a frequency resolution of 1.28 MHz and a spatial resolution of 4.4 m, in a measurement time of 200 seconds (2.0 s or 1950-time averaging × 100 frequency steps).

title: Integrating Knowledge Graphs and Complex Network Analysis for Effective Team Formation in New Product Development Projects
authors: Qing Yang, Pingye Tian, Ilias Krystallis, Ying Han, Tao Yao
abstract: Forming an effective project team to rapidly develop new products is a significant challenge that involves selecting project members and assigning them to sub-teams. This study proposes an innovative method for team formation by combining digital approaches (i.e., knowledge graphs) with traditional modeling methods (i.e., complex network analysis). First, to search candidate members for a target new product development (NPD) project, we build the NPD project knowledge graph and identify completed projects with high similarity to the target project from a complex network perspective (i.e., structural similarity and attribute similarity). We select the members of these completed projects as the candidate team member set for the target project. Second, to calculate the dependency strength among members, we match members and activities based on expertise consistency and propose a method for calculating dependency strength under two scenarios by building a “activity-organization” network. Finally, to partition the team network into several sub-teams and identify overlapping members, we propose an improved Linkcomm algorithm to cluster the network. An industrial case validates the proposed method. To ensure research rigour, we conduct robustness checks (i.e., parameter test and added-variable test), sensitivity analysis and comparative experiments against the traditional model. This paper contributes to both theory and practice of project team formation.

title: Influence Factors of Space-to-Space ISAR Imaging Induced by Radial Impulsive Thrust
authors: Litian Chen, Yun Zhang, Long Li, Hongbo Li, Haoxuan Yuan, Yalin Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: As space activities rapidly evolve from static deployment to dynamic interaction, the demand for space-based imaging of on-orbit targets has significantly increased, garnering widespread attention. Most current space-based imaging models focus on static scenarios, and systematic studies on space-based inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR) imaging technology—especially for dynamic interaction scenarios—remain scarce. This paper examines the imaging characteristics of a space-based ISAR system observing space targets under radial impulsive thrust. Based on orbital dynamics, a maneuver-induced space-to-space imaging geometry model is constructed, along with the derivation of the corresponding slant-range model and echo signal formulation. The effects of varying radial impulsive thrust magnitudes on key imaging parameters between two satellites are systematically analyzed, and the feasibility and constraints of maneuver-induced ISAR imaging driven by radial impulsive thrust are evaluated. In particular, a co-orbital configuration is adopted to deliberately suppress natural line-of-sight (LOS) variation caused by orbital altitude differences and mutual inclination, such that the evolution of the imaging geometry is dominated by the maneuver itself, enabling a clearer interpretation of maneuver-induced imaging mechanisms. The results reveal that favorable imaging conditions emerge only when the maneuver drives the observation satellite into a close-approach regime, forming time windows with practical imaging value. Meanwhile, although radial impulsive thrust accelerates angular accumulation under high-resolution conditions, it also reshapes the imaging projection plane (IPP) and strengthens motion-induced phase errors, thereby imposing stricter requirements on compensation accuracy. The study provides a useful reference for modeling, evaluation, and strategy design of space-based ISAR in maneuvering scenarios.

title: A Hybrid CI–MTR–PE Path Loss Model for Maritime Evaporation-Duct Propagation
authors: Yanchun Miao, Xiaofeng Zhao, Jue Wang, Ruifeng Gao, Shu Sun
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Accurate modeling of maritime radio propagation remains challenging due to the complex interplay between short-range coherent interference and long-range atmospheric ducting effects. This letter proposes a physics-guided hybrid CI–MTR–PE model that integrates a close-in (CI) path loss formulation, a modified two-ray (MTR) interference core, and a parabolic equation (PE)-based environmental correction. A cosine-based weighting function enables a smooth transition from coherent MTR-dominated propagation to PE-dominated loss with increasing distance. Using 5.8 GHz shore-to-ship measurements, the proposed model achieves an RMSE of 2.68 dB, improving by 1.3 dB over the dual-slope CI–MTR model and by 2.8 dB over the standalone PE prediction, while retaining clear physical interpretability and modest complexity.

title: Investigation of Standing Wave Phenomena in Electromagnetic Radio Frequency Heating of Aluminum Frames for Wafer-Level Thermo-Compression Bonding
authors: Sushant Panhale, Christian Hofmann, Sounak Nandi, Marc Mühmel, Martin Kroll, Till Clausmeyer
abstract: This study investigates the influence of electromagnetic (EM) standing wave effects on the temperature distribution in bond structures during medium- to very-high-frequency (MF–VHF) induction heating for wafer-level thermo-compression bonding (TCB). Aluminum bond frames were patterned on a 6-inch borosilicate glass wafer and subjected to EM excitation at frequencies of 2 MHz, 13.56 MHz, 40 MHz, and 70 MHz. A custom-designed induction coil was developed using an iterative simulation approach to achieve uniform wafer-level heating at 2 MHz. To analyze the standing wave phenomenon, coupled EM-thermal finite element (FE) simulations were performed to study heating and temperature distributions, followed by experimental validation using dedicated MF–VHF induction heating setups. For the experimentally validated setup within this study, standing wave behavior was observed to get pronounced at 13.56 MHz, intensifying at higher frequencies. This leads to non-uniform EM field distributions and irregular heating patterns across the wafer, which poses a significant challenge to achieving homogeneous temperature profiles. The results highlight the need for optimized coil designs for reliable wafer-level packaging, and present concepts for their compensation including coil splitting, multi-frequency excitation, and standing wave compensation via radio frequency (RF) switches.

title: Measurements of a Quasi-Optical Launcher Antenna for the 2 MW W7-X Gyrotron
authors: Moritz S. Misko, Lukas Feuerstein, Stefan Illy, John Jelonnek, Jianbo Jin, Manfred Thumm, Dietmar Wagner
abstract: The experimental verification of a quasi-optical TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">28,12</inf>-mode launcher antenna for the Wendelstein 7-X (W7-X) gyrotron is presented. A step-type mode converter was used to convert the TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">28,10</inf> mode, excited in an existing mode generator to the targeted TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">28,12</inf> mode. The TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">28,12</inf> field pattern was measured with high spatial resolution, achieving a scalar mode purity of 84.6 % and a counter-rotating component below 0.2 %. The measured mode purity fits well to the theoretical design values. The launcher radiation pattern is in excellent agreement with simulation and has a scalar correlation factor of 96.4 %. These results demonstrate the reliability of the step-type mode converter. It provides a reliable and efficient method for mode conversion and preliminary launcher verification with already at KIT existing mode generators.

title: Do Citizens Trust in Public Institutions? An Overview of Trust in States and in Public Institutions
authors: Csilla Ilona Mizser
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Trust has a cohesive force, it is an organizing force, and counteracts disunity in organizations. It can provide a forum for common understanding and interpretation, and is suitable for remedying misunderstandings and misinterpretations. It is a supporting element in the interpretation of reality, which is a challenge of all times, especially with regard to states and public institutions. But why do we trust an institution, or why not? What makes citizens trust the state more, and what objective factors destroy their trust? Today we are experiencing a period burdened by wars, the effects of which are felt in every part of the world. Mental and physical security is at stake - it can be said that the citizens of states are exposed to the highest risk. In the meantime, the OECD's trust research of the past two years can be analyzed with curiosity and contains lessons, with numerous rankings, comparisons, and lessons. It should be emphasized that states can do something to increase the sense of security and are doing so in line with the initiatives formulated following the 2021 OECD trust survey.

title: Do I Have Your Attention Now? Supporting Attention in Dynamic Virtual Environments
authors: Chenkai Zhang, Ruochen Cao, Andrew Cunningham, Allison Jing, James A. Walsh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: There are many problem domains where users have to pay attention to a series of targets. In this paper, we evaluated visual cues to support attention in dynamic situations in virtual environments. Three immersive visualizations (Emphasis, Deemphasis, Emphasis+Deemphasis) have been examined across four different attention types (sustained, alternating, divided, and selective attention) to support the user's attention on targets. Emphasis highlights targets of interest using outlines; Deemphasis removes details with masks, and Emphasis+Deemphasis combines both techniques. An experiment with 34 participants examined the cues through four scenarios that required different attention types in dynamic situations. Results indicated that while Emphasis supported significantly faster reactions in most attention types, the other two visual cues showed superiority across other measurements. Although evaluated in a virtual environment, the findings offer guidance for designing visual cues in other dynamic situations that involve different attention types.

title: Obstacle Straddling and Avoidance Integrated Path Planning and Tracking for Autonomous Driving on Unstructured Roads
authors: Congshuai Guo, Shida Nie, Hui Liu, Bai Li, Fawang Zhang, Yujia Xie, Lijin Han
abstract: Unstructured roads frequently feature segments with a complex distribution of obstacles of varying sizes. Conventional path planning and tracking methods uniformly consider all obstacles as non-straddleable, employing a singular strategy of avoidance. However, this inflexible approach can adversely affect the vehicle's safety and stability. Therefore, the critical challenge lies in the rational management of diverse obstacles and the determination of the most effective corridor to address these complexities. To address the challenge, an obstacle straddling and avoidance integrated path planning and tracking method is proposed for unstructured roads. Specifically, to accurately represent the safe avoidance space of straddleable obstacles, obstacle safe avoidance bounding box (SABB) is designed. Concurrently, a risk field that accounts for vehicle status and road conditions has been developed to quantify the risks of non-straddleable obstacles. In addition, to ascertain the optimal corridor, multimodal evaluation paths are generated based on the SABB and the planned path, and an evaluation function is established, leading to the proposal of an optimal corridor decision-making method. Furthermore, to facilitate efficient navigation on unstructured roads, path planning and tracking are consolidated into a single optimization problem aimed at determining the optimal control inputs. Ultimately, co-simulation and experiment have been conducted to substantiate the effectiveness of the algorithm proposed. The results indicate that in complex scenario, the mean absolute steering angle and yaw rate are reduced by 10.2% and 7.6%, respectively, thereby enhancing vehicle operational safety and stability.

title: Overcoming Resistance to Change for Sustainable ICT Integration in Logistics: From Case Studies to Best Practices
authors: A. Sahraoui, Y. Tliche, A. Kacem
abstract: In the supply chains' context, the integration of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) has the potential to significantly enhance operational performance. However, resistance to change remains a major barrier to successful ICT implementation. This study investigates how resistance manifests and affects the adoption process of an ICT solution within a large logistics organisation in Le Havre, France. A mixed-methods approach combining participant observation, semi-structured interviews and survey analysis is employed to identify behavioural, organisational and procedural obstacles. Findings reveal that inadequate communication, insufficient training and workforce turnover may critically undermine ICT project success. Moreover, a complementary and exploratory statistical analysis based on Partial Least Squares and robustness checks suggests that satisfaction, perceived added value and software familiarisation are the most influential factors affecting adoption success, while ease of use and comfort appear to be not significant. These findings indicate the importance of user engagement and expectations in the performance of ICT tools and provide insights for managing resistance and aligning technology initiatives with organisational capabilities.

title: Bilevel Optimization for Green Hydrogen-to-Ammonia Synthesis System Considering Complex Dynamic Processes and Uncertainties
authors: Yansong Zhang, Yixin Liu, Bo Zhao, Li Guo, Hongkun Lv, Yuxuan Zhang, Chouwei Ni, Kang Zhang, Chengshan Wang
abstract: The wind-powered green hydrogen-to-ammonia system offers a promising pathway for utilizing marine renewable energy; however, its economic viability is constrained by the strongly nonlinear dynamics of ammonia synthesis and the inherent stochasticity of wind power generation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a bilevel scheduling framework. In the upper layer, a reduced-order Trend- and Structure-constrained Physics-Informed Neural Network (TS-PINN) model is developed by embedding trend priors and mechanistic constraints, effectively capturing the nonlinear characteristics of the ammonia synthesis process with significantly reduced computational effort. An improved Multi-Objective Particle Swarm Optimization (MOPSO) algorithm is then employed to co‑optimize operational strategies and key reactor setpoints, including bed temperature and pressure. In the lower layer, a Wasserstein‑distance‑based distributionally robust optimization (DRO) model is established to coordinate hydrogen production and storage operations to hedge against wind power uncertainty. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves an effective balance among modeling accuracy, economic performance, and operational robustness, while accounting for coupled reaction–heat‑transfer dynamics and renewable generation uncertainty.

title: Magnetic Field Uniformity as a Metric for RSFQ Circuit Bias Current Supply Optimization
authors: Minghui Niu, Minghui Zhang, Shucheng Yang, Xi Gao, Bicong Weng, Jie Ren
abstract: A key challenge in scaling up rapid single flux quantum (RSFQ) circuits is managing the magnetic interference caused by their bias current distributions. This paper establishes a simulation-driven framework for optimizing RSFQ circuit bias current supply configurations using magnetic field uniformity as the primary metric. Through Ansys HFSS 3D electromagnetic simulations and subsequent data processing, we demonstrated that magnetic field uniformity—rather than absolute intensity—exhibits a stronger correlation with circuit success probability. We developed a quantitative scoring metric to systematically compare and select bias current configurations. Applying this scoring metric to an 8-bit central processing unit (CPU) series, we found that the fully functional version exhibits 6.0% lower magnetic field non-uniformity in the arithmetic logic unit (ALU) block compared to the non-functional one. The proposed method provides a viable approach to optimizing the layout of large-scale RSFQ circuit, specifically to the design of the bias current supply network.
