title: PowerIntelBench: Benchmarking Evidence-Grounded and Explainable Reasoning in Power Knowledge Management
authors: Tao Yang, Jiajia Han, Cai Zhang, Junjie Huang
abstract: This paper proposes PowerIntelBench, a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for assessing large language models (LLMs) in power standard knowledge management. The benchmark systematically covers essential tasks in standard knowledge organization and retrieval, including novelty detection, cross-standard comparison, source attribution, temporal evolution tracing, fuzzy term interpretation, content-based QA, hallucination-resistant judgment, standard choice questions, and image-based QA. It achieves traceable and auditable evaluation through evidence-binding and chain-of-thought assessment. Unlike general benchmarks that focus on language understanding, PowerIntelBench emphasizes the structured, multi-version, and multimodal nature of power standards, addressing real-world needs in standard compliance, version control, and engineering decision support. Experimental results show that current LLMs perform adequately in straightforward standard QA tasks but exhibit significant limitations in cross-version reasoning, ambiguity resolution, multimodal understanding, and evidence traceability. Domain-specific fine-tuning further demonstrates notable improvements in both response quality and reasoning consistency. PowerIntelBench provides a tailored benchmark for power standard knowledge management and lays a foundation for future model optimization and industrial deployment.

title: Rain-Dominated Free-Space Optical Channels with Statistical Characterization and Experimental Validation
authors: Weirao Gao, Keyan Dong, Lun Jiang, Yansong Song, Tong Wang, Jiayao Zhou
abstract: Free-space optical (FSO) communications—owing to their high data rates, license-free spectrum usage, and enhanced security—hold great promise for metropolitan and long-distance links. However, atmospheric turbulence and rain/fog-induced absorption and scattering in urban environments severely degrade link reliability. Building on our previous composite-channel modeling framework, this work reconstructs a composite atmospheric attenuation model for rainy conditions that explicitly incorporates rain-induced attenuation and visibility, and analyzes their effects on turbulence statistics and bit error rate (BER). Both theoretical analysis and experimental measurements show that, although the mean received power is substantially reduced under rain, the statistical distribution of received irradiance remains well described by a Gamma–Gamma model. Measured BER curves indicate pronounced degradation, with BER worsening from approximately 1E-7 in light rain to around 1E-2 in heavy rain. Validation using a 1-km urban rainy-day field link demonstrates the applicability and limits of the proposed statistical model under rain-dominated conditions, providing theoretical and experimental guidance for modeling and performance assessment of FSO systems in adverse weather.

title: A BCI-controlled UAV system with peripheral high-frequency SSVEP
authors: Zhaohui Li, Zexin Pang, Pengyu Huang, Hongyan Cui, Xiaogang Chen
abstract: With the advancement if Internet of Things (IoT) and brain-computer interface (BCI) technologies, BCI-controlled unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have demonstrated significant potential to catalyze innovation across diverse domains. However, the existing BCI-controlled UAV systems are constrained by inherent limitations, including restricted degrees of freedom (DOF) in control, excessive architectural complexity, and non-naturalistic human-machine interaction. This study proposed BCI-controlled UAV system with peripheral high-frequency steady-state visual evoked potential (SSVEP). The visual flicker stimulus regions with 31-33.75 Hz alongside the targets were arranged in an alternating configuration along the edges of the stimulus interface. The modified TDCA algorithm was proposed to decode the different patterns among targets and stimulus regions. System optimization, validation of feasibility and practical utility were conducted through offline, online validation and online control experiments. In the online validation experiment, the accuracy and information transfer rates (ITR) across eleven subjects were 90.40%  7.18% and 55.09  9.79 bits/min, respectively. In the online control experiment, real-time footage captured from the UAV was displayed at the center of the interface as visual feedback, and all subjects were able to complete the assigned flight navigation tasks successfully. In this study, the proposed BCI-controlled UAV system supported naturalistic, user-friendly interaction by employing peripheral high-frequency stimuli, while maintaining comparable performance to existing systems.

title: Advancing GaN HEMT Trap Modeling Using MHz Load-line Measurement Techniques
authors: Cristina Andrei, Petros Beleniotis, Ulrich L. Rohde, Matthias Rudolph
abstract: Understanding trapping effects in Gallium Nitride (GaN) high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs) is fundamental for boosting their performance in RF and microwave applications. Traditional methods often depend on synchronized pulsed I-V measurements, which can be complicated, costly, and require extensive equipment. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the simplified measurement system based on MHz load-line techniques. By incorporating frequency offset harmonic generation, this system effectively covers the I-V plane, allowing for precise extraction of trap model parameters and/or evaluation of the device regarding current collapse. Validation demonstrates that this approach effectively mimics real-world transistor operations, improving efficiency, reducing costs, and simplifying data collection. Although not novel, its application remains significant in advancing semiconductor device characterization, making it more accessible and practical across different research settings.

title: Thermal Imaging-based Perception for Automotive Edge AI Applications using a Lightweight Custom YOLO Model
authors: ModhaSreya Lade, Bethi Pardhasaradhi, Linga Reddy Cenkeramaddi
abstract: Thermal sensing plays an important role in automotive perception. Yet, most existing thermal datasets and thermal perception machine learning (ML) models are optimized for high-performance computing platforms. This work proposes a lightweight dual-attention fusion YOLO model designed for thermal imaging-based perception to run inference on edge devices. The proposed YOLO integrates efficient channel attention (ECA) and coordinate attention (CA) to enhance feature selectivity while maintaining compactness. The proposed model is evaluated on several thermal datasets, including ITDAV-25, OSU thermal, OSU color, FLIR ADAS, and C3I automotive thermal, which primarily target pedestrians and vehicles. To support practical deployment, a cloud-edge co-design strategy is introduced. All high-compute operations, such as data handling and training, are performed in the cloud, while the optimized model runs locally on the sensing device. This architecture reduces communication overhead, improves latency, and transforms thermal cameras into intelligent sensing nodes capable of autonomous operation. The trained model is benchmarked on several edge devices like Raspberry Pi 5, Rock Pi 4C+, Raspberry Pi Zero, and Jetson AGX Xavier to check the feasibility of real-time inference. The model is only 13.5MB in size, 6.59M parameters, and 15.3GFLOPS. Results show that the proposed model delivers improved detection accuracy over baseline YOLO variants like v5, v8, v9, v10, and v12 while preserving efficiency suitable for deployment in next-generation automotive sensor systems.

title: Balancing Innovation and Integrity: Employee Perceptions of AI in the Workplace: A TPB–TOE Integrated Analysis
authors: Luna Mohanad Al-Akhras, Andrea Tick
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: This study investigates the organizational impact of artificial intelligence (AI) across ethical, psychological, and structural implications through a Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) and the Technology-Organization-Environment (TOE) framework analysis. The research applies quantitative analysis to assess attitudes and behavior towards AI. Results reveal that while AI enhances efficiency and productivity, it also generates concerns regarding job security, transparency, and ethics. Organizational preparedness and effective communication were key factors shaping employee acceptance. The study recommends transparent AI strategies, continuous reskilling, and adherence to ethical principles to promote responsible, human-centered AI implementation.

title: Autonomous Vehicle Surveying Radio Map Based Pathfinding and Backtracking Navigation with Smartphone
authors: Xiaodong Gong, Jingbin Liu, Jianya Gong, Wei Zhang
abstract: The growing demand for real-time location services requires a solution to seamlessly bridge the navigation gap between autonomous vehicles (AVs) and indoor destinations, particularly for guiding users back to their parking spot. Current solutions struggle to provide accurate, continuous localization after the user exits the AV. To solve this problem, this paper proposes an autonomous vehicle surveying radio map based pathfinding and backtracking navigation solution that utilizes the autonomous vehicle to construct a high-quality Bluetooth fingerprint radio map efficiently during the driving process. Once the user enters the building, a pathfinding positioning algorithm records the route, and a novel backtracking navigation algorithm uses the user's smartphone to guide them back to the parking location. The experimental results demonstrate that using the AV for surveying increases data collection efficiency by 15 times compared to the total human-hours required of manual methods. Furthermore, the system significantly improves robustness against device heterogeneity, reducing the positioning error difference from 5.481 m to 0.827 m. This integrated approach enhances overall smartphone indoor positioning accuracy, decreasing the error from 2.425 m to 1.772 m.

title: AKgram: A Robust Adaptive Framework for Bearing Fault Diagnosis Under Speed-Varying Condition
authors: Chuntao Zhang, Sen Hu, Qiuyang Zhou, Yonghao Miao
abstract: Rolling bearing fault diagnosis under speed-varying condition (SVC) presents a significant challenge due to the non-stationary nature of vibration signals. The narrowband filtering demodulation (NFD) methods, while effective under constant speeds, faces severe limitations under such non-stationary operations. Specifically, their traditional feature indicators fail to characterize the non-stationary fault impulses, leading to significant performance degradation. Furthermore, the inherent fixed frequency band segmentation strategy of these methods can also impair their diagnostic accuracy. To address these limitations, this paper proposes AKgram, a novel adaptive diagnostic method designed specifically for the SVC. First, the fluctuation trend spectrum (FTS) is derived from the signal spectrum to realize adaptive frequency band segmentation, constructing a multi-level tower-shaped boundary distribution map while preserving full spectral component integrity and overcoming fixed segmentation limitations. Then, average kurtosis (AK) is introduced as a robust indicator to quantify fault-induced impulses and extract non-stationary fault features under SVC. Finally, AK values of segmented sub-bands are visualized in the tower-shaped map to form the AKgram, with the sub-band of maximum AK identified as the optimal demodulation band for direct fault feature extraction. The effectiveness and superiority of AKgram are validated via simulation and experimental data from a variable-speed bearing test bench, outperforming the classical Kurtogram method in optimal band identification and fault feature extraction.

title: Novel Approaches to Optimize User Experience and Network Resource Utilization for Delivering SVC Video Streaming in SDN-Supported Networks
authors: Dung H. P. Nguyen, Shu-Hui Wu, Wan-Yuan Liang, Shao-I Chu, Bing-Hong Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: With the growing necessity for remote work and the emergence of the new generation of mobile networks, videoconferencing using the WebRTC protocol is one of the most widely utilized applications today. Nevertheless, due to the diversity of video playback devices, the network environment is becoming increasingly heterogeneous. In addition, current network instruments do not support multicast delivery. These pose substantial challenges in encoding and distributing video over limited resource networks, especially for videoconferencing, where one needs to deliver video content from one presenter to a group of receivers. While scalable video coding (SVC) is a feasible solution to this issue in terms of encoding, the centralized management fashion and programmable switches used in software-defined networking (SDN) offer many options for distributing video streaming. Hence, delivering SVC video over SDN is a helpful remedy for WebRTC services. In this paper, we first investigate the problem of SVC video delivery for WebRTC services over SDN (SVDWS), in which we aim to enhance the total quality of experience (QoE) of receivers with the minimum network resources. We then propose novel techniques to address the SVDWS problem. Eventually, we conducted simulations to evaluate the performance of the proposed methods. Through simulations, our techniques show better performance compared with the current representative method.

title: An adaptive extraction method for fault features of in-wheel motor bearing using MSDCPO-based RRSVMD
authors: Hongtao Xue, Liang Zhang, Xuan Wang, Yanlong Xu, Xupeng Ouyang, Xiaoyi Bao, Zongkang Jiang, Huiyu Yue, Peng Chen
abstract: To address the challenge that weak faults in in-wheel motor bearings are often obscured by strong noise, making fault features difficult to extract accurately, this paper proposes a robust residual driven successive variational mode decomposition (RRSVMD) optimized by a multi-strategy dynamic crested porcupine optimizer (MSDCPO). The core contributions are threefold: (1) A RRSVMD method is introduced, incorporating a residual bandwidth inequality constraint mechanism. Unlike standard SVMD, this framework strictly bounds the spectral spread of each mode based on residual energy, effectively eliminating mode mixing and over-decomposition; (2) An impulsiveness-sparsity factor (ISF) is developed to capture both sparsity and impulsiveness of IMFs, serving for adaptive penalty factor initialization and sensitive mode selection; and (3) An MSDCPO-based RRSVMD framework is established, where a modified MSDCPO generates diverse candidate penalty factors through four complementary search mechanisms to achieve efficient parameter tuning. Simulation and experimental studies demonstrate that the proposed method achieves reliable extraction of weak fault features under typical bearing faults and external disturbances. Comparative studies confirm its superior performance and robustness. The proposed approach thus provides an effective and efficient tool for the fault diagnosis of in-wheel motor bearings under strong noise operating conditions.

title: Neurodynamics-Aided Zhang Update (ZU) Method for Static Rectangular Linear Systems
authors: Yunong Zhang, Jiaming Zhou
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: The static rectangular linear systems (SRLS) problems arise in many applications such as data fitting, inverse problems, and intelligent robotics, where accurate and efficient methods are crucial. Conventional direct methods, including normal equation methods based on Cholesky factorization and QR factorization based methods, are highly accurate but require expensive matrix factorizations, thereby incurring high computational cost for large-scale problems solving. In contrast, iterative methods such as the least-squares QR (LSQR) and the least-squares minimal residual (LSMR) methods avoid explicit factorizations, leading to lower memory usage and computational cost per iteration, thereby being more suitable for largescale problems solving. However, their convergence speed and reliability deteriorate markedly when the coefficient matrix is severely ill-conditioned. To address those drawbacks, we combine the Zhang neurodynamics (ZN) framework with quasi-Newton iteration, and propose a new Zhang update (ZU) method for the SRLS problems solving, as a new thing. Numerical results illustrate that, for solving highly ill-conditioned SRLS problems, the ZU method achieves faster convergence and exhibits stronger convergence capability than the LSQR and LSMR methods.

title: A Natural-Scene Edge Method for MTF Estimation in High-Resolution SAR Images
authors: Xin Lin, Yang Lu, Jing Liu, Yingchi He, Guozhong Chen, Kaizhi Wang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) is an active microwave sensor capable of high-resolution imaging and plays an important role in terrain observation and disaster monitoring. Therefore, the quality assessment of SAR images is of great importance. The Modulation Transfer Function (MTF) is widely used to characterize spatial resolution and detail preservation, and has been introduced into SAR image quality assessment. However, existing MTF-based methods often rely on point targets or dedicated test fields, making direct measurement from a single SAR image difficult. To address this issue, this paper proposes an MTF measurement method for SAR images based on natural edge structures. Our method establishes a set of edge-selection criteria specifically designed for SAR image and achieves automatically standardized extraction of high-quality test edges from natural scenes. By computing the MTF from these natural edges, the spatial frequency response and high-frequency detail preservation capability of the system can be quantitatively evaluated, enabling on-orbit image quality assessment without artificial targets. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach effectively distinguishes the resolution performance of different systems and provides an automated solution for quantitative SAR image quality evaluation.

title: UniGCL: Unifying Graph Convolution and Contrastive Learning for Recommendation
authors: Changlong Zheng, Yonghui Yang, Wei Shi, Jinglong Li, Chiyu Han
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) are widely adopted backbones for collaborative filtering due to their simplicity and performance. To address data sparsity, Graph Contrastive Learning (GCL) has been integrated, with its effectiveness often attributed to promoting node representations' uniformity. However, we uncover an under-explored conflict: the messagepassing mechanism of GCNs inherently amplifies popular nodes, while the uniformity objective of GCL tends to dilute their discriminative power. This induces a “seesaw effect”: enhancing long-tail recommendations often comes at the expense of popular item performance, and an excessive pursuit of uniformity may blur essential semantic distinctions. To ameliorate this conflict, we propose UniGCL, a unified framework that harmonizes GCN and GCL by deliberately rebalancing the influence of popular nodes. First, we enhance the GCN backbone with inverse logarithmbased normalization, which adaptively strengthens the influence of popular nodes in representation propagation. Second, we propose a triple contrastive learning module grounded in inverse propensity scoring (IPS) to evaluate alignment and uniformity across users, items, and interaction pairs, thereby mitigating the over-emphasis on popular nodes during training. Consequently, UniGCL achieves a dual adjustment: it leverages the inherent aggregation of popular nodes during message passing, while counteracting their dominance in the contrastive phase. This strategy enables the model to excel in long-tail recommendations without compromising the discriminability of popular items, effectively mitigating the seesaw effect. The effectiveness of the proposed UniGCL framework is validated through extensive experiments on three public benchmarks. Specifically, UniGCL surpasses the strong baseline VGCL in terms of NDCG@20 by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9.2 \%, 2.7 \%$</tex>, and 3.1% on the Douban-Book, Yelp2018, and Amazon-Book datasets, respectively. Furthermore, we present a comprehensive group-wise evaluation to validate its success in mitigating the seesaw effect. Our code can be found at: https://github.com/ChanglongZheng/UniGCL.

title: Test-Time Learning Framework for Distribution Shifts in Sequential Recommendation
authors: Chiyu Han, Mi Tian, Changlong Zheng
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Sequential recommendation aims to predict the next item that users are likely to interact with based on their historical behaviors. In practical scenarios, user preferences and item popularity often evolve over time, resulting in distribution shift. Although existing generalization models have achieved certain success, their adaptability often remains limited under highly dynamic conditions. Current generalization methods typically leverage distributionally robust optimization (DRO) to optimize “worst-case” losses, manually designed data augmentation to enrich the training distribution, or invariant learning to capture stable user representations. Despite their effectiveness, DRObased models may have a restricted modeling scope, while manual data augmentation could introduce noise, potentially distorting the captured user preferences. Moreover, invariant learning requires the construction of diverse “environments,” which is challenging. Furthermore, static generalization capabilities may struggle to adapt to an unseen and evolving testing distribution. Test Time Training (TTT) fine-tune models during the inference phase to adapt to varying test distributions. However, existing TTT-based recommenders are often model-specific and frequently rely on historical interactions that may be inaccessible. In this paper, we propose a Test-Time Learning Framework for Distribution Shifts in Sequential Recommendation (TTLS), which can be seamlessly integrated into various pre-trained models without requiring historical data. The core of TTLS lies in enhancing model confidence during fine-tuning while mitigating the risk of forgetting historical knowledge. Specifically, we utilize cross-entropy and entropy minimization losses to update parameters during inference, followed by a linear weight interpolation between the intermediate and original models. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority and robustness of our method. The code is available at https://github.com/amiba11/TTLS.

title:
A 0.062 mm
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    18–24 GHz Mixer-First Receiver for 6G FR3 Dense xMIMO Arrays
authors: Sourya Prakash Rout, Padmanava Sen
abstract: This paper presents an 18–24 GHz mixer-first receiver in 22 nm Fully Depleted Silicon-On-Insulator (FDSOI) Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (CMOS) optimized for thermal and area constraints in dense Sixth Generation (6G) Frequency Range 3 (FR3) Extreme Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (xMIMO) arrays. The design achieves a measured conversion gain of 18.8 dB (1.5 dB variation), a simulated noise figure of 7.4–9.1 dB, and an Input 1 dB Compression Point (IP1dB) of −11 dBm to −8 dBm across the 18–24 GHz band, all within a 0.062 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> core area - a 90% reduction compared to state-of-the-art. By employing high-drive quadrature generation with centralized Local Oscillator (LO) distribution, the receiver consumes only 51 mW core power. The design demonstrates that balancing per-element compactness, linearity, and thermal efficiency through system-level architecture is essential for scalable dense xMIMO integration.

title: Transient Induced Loss of Protection During Fault
authors: John Babu, Craig Marien, Paul Melzen, Matt Leclair
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Eversource Energy experienced an event where System 1 and System 2 protection failed to operate due to a significant DC system surge due to a high energy lightning strike. Analysis of the lightning-related transient event indicated electrical relays and other Protection & Control (P&C) equipment were subjected to surge conditions that exceeded the industry standard design for surge withstand ratings. Root-cause analysis revealed severe protection system equipment failures, unintended operation and/or misoperation at a critical 345kV substation facility.System 1 and System 2 protection groups shut down because of the lightning-strike-induced surge at one terminal of a 345 kV transmission line. Neither System 1 nor System 2 local relay protection cleared the SLG fault resulting from the lightning strike. The fault continued for over 1.5 seconds until protection at the remote terminals tripped as designed via time-delayed elements.Eversource conducted extensive on site testing and collaborated with relay vendors to conduct forensic analysis. Both manufacturers were asked to conduct similar factory acceptance tests with an emphasis placed on the surge withstand specifications of each relay. Additionally, Eversource partnered with an industry expert to perform detailed grounding system modeling and transient analysis. The analysis simulated the lightning event to determine the resulting Ground Potential Rise/Ground Potential Difference (GPR/GPD) levels across the substation yard and to identify the mechanism upon which the surge was transferred to the relays. It became evident to Eversource that protective relay systems, when exposed to surges beyond their specified criteria, may not operate as intended or fail.This paper documents the event analysis and design practice modifications made by Eversource to limit coupling of transients from the ground system to secondary AC and DC control cables via the implementation of improved cable shielding and shield grounding methods.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning-Based Joint Phase Shift and Reflection Coefficient Optimization for RIS-Assisted Broadcast-Passive IoT Symbiotic Systems
authors: Qiang Liu, Sen Xu, Hua Xu
abstract: Broadcast-passive Internet of Things (IoT) symbiotic systems exploit ambient broadcast signals to simultaneously power passive devices and support their information transmission through backscatter communication. However, these systems still suffer from severe double-hop fading, limited coverage, and complex interference coordination between broadcast and passive IoT communications. This paper proposes a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted system architecture and formulates a joint optimization problem of RIS phase shifts and passive device reflection coefficient to maximize passive IoT throughput, subject to broadcast receiver quality-of-service (QoS) and passive device energy harvesting constraints. Given the non-convex and coupled nature of the formulated problem, we develop a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework based on proximal policy optimization (PPO), incorporating a power-domain state space representation and a piecewise reward function to balance throughput maximization with multi-constraint satisfaction. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves significant throughput gains with increasing RIS elements, while the PPO agent learns to push the operating point toward active constraint boundaries, achieving an effective balance among throughput maximization, broadcast QoS protection, and energy sustainability.

title: Photon number resolving Mo/AuPd transition edge sensors for visible up to NIR wavelength
authors: Matthias Schmelz, Vitaly Shvab, Katja Peiselt, Jürgen Kunert, Gabriel Zieger, Vyacheslav Zakosarenko, Gregor Oelsner, Ronny Stolz
abstract: We present results of the development of transition-edge sensors (TES) based on Mo/AuPd thermistors aiming for photon number resolution in the visible up to NIR wavelength. TES are deposited on an oxidized solid Silicon substrate and make use of electron-phonon decoupling at mK-temperatures generating a weak link for thermal relaxation. The Mo/AuPd bilayers have a tuned critical temperature of approximately 180 mK and are being read out by making use of a series SQUID array, both implemented in a setup in an adiabatic demagnetization refrigerator. Optical pulses, generated via gated laser sources at room temperature, are fed via an optical single mode fiber to the TES. Their absorption is measured as a TES pulse response with a SQUID array. By means of pulse amplitude histograms (PAH), single photon resolution in the visible to NIR has been achieved. We've proven TES pulse response originating from single photon absorption in the TES via fitting the PAH with a Poissonian distribution. For 405 nm laser wavelength, we've achieved an energy resolution of about 0.7 eV, determined via analysis of the raw measurement data using PAH.

title: IEEE Standard for a Functional Architecture of Distributed Energy Efficient Big Data Processing
authors: N/A
abstract: An energy-efficient functional architecture for big data transmission and processing is specified in this standard. The architecture considers distributed processing nodes in addition to centralized data centers to process big data chunks. Distributed processing nodes provides processing closer to big data sources, which reduces the networking power consumption. The performance of the energy-efficient functional architecture is compared to classical centralized processing of big data under different use cases representing two of the main characteristics of big data: volume and variety.

title: Asynchronous Distributed Learning based on Gradient Coding
authors: Chengxi Li, Ming Xiao, Mikael Skoglund
abstract: In this paper, we study the problem of distributed learning (DL), where different devices take varying amounts of response time to complete local computations during training. To deal with this problem, existing gradient coding (GC) techniques are designed in a synchronous setting, where the server waits for a fixed number of the fastest devices to transmit the coded gradients in each iteration to fully recover the global gradient. However, there are significant inefficiencies in these GC techniques, since the computations performed by the slow devices are wasted and their information is not required by the server to update the global model. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel asynchronous DL method based on GC (AGC). In AGC, the training data subsets are replicated and allocated to the devices redundantly, and the devices encode local gradients of its local subsets and send the coded gradients to the server. The server updates the global model in an asynchronous manner based on the coded gradients received from a certain number of devices, which may be computed from the global models back in history as well as the most recent one. In this way, the computations of the slow devices can also be exploited, helping to increase training efficiency and reduce training time. We provide a convergence analysis for AGC in asynchronous settings with frequent global synchronization, and show that it achieves an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(1/ √ T)$</tex-math></inline-formula> convergence rate. Finally, numerical results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method compared to the baseline methods, where AGC achieves better learning performance after the same training time.

title: Thickness Monitoring of Ferromagnetic Pipes with Multiple Insulation Media Based on Pulse Eddy Current Orthogonal Probes and Segmented Late-slope Extraction Algorithm
authors: Xinyi Ji, Haoyang Mi, Meng Zhang, Kuiling Shen, Dongzhi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The non-destructive, stable, and accurate monitoring for the thickness of ferromagnetic pipes is of paramount importance in the petrochemical field due to its high-temperature, high-pressure, and corrosive environments. In this study, we proposed the pulse eddy current orthogonal probes and segmented late-slope extraction algorithm to achieve the thickness monitoring of pipes with multiple insulation media. The induced voltage for the pulse eddy current probes was simulated by COMSOL, and the optimal structure and parameters was quantified by the sensitivity. Based on the experiments and simulations, the orthogonal structure is superior to the coaxial structure for the pulse eddy current probes. A pulse eddy current testing (PECT) system and a late-slope optimization extraction algorithm was designed for the test of pipe thickness. The results show that the PECT system achieved a testing error of less than 3% for specimens with a thickness of 1.5-12 mm at 50 mm lift-off, and still maintain a testing error of less than 5% under the interference of multiple insulation media. This verifies that the proposed system ensures high-precision and stable monitoring, and demonstrated excellent anti-interference ability for engineering applications.

title: KalmanKAN: A Numerically Stable Neural Network Aided State Estimation Method
authors: Yucheng Li, Shiyuan Wang, Yunfei Zheng, Xiao Liu, Lei Li, Wei Yu, Xinwei Li
abstract: Existing deep neural networks (DNN)-aided Kalman filters typically learn the Kalman gain (KG) or parameterize covariances to compensate for missing model information. However, model mismatch can lead to ill-conditioned or excessively aggressive updates, which may destabilize the estimation process. To address these challenges, we propose a numerically stable state estimation method called KalmanKAN (KKAN). First, we introduce a spline-based architecture that enhances interpretability and regularization, promoting stable parameter updates. Then, KKAN imposes posterior constraints to mitigate instability in the learned KG. Additionally, to leverage the high flexibility offered by DNN-aided filters, a Mahalanobis-distance (MD) based approach is introduced for setting the constraint strength hyperparameter. This strategy tightly couples the posterior constraint with the learned filter, thereby enhancing the stability and performance. Finally, extensive experiments on a mismatched sinusoidal model, lorenz model, and the real-world Michigan NCLT dataset demonstrate stable performance even under significant nonlinearity and model mismatch.

title: An End-to-End Neural Codec for Bone-Conducted Microphone Speech
authors: Xiaolong Hu, Zhe Chen, Fuliang Yin
abstract: Simultaneously performing speech coding and quality enhancement within a single network remains a challenging task in bone-conducted microphone (BCM) speech processing. To address this issue, an end-to-end neural codec for BCM speech is proposed in this paper. Specifically, Dual Residual Vector Quantization (DRVQ) and Residual Fusion Unit (RFU) modules are designed based on feature fusion techniques to alleviate precision loss during quantization. To capture the nonlinear relationships between low-frequency and high-frequency components of BCM speech, an attention mechanism named TFiLM-MHAM, which combines Temporal Feature-Wise Linear Modulation and Multi-Head Attention Mechanism, is employed. Additionally, the Adaptive Feature Scaling Module (AFSM) is introduced to enhance the model’s representational capacity by dynamically adjusting feature importance. To further improve the reconstruction of high-frequency components, the Dual Channel-Spatial Attentive Fusion (DCSAF) mechanism is applied. The proposed method enables efficient BCM speech coding and quality improvement within a unified architecture, supporting different bitrates without modifying the network or requiring retraining. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Numerical Investigation of Electromagnetic and Thermal Behavior of Multi-Bundled D-shaped Coils
authors: Takanobu Mato, So Noguchi
abstract: This study investigates the electromagnetic and thermal behaviors of D-shaped multi-bundled (MB) no-insulation (NI) REBCO pancake coils, which are one of the promising candidates for high-field magnets in fusion reactors. Numerical simulations of sudden discharge and charging tests are performed using the partial element equivalent circuit (PEEC) method coupled with thermal finite element method (FEM). Three cases with different numbers of bundled tapes are simulated while maintaining the same time constant. The simulation results show that Joule heat is mainly generated between the turns, whereas the temperature distribution remains nearly uniform due to rapid heat diffusion. The temperature evolution is almost identical among all cases, although the onset time of the normal-state transition differs slightly. The investigation results suggest that MB-NI REBCO coils with the same time constant exhibit comparable thermal stability during sudden discharge events and coil charging.

title: The General Data Protection Regulation Goes to School: Proportional Versus Top-Down Regulation in Primary and Secondary Institutions
authors: Francesco Ciclosi, Emma Zambarda, Giovanna Varni, Fabio Massacci
abstract: The General Data Protection Regulation was designed to rein in corporate giants, yet affects big and small organizations alike. We summarize an ethnographic study conducted in Italian schools and discuss the tradeoff between top-down compliance and risk-based operational resilience.

title: AISCT-SAM: Customized SAM-Med2D with 3D Context Awareness and Self-Prompt Generation for Fully Automatic Acute Ischemic Stroke Lesion Segmentation on Non-Contrast CT Scans
authors: Hulin Kuang, Xianzhen Tan, Shunuo Li, Shichao Kan, Jin Liu, Jiarui Sun, Jingyang Zhang, Chunfeng Yang, Wu Qiu, Jiulou Zhang, Yang Chen, Jianxin Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Lesion segmentation of acute ischemic stroke (AIS) patients on Non-Contrast CT (NCCT) scans plays a crucial role in rapid diagnosis and treatment planning. The direct application of promising semi-automatic SAM-Med2D to AIS lesion segmentation on NCCT scans: 1) does not achieve good performance; 2) cannot incorporate 3D volumetric context; and 3) requires time-consuming prompts and lacks effective domain-specific semantic prompts. Therefore, we propose a novel SAM-Med2D-based model (AISCT-SAM) with 3D context awareness and self-prompt generation for automatic AIS lesion segmentation. First, to better handle the high heterogeneity of AIS lesions between different subjects, we propose the adapter and low-rank adaptation with gate layer which updates parameters on both aspects of the token-mixer and channel-mixer for more comprehensive and flexible fine-tuning. Second, we introduce a plug-and-play depth adapter to extract richer 3D contextual information, while enhancing the local priors that the Transformer architecture lacks. Then, to enable fully automatic segmentation, we propose a self-prompt generator that generates multi-level semantic prompts by leveraging bilateral hemisphere differences. Finally, we devise a multi-level semantic prompt-guided mask decoder that fully takes into account the characteristics of NCCT images. AISCT-SAM was evaluated on the public AISD dataset, a private AIS dataset, and an external AIS dataset. Experimental results show that AISCT-SAM achieves Dice scores of 63.32%, 48.75%, and 43.81% on the three datasets, respectively, surpassing 20 state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, volumetric analysis and external validation suggest that AISCT-SAM could provide valuable reference information for AIS diagnosis and treatment decisions. Furthermore, AISCT-SAM also performs well on a public MRI dataset, indicating its potential generalization capability for other modalities.

title: Secure Distributed State Estimation in Nonlinear Cyber–Physical Systems Using Sensor Networks
authors: Hamed Kazemi, Khashayar Khorasani
abstract: This article proposes a resilient distributed state estimation framework for nonlinear cyber–physical systems (CPS) using sensor networks exposed to cyberattacks. The method integrates the distributed hybrid information fusion (DHIF) algorithm with a graph-based reconfiguration strategy that enables the system to detect and isolate false data injection (FDI) attacks on communication links and reconfigure the network to restore connectivity and maintain estimation performance. Each sensor node features a local estimator and an anomaly detector, allowing for fully decentralized adaptation to adversarial conditions. Upon detecting attack-induced disruptions, the system autonomously reconfigures its communication graph to maintain information flow and ensure continued system-wide collaboration. Theoretical analysis guarantees that estimation errors remain ultimately bounded despite network switching topologies. An unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) case study under limited sensing and coordinated attacks demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed framework in maintaining stable and accurate distributed state estimation.

title: 1.6 kV Vertical GaN Power MOSFETs with Hybrid Edge Termination
authors: Changfa Sun, Yuchuan Ma, Xiangze Kong, Yuanyuan Xie, Kai Cheng, Wei Mao, Chao Liu
abstract: We demonstrate 1600 V vertical GaN-on-GaN trench MOSFETs employing hybrid edge termination (HET-MOSFET) that integrates nitrogen ion implantation (NIT) with mesa etching termination (MET). The NIT structure introduces a resistive region to alleviate electric field crowding at the device edges, while the MET structure suppresses potential leakage paths associated with the standalone NIT scheme. As a result, the breakdown voltage is enhanced from 400 V in the MET-MOSFET to 1628 V in the HET-MOSFET. Moreover, thanks to the tailored p-GaN channel layer design, the HET-MOSFET exhibits a low specific on-resistance (R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON,sp</sub>) of 0.75 mΩ·cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and a channel mobility (μ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">CH</sub>) of 151 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/V·s. These results highlight the feasibility of vertical GaN trench MOSFETs for high-voltage and high-power electronics.

title: Channel Knowledge Map Construction via Guided Flow Matching
authors: Ziyu Huang, Yong Zeng, Shen Fu, Xiaoli Xu, Hongyang Du
abstract: The efficient construction of accurate channel knowledge maps (CKMs) is crucial for unleashing the full potential of environment-aware wireless networks, yet it remains a difficult ill-posed problem due to the sparsity of available location-specific channel knowledge data. Although diffusion-based methods such as denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) have been exploited for CKM construction, they rely on iterative stochastic sampling, rendering them too slow for real-time wireless applications. To bridge the gap between high fidelity and efficient CKM construction, this letter introduces a novel generative framework based on linear transport guided flow matching (LT-GFM). Deviating from the noise-removal paradigm of diffusion models, our approach models the CKM generation process as a deterministic ordinary differential equation (ODE) that follows linear transport paths, thereby drastically reducing the number of required inference steps. We propose a unified architecture that is applicable to not only the conventional channel gain map (CGM) construction, but also the more challenging spatial correlation map (SCM) construction. To achieve physics-informed CKM constructions, we integrate environmental semantics (e.g., building masks) for edge recovery and enforce Hermitian symmetry for property of the SCM. Simulation results verify that LT-GFM achieves superior distributional fidelity with significantly lower Frèchet Inception Distance (FID) and accelerates inference speed by a factor of 25 compared to DDPMs.

title: On the Optimality of Single-label and Multi-label Neural Network Decoders
authors: Yunus Can Gültekin, Péter Scheepers, Yuncheng Yuan, Federico Corradi, Alex Alvarado
abstract: We investigate the design of two neural network (NN) architectures recently proposed as decoders for forward error correction: the so-called single-label NN (SLNN) and multi-label NN (MLNN) decoders. These decoders have been reported to achieve near-optimal codeword- and bit-wise performance, respectively. Results in the literature show near-optimality for a variety of short codes. In this paper, we analytically prove that certain SLNN and MLNN architectures can, in fact, always realize optimal decoding, regardless of the code. These optimal architectures and their binary weights are shown to be defined by the codebook, i.e., no training or network optimization is required. Our proposed architectures are in fact not NNs, but implement the maximum likelihood decoding rule differently. Optimal performance is numerically demonstrated for Hamming (7; 4), Polar (16; 8), and BCH (31; 21) codes. The results show that our optimal architectures (which are not NNs) are less complex than the SLNN and MLNN architectures proposed in the literature, which in fact only achieve near-optimal performance. Extension to longer codes is still hindered by the curse of dimensionality. Therefore, even though SLNN and MLNN can perform maximum likelihood decoding, such architectures cannot be used for medium and long codes.

title: Joint Task Offloading and Resource Allocation in Multi-Hop Vehicular Networks
authors: Wenhao Wu, Xiaopei Chen, Zhizhao Lu, Yi Fang, Jiguang He, Zexiong Zeng, Zhijian Lin
abstract: The proliferation of smart vehicles and resource-hungry applications has imposed challenges to on-board systems. By exploiting the clustered vehicles in neighbor-following network (NFN), the multi-hop vehicular network (MHVN) enables cooperative communication among multiple vehicles, making it promising for vehicular edge computing (VEC). Nonetheless, to the best of our knowledge, the joint task offloading and resource allocation strategies in MHVN remains open due to challenges arising from the prevalence of link disruptions, the substantial variability in channel states, and the limited computational resource. With the above considerations, in this work, the task offloading and resource allocation are jointly optimized in the MHVN to minimize the offloading cost and maximize the task completion rate (TCR). However, the optimization problem turns out to be a mixed integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem. To this end, the problem is decoupled into two subproblems, which are solved by graph theory and improved water-filling algorithm, respectively. Furthermore, an iterative optimization algorithm is designed to mitigate the impact of the relaxation of delay constraints. Simulations are conducted to confirm the effectiveness of the proposed scheme.

title: Robust Input Shaping Vibration Suppression Control for a Rigid-Flexible Coupling Hoisting Robot Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Sipan Li, Bin Zi, Bin Zhou, Yuan Li, Zheng Hong Zhu
abstract: The vibration suppression for rigid-flexible coupling hoisting robots (RFCHRs) under compound motion has always been a focus in the engineering. Most vibration suppression control methods are overly complex in design, making them hard to effectively apply. Some methods that are relatively easy to deploy often fail to adapt well to changes in system uncertain parameters and external environmental disturbances, raising concerns about their robustness. To address this problem, a robust input shaping vibration suppression control method based on deep reinforcement learning (DRL) is proposed. Based on real-time environmental state feedback, this method utilizes the strategy gradient optimization capability of the proximal policy optimization with clipping (PPO-Clip) algorithm, enabling real-time updates of the shaper parameters according to changes in system parameters and external environmental. It can achieve faster vibration suppression for RFCHR, while also possessing strong adaptability to system parameter changes and anti-interference capability. Finally, experiments verify its effectiveness and superiority.

title: Bound on RIP of order $s+1$ for HTP Algorithm
authors: Sk Md Atique Anwar, Pradyumna Pradhan, Pradip Sasmal, Ramunaidu Randhi
abstract: The Hard Thresholding Pursuit (HTP) algorithm is a widely recognized method for sparse signal recovery, supported by rigorous theoretical guarantees based on the restricted isometry constants <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta _{3s}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta _{2s}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. However, the existing analysis framework has not been extended to lower-order restricted isometry conditions. In this work, we address this gap by establishing a novel recovery guarantee for HTP under a relaxed condition involving <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta _{s+1}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Specifically, we show that HTP achieves robust recovery whenever <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta _{s+1} < \sqrt{1/37} \approx 0.1644$</tex-math></inline-formula>, which surpasses the IHT-based bound of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta _{s+1} < 0.1545$</tex-math></inline-formula> based on the optimal step-length 1. To our knowledge, this result provides the first lower-order restricted isometry guarantee for HTP, in terms of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\delta _{s+1}$</tex-math></inline-formula>.

title: A Compact High-Linearity GaN Monolithically Integrated Multi-Loop Voltage Reference for Extreme Temperature Applications
authors: Yunsong Xu, Chenyan Zheng, Yichao Yang, Haotian Ji, Jiachen Duan, Hao Tian, Liang Jing, Yuanhao He, Ivona Z. Mitrovic, Jiangmin Gu, Ang Li, Wen Liu
abstract: Conventional electronics for extreme environments are typically restricted to single thermal regimes, limiting the feasibility of monolithic circuits capable of wide-range operation. To address this, this letter reports a monolithic voltage reference implemented in D/E-mode p-GaN HEMT technology for operation from -269 °C (5 K) to 250 °C. Based on impedance analysis of the conventional two-transistor reference, a multi-loop topology utilizing depletion-mode devices is introduced to suppress output line sensitivity and improve interference immunity. Experimental characterization demonstrates a stable 1.45 V output across a wide supply range of 5–56 V, achieving a line sensitivity of 0.003421 %/V and a power supply rejection of -50.46 dB at 100 Hz. These results establish the feasibility of high-stability biasing for monolithic all-GaN systems under extreme thermal conditions.

title: MultiPaint: A Unified Framework for Multi-task, Multi-object, and Multi-condition Video Inpainting
authors: Shiyuan Yang, Zheng Gu, Liang Hou, Xin Tao, Pengfei Wan, Xiaodong Chen, Jing Liao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Video inpainting modifies local regions in video while ensuring spatial and temporal coherence. However, existing methods-both traditional and recent diffusion-based ones-face key limitations: they lack unified support for both insertion and completion, and are restricted to single-object inpainting, making it difficult to handle multi-object scenarios involving grounding and interaction. In this paper, we propose MultiPaint, a unified framework for multi-task, multi-object, and multi-condition video inpainting. Firstly, we introduce dual-branch adapters to unify the insertion and completion tasks within a single model. Moreover, we propose a test-time scheduled feature composition strategy that enables multi-object inpainting with user-specified locations while better preserving interactions among objects, a setting that has been insufficiently addressed in prior work. Additionally, we introduce a multi-condition inpainting scheme that integrates text-guided, image-guided, and keyframe-guided modes via dynamic frame masking, providing more controllability in appearance customization. Extensive experiments show that MultiPaint achieves state-of-the-art performance on object insertion and scene completion among the recent works. We further demonstrate its versatility in downstream tasks including grounded video generation, object editing, object removal, image-guided inpainting, and long video inpainting.

title: Uncertainty of Heart Rate Variability Measured from Incomplete Inter-Beat Interval Sequence
authors: Valentina Casadei, Roberto Ferrero, Ram Kumar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Photoplethysmography recorded using wearable devices is often affected by movement or other disturbances that prevent a continuous accurate identification of heartbeats, resulting in an incomplete sequence of inter-beat intervals (IBI) available to the user. Inevitably, heart rate variability (HRV) indices calculated from such an incomplete sequence will be affected by uncertainty arising from the lack of information during the gaps in the sequence, but accurate and reliable tools to quantify that uncertainty are currently missing. This paper proposes a mathematically-rigorous method to quantify the uncertainty of any HRV index that results from short gaps in the IBI sequence. The method incorporates mathematical and physiological constraints in a Monte Carlo simulation, to reconstruct possible values of the missing samples with realistic probabilities and, from their distribution, estimate the uncertainty of each HRV index. The method is firstly validated by artificially creating gaps in a complete IBI sequence, and then it is applied to a full-night recording with real gaps, using simultaneous electrocardiography as reference. In both cases, the results are very promising and confirm the validity of the proposed approach, at least in presence of gaps of up to 20 missing heartbeats. Additional tests performed on a public dataset of similar recordings on different subjects are reported in the Supplementary File, and further support the validity of the proposed method.

title: GLEAM: A Multimodal Imaging Dataset and HAMM for Glaucoma Classification
authors: Jiao Wang, Chi Liu, Yiying Zhang, Hongchen Luo, Zhifen Guo, Ying Hu, Ke Xu, Jing Zhou, Hongyan Xu, Ruiting Zhou, Man Tang
abstract: Glaucoma is a leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide, with asymptomatic early stages often delaying diagnosis and treatment. Early and accurate diagnosis requires integrating complementary information from multiple ocular imaging modalities. However, most existing studies rely on single- or dual-modality imaging, such as fundus and optical coherence tomography (OCT), for coarse binary classification, thereby restricting the exploitation of complementary information and hindering both early diagnosis and stage-specific treatment. To address these limitations, we propose glaucoma lesion evaluation and analysis with multimodal imaging (GLEAM), the first publicly available tri-modal glaucoma dataset comprising scanning laser ophthalmoscopy fundus images, circumpapillary OCT images, and visual field pattern deviation maps, annotated with four disease stages, enabling effective exploitation of multimodal complementary information and facilitating accurate diagnosis and treatment across disease stages. To effectively integrate cross-modal information, we propose hierarchical attentive masked modeling (HAMM) for multimodal glaucoma classification. Our framework employs hierarchical attentive encoders and light decoders to focus cross-modal representation learning on the encoder. The attention module, named multimodal-channel graph attention (MCGA), boosts glaucoma classification performance by emulating two key clinical reasoning steps: first, it uses a multi-head modality gating mechanism to replicate ophthalmologists’ confidence scoring of fundus, OCT, and VF modalities; then, MCGA leverages a relational graph attention network to cross-examine structural-functional consistencies of weighted modalities. The experiments on GLEAM demonstrate that tri-modal fusion significantly outperforms single-modal and dual-modal configurations. Moreover, our proposed HAMM achieves superior performance compared with state-of-the-art multimodal learning methods. The dataset and code are publicly available via https://github.com/microewing/HAMM.

title: A Six-Port Receiver-Based Backscatterer
authors: Prabhav Manchanda, Cristina Andrei, Ulrich L. Rohde, Matthias Rudolph
abstract: Backscatter communication is widely used in passive IoT sensing and battery-free wireless systems, where low-power reflection-based modulation is essential. This paper presents a novel backscatter modulation technique using six-port receivers that leverages programmable ADC input impedance switching, eliminating the need for dedicated RF switches. The method exploits the internal buffer and programmable gain amplifier (PGA) states of a commercial ADC to create controllable impedance variations, transitioning between high-impedance and low-impedance states, thereby modulating reflection coefficients directly at the diode detector terminals with a standard six-port network architecture. Experimental validation is conducted across six frequencies from 843 MHz to 2.4 GHz, with input power levels ranging from −30 dBm to 0 dBm. The technique achieves significant modulation indices ranging from 0.58 (single-diode) to 0.80 (dual-diode) at 843 MHz on Input B, with notable secondary peaks at 1.82 GHz (m = 0.27–0.31) and 2.2 GHz (m = 0.10–0.23), demonstrating substantial backscatter signal generation capability across a wide frequency range from 843 MHz to 2.4 GHz. Results also reveal frequency-dependent directional backscattering behavior, with the modulation indices at Input A and Input B exhibiting distinct responses depending on the modulation scheme. This approach enables efficient single-antenna backscatter communication using existing commercial ADCs, offering a cost-effective, compact solution for IoT sensing networks, passive RFID systems, and battery-free wireless sensor applications.

title: MSCS: Multi-task Secure Aggregation with Cosine Similarity for Federated Learning in Mobile Crowdsensing
authors: Yang Hu, Yuanyuan Zhang, Mengya Lei, Gang Shen
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Mobile crowdsensing (MCS) systems usually involve a wide variety of heterogeneous data sources and multi-task learning scenarios, where participants contribute to different sensing objectives depending on diverse data distributions. To utilize these distributed data sources while ensuring privacy, federated learning (FL) has become a leading computational approach in MCS. It offers a decentralized framework that maintains user privacy by keeping raw data local. However, most existing FL methods for MCS are designed for singletask scenarios and do not address the complexities of multitask environments. In such cases, secure aggregation is especially important, as malicious participants (Byzantine nodes) may submit manipulated or incorrect model updates. These malicious contributions harm the accuracy of the aggregation process, ultimately degrading the overall model performance and spreading errors across related tasks. To tackle this issue, we introduce Multi-task Secure Aggregation with Cosine Similarity (MSCS), a novel method tailored for multi-task FL situations. MSCS uses cosine similarity to measure behavioral similarity between nodes, which helps in accurately identifying and removing malicious contributions while keeping computational costs low. Our approach offers robust performance and increased resistance against Byzantine attacks. Experimental results show that MSCS significantly enhances system resilience and prediction accuracy, effectively resisting adversarial behaviors.

title: Modeling of Conjugative- and Phage- Mediated CRISPR-based System against Antimicrobial Resistant Bacteria
authors: Chiara Cimolato, Sara Petrelli, Davide Favaro, Angelica Frusteri Chiacchiera, Simone Del Favero, Luca Schenato, Lorenzo Pasotti, Massimo Bellato
abstract: Objective: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses a significant threat to global health by diminishing the effectiveness of conventional antibiotics. This study aims to assess, using a systems biology approach, a potential synthetic biology-based strategy that employs engineered conjugative probiotic bacteria and bacteriophages to combat AMR, examining the implications of implementing targeted gene silencing as an alternative to direct bacterial killing. Methods: A comprehensive mathematical model was developed to describe the dynamics of the delivery systems (engineered conjugative probiotic bacteria and engineered phages) and the antimicrobial actuators being studied (genome cutting via CRISPR systems and AMR-gene silencing through CRISPR interference), also compared to traditional phage therapy (selection of phages capable of killing pathogens through bacterial-specific viral infection). The target population includes antibiotic-resistant bacteria competing with other probiotic bacteria for colonizing the host environment. The model explicitly incorporates parameters for mutations that affect actuator functionality and simulates their impact on overall therapeutic performance. Results: Simulations show how variations in actuator efficiency and emergence of new mutations in target pathogens affect the long-term suppression of resistance genes. Including mutational effects provides insights into system robustness and guides optimal therapeutic design choices. Conclusion: The proposed modeling framework effectively captures key biological and mechanistic aspects of engineered therapies, enabling the prediction and optimization of each intervention against resistant pathogens. It highlights engineered phages and CRISPR interference as the most promising candidates for the design of new engineered biological therapeutics. Significance: This study establishes a quantitative foundation for rational design and dosage optimization in engineered phage- and bacterial-based therapies, advancing the use of synthetic biology methods to fight antimicrobial resistance.

title: Self-Organized Neural Network-based Control for Uncertain Electro-hydraulic Servo Actuator with Output Constraint and Input Saturation
authors: Hoai Vu Anh, Truong Wan Kyun Chung
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: To significantly improve the tracking performance of electro-hydraulic servo actuators (EHSAs) subject to unknown disturbances, uncertainties, and unstructured system dynamics, this paper proposes a control strategy based on a self-organizing radial basis function neural network (SORBFNN) augmented by a finite-time disturbance observer (DO). The SORBFNN, which employs time-varying Gaussian basis functions, is first utilized to effectively approximate unstructured control coefficients. Simultaneously, a DO is designed to suppress external disturbances and mismatched uncertainties. Furthermore, to further enhance system robustness, output constraints and input saturation techniques are incorporated into the design. The stability of the closed-loop system is rigorously demonstrated using the Lyapunov stability theorem, and the method’s effectiveness is experimentally validated through comparative benchmarks on a rotary EHSA.

title: AIvaluateXR: An Evaluation Framework for on-Device AI in XR with Benchmarking Results
authors: Dawar Khan, Xinyu Liu, Omar Mena, Donggang Jia, Alexandre Kouyoumdjian, Ivan Viola
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) on extended reality (XR) devices has great potential to advance the field of human-AI interaction. In case of direct, on-device model inference, selecting the appropriate model and device for specific tasks remains challenging. In this paper, we present AIvaluateXR, a comprehensive evaluation framework for benchmarking LLMs running on XR devices. To demonstrate the framework, we deploy 17 selected LLMs across four XR platforms-Magic Leap 2, Meta Quest 3, Vivo X100s Pro, and Apple Vision Pro-and conduct an extensive evaluation. Our experimental setup measures four key metrics: performance consistency, processing speed, memory usage, and battery consumption. For each of the 68 model-device pairs, we assess performance under varying string lengths, batch sizes, and thread counts, analyzing the tradeoffs for real-time XR applications. We finally propose a unified evaluation method based on the 3D Pareto Optimality theory to select the optimal device-model pairs from the quality and speed objectives. Additionally, we compare the efficiency of on-device LLMs with client-server and cloud-based setups, and evaluate their accuracy on two interactive tasks. We believe our findings offer valuable insights to guide future optimization efforts for LLM deployment on XR devices. Our evaluation method can be followed as standard groundwork for further research and development in this emerging field.

title: 6DMA-Enabled ISAC for Low-Altitude Economy
authors: Yingchao Jiao, Xuhui Zhang, Chunjie Wang, Shuqiang Wang, Yanyan Shen, Kejiang Ye, Chengzhong Xu
abstract: In this paper, we investigate a six dimensional movable antenna (6DMA) enable integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) network in low-altitude economy. The studied 6DMA can move in a three-dimensional space and rotate around its surface center, making full use of spatial freedom to adapt to the different location distributions of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) adjust channel conditions in time. However, since the rotation and location change of 6DMA requires the assistance of a physical device, it is unreasonable for 6DMA to change locations too frequently. Therefore, we propose a hierarchical deep reinforcement learning algorithm based on twin delayed deep deterministic policy gradient. The first layer optimizes the rotation and location of 6DMA with infrequent updates, and the second layer optimizes the UAV flight direction and base station transmit beamforming matrix in each time slot. Under the condition of satisfying the sensing intensity of the sensing target, the total data transmission rate to the UAVs is maximized. The numerical results show that the proposed 6DMA-enable ISAC algorithm through joint optimization of multiple variables performs significantly better than the partially movable algorithm and the fixed antenna position algorithm.

title: Resolving Stiffness Trade-Offs in Simultaneous Pressure and Vibration Sensing Using a Corrugated-Tube Fiber-Optic Sensor
authors: Yizheng Chen, Yan Tang, Jie Huang, Qi Zhang, Biyao Shi, Zewei Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This article proposes and experimentally demonstrates a corrugated-tube-based fiber-optic sensor capable of measuring pressure, vibration, or both simultaneously. To address the limited sensitivity of conventional diaphragm-based designs, the sensor incorporates an optimized corrugated tube that balances the conflicting stiffness requirements for pressure and vibration measurements. The corrugated tube, acting as a mechanical transducer, is integrated with an extrinsic fiber-optic Fabry–Perot interferometer (EFPI). The EFPI cavity is formed between a reflective surface at the sealed end of the corrugated tube and the cleaved end face of an optical fiber fixed within a mounting assembly. In this configuration, displacement of the corrugated tube induced by pressure and/or vibration directly leads to variations in the EFPI cavity length, which are quantified through interference signal analysis. A series of experiments was conducted using a hydraulic test pump and vibration testing equipment to simulate pressure changes and vibrational disturbances, respectively. The pressure calibration results show a linear relationship between cavity length variation and applied water pressure over a range up to 1 MPa, achieving a sensitivity of −236.75 μm/MPa with a resolution as fine as 10 Pa. Vibration tests demonstrate stable sensor responses over frequencies ranging from 5 to 200 Hz, with an average acceleration sensitivity of approximately 62.49 nm/g. Furthermore, experimental validation confirms the sensor’s capability for simultaneous measurement of pressure and vibration acceleration. With its simple structure, wide measurement range, high sensitivity, excellent mechanical robustness, and minimal temperature sensitivity, the proposed sensor is well suited for practical deployment in demanding industrial environments.

title: GLRT-Based Deep Metric Learning for Robust Remote Sensing Object Retrieval_supp1-3679517.pdf
authors: Yu Liu
abstract: Remote sensing object retrieval (RSOR) aims to identify images of the same object from large remote sensing image databases. However, existing RSOR methods often rely on simple and distribution-agnostic distance metrics, for example, Euclidean distance, that treat all the feature dimensions equally, making them susceptible to the influence of spurious features and vulnerable to variations such as noise, resolution degradation, and rotation variations. Inspired by the Neyman–Pearson theorem, we propose a generalized likelihood ratio test-based deep metric learning (GLRT-DML) approach for RSOR. GLRT-DML is a distribution-aware metric learning framework that leverages distribution information from feature embeddings across the dataset to suppress the influence of spurious features while emphasizing informative ones. This process facilitates the construction of a robust metric space for RSOR. Extensive experiments on ship, aircraft, and vehicle retrieval tasks demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed GLRT-DML approach compared with state-of-the-art RSOR methods.

title: Highly Sensitive VO-Assisted Conformal Reflective Polarization Converter-Based Sensor_supp1-3690636.pdf
authors: SARTHAK SINGHAL
abstract: In this research, a partial octagonal ring-shaped gold resonator is utilized to attain reflection type polarization conversion of over 80% for the insulator and conductor modes of vanadium dioxide, respectively, spanning the frequency range of 13.35–15.98 and 5.14–15.97 THz. The conductor and insulator modes attain more than 95% polarization conversion ratio (PCR) at 5.4929, 8.4779, 13.4529, and 15.7016 THz and 14.229 and 15.7215 THz, respectively, with an overall volume of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\times 10\times 4.5$</tex-math> </inline-formula> <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. Until a bending angle of ±50°, its full width at half maxima (FWHM) performance, that is, PCR <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\ge 0.5$</tex-math> </inline-formula> remains steady. Its maximum sensitivity when used as a refractive index sensor, bioalcohol sensor, bioalcohol concentration sensor, biosensor, and metal heavy ion sensor for the conductor mode of VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> is 2.172 THz/RIU, 28.28 THz/RIU, 497.5THz/RIU, 9.95THz/RIU, and 73877.25 nm/RIU, and 472.8144pm/ppm. Peak sensitivities for insulator mode are modified to 1.861THz/RIU, 18.98THz/RIU, 2.051 THz/RIU, 1.839 THz/RIU, and 4531.836 nm/RIU, and 31.72286 pm/ppm. Numerous polarization controller and multisensing applications can benefit from its compact volume, conformability, temperature tunability, and exceptionally high sensitivity properties.
