title: Self-Decoupling of Port and Radiation Pattern in a Dual-Port Co-Polarized Wideband Dielectric Patch Antenna
authors: Zhen Wu, Liang Zha, Chao Jun Ma, Yong Mei Pan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: A dual-port co-polarized wideband dielectric patch antenna (DPA) featuring self-decoupling of both port and radiation pattern is proposed for in-band full-duplex (IBFD) applications. An offset-fed slot partially excites the dielectric slab (DS), creating an excitation region and a coupling region, with a natural weak-field region at their interface. Leveraging this mechanism, this work employs two symmetrically offset slots to excite a rectangular DS. When the excitation signal is coupled from one of the slots into the DS, its TM₁₀₁ and TM₁₂₁ modes can be simultaneously excited, thereby achieving a wide impedance bandwidth. By simultaneously optimizing the feed-slot positions and DS dimensions, a weak-field region forms around the coupled port in both operating modes, thereby realizing self-decoupling between ports. Moreover, positioning the excitation slots near the center of the excitation region effectively mitigates distortion in the radiation pattern and preserves symmetry. Experimental results demonstrate an impedance bandwidth of 18.66% (4.81–5.80 GHz), with port isolation exceeding 23 dB and stable, symmetric radiation patterns across the entire band.

title: Design of a Holographic Standing-wave Antenna
authors: Somayeh Saeidi, Mohammad Amin Chaychi Zadeh, Sajjad Zohrevand, Ehsan Farokhipour, Andreas Rennings, Daniel Erni, Nader Komjani, Vahid Nayyeri
abstract: In this paper, for the first time, the design approach of a standing-wave antenna based on a holographic technique is presented. To the best of the authors' knowledge, no holographic standing-wave antenna (HSWA) design has yet been reported. In the design process, the amplitude and phase of the reference wave are carefully extracted. A center-fed holographic antenna with broadside radiation at the frequency of 18 GHz is designed. The HSWA consists of grounded hexagonal unit-cells with a side length of about 0.08λ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf>. The HSWA is implemented on Rogers RO4003C substrate with a thickness of 0.528 mm. The realized gain and aperture efficiency of the proposed compact antenna are 15.8 dBi and 75%, respectively.

title: Study of the Effect of Common Gain Section in Two-wavelength Laser for Terahertz applications: Measurement of Correlation between Modes
authors: Ramasubramanian Balasubramanian, Akhileshwar Mishra, Marcel Grzeslo, Yilmaz Uçar, Shuya Iwamatsu, Israa Mohammad, Jonas Tebart, Peter J.M. van der Slot, Klaus-J. Boller, David A.I. Marpaung, Andreas Stöhr
abstract: This work presents a novel measurement method to characterise the correlation between two modes of a two-wavelength laser employed to generate broadband signals from the microwave to the terahertz range through photomixing. A two-wavelength laser with a common gain section is analysed for this purpose. The effective refractive index of the gain section is subject to constant modulation (Free-Carrier Plasma Effect) due to the production and recombination of free charge carriers. The common gain section aims to synchronize the influence of effective refractive index fluctuations across the respective cavity lengths and to increase common-mode noise rejection. We can verify whether the modes are synchronised with respect to the emission wavelengths with this approach. This phenomenon is verified by plotting the frequency of the signal generated by the modes separately with an ultra-stable two-wavelength reference laser, calculation of correlation coefficient between the signals also validates this phenomenon. In addition, the instantaneous timing jitter of the two signals shows correlated behaviour at the terahertz range (5 THz).

title: A SiGe 270 GHz Radar Transmitter Featuring a ×16 Multiplier Chain and On-Chip Antennas Combined with a Highly Directive Lens
authors: Stephan Hauptmeier, Kennet Braasch, Michael Höft, Nils Pohl
abstract: This work presents a 270 GHz radar transmitter for high dynamic range applications. The proposed MMIC uses a ×16 frequency multiplier chain with a particular focus on the output doubler stage. This stage employs an I/Q doubler topology combined with a power-combining network, achieving −0.6 dBm output power at 259 GHz, close to the technology’s transit frequency, which is about 300 GHz. An integrated 2×1 antenna array enables in-air power combining and provides an EIRP of 1.7 dBm at 270 GHz. Using a highly directive dielectric lens, the EIRP can be further increased to 31 dBm.

title: Mutual Learning and Adaptive Ensembling of Pre-Trained Language Models for Hierarchical and Flat Scientific Text Classification
authors: Zhyar Rzgar K Rostam, Gábor Kertész
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Automatic text classification is essential for the efficient categorization of scientific content. In this study, we investigate both flat and hierarchical classification on the Web of Science datasets WoS-46985 and its two variants, WoS-11967 and WoS-5736, using three pre-trained language models (PLMs): BERT, SciBERT, and BioBERT. For flat classification, we independently fine-tune the models for domain-level and fine-grained (leaf-level) category prediction, using dynamic learning rate selection and early stopping. We propose a mutual learning framework where pairs of PLMs exchange high-confidence predictions, integrated with an adaptive ensembling strategy for improved robustness. For hierarchical classification, we adopt a two-stage pipeline: first, predicting domain-level labels using an adaptive ensembling of two PLMs, and second, training domain-specific SciBERT models to predict area-level labels conditioned on the predicted domain. SciBERT consistently outperforms BERT and BioBERT across all classification levels. The mutual learning and hierarchical approaches achieve the highest accuracy across all datasets, especially with the SciBERT-BioBERT adaptive ensembling. Our approaches improve upon prior work, demonstrating that integrating specialized knowledge with collaborative and adaptive strategies enhances performance in hierarchical scientific text classification.

title: Pressure-Temperature Decoupling FBG Sensor Based on Diaphragm Center-Edge Push-Pull Complementarity
authors: Xiangzuo Chen, Qihui Cai, Han Song, Xingzhuang Liang, Ang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Aiming at the urgent need for simultaneous and accurate monitoring of fluid pressure and temperature in a wide temperature range environment, this paper proposes a pressure-temperature decoupling FBG sensor based on the center-edge push-pull complementarity of the diaphragm. The sensor uses the strain distribution characteristics of the titanium alloy TC4 rectangular diaphragm under uniform load to determine the FBG placement position, thereby driving the center wavelengths of the two FBGs to drift in opposite directions to achieve pressure-temperature decoupling. Through theoretical modeling and finite element simulation, the diaphragm structure size is optimized to ensure the sensing sensitivity and strain distribution characteristics. The experimental results show that the sensor is stable in the range of ‒55–150 °C and 0–2 MPa. The absolute values of the sensitivity of the positive and negative strains generated by the two gratings at 25 °C are 561.25 and 950.75 pm/MPa, respectively, and the fitting determination coefficient R2 is greater than 0.999. In order to further improve the decoupling accuracy of temperature and pressure parameters, the Poly2D surface fitting algorithm is used to decouple the wavelength data. The pressure and temperature measurement errors of the sensor in the full range are better than ±0.8 %. The sensor has the advantages of compact structure and light weight, and provides a reliable technical solution for real-time multi-parameter monitoring of fluid state in various wide temperature zones.

title: Breast Tumor Classification Using a Hybrid System C ombining a CNN with Genetic Algorithm and K-means for Mammography Images
authors: Reima Almajdoub, Eslam Sheta, Muhammad Siddiqui
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Maghreb Meeting of the Conference on Sciences and Techniques of Automatic Control and Computer Engineering (MI-STA)
abstract: Accurate breast tumor classification from mammography images remains a critical challenge in computer-aided diagnosis. The main objective of this study is to develop an efficient and interpretable hybrid framework for accurate breast tumor classification and segmentation from mammography images to support early diagnosis. To achieve this objective, this study proposes a hybrid framework that integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for tumor classification with a Genetic Algorithm (GA) and an optimized K-means clustering approach for tumor segmentation. The methodology comprises three stages: (1) image preprocessing, including background removal, pectoral muscle elimination, denoising via optimally selected filters, and contrast enhancement using Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE); (2) feature extraction of 13 texture-based features from the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and statistical descriptors, which serve as input to a fully connected CNN classifier; and (3) tumor segmentation using GA-optimized K-means clustering, where the GA determines the optimal number of clusters. Filter selection is performed systematically by evaluating Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), identifying the Gaussian filter (PSNR $=55.02 \mathrm{~dB}$) and Coiflets wavelet ($\operatorname{PSNR}=50.16 \mathrm{~dB}$) as optimal choices for spatial and wavelet domains, respectively. The framework is evaluated on 3,978 mammography images (2,628 benign and 1,350 malignant) from the RSNA Breast Cancer Detection dataset using stratified 5-fold cross-validation. The scientific contribution of this work lies in the integration of optimized preprocessing, texture-based feature extraction, deep learning classification, and GA-driven clustering into a unified framework that improves both classification accuracy and tumor localization interpretability. The proposed CNN achieves training, testing, and validation accuracies of 97.42 %, 97.86 %, and 97.86%, respectively. The proposed study confirms that each preprocessing stage contributes measurably to the final performance. Compared to recent methods used, the proposed framework demonstrates competitive accuracy while providing interpretable segmentation outputs.

title: X-Band Electrical Balance Duplexer for In-Band Full-Duplexing in GaN-on-Silicon Technology
authors: Ting-Li Hsu, Muh-Dey Wei, Renato Negra
abstract: An integrated electrical balance duplexer (EBD) for in-band full-duplex (IBFD) using a fast orthogonal searching methodology is designed and implemented in GaN-on-Silicon MMIC technology for X-band operation. The EBD mainly consists of a hybrid coupler and a reconfigurable balancing network composed of a Wilkinson power divider (WPD), phase shifters and two voltage-controlled resistors. By tuning the impedance at the balancing port along two orthogonal directions, i.e., I and Q, on the Smith chart independently, the optimum isolation of the EBD can be reached with the proposed balancing network. This approach can reduce the searching time for the optimum impedance. Simulated isolation of the EBD reaches 64 dB at 10 GHz. The designed circuit occupies an area of 4.79 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: ISO/IEC/IEEE Software and systems engineering -- Incident management
authors: N/A
abstract: The purpose of this international standard is to define a generic process for incident management that can be used by any organization when performing any form of software and systems development, testing or operations. The processes defined in this document can be used in conjunction with any development life cycle model (e.g. incremental, waterfall, evolutionary, agile).

title: A Robotic mmWave Radar Based Compact Antenna Test Range
authors: Tobias Körner, Kristof Dausien, Ilona Rolfes, Jan Barowski
abstract: This paper presents a compact antenna measurement setup designed for D-band applications. The setup integrates a robotic arm, a millimeter-wave radar module, and a parabolic reflector, enabling flexible characterization of antennas. The shift towards higher operating frequency offers increased usable bandwidth. While this leads to higher range resolution and higher data rates in sensing and communication applications, the aditional available bandwith can also be leveraged to enable much more compact antenna test solutions. Contrary, millimeter wave systems often utilize high gain antennas to overcome increased free-space attenuation, causing large far-field distances that limit dimensions of the antenna measurement setup. Therefore, the proposed compact test range uses the near-field properties of a parabolic reflector to create a planar wavefront in a confined space. This setup allows for antenna measurements under quasi-far-field conditions. A key element of the setup is an electromagnetic switch placed near the focal region. This switch allows the relevant target peak to be isolated within the radar measurements, thereby improving measurement clarity and repeatability. Simulations and measurements validate the concepts suitability.

title: Personalized Long-Sequence Recommendation via Interest-Aware Large Language Models
authors: Wei Shi, Jimin Bao, Jinglong Li, Changlong Zheng
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Sequential recommendation requires modeling both users' historical interactions and current contextual preferences to deliver accurate predictions. With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), leveraging them for sequential behavior modeling has become a promising direction and has already shown encouraging results. However, existing methods often struggle when directly handling long interaction sequences, where important preference signals may be overwhelmed by noisy or low-relevance behaviors. Unlike textual sequences, user behavior sequences raise two critical challenges: (1) data sparsity, and (2) preference inconsistency caused by the dynamic evolution of interests. These challenges limit the effectiveness of directly applying LLMs to long-sequence modeling, as such models often struggle to capture shifting user intents and adapt slowly to interest drift. To address these limitations, we present Interestaware Recommendation (InterRec), a novel LLM-based framework that jointly models both short- and long-term interests for sequential recommendation. Inspired by the recency effect, a short-term module captures users' immediate preferences from their most recent interactions, while guided by habit theory, a long-term module leverages LLMs to uncover stable behavioral patterns from extended histories. Instead of directly using full or truncated sequences, the proposed method selects representative items to construct more informative preference contexts. A fusion mechanism then integrates these dual temporal perspectives into comprehensive preference representations. Extensive experiments on two public datasets from different domains demonstrate that InterRec consistently enhances LLM-based sequential recommendation, yielding an average improvement of 5% in HR@5.

title: A RFSoC-FPGA-Based Digital Radar System for Distributed UAV-Borne SAR
authors: Lucas L. Lamberti, Stefan V. Baumgartner, Gerhard Krieger
abstract: Recent advances in microchip manufacturing and Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) technology have sparked a great opportunity for new and innovative radar systems. With the combination of digital radar systems based on Radio Frequency System on Chip (RFSoC) and Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) technology, as well as modern UAVs, new highly configurable, multistatic radar systems become possible. Towards this end, a distributed, UAV-borne Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) system is developed at the German Aerospace Center (DLR). This Software-Defined Radar (SDR) system concept is described in this paper and key challenges are addressed.

title: Kernel Reboot: Breaking the Boundaries of Neural Tangent Kernels for Neural Fields
authors: Amir Mallak, Alaa Maalouf, Lior Wolf, Daniela Rus, Dan Rosenbaum
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Neural fields (NFs) map continuous coordinates to signals such as color or density, but fast high-quality reconstruction from sparse observations remains difficult. Classical Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) regression gives closed-form fits, yet it is fundamentally linear and cannot accumulate reusable task priors. We develop three algorithms that address these gaps. NTK-KIP learns a distilled support set of coordinates (and optional labels) so that a finite NTK can inpaint large missing regions from little observed data, yielding a compact non-linear representation instead of a raw kernel solve. MetaQuill meta-learns a shared initialization for an INR so that new scenes can be adapted by updating only a small task-specific weight offset, which provides true feature learning and a reusable prior. Finally, MetaQuill-KIP fuses both ideas: it seeds the task with a KIP-style non-linear warm start, then refines only that small offset around the meta-learned initialization. MetaQuill-KIP achieves high-PSNR reconstructions and semantically plausible inpainting under very sparse observations, while requiring only lightweight per-instance adaptation, whereas diffusion-style baselines typically depend on large pretrained generative priors and costly per-image tuning. This shows that NTK-driven neural fields can be made both non-linear and meta-learnable, narrowing the gap between analytic kernels and practical few-shot reconstruction.

title: Fast Aerial Channel Knowledge Map Construction via an Energy-Constrained UAV
authors: Changhe Chen, Jianbing Zheng, Feng Li, Fahui Wu, Yu Xu, Zhiwen Ding, Dingcheng Yang, Lin Xiao
abstract: Channel knowledge maps (CKMs) have been widely applied in resource allocation and performance optimization for wireless communication systems. CKMs are generally constructed from measurements gathered by user equipment or sensors dispersed within the area of interest. To construct aerial CKMs with precision, it is imperative to deploy unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to gather radio information in low-altitude regions. However, due to the limited onboard energy of UAVs, measuring every location in the area proves to be exceedingly inefficient. This paper focuses on constructing the Signal-to-Noise Ratio map, which serves as a core component of the CKM, and proposes an efficient CKM construction scheme for this purpose. In this scheme, UAVs autonomously select high-value measurement locations to collect data and complete the CKM estimation based on the collected data. The scheme comprises two steps: first, a deep learning-based CKM constructor is designed to update the map estimation and indicate the uncertainty metric for each potential location. Second, utilizing the uncertainty metrics, the UAV's trajectory is planned by proposing a dynamic trajectory planning algorithm. This algorithm enables the UAV to collect measurements at the most informative locations under constrained energy conditions, thereby achieving rapid CKM estimation. Numerical results demonstrate that, compared to the baseline, this method offers significant performance advantages in CKM estimation.

title:
Crystal and Magnetic Structures of Tetragonal Spinel Fe
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    V
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>8</sub>
                    Studied by Neutron and X-ray Diffraction
authors: Ming-Hsuan Liao, Chia-Chi Lu, Yu-Hsien Lin, Chin-Wei Wang, Wei-Ren Liu, Yu-Ting Lee, Chien-Lung Huang, Chun-Chuen Yang
abstract: In this work, we investigate the transition metal oxide spinel Fe3V3O8 using combined X-ray and neutron powder diffraction techniques to elucidate its crystal and magnetic structures. The results indicate that the compound retains a tetragonal crystal structure with space group I41/amd throughout the temperature range from 3.3 K to 475 K. The magnetic structure is described by the magnetic space group I41'/am'd, and no structural or magnetic phase transition is observed within this temperature window. At low temperatures, the system exhibits a canted antiferromagnetic ordering. Magnetization measurements reveal anomalies near 30 K and 100 K, corresponding to the μy component exceeding μz and reaching its maximum value, respectively. A pronounced magnetic hysteresis associated with magnetic domain formation is observed at 2 K, which becomes significantly suppressed above 300 K. Specific-heat measurements reveal a weak and broad anomaly around 180 K, likely associated with spin-glass-like behavior.

title: Matrix Pencil-Based Dynamic Event-Triggered Cooperative Formation Control for Multiagents
authors: Hefu Ye, Zheng Zhou, Yongduan Song, Yicong Zhou
abstract: Cooperative formation control is essential for multiagent systems in applications such as surveillance, logistics, and environmental monitoring. In this article, we propose a matrix pencil-based decentralized control scheme that ensures prescribed-time convergence without interagent communication. Both static and dynamic event-triggered protocols are developed to significantly reduce control updates while maintaining stability. The dynamic version further extends interevent intervals compared with its static counterpart. A key contribution is a low-conservatism gain design method using matrix pencils, enabling controller parameters to be directly selected from precomputed tables, which greatly simplifying implementation. Zeno behavior is rigorously excluded for both schemes, and the effectiveness of the proposed methods is verified through simulations and hardware experiments. This work provides a practical solution for communication-free multiagent systems operating in resource-constrained environments.

title: Revisiting Text-Guided Style Transfer through Mamba: A Fast and Memory-Efficient Architecture
authors: Filippo Botti, Teresa Calzetti, Alex Ergasti, Tomaso Fontanini, Andrea Prati
abstract: Text-guided style transfer aims to stylize a content image according to a textual description while preserving the image structure. Unfortunately, many recent approaches rely on either attention-based fusion or diffusion backbones. On one hand, transformers require high memory consumption due to their quadratic complexity, while diffusion-based methods require iterative denoising, making inference slow and computationally expensive. In this work, we propose a lightweight architecture based on Mamba state-space models. Our key idea is to perform cross-modal fusion inside the Mamba dynamics by conditioning the state-space parameters on the text embedding while processing content tokens, avoiding attention and enabling linear-complexity fusion. Additionally, we represent the content image as a sequence of multi-scale frozen VGG features instead of learned patch embeddings, reducing sequence length and improving content encoding. Experiments at 512×512 resolution show that our method achieves strong prompt alignment while maintaining high structural similarity to the input and it provides a favorable quality-efficiency trade-off compared to open baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/FilippoBotti/TGSTM.

title: A Hybrid Kepler Optimization Algorithm Based on Q-Learning for Solving Permutation Flow Shop Scheduling Problems
authors: Xiangbo Qi, Zhijing Zhang, Jiaxi Zhang, Yan Song
abstract: The permutation flow shop scheduling problem (PFSP) is a significant challenge in production scheduling, considerably impacting industrial efficiency and competitiveness. To minimize the makespan in PFSP, this paper proposes a hybrid Kepler optimization algorithm (H-KOA) that incorporates a Q-learning mechanism and multiple enhancement strategies. The core innovation is the integration of Q-learning, which adaptively and dynamically adjusts key algorithm parameters via reinforcement learning, thereby effectively balancing global exploration and local exploitation. Furthermore, the H-KOA employs the Nawaz-Enscore-Ham (NEH) heuristic to generate a high-quality initial solution and integrates a local search strategy to deepen its exploitation capability. To evaluate the Q-learning mechanism, comprehensive ablation experiments are conducted. Comparative experiments with other advanced intelligent optimization algorithms are performed using standard benchmark instances. The results demonstrate the significant advantages of the proposed H-KOA in solving PFSP. Its practicality and effectiveness are further validated through an application to the production scheduling of straight-seam steel pipes.

title:
Influence of DNA Solution on Dark and Deep-UV Response of Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    /p-Si Schottky Diodes
authors: Chia-Hsun Chen, Tsung-Ying Yang, Yi-Yen Chen, Shu-Bai Liu
abstract: This study investigates the influence of DNA solution on the photoelectric characteristic of amorphous Ga₂O₃/p-Si Schottky diodes. Experiment results reveal that the presence of a DNA droplet on the Ga₂O₃ surface significantly influences the device behavior. Upon applying a DNA droplet onto the devices, the dark current increased from 3.22×10⁻⁶ A to 8.31×10⁻⁶ A, owing to the interfacial charge redistribution induced by negatively charged phosphate backbone and its associated counter ions. Conversely, under 254 nm deep–ultraviolet (DUV) illumination, the photocurrent decreases from 1.04 × 10⁻⁴ A to 3.06 × 10⁻⁵ A due to the strong deep–ultraviolet absorption of DNA nucleobases. To analyze junction parameters, the Ocaya–Yakuphanoğlu (O–Y) method was employed, overcoming the non-physical fluctuations of conventional models. The O–Y method extraction confirmed that the presence of DNA droplet reduces the barrier height, explaining the increased leakage current. These findings establish a foundation for future Ga₂O₃-based deep–ultraviolet biosensing platforms.

title: S$^{3}$RNet: Sparse Spatial–Spectral Representation with Hybrid Knowledge Distillation for Efficient Multispectral and Hyperspectral Image Fusion
authors: Chih-Chung Hsu, Chia-Ming Lee, Yu-Fan Lin, Chih-Chien Ni, Li-Wei Kang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Multispectral and hyperspectral image fusion is a key enabler for real-time and near real-time remote sensing analysis, where low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSI) must be combined with high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSI) under tight computational and bandwidth constraints. Although recent deep learning based fusion methods have achieved impressive performance, many of them rely on deep and heavy architectures that are difficult to deploy on resource-limited platforms and are highly sensitive to noise corruption. To address these issues, we propose the Sparse Spatial-Spectral Representation Network (S<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula>RNet). The proposed framework introduces three main components: (i) a Multi-Branch Fusion Network (MBFN) that captures specialized and complementary spatial-spectral features across parallel branches; (ii) a Dense Feature Aggregation Block (DFAB) that exploits dense connectivity for efficient feature extraction and propagation; and (iii) a Spatial-Spectral Adaptive Weight Block (SSAWB) that learns content-aware sparse representations to suppress noise and enhance robustness. In addition, we develop a Hybrid Online Knowledge Distillation (HOKD) strategy that transfers knowledge from a high-capacity teacher network to a lightweight student network, striking a favorable balance between reconstruction fidelity and model complexity. Extensive experiments and analyses demonstrate that S<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{3}$</tex-math></inline-formula>RNet delivers state-of-the-art fusion accuracy, strong robustness to various noise levels, and competitive runtime, making it well suited for practical remote sensing applications that require efficient MS–HS image fusion. The code will be publicly available.

title: Visibility Convolutional Neural Networks for Label Distribution Learning of Facial Emotions
authors: Abeer Almowallad, Victor Sanchez
abstract: Human emotion recognition from facial expressions depicted in images is an active area of research in computer vision and machine learning. Facial expressions usually represent a mix of basic emotions, each with a different intensity. Hence, measuring these intensities from facial expression images is a challenging task. Previous studies have solved this task by using label-distribution learning (LDL). Models based on LDL predict a probability distribution for each face image, where the distribution represents the intensities of the emotions depicted in the image. In this paper, we propose an LDL framework that introduces a novel convolutional (Conv) layer called the visibility convolutional layer (VCL). The VCL preserves the advantages of traditional Conv layers, in terms of using filters to extract features, while reducing the number of learnable parameters and allowing for the extraction of strong texture features. Our LDL framework, which we call VCNN-ELDL, uses the features extracted by the VCLs and those extracted by traditional Conv layers to predict a probability distribution for each image using supervised learning. We evaluate VCNN-ELDL on the widely-used $s$-JAFFE and $s$-BU-3DFE datasets, which are annotated with probability distributions as the ground truth representing the mix of six basic human emotions, i.e., happiness, sadness, anger, fear, surprise, and disgust. Our results show that our framework can accurately predict the probability distribution representing the mix of emotions depicted in face images by attaining a stronger performance than state-of-the-art LDL methods.

title: OTQuSbox Framework: A Quantum-Assisted Session-Based S-Box Construction with Secure Seed Synchronization
authors: Annas W. Malik, Ghulam Mustafa, Beomkyu Suh, Ki-Il Kim
abstract: Conventional Substitution-boxes (S-boxes) used in symmetric-key cryptography are typically static or generated through deterministic pseudo-random processes, making them vulnerable to long-term structural analysis, precomputation, and learning-based cryptanalytic attacks. To address these limitations, this paper proposes OTQuSbox, a quantum-assisted session-based framework for constructing dynamic one-time 8×8 S-boxes with secure seed synchronization. The proposed model combines quantum-derived entropy with deterministic algebraic transformations in GF(28), including multiplicative inversion, primitive-element exponentiation, and affine hardening, to achieve both unpredictability and mathematical verifiability. A noisy 8-qubit quantum circuit with whitening is employed to generate high-entropy seeds, while an ephemeral Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDH)-based protocol enables communicating parties to securely reconstruct identical session-specific S-boxes without transmitting the S-box itself. Experimental analysis demonstrates that the generated S-boxes satisfy essential cryptographic criteria, achieving maximum nonlinearity of 108, average SAC of 0.4998, BIC nonlinearity of 103.71, differential uniformity of 10, and linear probability of 0.0625. NIST SP 800-22 randomness evaluation over 1000 trials further confirms the statistical quality of the proposed quantum seed generator. Unlike conventional static designs such as the AES S-box, OTQuSbox introduces instance-level structural diversity while preserving deterministic validation and practical efficiency, making it a promising candidate for adaptive and security-critical cryptographic applications.

title: Characterization of Parasitic Impacts on the Effective Bandwidth of VSCC AEF
authors: Tahmid Ibne Mannan, Subarto Kumar Ghosh, Seungdeog Choi, Masoud Karimi-Ghartemani
abstract: This paper comprehensively investigates the impact of the parasitic parameters (equivalent series inductance (ESL)) of injection capacitors on the effective bandwidth of active EMI filter (AEF) with feedback voltage-sensing current-cancellation (FB VSCC) topology. VSCC AEF has been widely used due to its attractive implementation features with limited RC components, reducing bulky and heavy passive filters while providing accurate sensing and precise noise cancellations. However, its performance can be significantly degraded by its injection capacitors' ESL. In higher voltage applications with non-negligible ESLs, this can significantly compromise the noise attenuation bandwidth of AEF. To address these concerns, this paper provides a detailed analysis of the impact of ESL on FB-VSCC AEF. Specifically, a comparative study is done on (i) insertion loss, (ii) loop gain stability, and (iii) common-mode (CM) EMI mitigation capabilities by utilizing diverse capacitors with different ESL. Theoretical wideband frequency responses are mathematically derived and characterized, and ESL's high-frequency impacts are analyzed at frequency breakpoints to better understand insertion loss and loop gain stability changes clearly. Parasitic impact's persistence against different AEF design parameter variations is analyzed, along with experimentally validated implications on hybrid EMI filter (HEF) design. Experimental results show consistent results with a significant degradation in insertion loss (5∼9 dB) and phase margin (16°), while CM EMI increases 5-8 dB with higher ESL.

title: Heteroscedastic Diffusion for Multi-Agent Trajectory Modeling
authors: Guillem Capellera, Antonio Rubio, Luis Ferraz, Antonio Agudo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multi-agent trajectory modeling traditionally focuses on forecasting, often neglecting more general tasks like trajectory completion, which is essential for real-world applications such as correcting tracking data. Existing methods also generally predict agents' states without offering any state-wise measure of heteroscedastic uncertainty. Moreover, popular multi-modal sampling methods lack error probability estimates for each generated scene under the same prior observations, which makes it difficult to rank the predictions at inference time. We introduce U2Diffine, a unified diffusion model built to perform trajectory completion while simultaneously offering state-wise heteroscedastic uncertainty estimates. This is achieved by augmenting the standard denoising loss with the negative log-likelihood of the predicted noise, and then propagating the latent space uncertainty to the real state space using a first-order Taylor approximation. We also propose U2Diff, a faster baseline that avoids gradient computation during sampling. This approach significantly increases inference speed, making it as efficient as a standard generative-only diffusion model. For post-processing, we integrate a Rank Neural Network (RankNN) that enables error probability estimation for each generated mode, demonstrating strong correlation with ground truth errors. Our method outperforms state-of-the-art solutions in both trajectory completion and forecasting across four challenging sports datasets (NBA, Basketball-U, Football-U, Soccer-U), underscoring the effectiveness of our uncertainty and error probability estimation.

title: Unlocking Shared Spectrum for Satellite- Terrestrial Integration: A System-Level Perspective
authors: Xinyi Huang, Shi He, Bodong Shang
abstract: The integration of satellite and terrestrial networks promises ubiquitous connectivity but exacerbates spectrum scarcity, underscoring the need for more intelligent spectrum sharing (SS) strategies. However, the dynamic nature of non-terrestrial networks (NTNs), characterized by satellite mobility and spatial irregularity relative to any point on the ground, poses new challenges to the design and evaluation of SS schemes. This article advocates a system-level perspective grounded in stochastic geometry (SG) to address these challenges. Using SG as a system-level analytical tool, this article explains how it enables the design and assessment of spectrum access policies and interference mitigation mechanisms in satellite-terrestrial integrated networks (STINs). Specifically, we discuss two representative spectrum access policies: protection zone (PZ)-based exclusion and spatial spectrum sensing (SSS)-based opportunistic access, and examine how coordinated beamforming (CBF) operates as an interference mitigation mechanism within shared-spectrum frameworks. A case study further demonstrates the power of SG-driven modeling for scalable, efficient SS deployment. This article aims to provide researchers and practitioners with analytical insights and practical tools to unlock the full potential of shared spectrum in future STINs.

title: Transmissive Programmable Metasurface Based on Multi-Mode Resonance with a Single Liquid Crystal Layer_supp1-3691065.pdf
authors: Fan-Yi Meng
abstract: To address the limitations of liquid crystal (LC)-based transmissive programmable metasurfaces in the microwave bands—specifically restricted phase tuning ranges and excessive LC layer thicknesses—this work proposes a novel via-free transmissive LC unit cell and metasurface design based on multi-mode resonance theory (MRT). By integrating four scaled LC resonant structures within a single unit-cell plane, the design leverages multi-modal coupling to achieve a continuous transmissive phase shift exceeding 360° with an LC layer of only 9 μm, effectively overcoming the phase-range deficiency inherent in LC transmissive elements. This architecture is highly compatible with existing LC fabrication processes and mitigates the risk of LC leakage. Experimental results demonstrate that a 42×42 elements one-dimensional transmissive array based on the proposed unit cell achieves a wide-angle beam scanning capability of up to 70° from 23.6 GHz to 24.9 GHz, exhibiting excellent beam reconfigurability. This study proposes a novel design paradigm for realizing low-cost, high-performance continuously tunable microwave transmissive metasurfaces, which hold broad application prospects in future communications, high-resolution imaging, and electronic countermeasures.

title: Exploring YOLO Architectures for Radar-Based Flying Object Classification Using Micro-Doppler Signatures
authors: Megha Kataria, Brejesh Lall
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: The increasing use of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) across civilian and defense applications has created a need for reliable, real-time drone classification methods. Radar-based micro-Doppler signatures provide distinctive motion patterns that enable effective target identification under various environmental conditions. While deep learning has been applied to radar spectrograms, most studies use conventional classifiers that lose spatial detail critical for interpreting micro-Doppler features. This article presents the first systematic benchmarking of the You Only Look Once (YOLO) family (YOLOv1–YOLOv8) for radar-based UAV classification. Each model was adapted from its detection architecture by redesigning the detection head for classification and trained using the DIAT–μSAT dataset containing six aerial target classes. Models were evaluated across multiple metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, model size, and inference speed. Grad-CAM visualization was used to analyze spatial attention across YOLO versions. Results show that YOLOv8 achieves the best balance between accuracy (93.1%) and efficiency, outperforming both earlier YOLO models and standard CNN-based classifiers. The study establishes a unified benchmark for YOLO architectures on radar data and demonstrates their potential for efficient real-time radar classification.

title: Building an Open Source Operational Technology Pentesting Platform: Lessons From LINICS
authors: Awais Rashid, Joseph Gardiner, Louise Evans
abstract: Information technology security professionals have access to open source platforms such as Kali Linux. But no equivalent exists for operational technology (OT). We discuss architecting, building, and releasing LINICS (Linux for ICS), an OT pentesting and security analysis open source platform.

title: From Automated to Autonomous: Hierarchical Agent-native Network Architecture (HANA)
authors: Binghan Wu, Shoufeng Wang, Yunxin Liu, Ya-Qin Zhang, Joseph Sifakis, Ye Ouyang
abstract: Realizing Level 4/5 Autonomous Networks (AN) demands a shift from static automation to agent-native intelligence. Current operations, reliant on rigid scripts, lack the cognitive agency to handle off-nominal conditions. To address this, this letter proposes a hierarchical multi-agent reference architecture enabling high-level autonomy. The framework features a Dual-Driven Orchestrator that coordinates specialized Executive Agents, supported by a shared Public Memory for unified domain knowledge. A key innovation is the integration of agent self-awareness, which empowers the system to harmonize deliberative strategic governance with reflexive fault recovery. We instantiate and validate this architecture within a 5G Core environment. Case studies demonstrate that the system sustains critical throughput under congestion and reduces Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) by 86%, confirming its efficacy in unifying strategic planning with operational resilience.

title: A Two-Degree-of-Freedom DPWM Strategy for Loss-Harmonic Collaborative Optimization in Three-Phase VSCs
authors: Haizhou Ying, Ye Tao, Chenxin Dong, Xinchen Chen, Shuolin Zhang
abstract: With the trend toward miniaturization and high integration in urban distribution networks, the thermal management of three-phase voltage-source converter (VSC) faces severe challenges due to high switching frequency and power loss-driven junction temperature (Tj) fluctuations. Traditional discontinuous pulse width modulation (DPWM) strategies often suffer from a misalignment between clamping windows and peak current regions under non-unity power factor conditions, leading to suboptimal cooling performance. This paper proposes an improved DPWM strategy with two-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) adjustment characteristics. By introducing the clamping width angle ψ and the offset angle δ as independent control variables, the proposed strategy enables flexible reshaping of the power device commutation logic. An adaptive phase compensation mechanism is established to ensure the clamping window tracks the load current peaks in real-time, effectively maximizing switching loss suppression. Furthermore, the variable ψ provides a quantitative trade-off between conversion efficiency and total harmonic distortion (THD). The proposed algorithm is based on instantaneous logic judgment with low computational overhead, making it suitable for digital control platforms. Simulation results obtained from the PLECS electro-thermal coupling platform verify that the proposed strategy reduces the average junction temperature by approximately 10.3°C compared to traditional 30° DPWM while maintaining a low THD of 3.08%. The research demonstrates that the 2-DOF regulation mechanism successfully achieves collaborative optimization of thermal reliability and power quality.

title:
Hybrid Physics-Data Driven Approach for Pressure Estimation in CO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    -Rich Gas Injection Operations
authors: Mateus A. Fernandes, Eduardo Gildin, Marcio A. Sampaio
abstract: Reliable monitoring of bottom-hole pressure (BHP) in gas injection wells is essential for reservoir management, operational safety, and the success of Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) and Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage (CCUS) projects. Particularly in offshore oilfields where the carbon dioxide content in the production is relevant, its reinjection mixed with hydrocarbon gases presents unique difficulties arising from the complex physical behavior, since flow often occurs in supercritical state. Additionally, measurements from permanent downhole gauges (PDGs) may be absent. Traditional mechanistic models and purely data-driven machine learning (ML) approaches each have limitations in handling the nonlinearities and uncertainties inherent in these systems. In this work, we address these limitations by developing a Physics-Guided Neural Network (PGNN) that combines first-principles modeling with supervised learning through a hybrid loss formulation. The methodology is validated using a comprehensive dataset from an offshore oilfield in the Brazilian Pre-salt, encompassing multiple injection cycles and varying gas compositions. Results demonstrate that the hybrid approach achieves robust BHP estimation, with error metrics around 1%, outperforming both conventional physical models and standard neural networks, especially when dealing with limited or noisy datasets. The proposed virtual sensor solution enables reliable BHP monitoring in injector wells, minimizing operational expenditures and supporting safe, efficient, and sustainable reservoir management and CO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> storage.

title: A Guided Safe Reinforcement Learning Framework for Adaptive Energy Management in Green Hydrogen Production Systems
authors: Lupembe N. Mukena, T. Wanjekeche, Andreas T. Ndapuka
abstract: The rapid expansion of variable renewable energy (VRE) presents both opportunities and challenges for sustainable energy systems. Green hydrogen is emerging as a key solution for large-scale storage and decarbonization of hard-to-electrify sectors. However, direct coupling of electrolyzers to VRE introduces operational volatility, accelerated degradation, and complex dispatch challenges that conventional rule-based and optimization-based methods cannot fully address. This paper proposes a Guided Safe Reinforcement Learning framework for adaptive energy management in green hydrogen systems. The method integrates Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) with a deterministic two-branch safety shield that ensures feasibility at execution time by construction, regardless of the action proposed by the policy, guaranteeing that battery state-of-charge limits and electrolyzer operating mode constraints are satisfied at every timestep. The framework further incorporates an intervention-aware corrective reward signal which converts each shield event into a structured learning signal and a Self-Guided Memory (SGM) mechanism that replays shield-corrected feasible transitions to bias the policy toward constraint-satisfying regions, causing shield intervention frequency to self-reduce as the policy improves. The framework is validated on a HOMER-sized green hydrogen system using year-long renewable profiles of Purros area in Namibia. Under identical safety enforcement and across four independent random seeds, the proposed guided agent achieves a 3.5% increase in hydrogen production (p = 0.003) and a 29.2% reduction in electrolyzer cycling cost (p = 0.007) relative to a shielded SAC baseline, with additional reductions in renewable curtailment (12.9%) and battery degradation cost (12.1%). Structural analysis further shows reduced ramp volatility, with mean electrolyzer ramp magnitude decreasing from 1.87 MW to 1.62 MW (14% reduction) and lower ramp dispersion. The guided agent also reduces the safety-shield intervention rate from 17.7% to 10.9%, indicating improved safety autonomy.

title: Smart Defense: Artificial Intelligence for Financial Cybersecurity
authors: Zoltan Rajnai, Yue Wu, Andrea Tick, Aniko Horvath-Kiss, Beatrix Fregan, Attila Nagy
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: One of the most important challenges of our time is the cybersecurity of the online space, which is also a key issue of the information society and technology. This security challenge is particularly important in the banking sector for both the company and its customers. Artificial intelligence (AI) and automated security solutions can provide significant assistance in implementing information security, fighting fraud and preventing cyberattacks.

title: DrumBuddy: A MIDI-Based Drum Transcription and Practice System for Electronic Drums
authors: Balázs Szabó, Ádám Pintér
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Practising drums efficiently requires accurate rhythmic execution, structured notation, and continuous feedback on timing and consistency. Manual transcription of drum performances is time-consuming and distracts musicians from focusing on playing. This paper presents DrumBuddy, a software system that converts drumming performed on electronic drum kits into structured digital drum notation in real time using MIDI input. The system is built around a desktop client-server architecture: the client application connects to an electronic drum kit via a MIDI interface, processes incoming events, and renders drum notation, while a backend service provides user management, sheet storage, and synchronisation through a REST API. DrumBuddy supports both automatic recording and manual editing of drum sheets, as well as comparison between multiple takes of the same exercise, enabling drummers to identify and correct mistakes. The paper discusses the theoretical background of drum notation and MIDI, presents the architecture and implementation of the system, and reports on testing activities and potential directions for future work.

title: S $^{2}$ VG: 3D Stereoscopic and Spatial Video Generation via Denoising Frame Matrix
authors: Peng Dai, Feitong Tan, Qiangeng Xu, Yihua Huang, David Futschik, Ruofei Du, Sean Fanello, Yinda Zhang, Xiaojuan Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: While video generation models excel at producing high-quality monocular videos, generating 3D stereoscopic and spatial videos for immersive applications remains an underexplored challenge. We present a pose-free and training-free method that leverages an off-the-shelf monocular video generation model to produce immersive 3D videos. Our approach first warps the generated monocular video into pre-defined camera viewpoints using estimated depth information, then applies a novel frame matrix inpainting framework. This framework utilizes the original video generation model to synthesize missing content across different viewpoints and timestamps, ensuring spatial and temporal consistency without requiring additional model fine-tuning. Moreover, we develop a disocclusion boundary re-injection scheme that further improves the quality of video inpainting by alleviating the negative effects propagated from disoccluded areas in the latent space. The resulting multi-view videos are then adapted into stereoscopic pairs or optimized into 4D Gaussians for spatial video synthesis. We validate the efficacy of our proposed method by conducting experiments on videos from various generative models, such as Sora, Lumiere, WALT, and Zeroscope. The experiments demonstrate that our method has a significant improvement over previous methods. Project page at: https://daipengwa.github.io/S-2VG_ProjectPage/

title: Optimal Quantum ( r , δ)-Locally Repairable Codes From Matrix-Product Codes
authors: Meng Cao, Kun Zhou
abstract: Classical locally repairable codes (LRCs) are essential for efficient data recovery in large-scale distributed storage systems. Quantum LRCs, as the quantum counterparts of classical LRCs, have recently attracted increasing attention due to their potential applications in quantum data storage. This paper aims to investigate optimal pure quantum (r, δ)-LRCs using matrix-product (MP) codes. We begin by establishing a general method to construct optimal classical (r, δ)-LRCs from MP codes. Then, we develop an effective method to derive optimal classical (r, δ)-LRCs from MP codes with nested constituent codes. We establish a necessary and sufficient condition under which an MP code with nested constituent codes induces an optimal pure quantum (r; δ)-LRC. We also offer a feasible approach to construct optimal pure quantum (r, δ)-LRCs from MP codes with non-nested constituent codes. We present three new infinite families of optimal classical (r, δ)-LRCs involving the Hermitian or Euclidean duality relation, which serve as the constituent codes of MP codes to produce optimal pure quantum (r, δ)-LRCs. Finally, we construct five new infinite families of optimal pure quantum (r, δ)-LRCs via Hermitian and Euclidean dual-containing MP codes. Notably, the constituent codes in these MP codes include both nested and non-nested cases. Compared with known results, our constructions yield optimal classical (r, δ)-LRCs and optimal pure quantum (r, δ)-LRCs that not only have flexible parameters but also present new characterizations of the Hermitian and Euclidean dual-containing pairs.

title: Toward Smart Laboratories: A Robotic Gripper Integrating MSSPP Sensing for Simultaneous Grasping Monitoring and Material Identification
authors: Yongze Li, Shuhao Zhang, Xuan Li, Kuihan Chen, Zhiqiang Lan, Minglu Zhu, Tao Chen
abstract: The operation in high-risk laboratory environments, such as biochemistry and materials synthesis, often exposes humans to various hazardous substances. Alternatively, intelligent robots with multidimensional tactile sensors are frequently studied. However, it remains challenging to offer the capability of material identification, while maintaining the tactile sensing within the same compact device. In this study, we developed an intelligent perception system integrated into a robotic gripper with a magneto-spoof surface plasmon polariton (MSSPP) sensor. This system simultaneously performs grasping monitoring and material identification. It enables real-time force sensing via a silicone converter and phase-sensitive detection, and realizes noncontact identification of materials in ten types of centrifuge tubes by analyzing microwave dielectric responses within the 4.78∼4.94 GHz band. Machine learning algorithms are also applied to assist classification. Experimental results demonstrate its high sensitivity to both mechanical and dielectric parameters. The proposed sensor offers a multimodal, compact, and cross-media sensing technique for intelligent robots and robotic teleoperation in high-risk laboratory environments.

title: Calibration-Target-Assisted Multistatic Localization and Synchronization: A Computationally Efficient Multi-Stage Solution Using TD Measurements
authors: Qinke Qi, Youming Li, Xiang Yan, Guilu Li, Wen Liu
abstract: This letter presents a multi-stage solution to the calibration-target-assisted multistatic localization problem based on time delay (TD) measurements, in the presence of unknown receiver clock drifts and offsets caused by clock imperfections. In the first stage, the common clock offset is mitigated by differencing the direct and indirect-path TD measurements from the calibration target, providing a weighted least squares (WLS) estimate of the clock drift. In the second stage, the previously obtained drift estimate is used along with the direct-path measurements to produce the closed-form estimate of the transformed clock offset. In the third stage, localization is achieved by solving a constrained WLS problem constructed using these estimates together with the indirect-path TD measurements related to the unknown target. Simulations confirm that, in the small-noise/large-receiver regime, the resulting target position estimate approaches the Cramèr–Rao lower bound (CRLB) corresponding to the joint estimator that utilizes the complete measurement set.

title: Efficiency Optimization Control of a Parallel Battery Energy Storage System with Dual DC-Bus Partial Power Conversion
authors: Chao Fu, Hanqing Liu, Yuwei Sun, Qing Guo, Yanxiang Li, Long Ma
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: To optimize the efficiency of the dual DC bus partial-power-paralleled battery energy storage system, this paper analyzes the efficiency characteristics and high-efficiency operating region of the topology, and proposes an efficiency optimization control method. Specifically, the efficiency characteristics are first analyzed according to the operating principles of the topology. Current-stress and RMS current models are then developed for both the bus voltage control unit (BVCU) and the balancing unit (BU), based on which the system’s high-efficiency operating region is derived. By combining the efficiency characteristics with the identified high-efficiency operating region, an automatic system-efficiency optimization algorithm based on dc-bus voltage regulation is proposed. Moreover, an indirect system-efficiency evaluation approach is adopted, which does not require any additional sensors or hardware cost. Finally, experimental results demonstrate that the proposed efficiency optimization control strategy effectively improves the operating efficiency of the parallel battery energy storage system without introducing extra cost, while having no adverse impact on system operational stability.

title: LLTrack: Parallel Association Framework for 2D Multi-Object Tracking
authors: Jiacheng Liu, Huihuang Zhao, Guangyong Zheng, Zhong Chen, Xinxin Li
abstract: Multi-object tracking (MOT) is a fundamental task in computer vision and remains challenging in crowded scenes with frequent occlusions, target deformation, and ambiguous inter-object similarity. Existing tracking-by-detection methods usually rely on either a single association cue or a serial combination of motion and appearance cues, which can underuse their complementary strengths when association conflicts occur. In this paper, we present LLTrack, a 2D multi-object tracking framework that improves the association stage through a parallel matching and conflict-resolution mechanism. Specifically, motion-based and appearance-based associations are computed in parallel, and conflicting matches are resolved according to a motion-level reliability indicator. In addition, we incorporate a lightweight attention-based appearance module (ABM) to improve the discriminability of re-identification features, and we use an auxiliary loss design to stabilize training. Experiments on MOT17, MOT20, and DanceTrack demonstrate the competitive performance of LLTrack. These results indicate that the proposed framework is effective for 2D MOT benchmarks with diverse motion patterns, while its extension to broader intelligent vision systems is left for future study.The source code is available at https://github.com/ljc4336/LLTrack.

title: Scalable Graph Clustering via Structure-Attribute Fusion and Linearized Global Attention
authors: Zheng Huang, Shixin Yao, You Huang, Yong Zhou, Tianjun Wu, Hengzhu Yi, Yuan Cao
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Graph clustering has emerged as a fundamental task in network analysis. Despite recent advances in GNN for graph representation learning, yet existing methods face two critical limitations. First, current approaches inadequately integrate structural information with node attributes, leading to suboptimal cluster representations. Second, capturing global structural patterns incurs prohibitive computational complexity, particularly for large-scale graphs. To address these challenges, we propose a novel scalable graph clustering framework via structure-attribute fusion and linearized global attention (SAFLGA). Our method introduces a comprehensive structureattribute fusion mechanism that effectively combines topological information with node features through multi-level integration. Furthermore, we design a linear-complexity self-attention mechanism that efficiently captures long-range dependencies while maintaining computational tractability. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves superior clustering performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, while significantly reducing computational overhead.

title: Spatial-Temporal Unsupervised Background Reconstruction Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Zhangteng Ma, Xiaorun Li, Shuhan Chen
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: With the rapid development of deep learning(DL) techniques, various infrared(IR) small target detection methods have been proposed. However, many existing networks mainly rely on supervised learning for single-frame detection, which not only incurs heavy labeling costs but also ignores abundant spatial-temporal information between infrared sequences. In this paper, we develop a spatial-temporal unsupervised background reconstruction network (ST-UBRNet) for infrared small target detection. Specifically, adjacent infrared frames are first organized into 3-D spatial-temporal tensors as the data foundation. Based on the designed multi-scale 3D spatial feature extraction module, the network effectively captures local details and contextual background information under different receptive fields. Then, a spatially deformable 3D residual enhancement module is introduced to improve the representation of irregular local structures. After that, a temporal aggregation module compresses the extracted 3-D features into a compact 2-D representation, followed by a reconstruction branch to generate the background image for the intermediate frame. Finally, the residual map between the original image and the reconstructed background is computed to obtain a target map. By focusing on unsupervised background modeling rather than direct supervised target learning, the proposed method avoids expensive pixellevel annotations and provides an effective solution for infrared small target detection. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves satisfactory target enhancement and background suppression, showing promising performance in complex infrared scenarios.

title: Roll-to-roll fabrication of superconducting flexible shielded high-density interconnects for scalable quantum computing
authors: Elias Meltzer, Martijn Goedbloed, Jargal Hepp, Carla Morán Guizán, Manish Pandey, Johannes Weber, Daniela Zahn, Maryam Faghih, Rui Pereira, Christoph Kutter
publish: 2025 Smart Systems Integration Conference and Exhibition (SSI)
abstract: A high density wiring solution is indispensable for the scaling up of superconducting qubit-based quantum computers. With increasing numbers of qubits, implementing conventional coax cables with SMA connectors inside a cryostat is no longer feasible. Here we propose the roll-to-roll manufacturing route for a thin and flexible high-density interconnect solution based on niobium and polyimide together with a PCB-direct connector. The feasibility of this process is demonstrated by fabrication of 150 mm long flexible cables, featuring 16 shielded transmission lines which become superconducting below 6 K.

title: Leaky Coaxial Cable Testbed for Indoor Wireless Communication
authors: Hefdhallah Sakran, Charbel Lahoud, Klaus Mößner
abstract: Leaky coaxial cable (LCX) is one of the enablers that support efficient wireless communication in confined areas such as indoor spaces and tunnels, where its radiation characteristics offer significant advantages over a point antenna. Our study outlines the technical requirements for implementing 5G over LCX, encompassing critical factors such as a physical layer (PHY) key performance indicators (KPIs), reliability, coverage, and specific usage scenarios. Measurements were conducted at 3.7 GHz using the OpenAirInterface Software Alliance’s 5G NR core network (OAI 5G CN), gNB, and software-defined radio (SDR) at the Chemnitz University of Technology in Germany. We propose a path loss model to improve its applicability to LCX-based systems, providing valuable insights into the propagation characteristics and potential challenges of integrating 5G with LCX in a realistic indoor environment.

title: Airborne Software Requirements Generation Method Based on Memory-Enhanced Graph RAG
authors: Xinyu Hong, Linjuan Fu, Yige Zhao, Wei Chen, Songran Qiao, Xiaoliang Xu
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Requirements generation plays a critical role in the aerospace field. However, this process has long relied on manual effort by domain experts, leading to issues such as prolonged iteration cycles and human error. While the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) method can effectively generate requirements by integrating domain documents, it often fails to incorporate the crucial expert experience inherent to requirements engineering. To address this gap and the prevalent decoupling of documentbased RAG from expert experience, this paper proposes a memory-enhanced Graph RAG method for airborne software requirements generation. The method integrates a memory system to capture and utilize expert feedback. Specifically, the method first preprocesses documents to extract triples and construct a knowledge graph. It then extracts key information via expert interaction and employs a shortest-path-priority retrieval strategy to obtain relevant content for generation. Finally, based on expert feedback, it extracts new triples, performs node merging and establishes relationships to update the knowledge graph, thereby capturing expert experience. This research enhances the quality of generated requirements by integrating Graph RAG with memory systems.

title: Incorporation of Anti-Reflective Structures into a 3D Printed Antenna-Lens System
authors: Frederik Eberhard, Frederike Bartels, Alexander Koelpin
abstract: This paper introduces an approach for increasing the aperture efficiency of a high-gain antenna-lens system. Different anti-reflective (AR) impedance matching layers (IMLs) designed for 3D printing are added to the lens and evaluated based on the reduction of reflections and improvement in gain. The chosen system ensures a reflection coefficient below -20dB and increases the gain by 0.44 dB in simulation. After additively manufacturing the system and metal coating, measurements validate the simulation results.

title: Synchronization Method for High-Data-Rate Communication Using Phase-Coded FMCW in Incoherent Joint Sensing and Communication Systems
authors: Mohamad Alami El Dine, Patrick Fenske, Tobias Kögel, Martin Vossiek
abstract: Phase-coded frequency-modulated continuous wave (PC-FMCW) signals have emerged as a promising waveform, enabling high-performance radar sensing and efficient communication in joint radar and communication (RadCom) systems. However, achieving reliable high-data-rate communication in such systems requires robust synchronization between inherently incoherent RadCom units. This paper introduces a novel synchronization method tailored for incoherent PC-FMCW communication links, leveraging a Costas-loop-based synchronization architecture. The proposed approach is validated through comprehensive simulations and experimental measurements conducted at 25 GHz, which demonstrate its effectiveness for high-rate data transmission in next-generation RadCom systems.

title: Performance Evaluation of a Hybrid DSP–Machine Learning Steering-Model Using Radar Data in Single-Target Scenarios
authors: Cândido Vieira, Hans-Peter Beise, Volker Lücken, Andreas R. Diewald
abstract: We present a comparative analysis of the Steering-Model, a hybrid DSP–Machine Learning approach for Direction-of-Arrival (DoA) estimation, against the FFT-based spatial-spectrum technique and the Multiple Signal Classification (MUSIC) algorithm. FFT-based methods provide computational efficiency, yet their resolution is constrained by finite aperture and spectral leakage. In contrast, MUSIC achieves super-resolution through eigenvalue decomposition and subspace separation, but incurs high computational cost and requires accurate source-count estimation. The proposed Steering-Model introduces a learnable Steering-Layer that replaces fixed subspace separation and steering vectors with trainable real- and complex-valued weights, formulating DoA estimation as a multi-label classification task over a predefined angular grid. The architecture comprises 52,152 parameters (305 KB), employs the Wasserstein distance as the loss function, and eliminates eigenvalue decomposition and source-count estimation requirements. Evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrates that the Steering-Model achieves sharper spectral peak resolution than FFT-based methods across various SNR levels and reduces computational runtime by up to 80% compared to MUSIC. Notably, although trained exclusively on single-target synthetic data, the model generalizes to real radar data and multi-target scenarios, resolving sources with angular separations as small as 10° in a 12-receiver array under moderate SNR conditions. The Steering-Model offers a practical alternative that balances computational efficiency and angular resolution, suitable for embedded implementations in sensor networks.

title: Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis via Broad Learning with Pyramid Residual Attention from FDG-PET Images
authors: Yuhao Qiu, Junwei Duan
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a common neurodegenerative disorder, and its early diagnosis is of great significance to slow disease progression and formulate effective treatment strategies. Positron Emission Tomography (PET) can reflect changes in brain metabolic activity and provides important imaging evidence for the early detection of AD. However, due to subtle metabolic differences between AD patients and cognitively normal (CN) subjects, traditional methods still have limitations in feature extraction and discrimination. To address this issue, this paper proposes an Alzheimer's disease diagnosis model based on attention mechanisms and a Broad Learning System, termed BLAAD-Net (Broad Learning with Attention for Alzheimer's Diagnosis Network). The model first uses ResNet to extract deep features from PET images, then applies a pyramid residual attention mechanism for multi-scale feature enhancement, highlighting key brain regions. Finally, fused features are input to a Broad Learning System for efficient classification. The Results obtained from the ADNI PET data indicate that BLAAD-Net achieves superior performance compared to traditional convolutional models and individual classifiers, thus verifying the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed approach for early AD detection.

title: Centralized Protection in Distribution Substations: Testing Modular Relays with Conventional CT, VT Inputs and Process Bus Architecture
authors: Juan Santillan, Eric Curiel, Alexandr Stinskiy, Evandro de Oliveira
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: The increasing demand for electrical energy and the integration of distributed energy resources (DERs) are driving the need for new substation deployments and equipment upgrades to maintain safe and reliable service. Minimizing the physical footprint of distribution substations has become increasingly important and can be achieved through modular design in both primary switchgear and protection and control systems. Modular or centralized protection relays, capable of protecting multiple assets within a single device, represent a key element of this approach.This paper presents a case study on the implementation and testing of Centralized Protection and Control (CPC) relays for a distribution substation. The project was conducted in two phases. The first phase evaluated a CPC relay equipped with nine sets of three-phase CT inputs and one set of VT inputs, configured to protect a 69 kV transformer bank, a medium-voltage busbar, and two 12 kV feeders. The second phase tested relay with process bus capabilities, subscribing to IEC 61850-9-2LE sampled value streams from nine merging units for the same substation configuration.Extensive laboratory testing was performed to assess relay performance under a wide range of abnormal and fault conditions. Relay and Merging Unit equipment from different vendors was included to validate interoperability. Internal relay logic was engineered to comply with well-defined Southern California Edison (SCE) protection standards.Authors discuss a comparative performance assessment between relays with conventional CT/VT inputs and IED receiving IEC 61850-9-2LE SMVs from the Merging Units. The results show that while conventional inputs provide proven reliability, the process bus configuration significantly reduces wiring complexity, improves scalability, and deliver comparable protection performance when proper time synchronization is ensured.
