                    Sampling, the Forget Model, and Beyond
authors: Honghao Lin, Hoai-An Nguyen, William Swartworth, David P. Woodruff
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: Lower Bounds on Conversion Bandwidth for MDS Convertible Codes in Split Regime
authors: Lewen Wang, Sihuang Hu
abstract: We propose several new lower bounds on the bandwidth cost of MDS convertible codes using a linear-algebraic framework. The derived bounds improve previous results in certain parameter regimes and match the bandwidth cost of the construction proposed by Maturana and Rashmi (2022 IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory) for r<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">F</sup>≤r<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">I</sup>≤k<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">F</sup>, implying that our bounds are tight in this case.

title: UAV Carrier Enabled Vehicular Crowdsensing by Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning with Mutual Policy Divergence and Attentive Memory Update
authors: Qiran Zhao, Chi Harold Liu, Jianxin Zhao, Guozheng Li, Guangpeng Qi, Xu Ji, Duo Xu, Jon Crowcroft
abstract: Vehicular Crowdsensing (VCS) has emerged as a promising paradigm that leverages the complementary strengths of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) for large-scale urban sensing and data collection. In this paper, we consider a UAV-carrier-enabled VCS campaign in which UGVs dynamically dispatch and recall UAVs within the workzone, where UAVs sense points of interest (PoIs) and UGVs facilitate data collection, with the goal of maximizing the total collected data volume and geographic fairness, while minimizing overall energy consumption. We propose a heterogeneous multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) framework, called “HADRL-VCS”, consisting of an attentive memory-integrated information exchange mechanism that enables UAVs and UGVs to fuse newly received information with historical memory, thereby expanding the collective sensing range and enhancing cooperative decision-making. We also propose a mutual policy divergence-driven exploration strategy designed to explicitly promote diverse exploration and complementary role differentiation among heterogeneous UAVs and UGVs. Extensive experimental results based on realistic simulations using real-world urban maps from Guangzhou, China, and Madrid, Spain, show that HADRL-VCS achieves better performance over five baselines in terms of data collection ratio, geographic fairness, sensing range expansion ratio, overlap ratio, and efficiency.

title: You, Me or Us? A Co-Creative System for Pattern Design Integrating Design Principles and Adaptive Agency
authors: Joana Rovira Martins, João Miguel Cunha, Ana Boavida, Pedro Martins
abstract: Design principles provide a foundation for creating coherent and comprehensible Graphic Design solutions by offering a visual vocabulary to structure ideas. Reflecting on these principles during the creative process fosters deeper understanding and enhances creative thinking. This paper presents (1) a novel human-computer co-creative system that integrates (2) a real-time visualisation of design principles to guide graphic pattern development, and (3) an interaction model supporting varying types of creativity and levels of autonomy. This model enables users to interact as autonomous creators, co-creators sharing tasks, or evaluators guiding the agent, thus allowing them to adapt the process to their creative needs while reflecting on the principles' impact on pattern creation. To evaluate the interaction dynamics and user experience of a principle-driven workflow, we conducted a mixed-methods user study with 30 participants, considering aspects such as the use of design principles and the different levels of autonomy. The results suggest that the explicit use of design principles facilitates creative exploration and expressiveness. Users stated they valued the system's ability to accommodate different creative processes and improve visual literacy in Graphic Design.

title: Optimal Design of Compact Ultra-Wideband Transmissive Linear-to-Circular Polarization Metasurfaces_supp1-3691925.docx
authors: Yan Nan Jiang
abstract: The ellipticity angle β is proposed as a circular polarization discrimination metric to overcome the inherent limitations of traditional methods involving amplitude-phase difference or axial ratio. Meanwhile, the topological structure of the high-performance linear-to-circular (LTC) polarization conversion metasurfaces (PCMs) is derived theoretically. Further, based on the topological structure and β, this study employs a physics-informed genetic algorithm for the optimal design of a compact transmissive LTC PCM featuring ultra-wideband response and easy fabrication. The simulations demonstrate that the optimized LTC PCM operates in the frequency range of 4.2- 11.3 GHz with a fractional bandwidth of 91.6%, and a mean polarization conversion efficiency of 70%, which verifies the excellent performance; the measured results are in agreement with the simulations, thus validating the proposed design method. This investigation can be applied in antenna designs, optical devices, and remote sensing systems.

title: Human-Like Multimodal Fake News Detection via Reflective Summarization and Large&#x2013;Small Model Collaboration_supp1-3685634.pdf
authors: Tengfei Liu
abstract: While multimodal fake news detection methods have made progress in aligning multimodal semantics, they still face significant challenges in analyzing background context, emotional tone, and the overall plausibility of news content. To address these limitations, we propose a novel human-like collaborative framework for multimodal fake news detection, which integrates large and small models. Specifically, we exploit large vision–language models (LVLMs) to perform deep semantic analysis and reflective summarization of news cues. By leveraging the contextual understanding, knowledge recall, and logical reasoning capabilities of large models, the proposed approach improves the accuracy and reliability of fake news detection. It comprises three key components: 1) designing a chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting strategy for the LVLM to analyze news content, including evaluating image credibility, identifying potential tampering, extracting linguistic styles, detecting emotional tones, uncovering logical connections within the text, and verifying factual accuracy; 2) independently reflecting on and summarizing the lengthy analytical outputs from both image and text modalities to reduce redundancy. The resulting summary is then encoded into compact representations using pretrained text encoders and integrated with the original multimodal features; and 3) proposing a progressive fusion mechanism that enables collaboration between large and small models, allowing effective utilization of deeply fused features at the surface level. Extensive experiments conducted on three benchmark multimodal fake news datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in multimodal fake news detection tasks. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/xxx</uri>

title: Kalman Filter-Based Optimization of UWB Ranging and Its Application in AGV Following Systems
authors: Jie Liu, Min Nian
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: To address high noise and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) interference in UWB ranging, this paper proposes a Kalman filterbased optimization for distance and angle data, applied to an AGV following system. By employing dual UWB modules and a constant velocity motion model, dedicated Kalman filters for distance and angle are designed to achieve real-time smoothing and correction of observed data. Static simulation results indicate that filtering reduces root mean square errors (RMSE) for distance and angle by 8.1 % and 12.3 %, respectively. In the dynamic simulation that follows, positioning error is reduced by 12.2 %, effectively suppressing random noise and outliers, and improving stability and accuracy. The proposed method offers low computational complexity and strong real-time performance, making it wellsuited for embedded platforms.

title: ADRAN – A Fully Digital L-Band Testbed for Multistatic Radar Sensing
authors: M. Jahangir, N. Chen, D. White, D. Healy, G. Donlan, Y. Singh, M. Antoniou
abstract: The Advanced RAdar Network (ADRAN) is a fully digital L-band testbed developed at the University of Birmingham for multistatic radar research and the evaluation of ultra-low phase-noise oscillators. The system provides element-level digitisation, flexible beamforming, and operation with a range of external reference sources, enabling controlled studies of coherence and oscillator performance across monostatic and bistatic geometries. Recent additions—a transportable transmitter and an SDR-based passive receiver—extend the available multistatic configurations. ADRAN therefore offers a versatile platform for developing synchronisation strategies, refining multistatic processing, and assessing how oscillator phase noise affects coherence and ultimately limits detection sensitivity in distributed radar systems.

title: Identifying Conductor Slap and Mitigating Impact on Distribution Systems
authors: Troy M Watkins, Cory J Hamlin, T. A. Short
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Consumers Energy investigates circuit lockouts that are identified by the line crews as “No Cause Found.” As these events were analyzed, a trend emerged. The majority of these events involved a line-to-line fault, followed shortly by a higher magnitude line-to-line fault on the same two phases. Consumers Energy concluded that these events were caused by conductor slap. Conductor slap could result in customer outages and extended outage times, as line crews patrol for a fault that is no longer on the system. Mitigation options were evaluated to find a solution that could be implemented easily across their system. Mitigation options can include structural changes or changes to protection, including clearing times and reclosing. Consumers Energy revised their reclosing standard to allow their system to recover after a conductor slap.

title: Bioinformatics-Guided NSGA-II Pathway-Sparse Optimization for Interpretable Neurodegenerative Risk Detection
authors: Attila Biró, Levente Kovács, László Szilágyi
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Early detection of neurodegenerative and cognitive disorders, such as dementia and Alzheimer's disease remains a central challenge in cyber-medical systems, where predictive models must balance diagnostic accuracy with interpretability and computational efficiency. This work introduces a bioinformatics-guided, pathway-sparse multi-objective optimization framework based on NSGA-II to produce interpretable digital biomarkers from multimodal SAPI assessments. Using the real SAPI feature matrix-including cognitive tests, gait and activity variability metrics, speech-language indicators, affective signals, sleep/circadian measures, and self-reported functional scales-features are grouped into clinically meaningful pathways (e.g., Memory-Executive Function, Orientation, Language/Speech, Sleep/Circadian, Gait/Motor Activity, Affective Symptoms, Daily Function/ADL). Each NSGA-II individual jointly encodes a binary feature mask restricted by the pathway and Random Forest hyperparameters, optimized over two competing objectives: (i) maximize the weighted F1-score for neurodegenerative screening classification, and (ii) minimize a combined gene- and pathway-level sparsity index. Experiments in the SAPI neurodegenerative cohort demonstrate that the proposed method discovers Pareto-optimal families of compact, interpretable models, achieving precision comparable to a fullfeature Random Forest baseline while reducing feature usage by more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$60-80 \%$</tex>. The proposed framework provides a generalizable and cyber-medical-ready optimization strategy for an explainable assessment of neurodegenerative risk.

title: Multi-Reading Habits Fusion and Cross-Platform Semantic Consistency Reasoning Model for Fake News Detection
authors: Weihang Wang, Shenwu Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: To address the challenge of cross-platform dissemination of fake information on social media, we propose a multimodal consistency reasoning model for cross-platform fake news detection, namely MHF-CSCR Model (Multi-Reading Habits Fusion with Cross-Platform Semantic Consistency Reasoning Model). The model comprehensively utilizes both textual and visual modal features. It introduces a Multi-Reading Habits Fusion (MHF) mechanism to simulate users' cognitive behaviors under different reading paths, while enhancing cross-platform generalization via the Cross-Platform Semantic Consistency Reasoning (SCR) module for semantic consistency analysis. Furthermore, through an adversarial learning mechanism, the model can acquire platform-independent universal features, thereby enhancing its cross-platform transferability. Experimental results on the Weibo and Twitter datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing unimodal and multimodal baseline models in multiple metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and remarkably improves the model's robustness in complex cross-platform social environments.

title: Controlled Mold Detection Using an Electronic Nose Prototype with Decision Tree and Random Forest Classification
authors: Rayhan G. Ladines, Sophia Hannah A. Gabo, Ramon G. Garcia
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Mold growth poses significant health and structural risks, necessitating early and reliable detection methods. Conventional mold identification techniques are often time-consuming and invasive, limiting their practicality for continuous monitoring. This study presents the integration of commercially available metal oxide semiconductor (MOS) gas sensors and environmental sensing modules into an electronic nose (e-nose) prototype for controlled mold detection. Laboratory experiments were conducted to collect steady-state volatile organic compound (VOC) response data from Penicillium spp., substrate-only samples, and clean-air conditions. Decision Tree and Random Forest classifiers were developed to analyze sensor response patterns, and Group Cross-Validation was employed to prevent data leakage from segmented exposure windows. The optimized Decision Tree achieved a test accuracy of 94%, while the Random Forest achieved 92% under identical evaluation conditions. The results show that MOS sensor response patterns can distinguish mold from non-mold conditions under controlled laboratory settings. These findings support the feasibility of a low-cost e-nose for preliminary controlled mold detection, although broader validation with additional mold species and real indoor environments is still needed.

title: Portable Electrochemical Platform for Point-of-Care Bilirubin Sensing for Liver Diagnostics
authors: Nishchitha N K, Wenzhou Wu, Sanket Goel
abstract: Objective: Elevated bilirubin serves as a subtle yet early indicator of liver dysfunction, highlighting the need for a rapid, accessible, and reliable point-of-care solution. To the best of our knowledge, this study presents the first portable electrochemical platform for point-of-care bilirubin sensing for liver diagnostics. The portable device features onboard power management and a customized analog front end with precise signal acquisition, conditioning, and filtering to support Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Differential Potential Voltammetry (DPV), and amperometric techniques for the electrochemical detection of any analyte. Herein, the optimised gold nanoparticles/SPCE enables effective bilirubin adsorption and subsequent oxidation at ∼0.35 V. An analyte-selective nafion–chitosan membrane suppressed interferences, achieving a 0.3 μM detection limit at pH 7.4. The on-device detection was evaluated by utilising the standard electrochemical setup, followed by high selectivity, repeatability, and reproducibility. Real-sample testing revealed ∼9.5% deviation from clinical values, confirming strong agreement with conventional methods. The on-device bilirubin detection through the amperometry technique delivers results both on-device and via a wireless user interface. In the future, the developed device can potentially detect other bioanalytes with minor modifications in terms of suitable chemistry.

title: ESCALAS: SiGe-BiCMOS RFICs with Built-In Self-Test and Analog Predistortion for Ka-, Q- and V-Band Satellite Payloads
authors: Stefan Koch, Andreas Fischer, Christian Bohn, Michael Jutzi, Lars Baumgärtner, Alexander Scharpf, Björn Klingenberg, Michael Schick, Gunter Fischer, Frank Herzel, Alexander Haag, Kateryna Smirnova, Anil Aslan, Simon Klimenko, Ahmet Cagri Ulusoy, Mathias Pius Scharpf, Burak Güven Özat, Benjamin Schoch, Ingmar Kallfass
abstract: This work presents compact SiGe-based RF-Integrated Circuits (RFICs) and individual building blocks for Ka-, Q- and V-band satellite communication (SatCom) payloads developed during the program ESCALAS sponsored by the German Aerospace Center (DLR). A digital interface enables Built-In Self-Tests (BIST) for autonomous calibration and in-orbit recalibration. Additionally, broadband analog predistortion (APD) linearizers are introduced. A Ka-band BIST was realized to tune third-order intermodulation products. Measurement results demonstrate excellent high-frequency performance, and radiation tolerance, confirming suitability of the presented RFICs for next-generation SatCom systems.

title: An Experimental Study of the Machining of Coldforming Tool Steels in an Educational Environment
authors: Béla Mészáros, Zoltán Balogh, Enikő Nagy
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: This study presents an educational methodology that places the active involvement of students at the center of real machining experiments. Mechanical engineering students investigate the milling of various tool steels while independently performing measurement protocols, recording and evaluating cutting-force and surface-roughness data, and processing the results using two-factor ANOVA. The primary aim of the experimental framework is not technological optimization, but rather the development of students measurement competencies, data-processing skills, statistical reasoning, and ability to interpret results within a real manufacturing environment. The technological insights gained during the experiments-such as the influence of tool geometry and feed per tooth on passive forces and surface roughness-serve to support the learning process and illustrate the practical relevance of data-driven engineering thinking. The presented approach demonstrates that complex, industry-relevant experiments can be effectively integrated into engineering education, contributing to the development of a modern, research-based, practice-oriented learning environment.

title: 2026 IEEE Power and Energy Conference at Illinois (PECI)
authors: N/A

title: Off-Grid SBL for Single-Snapshot UPA AoA via 1D Manifold Linearization and 2D Separable Recovery
authors: Xingyang Wang, Yuanzhi He, Zheng Dou
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: For single-snapshot and strongly dynamic scenarios, we study angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimation with uniform planar arrays (UPAs). We propose two off-grid schemes based on sparse Bayesian learning (SBL): a 1D off-grid SBL that starts from a coarse grid and applies a first-order linearization of the array manifold, jointly estimating the sparse angular spectrum and off-grid offsets within a unified framework while updating hyperparameters via the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm; and a 2D off-grid SBL two-step method that leverages UPA separability together with a direction-cosine transform to decouple the 2D angle domain into two parallel subproblems in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {(\xi,\eta)}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, achieving comparable accuracy with markedly reduced complexity. Simulations under a single snapshot, an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {8\times 8}$</tex-math></inline-formula> UPA, and 2.4 GHz show that both off-grid SBL methods outperform other single-snapshot estimators across a wide signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) range and are robust to fractional (off-grid) angles and grid mismatch. The 1D scheme excels in multi-source and high-accuracy regimes, whereas the 2D scheme converges faster and is more computationally efficient. Both typically attain the best normalized mean-squared error (NMSE) on moderately dense grids and converge within 10–15 iterations. We present the full model, derivations, complexity analysis, and system-level experiments, offering a practical and scalable solution for high-accuracy AoA estimation with single-snapshot UPAs.

title: Possible variants of a future 2-MW, 140-GHz gyrotron for the ECRH system at W7-X
authors: Emmanouil Deliprimis, Zisis C. Ioannidis, Konstantinos A. Avramidis, Stefan Illy, John Jelonnek, Manfred Thumm, Ioannis G. Tigelis
abstract: The scheduled upgrade of the Electron Cyclotron Resonance Heating (ECRH) system at the stellarator Wendelstein 7-X (W7-X) has now focused studies from the available 1.5-MW, 140-GHz gyrotron (TH1507U), towards a future 2-MW, 140-GHz gyrotron. To advance in 2-MW operation a higher-order operating mode than the TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">28,10</inf> mode used in the 1.5-MW gyrotron is required and its selection depends on various criteria. This paper explores operation with three TE mode candidates by taking into account the compatibility with the existing infrastructure at W7-X as well as the possibilities of similarity to existing successful tubes. Using either the conventional or the coaxial-cavity concept was also investigated.

title: Enhance UAV Network Resilience by Malicious Traffic Detection: A Twin Graph Encoder Approach
authors: Xuzeng Li, Tao Zhang, Jiacheng Wang, Jiangtian Nie, Jian Wang, Xuangou Wu, Zhen Han, Jiqiang Liu, Dusit Niyato, Dong In Kim
abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) networks are increasingly exposed to widespread and various network attacks due to their fully distributed nature and the limited defensive capabilities of individual devices. Existing defense strategies rely on network connectivity and UAV status information, which overlook information of network traffic. Malicious traffic detection offers a promising solution to achieve fine-grained attack detection. However, the dynamic nature and complexity of UAV networks limit the effectiveness of traditional traffic detection methods. Current approaches either fail to fully exploit the raw characteristics of traffic or do not consider the timeliness requirements of UAV networks. To address these challenges, we propose a novel twin graph encoder neural network, which can extract features of raw traffic bytes for efficient traffic detection. First, we propose a decoupled architecture for model training and inference to enable efficient detection of malicious traffic in UAV networks. Second, we propose a novel modeling method that models traffic as the co-occurrence graph and word frequency graph based on raw bytes. Then, we propose TGE-ETD, a Twin Graph Encoder for Encrypted Traffic Detection. TGE-ETD consists of a set of twin graph encoders that effectively extract intrinsic traffic features from graphs constructed from raw bytes. In addition, TGE-ETD employs a global attention pooling mechanism to effectively distinguish the feature contributions of different bytes. Finally, we conducted extensive experiments on a real UAV traffic dataset and four real-world network traffic datasets. TGE-ETD achieved an improvement of 1%-20% over the baseline methods by reducing the number of parameters by 20 times. Tested on multiple UAV hardware devices, TGE-ETD can achieve millisecond-level traffic detection.

title: Trust Is the Bottleneck
authors: Heena Rathore
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is transforming health care across diagnostic imaging, clinical decision support, and personalized medicine. However, despite rapid technical advances, trust remains the principal bottleneck limiting clinical adoption. This report synthesizes insights from the second IEEE EMBS Workshop on AI and Healthcare, which centered on the multidimensional nature of trust in medical AI systems. Through keynote demonstrations of AI-enabled ultrasound and an interdisciplinary panel discussion spanning medicine, computational oncology, defense research, and scientific publishing, the workshop examined trust as a construct grounded not only in accuracy, but also in transparency, accountability, and interpretability. Audience polling and case-based discussions revealed that public and clinical confidence in AI is conditional and context-dependent, particularly in high-stakes decision environments. The workshop highlighted key tensions between performance and explainability, emphasized the need for explicit accountability frameworks, and underscored the importance of lifecycle oversight from data provenance to post-deployment validation. As AI becomes increasingly embedded in clinical workflows, its impact will depend less on algorithmic capability alone and more on its ability to earn and sustain trust. The discussions reinforce a critical conclusion: trustworthy healthcare AI requires validation, human-centered design, interdisciplinary collaboration, and sustained governance to evolve from promising technology to a reliable clinical partner.

title: SlimFit-Gens: Towards Low Bandwidth One-on-One Video Calls on COTS Smartphones
authors: Jingzhou Zhu, Lizhi Sun, Fang Cao, Bo Yang, Peiwen Dong, Wendi Li, Yixin Xu, Hao Wu, Hao Han, Fengyuan Xu, Sheng Zhong
abstract: Mobile video calls play an essential role in our daily lives. However, in bandwidth-limited scenarios (e.g., inadequate cellular coverage, congested satellite links, and metered connections), users often experience poor quality of experience (QoE) during video calls. While recent advances in deep learning have demonstrated significant improvements in video compression over traditional methods, existing approaches are ill-suited for bidirectional video streaming on smartphones. The primary challenge lies in simultaneously achieving high video quality, computational and bandwidth efficiency, and practical usability on constrained mobile devices. In this work, we present SlimFit-Gens, the first practical video calling system for smartphones capable of delivering real-time 480p video at as low as 30 kbps. SlimFit-Gens addresses the challenge with joint algorithm and system-level optimizations. The core technique is a fine-grained model personalization design tailored for mobile video calling, enabling high-fidelity video generation at low model complexity. SlimFit-Gens achieves effective personalized adaptation through a novel two-stage personalization mechanism working upon an optimized model architecture. It also incorporates a privacy preserving, resource-efficient system design, featuring TEE-based (e.g., Confidential VM/NVIDIA Confidential Computing) fine-tuning on the server side and heterogeneity-aware inference on the device side. We implement SlimFit-Gens on four commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) smartphones with different system-on-chip (SoC) configurations and conduct extensive evaluations. Compared to prior work, SlimFit-Gens simultaneously improves generation quality with a 0.09-0.12 reduction in LPIPS and system efficiency through a 1.6-1.8× increase in video frame rate.

title: Nested Preanalyzed and Real-Time Corrected Matching Area Selection Method for Underwater Gravity-Aided Inertial Navigation
authors: Zihan Zhang, Bo Wang, Wei Xiao, Zhihong Deng, Mengyin Fu
abstract: Gravity-aided inertial navigation system (GAINS) is an effective passive navigation method, which represents significant progress in underwater navigation. The matching area selection algorithm is one of the key techniques. Distinctive from conventional methods, in this article, a nested method for matching area selection is proposed, which establishes a joint observation of gravity anomalies at the current positions and auxiliary positions to obtain more abundant observational information. Comprehensively considering factors such as sensors, gravity anomaly map, and trajectory, etc., this method measures the matching capacity from a system perspective rather than the 2-D image characteristics, and quantitatively estimates the matching biases. The possible maneuvering navigation and other changes of trajectory and attitude angles are focused on, and a preanalysis and real-time correction method is designed accordingly. Experimental results show that the proposed method can select matching area more accurately and efficiently, and makes it easier to set the matching area selection criteria than conventional algorithms.

title: Risk-Sensitive Control With Multi-Armed Bandit Adaptive Sampling
authors: Brian W. Rybicki, Jill K. Nelson
abstract: We propose a risk-sensitive (RS) control algorithm that optimizes the entropic risk measure (ERM). RS control minimizes the impact of high cost but low probability outcomes in dynamic decision making. ERM is a time-consistent, convex measure of risk with tunable sensitivity. Our ERM-sensitive method combines online adaptive multistage sampling, RS upper confidence bound action selection, and sample-based estimation of ERM. Unlike many other RS control approaches, our method is applicable to problems with both discrete and continuous state spaces, and it does not require knowledge of the underlying transition probabilities. This makes our approach particularly useful in a wide variety of RS reinforcement learning domains. We show <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$O(\log M / M)$</tex-math></inline-formula> errorbounded convergence under mild conditions and demonstrate the numerical performance on two RS benchmark problems with discrete and continuous state spaces, respectively.

title: A Two-Stage Analytical Modeling Approach for Consequent-Pole Surface Mounted Permanent Magnet Machines with Shaped Iron Poles
authors: Vahid Zamani Faradonbeh, Mohammad Alaei Faradonbeh, Ebrahim Amiri, Amir Rashidi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: This paper presents an analytical solution to simplify the complexity of the subdomain model in Consequent-Pole Surface Permanent Magnet (CP-SPM) Machines with shaped iron poles. The original two-dimensional (2-D) problem is reformulated into a two-stage process, comprising a one-dimensional (1-D) analysis followed by a simplified 2-D problem with a single boundary condition. In the first stage, the radial flux density in the air gap is calculated using the Magnetic Equivalent Circuit (MEC) for the Permanent Magnet (PM) field and the Winding Function (WF) method for the armature reaction. The effect of stator slots in the radial flux is modeled using the flux path function method, while the influence of the non-uniform air gap, caused from the shaped poles, is captured through an appropriate analytical function. In the second stage, the tangential flux density is derived by solving a 2-D Laplace equation, subject to only a single boundary condition. The effect of stator slots on the tangential flux is accounted for using virtual surface currents placed along the lateral sides of the stator slots. The proposed hybrid approach reduces computational time while maintaining high accuracy, as confirmed through comparison with the subdomain model, finite element (FE) analysis, and experimental testing on the fully developed, production-ready version of the motor intended for the kitchen hood application.

title: SWD-Net: A Benchmark Dataset for Small Water Body Detection from Very High-Resolution Imagery
authors: Di Liu, Chengbin Deng, Pontus Olofsson, Songkun Yan, Jiao Wang, Isabelle Hu, Yang Hong
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Small and seasonal water bodies store a substantial portion of inland surface water and play a key role in local hydrology, ecosystem resilience, and agricultural sustainability. Yet their small size, irregular geometry, and frequent seasonal variability hinder accurate detection in global water products derived from coarse-resolution imagery. To address this limitation, we present Small Water Body Detection (SWD-Net), a high-resolution, expert-annotated benchmark dataset for small water-body detection from aerial and satellite imagery. SWD-Net consists of 10,085 image–mask pairs (1,024 × 1,024 pixels) sourced primarily from NAIP, Google Earth, and LoveDA, spanning diverse climatic and land-cover conditions. Baseline experiments using five representative segmentation architectures, including U-Net, Mamba U-Net, ViT U-Net, SegFormer U-Net, and Swin U-Net, demonstrate clear architectural contrasts, with transformer-based networks achieving the most accurate delineation of fine-scale and fragmented ponds. Among them, Swin U-Net achieved the best overall performance, with an IoU of 93.53% and an F1-score of 96.56% on the test set. All data, pretrained weights, and evaluation codes are publicly released to facilitate transparent and reproducible research in fine-scale hydrological mapping, and seasonal water monitoring.

title: Concurrent Composition for Differentially Private Continual Mechanisms

title: From Smartphones to Mobile Controllers: An Android-Based Mobile Solution Enabling Real-Time Algorithm Design, Control, and Supervision
authors: Jahan Zaib Bhatti, Guo-Ping Liu, Muhammad Noman Hasan, Nasim Ullah, Lukas Prokop, Vojtech Blazek, Muhammad Zubair Islam
abstract: Modern Networked Control Systems (NCSs) leverage wireless sensors and actuators, enabling smartphones to function as capable control terminal for remote laboratories. However, existing web-based and native mobile interface approaches face several challenges, including under utilization of device resources, fragmented workflows across disparate systems, reliance on root privileges, and restrictions on accessibility and usability. In this paper, we propose a novel unified native Android-based application and framework named Mobile Controller and Supervisory Client (MCSC), which transforms Android based normal smartphones into comprehensive remote laboratory terminals, operating as efficient mobile controllers. It integrates visual algorithm design, real-time control process execution, and data analysis into a single platform. The proposed MCSC deals with accessibility barriers by integrating visual algorithm design, real-time control execution, and data analysis within a single client application that implements touch-optimized user interface. Built on a three-tier distributed architecture, MCSC offloads compilation tasks to Servers Tier while a Mobile Algorithm Run-Time (MART) service executes algorithms locally without elevated privileges. The system features a block-based programming interface with 80+ functional blocks, customizable supervisory dashboards, and responsive operations via Kotlin coroutines. Extensive experiments on an Air-Bearing Spacecraft (ABS) simulator platform demonstrate that the proposed system achieves average control loop response times of 336.777 ms in a local wireless network. Moreover, user evaluation with 32 engineering students achieved 89.26% satisfaction for technical performance and 85.44% for interface usability. The results indicate that the MCSC system can successfully provide real-time remote laboratory access while maintaining technical rigor for advanced control systems applications.

title: Toward High-Precision Positioning for Enhanced Long-Range Navigation Systems: Principles, Progresses, and Practices
authors: Yuzhou Li, Di Liu, Xing Xia, Yan Dong, Zheng Dou, Min Sheng
abstract: The enhanced long-range navigation system (eLoran), a terrestrial navigation system renowned for its strong anti-interference ability and long transmission distance, is internationally acknowledged as the most resilient backup for global navigation satellite systems. In this article, we first elaborate the positioning principle of the eLoran, from which we derive that three modules at the receiver, namely, sky-ground waves recognition, additional secondary factor estimation, and coordinate calculation, are critical to improve positioning accuracy. We then provide an in-depth review on the recent progresses on the design of each module, and rigorously discuss the main challenges imposed on the related algorithms for each module and possible future research directions. Finally, we adopt the practical measurement data to exhibit the state-of-the-art positioning results achieved by the currently most representative algorithms.

title: InfAMAX: Bridging the Compute-Memory Gap in Intel AMX for Efficient LLM Inference
authors: Kyeonghyeon Ryu, Yunhyeong Jeon, Gyeongseo Park, Minho Kim, Daehoon Kim
abstract: Intel Advanced Matrix Extensions (AMX) offers a theoretical peak of over 90 TFLOPS for LLM inference, yet practical efficiency often plateaus at 5–40% of its potential. Our analysis reveals that this performance gap stems from a fundamental architectural mismatch: bursty AMX memory accesses frequently saturate Line Fill Buffers (LFB), and naive multi-socket scaling triggers prohibitive synchronization overhead. To bridge this gap, we propose <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">InfAMAX</monospace>, an LLM inference framework built upon two key ideas. First, we introduce decoupled tile-pipelining and multi-level adaptive prefetching to maximize data reuse and mask LFB-induced latencies across the cache hierarchy. Second, we employ a NUMA-aware resource management strategy that enforces strict thread affinity and shape-aware partitioning to eliminate cross-socket cache coherence traffic. Experimental results show that <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">InfAMAX</monospace> improves end-to-end inference throughput by up to 2× (reducing decoding latency by 2×) over state-of-the-art libraries, providing a scalable and efficient foundation for high-performance LLM serving on modern CPUs.

title: Generative AI in Business: Applications, Labor Market Implications and Business Proposals
authors: Kitti Tristen Dán, Andrea Tick
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: In recent years, rapid technological advancements have fundamentally transformed both everyday life and reshaped various economic sectors. Artificial Intelligence (AI) has played a significant role in driving these transformations, reshaping industries, workplaces, and organizational structures. One emerging area of particular interest is generative artificial intelligence (GenAI), which can produce complex content that once considered the sole domain of human creativity and expertise. This development has opened new possibilities across a wide range of industries. The present research applies quantitative analysis to explore the market labor implications of AI transformation and strives to develop some business proposals. The research revealed that GenAI can effectively substitute for monotonous or administrative tasks, but its use must be deliberate and mindful. Furthermore, organizations must develop training strategies to support employee technological adaptation. Finally, the long-term benefits of GenAI are achievable only if deployment occurs in ethical and security-conscious environments.

title: A High-Precision Target Angle Estimation Method with Spatial Feature Self-Enhancement
authors: Houhong Xiang, Kai Tu, Hao Hu, Saiqin Xu, Bilei Zhou
abstract: At present, deep learning-based radar target elevation angle estimation methods usually rely on accurate angle labels for training, which improves estimation performance but also leads to high data acquisition and labeling cost as well as limited generalization capability. To address this issue, this letter proposes a radar target elevation angle estimation method based on spatial feature self-enhancement. A self-supervised spatial feature enhancement network is constructed to exploit the potential spatial feature correlations in noisy data and generate noise-suppressed spatial signals without requiring labeled training data. Combined with super-resolution elevation angle estimation algorithms, the proposed method achieves high-precision target elevation angle estimation in unlabeled scenarios. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively improves estimation accuracy and outperforms existing deep learning-based and conventional methods.

title: Discrete-Time Complex Vector Dual Internal Mode Decoupling Control Method with Delay Compensation for CSI-Fed PMSM System
authors: Quntao An, Yuzhuo Lu, Ping Zheng, Xiaoguang Zhang
abstract: This paper proposes a dual internal model (DIM) decoupling control method with delay compensation, in which the internal model principle is applied to both the voltage loop and the current loop to solve the high order and strong coupling problems of the permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) system fed by the current source inverter (CSI). By means of deriving the complex-vector model of the CSI-fed PMSM system, the influence of cross-coupling caused by coordinate transformation is analyzed and the decoupling performance of the DIM decoupling control method is quantitatively evaluated. To overcome the influence of control delay and angle coupling, the model of the DIM decoupling control method in the discrete domain is derived. The codesign method of controller parameters and the delay compensation coefficient is introduced and compared with the traditional decoupling method. Finally, the experimental results show that the proposed decoupling method can improve the control performance in both dynamic and steady states.

title: On Reflection Shadows from Polygonal Approximations of Round Structures in Ray Tracing
authors: Lorenz H. W. Loeser, Thomas Kürner
abstract: Round and curved structures pose a challenge for ray tracing simulations, since their polygonal approximation creates discontinuities that lead to reflection shadows, i.e., missing specular reflection paths. This work analyzes the impact of the polygon order (vertex number of regular polygon) on the recovery of reflection paths. The results show that increasing polygon order does not increase the number of recovered paths but merely changes the distribution of reflection shadows, underlining the necessity of including additional propagation mechanisms beyond specular reflection for accurate channel characterization.

title: Reducing Noise: Hybrid Static Application Security Testing–Large Language Model Pipeline for Code Security
authors: Vaibhav Agrawal, Kiarash Ahi
abstract: This article discusses a hybrid triage pipeline that integrates static application security testing with large language models to provide an intelligent triage and automated exploit generation, reducing false positives by 91% in real-world deployments.

title: Two-Relay Decode-and-Forward for the Degraded Broadcast Channel: Achievable Rate Region with Orthogonal Channel Components
authors: Mohammad Al Bataineh, Dima Turki, Zouhair Al-Qudah
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Relay-aided transmission is a promising approach to extend coverage and increase throughput in wireless networks, yet characterizing the achievable rate region of broadcast channels with multiple decode-and-forward relays over orthogonal links remains an open problem. This paper derives the achievable rate region (ARR) of the two-user degraded broadcast channel aided by two parallel full-duplex decode-and-forward (DF) relays operating over orthogonal channel components. The orthogonal structure, motivated by practical frequency-division duplex (FDD) deployments, assigns dedicated frequency bands to the source-to-relay and source-to-destination links, eliminating self-interference at the relays without requiring half-duplex switching. The ARR of this broadcast-relay channel (BRC) for the discrete-memoryless (DM) model is established under the conditions of utilizing superposition block Markov encoding (BME) at the source, backward decoding (BD) at the destinations, and DF at the relays. A matching converse is provided, establishing tightness of the inner bound under the DF coding strategy. Then, the results are extended to the AWGN channel, yielding closed-form rate expressions in terms of channel gains, power allocation factors, and signal correlation coefficients. Furthermore, our numerical examples demonstrate that the ARR (i) includes the original degraded BC rate region, (ii) is enlarged considerably as the number of relays increases, and (iii) is strongly influenced by the relay-to-destination channel gains and the relay power, and (iv) is less sensitive to the source-to-relay gains when the relay-to-destination links are already strong.

title: Mobility Function and Aperiodic Electrocortical Activity in Younger and Older Adults
authors: Charlotte R. DeVol, Chang Liu, Jacob Salminen, Erika M. Pliner, Arkaprava Roy, Chris J. Hass, David J. Clark, Todd M. Manini, Rachael D. Seidler, Daniel P. Ferris
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Mobility declines with age to the extent that walking speed is often considered a vital sign. Identifying electrocortical changes behind this decline would aid with early identification and intervention. Electroencephalography (EEG) metrics may provide insight into neural factors contributing to mobility decline with aging. Recent research has shown a differentiation in aperiodic EEG across age groups, cognitive abilities, and populations with neurological injury. Aperiodic EEG is defined as the broadband, or non-oscillatory, component of the EEG power spectrum described by an exponent and offset. However, it is unknown if aperiodic EEG differs between mobility tasks or brain regions. The purpose of this study was to 1) compare aperiodic EEG in healthy older and younger adults at rest and while walking and 2) determine if oscillatory and aperiodic EEG in sensorimotor brain regions are predictors of a slower walking speed, regardless of age and other demographic factors. We analyzed EEG collected while participants were sitting at rest and walking on a treadmill in 31 younger adults (age: 24 ± 4, mean ± s.d.) and 59 older adults (age: 74 ± 6), with no known cognitive decline or neuromuscular impairment. We found that older adults had lower aperiodic exponent and offset at both rest and during walking, but only a subset of brain regions showed age group differences. Using machine learning methods, we found that right sensorimotor alpha power, left sensorimotor aperiodic offset, and left sensorimotor beta power had the largest effect on individualized walking speed, after the demographics of age, waist circumference, and sex. These results suggest age differences in aperiodic EEG are regionally specific, and that aperiodic and oscillatory EEG describe differences in individualized walking speed that demographics alone cannot.

title: Enhanced, Low-Cost Circuit for Four-Wire Resistive Sensor Interfacing
authors: Sreehari R S, Elangovan K, Ferran Reverter
abstract: This paper presents a low-cost interface circuit for accurate measurement of four-wire resistive sensors, with emphasis on RTD-based temperature sensing over a wide operating span. The circuit employs a switched Op-amp configuration controlled by a unipolar square-wave signal with 50% duty cycle, thereby eliminating the effect of lead-wire resistance without requiring matched components or bipolar excitation. The average output voltage represents the sensor resistance and reduces errors due to wire resistance, switch-ON resistance, and their mismatches. Theoretical analysis shows that dominant nonidealities, mainly Op-amp offset voltage and input bias current, limit the maximum relative error to 0.007%. Experimental results using OP07 and CD4053 ICs demonstrate linear operation for sensor resistances from 60 Ω to 390 Ω, with a maximum nonlinearity of 0.06% and a relative error of 0.09%. A low-cost LMC555-based control signal generator is also implemented, yielding comparable performance with the prior art. The proposed circuit provides a robust and cost-effective solution for four-wire resistive sensor and RTD interfacing.

title: A Multi-Branch Concatenate Neural Approach for Intelligent Spectrum Sensing in Cognitive Radio Networks
authors: Md. Tofail Ahmed, Mousumi Haque, Yosuke Sugiura, Tetsuya Shimamura
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Spectrum sensing (SS) using machine learning (ML) or deep learning (DL) in an intelligent way is a current trend in cognitive radio networks (CRNs). The primary objective of any SS method is to enhance the detection probability of a primary user (PU) in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) environments. This paper proposes a multi-branch concatenate neural approach (MBCNA), designed as an aggregated neural network framework for SS in CRNs to improve PU detection capabilities. Additionally, we introduce a method for generating a dataset to serve as a training, validation, and testing data source for the proposed approach by utilizing the standard deviation of the real and imaginary parts, as well as the maximum and median values of the fast Fourier transform (FFT) of the received signals. This work considers quadrature amplitude modulation (QAM) over a Rayleigh fading channel with additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). The effectiveness of the proposed method is evaluated with performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrix, probability of detection, and false alarm rate. Simulation results demonstrate the validity of the proposed approach, and comparative analysis shows that it outperforms state-of-the-art DL methods. Specifically, the proposed method achieves 100.00% PU detection ability at an SNR of -10 dB.

title: Spatio-Temporal Coded Multiplexing Using Spherical-Gold Codes for Fading- and Delay-Resilient Beam Isolation in Multibeam Phased Arrays
authors: Yuan Ma, Mike Ballou, Kyle Richard, Hessam Mahdavifar, Najme Ebrahimi
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: Future integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems require simultaneous multibeam operation with low-latency hardware and robust isolation under synchronization error and fading. Conventional code-division multiplexing using Walsh–Hadamard codes is extremely time-sensitive. This letter demonstrates that conventional temporal-only coded multibeam arrays result in inter-beam sidelobe level (SLL) collapsing to within a few decibels of the main lobe and varying by more than 10–20 dB over delay. By embedding moderate-length Gold sequences into a spherical spatial codebook, the proposed Spherical-Gold scheme leverages both <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/\sqrt{N}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> temporal and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/\sqrt{M}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> spatial bounds, achieving effectively <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/$</tex-math> </inline-formula>(<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sqrt{N} \cdot \sqrt{M}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) correlation without increasing RF complexity. Measurement results and verification have been performed using an Analog Devices ADAR3002 Ka-band 256-element receiver, where four simultaneous beams demonstrate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\ge}15$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB beam isolation with <<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm 2.5$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB variation under timing error and fading, while temporal-only code-division multiple-access (CDMA) degrades to −5 to −7 dB inter-beam SLL with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gt \pm 8$</tex-math> </inline-formula> dB variation under timing error.

title: 110 to 460 GHz Double-Ridge Horn Antennas
authors: Martin Pittermann, Thomas Zwick, Akanksha Bhutani
abstract: Two designs for wideband double-ridge horn antennas are evaluated in terms of their manufacturability and performance. A gain of 16 dBi to 26 dBi is achieved and experimentally verified. These antennas cover the highest operating frequency and absolute bandwidth for double-ridge waveguide components published thus far, at 110 GHz to 460 GHz or a relative bandwidth of 123%.

title: Counterfactual Regret Minimization-Mixing for Noncooperative Stochastic Spectrum Games with Imperfect Information
authors: Zuyuan Zhang, Lingjia Liu, Nathaniel D. Bastian, Tian Lan
abstract: Spectrum sharing is a key enabler for 5G/6G. We model decentralized spectrum access as a noncooperative, stochastic, imperfect-information extensive-form game and show that running standard Counterfactual Regret Minimization (CFR) independently at each user can exhibit persistent cycling rather than converging to a stable equilibrium. We provide a constructive multi-player example and a mapping analysis explaining why the induced regret-matching update need not be contractive in general multi-player, non-zero-sum settings. To address this, we propose CFR-M2, a lightweight regret-mixing scheme that periodically aggregates a small amount of cumulative regret (not policy parameters) across users at a configurable communication interval. Our analysis shows that inserting an infrequent regret-consensus step over any connected communication graph preserves no-(counterfactual)-regret while contracting inter-agent disagreement in regrets. Consequently, the C´esaro-averaged play converges to an ε–extensive-form coarse correlated equilibrium (EFCCE) with ε=Õ(1/√T)+O(RMAX TCOMM/(1-ρ)T) where ρ is the second-largest eigenvalue modulus of the mixing matrix. Under additional structure (e.g., a strongly monotone pseudo-gradient or a strongly convex potential), the EFCCE collapses to a unique Nash equilibrium. Experiments on synthetic networks and a 5G Dynamic Spectrum Sharing (DSS) scenario (WINNER II channel model and practical parameter settings) show that CFR-M2 improves convergence stability and system reward over CFR and several learning/game-theoretic baselines, while requiring only infrequent communications.

title: Automatic Detection of Bacterial and Viral Pneumonia from X-Ray Scans
authors: Róbert Roman, Levente Kovács, László Szilágyi
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Automated detection of pneumonia in chest X-rays enhances diagnostic accuracy, allowing for faster and more reliable identification of cases, which is crucial for timely intervention and treatment. The ability to automatically distinguish between viral and bacterial pneumonia not only streamlines clinical decision-making but also ensures that patients receive the most appropriate and effective treatment, minimizing the risk of complications. To comply with the above formulated goals, this paper proposes a convolutional neural architecture that can effectively address the two-class and three-class classification problems. The proposed network consists of five consecutive convolutional blocks involving 2D convolution, batch normalization and max pooling, and four hidden dense layers in the decision making stage. The Five-fold cross validation based thorough evaluation revealed that the proposed network can exceed 95% accuracy in the detection of pneumonia and achieves 82% accuracy when distinguishing viral and bacterial pneumonia from negative cases.

title: Unlocking Virtual Transmission Capacity with Grid-Enhancing Technologies
authors: Md Thesun Alamin, Sumit Paudyal
publish: 2026 IEEE Power and Energy Conference at Illinois (PECI)
abstract: Transmission congestion is becoming a major barrier to renewable energy integration. Variable generation is putting more stress on the existing grid. At the same time, expanding the network is difficult due to high costs and permitting delays. In this context, this paper proposes coordinated utilization of three grid-enhancing technologies, i.e., dynamic line rating (DLR), optimal transmission switching (OTS), and battery energy storage systems (BESS) as an alternative to expansion planning. To ensure computational tractability due to large search space in optimization problem, a multi-stage candidate screening method is introduced to restrict binary switching decisions to a reduced set of high-impact tranmission lines. Weather-dependent thermal limits are derived using a steady-state heat balance model compliant with IEEE Std. 738–2023. The proposed formulation is evaluated on a modified RTS-GMLC test system. Results show that coordinated deployment of DLR, OTS, and VTL-based BESS reduces total operational costs about 16% and eliminates all thermal congestion, which demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed framework for enhancing transmission utilization.

title: Utility Experience in Virtualizing PAC Systems
authors: Dinesh Baradi, Anthony Sivesind
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: The virtualization of protection relaying and control functions using an open-architecture hardware platform has proven benefits. Hardware and software are de-coupled, allowing increased interoperability and more agile development and implementation. New applications, based on machine learning such as predictive maintenance and fault forecasting, will be more easily deployed amongst the vast amount of data being aggregated.This paper describes the concept of virtualization using different hypervisor technologies, how a fully functional protection and control software stack behaves in a virtualized environment and presents data from real-time performance testing as well as real-world results from field deployment.

title: Detection of Bearing Fault in PMSM Using Motor Current Analysis Based on Time-Shift and Fractional Linear Prediction
authors: Xiangjin Song, Yijie Wang, Kaijin Xu, Zhaowei Wang, Wenxiang Zhao, Qian Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: To enable more effective condition monitoring for permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) bearing faults, this paper introduces a technique that employs the square envelope of synchronized current residue via time-shifting (TS) of the fractional linear prediction (FLP)-filtered signal. Firstly, FLP is applied to each phase current to attenuate the fundamental component, its odd harmonics, and non-fault-related fractional harmonics. Subsequently, one phase is selected as the reference, and the remaining two phases are phase-shifted accordingly. Finally, the fault signatures across phases are synchronized by integrating these processed currents. The square envelope analysis on this synchronized current residue enhances the detection of bearing fault features. Experimental verification was performed under a comprehensive set of conditions, covering all key bearing fault types (outer ring, inner ring, and rolling elements) and four different load conditions, confirming the practical effectiveness of the suggested monitoring technique.

title: Enabling Bidirectional Co-Evolution Between Data Ecosystems and Generative AI
authors: Gaochang Xie, Renchao Xie, Zehui Xiong, Xiumei Deng, Dusit Niyato, Sumei Sun, Biplab Sikdar, Kwok-Yan Lam
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (GAI) has achieved remarkable progress across diverse scenarios by combining generalized intelligence derived from large-scale web datasets with specialized expertise obtained from domain-specific data. However, current patterns of data collection and utilization suffer from inconsistent quality, infrequent updates, data silos, and limited sharing, which constrain the sustainable development of GAI. In this context, this article proposes a next-generation generative data infrastructure (NG2DI) framework from a data-centric perspective to achieve the synergistic co-evolution of data ecosystems and GAI. Specifically, we first introduce the layered architecture of NG2DI and explain how it supports each stage of the data lifecycle. We then analyze the data flow dynamics driven by interactions among NG2DI participants and highlight the related technical implementation tutorial. Furthermore, to illustrate the bidirectional optimization between data ecosystems and GAI, we demonstrate that NG2DI can facilitate forward optimization of GAI capabilities and enable reverse feedback from optimized GAI to enhance NG2DI functions. To validate these dual capabilities, we present two case studies in industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) scenarios: one demonstrates NG2DI-enabled data trading and GAI agent fine-tuning, while the other evaluates GAI-enabled data synthesis and scheduling strategy generation for NG2DI.

title: MSCTFormer: A High-Resolution Remote Sensing Image Water Body Fine Extraction Network Based on a Hybrid CNN-Transformer Architecture
authors: Bo Song, Zhiyong Zhang, Zhili Chen, Qingyang Wang, Bo Li
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Water resources form a vital foundation for human survival and development, and their precise monitoring is crucial for addressing global climate change. Water body extraction based on remote sensing imagery serves as a primary technical approach. However, existing methods still face challenges such as blurred boundaries and significant scale variations when processing high-resolution imagery, resulting in insufficient segmentation accuracy. To address this, this paper proposes a residual network model integrating multi-scale contextual attention, called as MSCTFormer. This model employs ResNet50 as its encoder, while its decoder core incorporates three innovative modules: (1) Enhanced Multi-Scale Contextual Attention Module (EMSCA), which integrates multi-scale local feature extraction with cross-window global attention to effectively capture multi-scale water body characteristics; (2) Enhanced Feedforward Network Module (EFFN), which enhances local feature representation capabilities through deep separable convolutions and residual connections; (3) Dual-Path Feature Merging Module (D-FFM), which achieves complementary fusion of multi-level encoder features and decoder features through parallel spatial and passage paths, there-by optimizing boundary information. We evaluated the proposed model on two public water body datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that MSCTFormer achieves performance improvements of 0.94%-3.30% in IoU and 1.06%-2.91% in F1 score, providing an effective solution for water body segmentation in high-resolution remote sensing imagery.

title:
Generalized Integer-to-Integer Invertible Color Mapping via the ULU
                    <sub>0</sub>
                    Factorization
authors: Dan Li, Jiashu Zhang, Bormin Huang
abstract: Color mapping transforms visual data between color representations to enhance perceptual quality, improve compression efficiency by decorrelating color components, ensure consistent reproduction across devices, and facilitate analysis and visualization in computer vision applications. Integer-invertible color mappings enable exact reversibility for lossless image and video coding. Certain integer-invertible variants of the YCuCv and YCoCg color mappings have been adopted in standards such as JPEG-LS, JPEG-2000, JPEG-XL. However, these specialized formulations cannot be readily generalized to other widely used color mappings. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a general integer-invertible framework for arbitrary color transforms that achieves lower transformation errors than ad hoc integer-based formulations. This work proposes a lifting-based three triangular factorization, termed ULU0, which enables general integer-invertible color mappings for any non-singular transform matrix. Numerical experiments on five representative mappings (YIQ, YCuCv, YCoCg, YCbCr, and YUV) across forty high-definition and ultra-high definition images demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves complete integer-invertibility in the RGB domain while more accurately preserving the characteristics of the original non-invertible mappings.

title: ScalPipe: Scalable Collaborative Pipeline Inference for Distributed Heterogeneous Devices
authors: Nianfu Wang, Wanyou Wang, Xiaoxiong Zhong, Jingyu Liu, Gaotao Shi, Zhijun Li
abstract: Edge devices generate vast amounts of data, which are transmitted to the remote cloud for inference—a process that can lead to excessive network load and privacy risks. Edge collaborative inference has emerged as a promising solution to these challenges. However, existing collaborative inference methods remain constrained, particularly by transmission costs, limited flexibility in model partitioning, and redundant computation. These issues constrain the efficient utilization of edge resources and limit overall system performance. To address these limitations, we adopt scalable pipelines in the context of resource and model orchestration, enabling dynamic adaptation to varying workloads and improving resource utilization efficiency. Specifically, we propose ScalPipe, a collaborative pipeline inference framework that enables scalable orchestration of resources and model partitions for efficient edge inference. To better accommodate edge devices, ScalPipe employs a lightweight customized heuristic algorithm for resource-adaptive model partitioning. A fine-tuning algorithm dynamically adjusts the scheduling strategy when monitored inference times deviate beyond a predefined threshold during inference. We provide theoretical analysis to establish performance bounds and computational complexity of the proposed algorithms. Comprehensive experiments in heterogeneous environments demonstrate that ScalPipe consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods across diverse model architectures and evaluation metrics. ScalPipe reduces average inference latency by 20%-40% while achieving over 90% resource utilization, delivering a significant boost in overall performance.

title: Temporal Dynamics of Memory Poisoning in Web3-Style LLM Agents
authors: Abbas Yazdinejad, Hadis Karimipour
abstract: Memory-enabled large language model (LLM) agents, particularly those deployed in long-horizon, tool-using settings such as Web3-style autonomous workflows, introduce security risks that extend beyond single-prompt injection. By persisting and reusing information across interaction steps and sessions, these agents enable memory poisoning attacks in which adversarial inputs modify persistent agent state and influence future decisions after benign intermediate interactions. Recent work on context manipulation and “fake memories” demonstrates that adversarial content can be injected into an agent’s prompt-visible inputs or persistent memory; however, existing evaluations largely analyze such attacks at isolated interaction steps or static context snapshots, obscuring their temporal dynamics. In this paper, we present the first large-scale, trajectory-level measurement framework for analyzing temporal memory poisoning in memory-enabled LLM agents. We construct a schema-constrained dataset of 2,614 multi-step attack trajectories spanning four attack families, chain poisoning, policy rewriting, backdoor triggering, and slow drift, executed over shared persistent memory. We define temporal risk metrics over multi-step interaction trajectories that capture delayed activation, non-monotonic escalation, and the earliest point at which attacks become distinguishable from benign behavior. Our empirical results show that a substantial fraction of attacks remain indistinguishable from benign behavior until late-stage activation, despite exhibiting low or medium risk at all earlier steps. Slow-drift and backdoor-trigger attacks, in particular, systematically evade step-local evaluation until terminal interactions, while chain poisoning and policy rewriting exhibit non-monotonic risk trajectories. These findings demonstrate that memory poisoning risk is inherently temporal and cannot be reliably assessed using prompt-level or step-isolated evaluation, motivating trajectory-aware benchmarks for agent security.

title: Enhancing Stereo Matching Domain Generalization with Adversarial Domain Alignment
authors: Shiqian Zhao, Meiqing Wu, Kangjie Chen, Yi Xie, Tianlin Li, Siew-Kei Lam, Guowen Xu, Anran Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Recently, state-of-the-art stereo-matching networks trained on large-scale synthetic data have shown remarkable performance. However, their capacity to extrapolate effectively to unseen real-world data, i.e. different domains, remains a challenge. The major difficulty resides in the unforeseeable domain gap when generalizing from synthetic data to real-world data. In this paper, we introduce ADASM, an approach using adversarial domain alignment, designed to enhance the robustness and generalization of stereo-matching networks. It mainly consists of two modules: an end-to-end robustness optimizer and a domain-invariant feature learner. First, we adapt adversarial training into the stereo-matching task to reduce models' sensitivity to the perturbation in real-world samples. By introducing worst cases into the training space, we take unseen data into account and achieve robust disparity estimation for the end-to-end model. Then, via simulating the real-world noise with gradient-based perturbation, we construct a fictitious domain, which is taken as a referential distribution of the real-world noisy data, for further domain alignment. Specifically, we propose to utilize Maximum Mean Discrepancy to realize domain regularization between the original domain and the fictitious one. Finally, we fuse all aforementioned objectives and propose a unified, simple but effective loss function that can be adapted to all stereo-matching networks. The extensive experiments show that our method achieves a superior disparity estimation performance on various real-world benchmarks, including KITTI, Middlebury, and DrivingStereo. More importantly, ADASM obtains competitive or even better performance than the fine-tuning strategy, revealing its fine-tuning-free character.

title: Memory Confidentiality and Integrity: Flipping Bits in Hardware
authors: Florian Daxer, Jens Nöpel, Georg Sigl
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Solid-State Circuits Society
abstract: Scaling-induced reliability issues in Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM), disturbance faults, and multi-bit errors caused by Rowhammer attacks, increasingly exceed the correction capabilities of conventional Error Correction Code (ECC) schemes. Although Correction-as-a-Search (CaaS) techniques employ cryptographic hashes or authentication tags to detect and correct such faults, their suitability for embedded systems and for fully hardware-assisted implementations remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present the Memory Confidentiality and Integrity (MCI) module, a complete embedded hardware implementation of CaaS, and evaluate its performance, throughput, and area overhead on an FPGA. We combine CaaS with authenticated memory encryption and reuse existing authentication tags to enable error detection and correction without additional storage overhead. Our design supports correction of multiple bit flips by dynamically selecting correction strategies based on observed error patterns, recovering errors in under 200 ms. Finally, we analyze the typical distribution of bogus bits and show that MCI feasibly corrects up to 7 errors, maintaining a success rate of 97.63% for up to 5 errors.

title: Angle-Aware Real Time Transformers for Oriented Fine-Grained Ship Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Images
authors: Nicolae Catalin Ristea, Anamaria Radoi
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Transformer-based detectors, such as the DETR series, have gained significant popularity for object detection due to their strong performance across a variety of benchmarks. However, these architectures are typically limited by high computational costs, restricting their deployment in real-time and resource-constrained environments. To address this limitation, Real-Time DETR (RT-DETR) was introduced, offering an effective trade-off between speed and accuracy. Despite these advances, RT-DETR models are constrained to horizontal bounding boxes and lack the ability to detect oriented objects, a critical requirement in many fields, such as remote sensing. To bridge this gap, we propose AA-RTDETRv2, an angle-aware real-time transformer, specifically designed for oriented object detection and classification. To the best of our knowledge, AA-RTDETRv2 is the first end-to-end RT-DETR-based framework capable of real-time, rotation-aware detection and classification without relying on post-processing steps. Our model introduces two key components: (1) an angle-aware multi-scale encoder that enhances orientation-sensitive feature extraction, and (2) an Angle-Aware Uncertainty-Minimal Query Selection strategy that produces high-quality, rotation-informed object queries to improve detection and classification precision. The proposed AA-RTDETRv2 architecture was evaluated on two standard remote sensing benchmarks (i.e., ShipRSImageNet and DOTA) and achieved state-of-the-art performance-speed balance in oriented object detection and identification. Our method outperforms both NMS-based detectors and prior DETR variants while maintaining inference speeds suitable for real-time applications. These results underscore the effectiveness of AA-RTDETRv2 in addressing remote sensing challenges, including dense object distributions and arbitrary object orientations. The code is accessible at https://to-be-added.

title: SALDIRAY: Scalable and Adaptive Language Diagnostics for Remediation of Anomalies and Typos
authors: Emre Külah, Yusuf Mücahit Çetinkaya, Hande Alemdar
abstract: Natural language processing (NLP) systems working with real-world user data often encounter noisy, informal text, including typos, spelling variations, slang, and homophone substitutions. This noise can cause a severe drop in the performance of downstream models. This paper presents SALDIRAY (Scalable and Adaptive Language Diagnostics for Remediation of Anomalies and Typos), an integrated, domain-independent framework for text normalization that aims to boost the resilience of NLP systems across various model architectures and application types. It converts short, poorly formed text into standardized language through a synthetic noise modeling and standardization approach, instantiated in practice as a noise generation and normalization pipeline. This approach is enriched by incorporating curated slang and homophone lists to ensure linguistic realism. The process creates large amounts of paired noisy–clean data, which are used to fine-tune robust normalization models. Our detailed experiments consistently show that SALDIRAYimproves the accuracy and stability of systems across a wide range of tasks, including traditional machine learning–based classification, sentiment analysis, and instruction-tuned large language model tasks. The framework requires no task-specific supervision, operates as a modular preprocessing layer, and can be integrated into existing NLP workflows. The experiments confirm that the proposed framework offers a scalable and adaptive method for improving robustness to noisy text, particularly under controlled corruption settings, while showing promising transfer to real-world noisy inputs.

title: PaCR: An Agentic Preference-aligned TAP Conflict Handling
authors: Ronghua Li, Zi Liang, Yaxin Xiao, Qingqing Ye, Haibo Hu
abstract: With the increasing complexity of smart home environments, Trigger-Action-Programming (TAP) conflicts have emerged as critical issues, occurring when multiple automation apps compete for control over shared devices or interact in unintended ways. While existing approaches detect and resolve such conflicts using pre-defined rules or expert knowledge, they fail to account for the significant variability in individual preferences for conflict resolution and significantly degrade automation system utility. To address this gap, we propose PaCR (Preference-aligned Conflict Resolver), a novel agentic framework that resolves conflicts while aligning with users’ personalized preferences for automation behavior. PaCR employs a two-step conflict resolution process: first, it interactively gathers contextual information from smart home configurations to generate conflict resolution agents with user profiles that accurately depict user preferences and characteristics; then, the agents acting as users utilize semantic reasoning capabilities of LLMs to assist in generating personalized conflict detection and resolution strategies. Due to the agentic confict resolving feature, we evaluate the effectiveness of PaCR with existing datasets at scale. PaCR demonstrates strong capabilities in addressing conflicts based on users’ need and significantly improves conflict resolution efficacy and interpretability, offering a user-centric approach to managing smart home automation conflicts.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Vision–Language Learning Improves Chest X-Ray Retrieval
authors: Ahmad Elallaf, Yuktha Priya Masupalli, Gongbo Liang
abstract: Vision–language foundation models have emerged as powerful general-purpose representation learners, but their deterministic embeddings often fail to provide the reliability required for high-stakes biomedical tasks.
