We introduce MedProbCLIP, a probabilistic vision–language learning framework for chest X-ray and radiology report representation learning and retrieval. Evaluated on the MIMIC-CXR dataset, MedProbCLIP outperforms deterministic and probabilistic baselines in both retrieval and zero-shot classification, while improving the trustworthiness significantly.

title: Rotation and Power Optimization for Aircraft-Conformal Reconfigurable Antenna Arrays
authors: Shiyu Xiao, Chenxuan Gu, Junling Li, Jie Huang, Cheng-Xiang Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: The sixth-generation (6G) wireless communication calls for leveraging additional spatial degrees of freedom to boost high spectral efficiency and agile spatial control. Thus, we develop a modeling and optimization framework for aircraft-conformal reconfigurable antenna (RA) arrays that jointly adjust element rotations and user power allocations. The framework yields a more realistic aircraft scenario by enforcing conformal surface constraints and using a rotation-aware geometry-based stochastic model (GBSM). For modeling, we build a hierarchical coordinate chain from the airframe to each element on the conformal substrate. This chain is integrated with a GBSM comprising line-of-sight (LoS) and clustered non-line-of-sight (NLoS) components, rotation-dependent element patterns, and spatial consistency. For optimization, we adopt linear minimum mean-square error (MMSE) combining and maximize the system sum rate under a total-power limit and mechanical rotation limits via an alternating procedure. Simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation updates per-element rotations with projection and optional angle quantization, while water-filling assigns user powers. Simulations reveal non-negligible differences in sum rate across spherical, cylindrical, and planar patches. The design further demonstrates robustness to angular perturbations, indicating practical viability for aircraft-conformal RA arrays in airborne access networks.

title: Zone 3 Phase Distance Protection: Balancing Dependability, Security, and Compliance
authors: Navid Nassiri, Glenn Callaghan, Rochak Shiwakoti
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Remote backup protection has been a subject of debate in transmission system protection, particularly with respect to the application of Zone 3 phase distance elements. While Zone 3 can provide critical backup coverage under certain conditions, its use is also associated with potential misoperations that can lead to cascading power system outages. This paper examines the role of remote backup through a focused analysis of Zone 3 phase distance application. It explores when Zone 3 phase distance should be used, how to implement it effectively, and whether it remains useful given modern protection schemes and system visibility. The paper also evaluates reliability and security tradeoffs associated with Zone 3 phase distance operation, with special attention to NERC PRC-023-6 Transmission Relay Loadability requirements and their impact on phase distance element settings. Case studies and industry practices are presented to highlight the benefits and risks of Zone 3 protection, and to provide guidance on best practices to stakeholders for balancing dependability, security, and compliance in today’s evolving grid.

title: Free-space Material Characterization of Multi-layer Samples Applying Transmission Line Model at D-Band
authors: M. Fernanda Triviño-Cristancho, Jan Bawowski, Ilona Rolfes, Aline Friedrich, Simona Bruni, Pia Bergtholdt, Marta Arias Campo
abstract: This paper presents a free-space material characterization setup at D-band (110-170 GHz), using elliptical dielectric lens antennas. A transmission line model (TLM) has been applied, which enables the calculation of the dielectric properties of multi-layer samples, also under oblique incidence. The transmission parameters are evaluated without the need of two-stage calibration, which is mechanically challenging at these high frequencies. Thanks to the high aperture efficiency of the lenses, fed with a leaky-wave antenna, the assumption of planewave incidence is accurate without the need of additional lenses or mirrors. The non-destructive measurement approach is validated with know materials, showing good agreement with the literature. The characterization of epoxy based glues on alumina carrier is also carried out, showcasing the applicability of the method in multi-layer samples.

title: Design and Experimental Validation of 1.3 GHz Microwave Pulse Compressor Using a Side-Port Extraction
authors: Konstantinos E. Orfanidis, Vasiliki A. Katsara, Zisis C. Ioannidis, Stylianos P. Savaidis
abstract: This paper presents the design and experimental validation of a 1.3 GHz microwave pulse compressor (MPC) with a side output port. The cavity, formed by WR650 waveguides and an H-plane T-junction, stores energy and releases it via a gas discharge tube (GDT). The optimal design is based on an equivalent circuit model for the compressor, ensuring maximum gain and minimum leakage losses. Time-domain CST simulations were employed to optimize the cavity length for efficient output pulse formation during discharge. Experimentally, a configuration extending approximately six guided wavelengths (6λ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">g</inf>) was developed, both with and without a matching iris, at approximately λ<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">g</inf>/4 from the output port. The addition of the matching iris significantly improved performance, achieving extraction efficiency that approaches the ideal response. These results highlight the critical role of cavity length, output pulse rise time, and output-port matching in maximizing the efficiency of side-port MPCs.

title: A Trust based- Security Assisted- Task Allocation Framework for Crowdsourced Software Development
authors: Maria Abdullah, Muhammad Zahid Tunio
abstract: In a software crowdsourcing environment, establishing trust is essential for effective task allocation and completion. Conventional task allocation approaches frequently depend on limited or random performance indicators, resulting in increased task incompletion, missed deadlines, and reallocation. The two fundamental dimensions of trust, competence, and security, along with their measurable indicators, are investigated in this study. A Security Assisted Task Allocation Framework (SATAF) is proposed to address these dimensions. SATAF systematically evaluates the trustworthiness of employees before assigning tasks to them. A novel ‘Trust Establishment Technique’ is introduced, particularly designed to integrate performance indicators associated with these dimensions to compute trust scores for each employee. Best-fit employees based on trust scores are matched with the task requirements. To evaluate the impact of trust and to assess the performance of the proposed SATAF, a detailed experiment was conducted. The experimental results demonstrate that SATAF increases task completion rate by approximately 17% and decreases the average task reallocation rate by 36.5% for medium to high task loads. The results also indicate that SATAF shows improvement in deadline adherence by 30% and 59% reduction in performance inconsistency and extreme deadline violations. Further, SATAF achieves a relative gain of 22% in matching accuracy and 7% improvement in task success rate compared to a utility-based algorithm, thus verifying its effectiveness in the decision-making process of task allocation.

title: Integrating SAM Supervision for 3D Weakly Supervised Point Cloud Segmentation
authors: Lechun You, Zhonghua Wu, Weide Liu, Xulei Yang, Jun Cheng, Wei Zhou, Bharadwaj Veeravalli, Guosheng Lin
abstract: Current methods for 3D semantic segmentation propose training models with limited annotations to address the difficulty of annotating large, irregular, and unordered 3D point cloud data. They usually focus on the 3D domain only, without leveraging the complementary nature of 2D and 3D data. Besides, some methods extend original labels or generate pseudo labels to guide the training, but they often fail to fully use these labels or address the noise within them. Meanwhile, the emergence of comprehensive and adaptable foundation models has offered effective solutions for segmenting 2D data. Leveraging this advancement, we present a novel approach that maximizes the utility of sparsely available 3D annotations by incorporating segmentation masks generated by 2D foundation models. We further propagate the 2D segmentation masks into the 3D space by establishing geometric correspondences between 3D scenes and 2D views. We extend the highly sparse annotations to encompass the areas delineated by 3D masks, thereby substantially augmenting the pool of available labels. Furthermore, we apply confidence- and uncertainty-based consistency regularization on augmentations of the 3D point cloud and select the reliable pseudo labels, which are further spread on the 3D masks to generate more labels. This innovative strategy bridges the gap between limited 3D annotations and the powerful capabilities of 2D foundation models, ultimately improving the performance of 3D weakly supervised segmentation.

title: Statistical QoS Provisioning and Performance Optimization for Heterogeneous Users in Mixed RF-FSO Satellite-Aerial-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Xiaoyu Liu, Min Lin, Yiwen Liu, Chaoqun You, Tony Q. S. Quek
abstract: The satellite-aerial-terrestrial network (SATN) is a promising architecture to achieve seamless global coverage and meet diverse quality-of-service (QoS) requirements in next-generation wireless communications. To support such multi-layered connectivity, we consider a mixed radio frequency (RF) and free-space optical (FSO) architecture, where heterogeneous users access a high-altitude platform (HAP) via RF links, and the HAP, acting as an aerial relay, forwards the aggregated traffic to a satellite through an FSO backhaul. Existing transmission schemes for such mixed RF-FSO SATNs, however, are not well suited to providing differentiated statistical QoS guarantees for heterogeneous users. To address this limitation, we propose a mixed RF-FSO QoS-aware uplink transmission (MRQ-UT) scheme. Specifically, we impose statistical delay-QoS constraints at both the user and HAP buffers, thereby explicitly capturing heterogeneous constraints on queueing delay and buffer overflow. On this basis, we derive the system effective capacity using a two-stage tandem queue model, which captures the sequential queuing behavior over the RF access and FSO backhaul links. Building upon this model, we develop a tractable effective-capacity-based optimization framework and propose a statistical channel-aware joint power and beamforming algorithm that enhances QoS provisioning under imperfect channel state information. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed MRQ-UT scheme significantly outperforms benchmark schemes in terms of effective capacity and statistical QoS performance.

title: Affine Formation Maneuver Control for NUMVs under Unreliable Networks: A Reliability-Enhanced Interaction Strategy
authors: Yecheng Zhang, Jianming Miao, Jiayi Lin, Bing Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Reliable communication networks are essential for formation maneuver control (FMC) of networked unmanned marine vehicles (NUMVs). However, communication reliability may be degraded by congestion, channel contention, and random packet losses. Within this context, this paper develops an affine FMC scheme with a reliability-enhanced interaction mechanism (RIM) for NUMVs. An auxiliary system is first constructed to decompose the framework into a localization layer and a tracking layer. With this decomposition, unreliable communications are handled only in the localization layer without involving the nonlinear NUMV model. Then, an RIM is developed to cope with unreliable networks. By assigning a minimum communication interval and staggered transmission instants, communication congestion induced by frequent interactions and channel contention caused by simultaneous transmissions can be effectively alleviated. Finally, rigorous analysis and simulations validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

title: Scalable Harmonic Analysis for Frequency Response Characterization of Photodetectors With Dual-Wavelength Modulation
authors: Di Lei, Yinfeng Wu, Junfeng Zhu, Simou Wang, Xinhai Zou, Yali Zhang, Sha Zhu, Chao Liu, Yong Liu, Ninghua Zhu, Shangjian Zhang
abstract: We propose a dual-wavelength electro-optic modulation method for scalable, high-frequency characterization of photodetectors (PDs) based on harmonic analysis. A single-tone microwave signal at frequency f1 drives an electro-optic modulator to generate optical harmonics at nf1, which serve as a broadband swept-frequency stimulus for PD response measurement. A fixed-frequency reference—derived from the coherent beat of two optical carriers spaced by fs —enables real-time de-embedding of the modulator's response by monitoring its splitting ratio and modulation depth, thereby extending the measurable bandwidth by a factor of n beyond f1. With a high-extinction-ratio Mach–Zehnder modulator under small-signal drive and null bias, the method further enables rapid, calibration-free PD characterization without requiring explicit calibration of the modulator. In experiments, using a dual-wavelength laser with 80 MHz carrier spacing, we demonstrate two- and four-fold bandwidth extensions, achieving a 67 GHz measurement range with only a 16.75 GHz drive tone. Excellent agreement with conventional methods confirms the technique as a simple, accurate, and scalable solution for high-speed PD characterization.

title: Modeling and Voltage-Loop Design for Boost Converter with Output-Sourced Passive Ripple Constant On-Time Control
authors: Yi-Rong Huang
abstract: Output-sourced passive ripple constant on-time (OSPRCOT) control is a type of passive ripple constant on-time (PRCOT) control scheme. In previous studies, the OSPRCOT control has been widely used to improve transient performance because the output-sourced passive ripple (OSPR) provides an additional feedback path for the converter's output voltage. To design and evaluate the voltage-loop stability, a small-signal model (SSM) is required for OSPRCOT control. In prior work, a novel describing function (DF) method was developed to derive the SSM for PRCOT control. In this paper, the DF method is extended to model the OSPRCOT control by incorporating the differential equations of the output voltage. Subsequently, the developed SSM is used to design loop parameters and evaluate the loop stability. Finally, SIMPLIS simulations and experimental results validate the derived SSM and designed voltage loop.

title: Frequency-aware Lightweight Distillation for Infrared Small Target Detection
authors: Weimin Cai, Yinuo Zhang, Jingchao Guo, Zheyuan Cai, Xiaotong Tu, Xinghao Ding, Changqing Lin, Yue Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) on edge platforms is often hindered by the architectural disparity between lightweight student models and high-performance teachers. While heterogeneous distillation can bridge this gap, the frequency characteristics of infrared imagery often cause feature mismatch and noise misinterpretation. To address this, we propose Normalized Hybrid Pooling Knowledge Distillation (NHPKD), the first heterogeneous distillation framework specifically designed for IRSTD. NHPKD employs a dual-branch adaptive frequency filter to decouple noise suppression from target enhancement: a large scale adaptive average pooling branch filters high-frequency noise, while a small-scale max-pooling branch preserves salient target signals. Experiments show NHPKD consistently outperforms SOTA methods across disparate architectures and datasets, for example, boosting YOLOX-T's mAP50 (0.634 to 0.738) and F1-score (0.704 to 0.782) on NUAA-SIRST. With zero additional inference cost and marginal training overhead, NHPKD offers a highly efficient IRSTD solution for edge platforms. Code will be available at https://github.com/wimCCC/NHPKD.

title: Wildfire Challenges and Mitigation on Distribution Feeders: Practices and Experience at Xcel Energy
authors: Ryan Ebarb, Aaron Mead, Tyler McGrath, Howard Self, Bob Zarovniaev, Akram Saad, Gevene Lauby, Mayur Baviskar, Mathias Ng
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: As wildfire risks rise, utilities are working to identify vulnerable areas, assess current equipment and practices, and implement effective plans to reduce risk. This paper outlines Xcel Energy’s process for identifying high-risk zones, describes their standard mitigation practices and equipment, and details updates to protection and control strategies. It also presents examples of revised standards and discusses challenges in maintaining reliability while reducing wildfire risk.

title: Analyzing the Association Between Solar and Wind Generation and Electricity Prices in Germany Using GUHA Association Rules
authors: František Kurimský, Marek Pavlík, L'ubomír Antoni
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: This study employs the General Unary Hypotheses Automaton (GUHA) based approach, implemented via the CleverMiner tool, to analyze the association between solar and wind power generation and day-ahead electricity prices in Germany from 2015 to 2024. Utilizing hourly generation and market price data from the Energy-Charts portal, enhanced association rule mining reveals statistically significant and interpretable patterns linking renewable generation levels with electricity price fluctuations. The findings highlight time-specific and generation-dependent price behaviors, providing valuable insights for market participants and policy makers on renewable integration effects in the electricity market. The study demonstrates the utility of GUHA as a robust exploratory data mining method for complex energy market datasets.

title: Deep Balanced Prior for Refined Self-supervised Reconstruction of Snapshot Spectral Compressive Imaging
authors: Feifan Teng, Yizhen Jia, Ping An, Liquan Shen, Shuming Wang, Xin Yuan, Xia Hua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Snapshot spectral compressive imaging (SCI) is a cutting-edge technique that employs a two-dimensional (2D) detector to capture high-dimensional hyperspectral cubes in a compressed format. The core challenge of SCI systems is how to accurately reconstruct the three-dimensional hyperspectral cubes from the 2D measurements acquired by the sensors. Unlike conventional RGB images, spectral data exhibit strong inter-channel correlations and subtle differences that cannot be ignored. However, existing algorithms are limited due to the scarcity of available compressed spectral imaging data and the overfitting problem in supervised learning, resulting in the loss of fine textures and inter-channel distinctions. Inspired by untrained neural networks and the deep image prior (DIP), we propose a novel approach that integrates deep balanced prior (DBP) and global spectral prior into a framework. This integration results in a self-supervised framework tailored for spectral snapshot compressive imaging reconstruction. Our method surpasses state-of-the-art self-supervised algorithms by approximately 1.8dB when evaluated with synthetic datasets. Extensive experimentation across various datasets demonstrates that our algorithm consistently outperforms existing learning-based methods in reconstructing fine textures from measurements.

title: A Low Power, Broadband Receiver for E-Band Applications
authors: Pascal Stadler, Philipp Konze, Klaus Aufinger, Nils Pohl, Tobias T. Braun
abstract: This work presents a receiver MMIC in Infineon’s B11HFC 130nm SiGe technology that covers the entire E-band (60-90 GHz) with its bandwidth of 32.4 GHz (59.9–92.3 GHz) | 40.8 GHz (59.4–100.2 GHz) at a 50 Ω | 1 MΩ IF interface. It achieves this with 28.8 dBV/dBm (565 mV at 90 MHz IF) voltage-and 24.5 dB power gain of a 2-stage cascode low noise amplifier (LNA) (26 dB) and a Gilbert cell based mixer (0 dBV/dBm | 7.3 dB). Input / output referred compression points lie at IP1dB=-36 dBm / OP1dBV=-8.2 dBV (1 MΩ) | IP1dB=-33 dBm / OP1dB=-12 dBm (50 Ω), respectively. With only a current draw of 25.5 mA from a 3.3 V supply (P<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DC</inf> = 84.15 mW), a minimum noise figure (NF) of 8.8 dB was measured at 60 GHz. The receiver’s core size amounts to only 570 µm x 310 µm (0.177 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>), while the whole MMIC, including the pad frame and RF-/LO-baluns, takes up 600 µm x 1100 µm (0.66 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>).

title: Donut line protection scheme – Lessons learnt from transmission protection design for industrial load with no communication infrastructure
authors: Ram Viswanathan, Tu Nguyen, Keith Gaffney
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Differential protection scheme has been the most reliable protection for transmission lines to the extent that several utilities have standardized on this protection scheme. When new industrial loads tap onto an existing line, cost and speed to market play a major role in project decision. Telecommunication circuit costs millions and adds to the construction time for the project. This paper discusses the lessons learnt from recent projects and proposes a bold solution which integrates the benefits of overreaching and underreaching schemes when the customer station did not have any communication infrastructure.Paper starts with project summary, driver, and scope. Paper discusses standard communication scheme, project constraints and alternate solutions considered on two similar projects. Donut protection scheme is introduced, covering different fault scenarios. Details of protection design, settings, logic and coordination schemes are outlined. Lessons learnt from protection and control are then presented taking into consideration results from engineering analysis during front end loading and detailed engineering stages such as relay impact studies, planning stability analysis, facility studies, free path studies. Benefits of the scheme including reliability, selectivity, speed, simplicity and cost are also presented. Paper concludes by highlighting the need for such creative customer centric solutions, especially for temporary loads.

title: Gaussian Adaptive Patching Powered Fully-Connected Spatial-Temporal Graph for Multivariate Time-Series Data
authors: Yucheng Wang, Min Wu, Yuecong Xu, Jianfei Yang, Xiaoli Li, Lihua Xie, Zhenghua Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Multivariate Time-Series (MTS) data is crucial across diverse domains. With its sequential and multi-source (e.g., sensors) properties, MTS data inherently exhibits Spatial-Temporal (ST) dependencies, involving temporal correlations over time and spatial correlations between sensors. While Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been widely adopted to exploit these dependencies, existing methods typically capture spatial and temporal dependencies separately, overlooking correlations between Different sEnsors at Different Times (DEDT). Ignoring such correlations limits the comprehensive modeling of ST dependencies and hinders effective MTS representation learning. To address this, we propose a Fully-Connected Spatial-Temporal Graph Neural Network (FC-STGNN), which introduces two key components: FC graph construction and FC graph convolution. In graph construction, we design an FC graph that links all sensors at all times, with edge weights refined according to temporal distances. This design enables comprehensive modeling of ST dependencies by explicitly capturing correlations between DEDT. To exploit this graph, we introduce FC graph convolution with moving-pooling GNN layers, effectively capturing the ST dependencies for MTS representation learning. However, FC-STGNN relies on fixed-size patching, which may limit its ability to capture optimal local patterns for FC graph construction. To overcome this, we extend the framework to GAP-STGNN by incorporating Gaussian Adaptive Patching (GAP), dynamically learning patches with adaptive receptive fields to better capture local patterns. GAP further integrates an adaptive patch selection module that identifies informative patches while softly attenuating less relevant ones to maintain temporal continuity. Extensive experiments on multiple MTS datasets demonstrate that FC-STGNN and GAP-STGNN effectively capture comprehensive ST dependencies with improved FC graphs, achieving superior performance compared to SOTA methods. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Frank-Wang-oss/GAP-STGNN</uri>.

title: Is Generative AI an Illegal Crutch or Tutor in Generation Z's Learning Processes?
authors: Gábor Sterczl
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: The emergence of generative artificial intelligence (Gen AI) is currently one of the most serious challenges facing higher education, if not the most serious one. There is considerable uncertainty surrounding the extent and transparency of student use of Gen AI, thereby reinforcing the phenomenon known as “shadow learning.” This study aims to explore the habits, transparency, and motivations of Generation Z students in higher education regarding their use of generative artificial intelligence (Gen AI). The research focuses on attitudes toward academic integrity and how daily screen time and social media use affect these factors. The study was based on a voluntary, anonymous online questionnaire conducted in October 2025. The research reached the target group using snowball sampling, preceded by a preliminary pilot study. The cleaned sample consisted of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=363$</tex> active university students in Hungary (68.1 % male, 31.9 % female), 99.7 % of whom belong to the generation cohort born after 1996, so the sample can be considered representative of the age group under study. Data processing was performed using a mixed methodology. Quantitative analysis was performed in a Python environment (Pandas, SciPy): in addition to descriptive statistics, correlations between groups (e.g., gender, frequency of use, citation propensity) were examined using the chi-square test and Spearman's correlation analysis (at a significance level of p < 0.05). Qualitative responses to open-ended questions were processed using systematic thematic analysis based on hybrid coding in order to reveal semantic structures. The advancement of digital technologies has introduced significant challenges to educational systems, affecting both primary and higher education institutions. The results show that the integration of generative artificial intelligence into the everyday lives of Generation Z is at an advanced level: 87.4 % of respondents (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}=363$</tex>) use these tools at least weekly, with 43.0 % using them on a daily basis. An analysis of the purposes of use clearly supports the dominance of technology in education: the most frequently mentioned purpose was learning, followed by research and information gathering.

title: Design and Characterization of a Differential UWB LNA Intended for Space Applications
authors: Aishwarya Harneer Suresh, Gunter Fischer, Denys Martynenko, Falk Korndoerfer, Corrado Carta
abstract: This paper presents an Ultra-Wideband (UWB) (6 – 10 GHz) differential low-noise amplifier (LNA) intended for space applications with harsh environmental conditions (e.g. cosmic irradiation). It achieves a noise figure (NF) of 2.8 dB while maintaining a gain of 13.5 dB over the whole frequency band and showing a power dissipation of 19.5 mW. Two versions of the LNA were fabricated in IHP SG13G2 technology and IHP SG13G2Cu technology differing in the back-end-of-line (BEOL) metallization stack. A detailed investigation will show the pros and cons of the aluminum (Al) based metallization stack compared to the copper (Cu) based metallization stack. Advantages and disadvantages of each result in very similar performance of the two LNA versions.

title: IB-Mix: Information Bottleneck and Mixup for Class-Imbalanced Image Classification
authors: Wisdom Xornam Ativi, Wenyu Chen, Lazarus Kwao, Williams Ayivi
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Class imbalance causes deep classifiers to produce poorly structured representations for minority classes, leading to unstable decision boundaries and calibration errors. Existing solutions adjust sampling, loss weights, or logits, but operate after feature formation and leave representation collapse unaddressed. We introduce IB-Mix, a representation-level framework combining Information Bottleneck (IB) compression with classdependent Mixup and prototype calibration. IB regularization removes redundant variation from majority features, adaptive interpolation expands minority support in sparse latent regions, and a distribution calibration module aligns minority prototypes with the global embedding structure. Experiments on CIFAR-100-LT, ImageNet-LT, MedMNIST, and ISIC 2019 demonstrate consistent improvements in balanced accuracy and macro-F1 over strong baselines including PaCo, BCL, SADE, and SHIKE, with the largest gains under severe imbalance. These results show that correcting imbalance at the representation level is a viable alternative to post-hoc re-balancing.

title: Robotic Systems Tracking Error Measurement: A Data-Driven Output-Feedback Control Method
authors: Jun Zhao, Qiang Sun, Bingyi Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In industrial robotic systems, the widespread deployment of robots demands cost-effective control solutions, yet most existing control techniques require full state measurements from velocity and acceleration sensors, significantly increasing hardware expenses. Current methods attempting to overcome this limitation, such as observer-based output-feedback designs, introduce additional estimation errors and computational burden, making them less suitable for precise industrial tracking tasks. This paper proposes an adaptive data-driven method for output-feedback robust tracking control (OFRTC) that relies solely on measurable system outputs. The system model is reformulated as an augmented state equation incorporating a unified uncertainty term and the desired trajectory, which converts the tracking error regulation problem into an equivalent optimal control problem without requiring state observers. A modified tracking algebraic Riccati equation (MTARE) is derived and solved online through a novel learning algorithm employing vectorization and Kronecker’s product operations, eliminating the need for the input gain matrix and initial stabilizing control. Experimental validation on a SCARA robot confirms that the proposed method achieves high-precision tracking and improved energy efficiency while substantially reducing hardware complexity by removing velocity and acceleration sensors.

title: A Hierarchical End-to-End Learning Framework for MIMO Load Modulation under Stochastic Channels and Malicious Jamming
authors: Ercong Yu, Qiang Li, Hongyang Chen
abstract: Wireless channels are inherently vulnerable to malicious jamming. Recently, end-to-end (E2E) learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for intelligent physical-layer design, particularly in multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) load modulation array (LMA) systems, due to its channel-adaptive capability. By treating jamming as part of the channel state, we investigate robust E2E communication under adversarial conditions. However, two major challenges hinder practical deployment: (1) the high training and communication overhead caused by frequent retraining under dynamic channels and jamming, and (2) the unrealistic reliance on perfect channel state information (CSI) and feedback in resource-constrained systems. To address these issues, we propose Channel-Adaptive Hierarchical End-to-End learning (CAHE), a framework that reformulates E2E learning under stochastic channels as a dynamic mapping from channel realizations to optimal network parameters. CAHE consists of a channel-adaptive generator layer that dynamically produces encoder and decoder parameters, and an E2E learning layer that deploys these parameters for adaptive transceiver design. To reduce CSI feedback, we introduce a compact channel embedding mechanism for efficient representation. For jamming scenarios, we further propose a differential block-averaged channel estimation method to enable accurate anti-jamming channel estimation. In addition, we design a jamming-resilient network that embeds convolutional neural network (CNN)-learned jamming features into the decoder generator, enhancing perception of the communication state and improving robustness to random and dynamic jamming. Extensive analysis demonstrates the interpretability and feasibility of CAHE, and simulations confirm its significant performance gains over existing schemes.

title: From Analog CT, PT to Digital Process Bus - Hardware-in-the-Loop Protection Studies for Inverter-Dominated Transmission Systems
authors: Ulrich Muenz, Abhishek Banerjee, Alexandr Stinskiy, Suat Gumussoy, Swen Elpelt, Steve Sano, Tapas Barik
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: The global pursuit of decarbonization requires a significant increase in the integration of distributed energy resources (DERs), such as wind turbines and photovoltaic power plants. This transition is reshaping the grid into an inverter-dominated power system. However, inverter-based resources (IBRs) inherently provide limited fault current and may distort voltage waveforms during fault conditions. These characteristics, combined with increased system dynamics, call for new approaches to protection, as conventional schemes may no longer be effective.This paper is a continuation of our work presented last year, where a hardware test bed was introduced and initial results were discussed. Building on that foundation, we now include additional results from the hardware test bed, further validating protection performance in inverter-dominated scenarios. A new series of tests is being carried out in a protection hardware-in-the-loop (PHIL) environment using the OPAL-RT real-time simulation platform. In this setup, process bus communication is employed, and sampled measured values (SMVs) are supplied to protection relays to evaluate their behavior under various system conditions. Compared with analog current and potential transformer interfaces, SMV-based testing more faithfully mirrors modern digital substations while eliminating scaling and wiring limitations.The work is conducted within the U.S. Department of Energy funded project Protection-Inverter Co-Design for 100% Renewable Power Systems (PICO), which includes an application for Hawaiian Electric involving the transmission network of the Big Island grid. The study examines whether traditional protection methods - differential protection and Permissive Over- Reaching Transfer Trip (POTT) - remain viable at 100% IBR penetration, while also exploring advanced configurations combining line differential, POTT, distance, and overcurrent protection. The paper presents system configuration, results from the hardware test bed and PHIL process bus testing, waveform analysis, and key lessons learned.

title: New Energy Investment Strategies in the Electricity Supply Chain under Subsidy Mechanism
authors: Wei Chen, Yuxia Mou, Jing Chen
abstract: This paper investigates new energy investment strategies in the electricity supply chain (ESC) in the context of the global energy transition. A noncooperative–cooperative (nonco–co) biform game framework is used to construct a two-tier ESC model comprising an electricity generator and a retailer. By integrating upstream carbon quota mechanisms, Grandfathering (GM) and Benchmarking (BM), with downstream green certificate subsidies, the study reveals the logic of collaborative new energy investment through cost-sharing and price competition, and clarifies the effects of carbon quota mechanisms on investment decisions, carbon emissions, and corporate profits. The findings are as follows: (1) The BM provides stronger incentives than the GM for generators to expand new energy investment, whereas the GM is more effective in controlling total carbon emissions, keeping them below BM levels. (2) Under the BM, a higher unit carbon quota drives generators to expand new energy investment, along with a rise in total carbon emissions. From a profit standpoint, when the unit carbon quota is relatively low, both generators and retailers achieve higher profits under the GM; conversely, when the quota level is relatively high, both attain higher profits under the BM. (3) Under both mechanisms, an increase in the green certificate subsidy coefficient raises the cost-sharing ratio of new energy investment, stimulates higher investment volumes, and expands electricity demand. At the same time, the profits of both generators and retailers increase, while total carbon emissions decline; however, it also pushes up both wholesale and retail electricity prices.

title: Switching Impulse Discharge Characteristics of Long Air Gaps with Different Electrodes and Correction in High-Altitude Environment
authors: Zhang Zhijin, Zeng Dong, Jiang Xingliang, Zhang Zhiqin, Hu Jianlin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: The severe atmospheric environment at high altitudes and the variability in electrode shapes of power equipment complicate the insulation design of air gaps. In this paper, switching impulse discharge tests were carried out on 1‒5 m gaps of 15 different electrodes in 5 categories under the natural environment at an altitude of 1500 m. It is verified that the influence of electrode shape on the switching impulse 50% breakdown voltage (U50) of air gaps is independent of the gap distance (d) and absolute humidity (H). The electrode shape can cause the U50 of the 4 m gap to fluctuate in 1051.3‒1178.0 kV. Then, combined with finite element simulation, the spatial electric field non-uniformity of different electrode gaps was studied. The results show that with the increase of non-uniformity, the breakdown voltage of the gap decreases rapidly first, and then tends to be stable. Finally, the mathematical relationship among U50, d, H and electrode curvature radius (r) is obtained, which verifies that the atmospheric factors have the most significant influence on U50, followed by d and r. On this basis, gap factors (0.972–1.083) for different electrode gaps are proposed. The error between the U50 evaluation based on the gap factors and the experimental values is less than 5%.

title: VLA-Touch: Enhancing Vision-Language-Action Model with Dual-Level Tactile Feedback_supp1-3692345.mp4
authors: Jianxin Bi

title: Condition-Number Adaptive-Weight PINN (A-PINN): A High-Fidelity and Real-Time Forward Kinematics Solver for Stewart Platforms_supp1-3692332.mp4
authors: Donglei Sun
abstract: Parallel kinematic mechanisms (PKMs) are widely adopted in precision and heavy-load applications, yet obtaining the requisite forward kinematics (FK) for closed-loop control remains a challenge. FK in PKMs typically lack a unique closed-form solution, necessitating iterative numerical solvers that are prone to ill-conditioning near singular configurations. This letter proposes a condition-number adaptive physics-informed neural network that uses the normalized Jacobian condition number to modulate physics-based loss terms. The training objective enforces an implicit Sobolev-type regularization via operator consistency, encouraging kinematic feasibility while avoiding explicit supervision that depends on unstable Jacobian inversion in ill-conditioned regions. Real-world experiments verify 1 kHz operation as a kinematic observer for real-time velocity estimation, confirming the method's foundation for high-rate feedback and gradient-based control pipelines.

title: Comparing Morphology Features with Genomic Insights in Carcinoma, Nat, and Normal Colon Tissue Samples to Observe Tumor Heterogeneity: A Case Study for a Patient with Colorectal Cancer
authors: Marianna Dimitrova Kucarov, Miklós Vincze, Béla Molnár, Miklos Kozlovszky
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: A tumor diagnostic decision-support platform integrating morphology, morphometrics, and next-generation sequencing data was employed to analyze colorectal tissue samples. Pathologist-curated histological labels classified cell samples into normal, normal-adjacent tissue (NAT), and carcinoma groups. Comparative analyses revealed both intra- and intergroup associations between morphological alterations and chromosomallevel genomic aberrations across tumor progression. Normal epithelial cells displayed an average chromosomal mutation load of 4.70%, while -contrary to expectations - NAT samples exhibited markedly higher levels of 23.70%, approaching the 28.17% observed in carcinoma cells when compared with the human reference genome GRCh38.

title: Silicon Interposer with Advanced RF Technology Modules for BiCMOS Wafer-Level Packaging
authors: Matthias Wietstruck, Patrick Krüger, Thomas Voß, Sebastian Schulze, Muhammad F. Bashir, Selin Tolunay Wipf, Emre C. Durmaz, Kanaka Joy, Nafis Hasnayen, Batuhan Sütbas
abstract: This paper demonstrates a silicon interposer technology with advanced RF wafer-level packaging modules for mm-wave and sub-THz applications. A silicon interposer technology with an optimized BEOL is developed to provide low loss transmission lines with a minimum number of metal layers. In addition, a wafer-level packaging platform is demonstrated, which enables low-loss interconnections with an insertion loss <0.5 dB up to 170 GHz based on C2W Al-Al bonding interconnections. The silicon interposer technology can be applied as a versatile high performance BiCMOS chiplet and heterogeneous integration platform for sub-THz communication, radar and imaging applications.

title: Parameter-Efficient and Prompt-Free Adaptation of SAM3 for Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
authors: Kaizheng Wang, Xiaorun Li, Shuhan Chen
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Remote sensing semantic segmentation plays a crucial role in land-cover mapping, urban planning, and environmental monitoring. While vision foundation models like Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) have demonstrated remarkable generalization capabilities, adapting them to remote sensing imagery faces two critical challenges: the prohibitive computational cost of full fine-tuning and the infeasibility of manual prompt engineering for dense prediction tasks. To overcome these limitations, we propose PEP-SAM, a parameter-efficient and prompt-free framework designed to adapt the frozen SAM3 effectively. Our approach introduces a dual-path architecture that combines a frozen SAM3 ViT-H backbone with a lightweight trainable ResidualAdapter, bridged by Prior Injection Modules (PIM) for domain-specific feature alignment. To eliminate the need for manual prompts, we design a Self-Prompt Learning (SPL) module that autonomously generates task-adaptive prompt tokens through bidirectional attention interaction. Furthermore, we introduce a Multi-Scale Feature Aggregation (MSFA) decoder with mutual calibration blocks to refine multi-scale segmentation details. Experiments on the LoveDA dataset demonstrate that PEP-SAM achieves 53.1% mIoU using only 3.63% trainable parameters, while enabling fully automatic inference without user-provided prompts.

title: STCM: Structure-Temporal Collaborative Modeling for Cryptocurrency Laundering Detection
authors: Qiwen Liu, Guang Li, Jieying Zhou, Weigang Wu
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: In recent years, illicit cryptocurrency activities remain highly prevalent, with illegal transaction volumes increasing annually, causing substantial losses to blockchain-related platforms. Cryptocurrency's decentralized and pseudonymous nature enables criminals to better conceal fund transfer paths, making money laundering detection and tracking increasingly challenging. Most existing detection methods focus solely on the topology structure between accounts within the money laundering transaction network, while overlooking the crucial role that temporal information plays in such networks. To address these limitations, we propose a collaborative behavior-aware money laundering detection method (STCM) based on joint structuretime modeling. We first construct candidate groups for different transaction behaviors based on account structural and temporal features. We then jointly model structural interaction features and temporal dynamic features for these candidate groups. After model-based detection of these groups, a risk aggregation mechanism propagates the risk of collaborative behavior to the nodes, yielding a final money laundering score for each account. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method significantly improves the accuracy of money laundering detection while maintaining robustness across datasets.

title: Application for Pseudocode Interpretation and Code Analysis
authors: Lóránt Áron Perger, Ádám Pintér
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: The following paper describes the implementation of an application capable of interpreting and executing algorithms written in the “Pseudocode” programming language taught at Óbuda University. The process is explained, starting with the formalization of the language's rules from an informal specification, followed by the methods of transforming source code into an executable format, and concluded with showing the means of allowing students to look into the inner workings of the running code.

title: New Energy Investment Strategies in the Electricity Supply Chain under Subsidy Mechanism_supp1-3693120.docx
authors: Wei Chen
abstract: This paper investigates new energy investment strategies in the electricity supply chain (ESC) in the context of the global energy transition. A noncooperative–cooperative (nonco–co) biform game framework is used to construct a two-tier ESC model comprising an electricity generator and a retailer. By integrating upstream carbon quota mechanisms, Grandfathering (GM) and Benchmarking (BM), with downstream green certificate subsidies, the study reveals the logic of collaborative new energy investment through cost-sharing and price competition, and clarifies the effects of carbon quota mechanisms on investment decisions, carbon emissions, and corporate profits. The findings are as follows: (1) The BM provides stronger incentives than the GM for generators to expand new energy investment, whereas the GM is more effective in controlling total carbon emissions, keeping them below BM levels. (2) Under the BM, a higher unit carbon quota drives generators to expand new energy investment, along with a rise in total carbon emissions. From a profit standpoint, when the unit carbon quota is relatively low, both generators and retailers achieve higher profits under the GM; conversely, when the quota level is relatively high, both attain higher profits under the BM. (3) Under both mechanisms, an increase in the green certificate subsidy coefficient raises the cost-sharing ratio of new energy investment, stimulates higher investment volumes, and expands electricity demand. At the same time, the profits of both generators and retailers increase, while total carbon emissions decline; however, it also pushes up both wholesale and retail electricity prices.

title: Radar Target Simulator for Continuous-Wave Radar Applications Based on Direct Vector Modulation
authors: Bartosz Tegowski, Lennart Tobaben, Alexander Koelpin
abstract: This contribution deals with a radar target simulator (RTS) for continuous-wave (CW) radar applications. It relies on an attenuator-based vector modulator directly operating at the carrier frequency of the CW radar without requiring auxiliary local oscillator signals as well as down- and upconverters. Based on a calibration, the RTS adjusts arbitrary phase shifts and amplitude levels in a dynamic range of at least 40 dB enabling the replay of signal modulations introduced in real-world applications. Validation measurements of a 24-GHz prototype prove the effectiveness of the concept in characterizing the performance of CW radar sensors and demonstrate its capability to emulate real-world physical targets, which is demonstrated for the example of vital-sign sensing.

title: A 140 GHz Aperture-Coupled Stacked Patch Antenna in Multilayer PCB Technology
authors: Luca Valenziano, Thomas Zwick, Akanksha Bhutani
abstract: This paper presents an 140 GHz aperture-coupled stacked patch antenna based on a multilayer printed circuit board process, mainly designed for upcoming 6G Device-to-Device (D2D) communication applications. The measurement results of three samples are discussed and evaluated with the corresponding simulations. The antenna achieves a return loss better 10 dB from 126.5 GHz up to 147.2 GHz, resulting in a bandwidth of 20.7 GHz, corresponding to a relative bandwidth of 15.13%. A maximum gain of 7.0 dBi was measured at 136.4 GHz. The influence due to the measurement setup and fabrication tolerances is discussed. This work demonstrates a convenient solution for a scalable high-frequency antenna design with mass production capability. As a result, this concept provides a solution for upcoming D2D communication systems at frequencies around 140 GHz.

title: A Biaxial MEMS Capacitive Nanoforce Sensor Based on a Novel Bistable Bioinspired Swallow Structure for Somatic Cell Micromanipulation
authors: Wendi Gao, Yunfei Bai, Wenji Cui, Yujie Yang, Dehua Liu, Tao Luo, Libo Zhao, Dong Sun
abstract: Precise measurement of ultralow biaxial forces at the nano-Newton (nN) level is highly demanded for robotic micromanipulation of somatic cells. A novel microelectromechanical system capacitive nanoforce sensor was proposed to address this demand. Inspired by the stable gliding gestures of a swallow, a bistable swallow structure was introduced, offering high deformation flexibility with negligible mechanical cross-axis coupling. Considering its mechanical behaviors, a comb configuration and interconnection strategy were proposed to eliminate internal electrostatic coupling rotation. The fabricated sensor was packaged with a time-division multiplexing-based readout circuit, comprising a compact measurement system. The x-axis force sensitivity reached 0.9893 fF/nN, with a high resolution of 0.3658 nN and a linearity of 0.9994 over a range of 8.02 μN. Besides, the y-axis force sensitivity reached 0.2683 fF/nN, with a resolution of 0.8244 nN and a linearity of 0.9995 over a range of 8.11 μN. The noise floor was lower than 0.0665 nN/$\sqrt {\mathbf{Hz}} $ and 0.1688 nN/$\sqrt {\mathbf{Hz}} $ for x- and y-axes, respectively, and the cross-axis coupling error ratios were only 0.15% and 0.36%, respectively. The proposed sensor was adopted to manipulate mouse myoblast cells, allowing nN-level biaxial interaction force responses to be measured in real-time robotic manipulation for the first time.

title: Detection of Single-Phase Electric Arc Short-Circuit Faults in High-Voltage Transmission Lines Based on the Hilbert Transform
authors: Ali Rahmatifar, Mahyar Abasi, Ali Asghar Ghadimi
abstract: Rapid and reliable detection of single-phase arcing short-circuit faults in high-voltage transmission lines remains a fundamental challenge in power system protection. The unstable and nonlinear behavior of the secondary arc following circuit breaker opening can disrupt the critical automatic reclosing process, while model-based or frequency-domain methods often face limitations in handling the fast dynamics of such faults. This study proposes a novel single-terminal detection framework to address this issue, grounded in precise analysis of the faulted-phase voltage signal. The core methodology relies on the dedicated application of the Hilbert transform, which serves as the foundational tool by constructing an analytic signal in the phase space. This enables accurate, instantaneous computation of the signal’s frequency and envelope energy. The algorithm is structured in six key steps: Following extraction of the raw fault voltage data, the signal is segmented into two consecutive sliding windows after removal of initial switching transients. The Hilbert transform is then applied to the voltage signal, and the total envelope energy of the analytic signal is calculated in each window through integration of the squared magnitude. The varying nature of the envelope energy in arcing faults, contrasted with its relatively constant behavior in solid (metallic) faults, forms the basis for discrimination. From a physical perspective, the proposed indices reflect the transition of the system from the healthy operating state to the arcing fault condition. Under normal operation and in permanent (metallic) faults, the envelope of the analytic voltage signal remains nearly stationary, and its energy stays almost constant in consecutive analysis windows. In contrast, the random ignition and extinction of the electric arc introduce noticeable fluctuations in the voltage waveform, resulting in significant variations in the envelope energy. Accordingly, the proposed indices measure the relative change of the envelope energy between successive windows. To increase the robustness of the method against variations in line voltage and different operating conditions, two normalized indices, namely the relative sensitivity index and the mean-centered index, are introduced, which represent the energy variations in a relative manner. The final decision logic employs a threshold applied to these indices; while this threshold is not entirely independent of all network conditions, its dependence is substantially reduced due to the normalized formulation, allowing a consistent discrimination margin across a wide range of operating scenarios. The proposed method was validated through extensive simulations encompassing single-phase arcing faults with varying arc resistances as well as permanent faults. Sensitivity analysis and simulation results demonstrate that this energy- and instantaneous-frequency-based approach, while maintaining the simplicity of single-ended implementation and eliminating the need for two-terminal communication, achieves significantly higher detection accuracy compared to existing methods. It thereby provides a practical solution to ensure reliable operation of automatic reclosing mechanisms under diverse operating conditions.

title: ISAC-assisted Robust, Low Overhead Analog Beam Selection in mmWave Channels with Blockages
authors: Jashaswini Bhuyan, Neelesh B. Mehta
abstract: We evaluate the efficacy of an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)-assisted analog beam selection (IAABS) and tracking scheme with a low channel sounding overhead. We do so for a more realistic channel model that accounts for the presence or absence of the line-of-sight (LoS) path and its time evolution. When the LoS path is detected, an extended Kalman filter (EKF) tracks the angle, speed, and distance of the vehicle and the base station (BS) sends data along the beam direction closest to the angle estimated by the EKF. However, when the LoS path is blocked, the BS sounds in all possible directions to exploit non-LoS paths for communication and the EKF stops its updation step. Our numerical results show the importance of adapting the EKF based on LoS blockage detection. IAABS achieves a throughput close to digital beamforming despite its lower hardware complexity and outperforms conventional full channel sounding.

title: Development of a Database-Driven Simulation Environment for District Heating Systems in Hungary
authors: Ágnes Gerse, Adrienn Dineva, Rita Fleiner
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: District heating is seen as a key contributor to the decarbonization of the heat sector and by means of sectorcoupling technologies, a potential source of flexibility for variable renewable energy integration. To provide the basis for a database-driven simulations, applicable for renewable energy integration and sector-coupling studies, a modeling database was set up for Hungary, based entirely on open-access data. Aiming at a bottom-up modeling approach covering all heat production sites and heat supplier companies by settlements, a detailed inventory of heat production equipment was compiled that can serve as a basis for modeling heat production from the available local heat sources. For the demand side, a heat demand model was set up where parameters were estimated from historical annual energy data and temperature time series. As application examples, data analytics use cases are presented for the production infrastructure and heat demand simulation.

title: Model-Based Indirect Load Measurement for Aircraft Landing Gear Using FBG Sensors and Dynamic Graph Networks
authors: Xinda Yang, Hang Du, Lianqing Zhu, Chaofan Deng, Junchi Zhao, Zhiqiang Guo, Mingxin Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate measurement and quantitative evaluation of aircraft landing gear loads are essential for ensuring structural integrity and flight safety. Since no dedicated sensor can directly measure landing gear loads, the assessment must rely on indirect estimation from strain responses obtained by strain sensors. However, most conventional and learning-based approaches fail to model the spatial topology of measurement points or to account for variations caused by different buffer-stroke conditions, resulting in calibration inconsistency and reduced measurement precision. To address these limitations, we propose a model-based indirect load-measurement method that employs a Dynamic Graph Network (DGN) to estimate loads from distributed strain data. By integrating channel-wise self-attention with dynamic graph convolution, the model captures both local and global dependencies in the strain-load relationship, thereby enhancing robustness across varying buffer strokes. Ground load calibration tests were performed on a full-scale aircraft right landing gear, where FBG sensors measured strain responses associated with longitudinal (X), vertical (Y), and lateral (Z) load components under three representative buffer-stroke settings. Using K-fold cross-validation (K= 14), the proposed approach achieved high-accuracy load estimation with mean absolute percentage errors (MAPE) of (4.125 ± 1.743)%, (1.347 ± 0.421)%, and (4.196 ± 1.419)% in the X, Y, and Z directions, respectively. Furthermore, the overall measurement uncertainty of our bulit indirect load-measurement system was experimentally determined to be 2.5%, which indicates a compact overall uncertainty level for the proposed indirect measurement system and reflects the stable performance of the method. Source code is available at https://github.com/Mu-Tang/CSA-DGCN.

title: From Drafting to Data Driven Design : Key Considerations when Modernizing Protection and Control Designs using Next-Generation Intelligent Tools
authors: Justin Turner, Sean Mulherrin, Jared Driggers, Ed Grause
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: The electrical power industry is entering a period of unprecedented growth, with U.S. demand projected to rise at rates two to six times higher than historical trends, driven by data centers, electric vehicles, and the electrification of industrial, commercial, and residential systems. This acceleration is exposing the limits of legacy protection and control (PAC) design tools, which have remained largely unchanged for more than two decades. Electrical power system design projects are reaching record levels, even as workforce losses outpace new hires, forcing PAC engineers and designers to deliver more with fewer personnel. To meet future design challenges, PAC engineers and designers should consider evaluating, adopting, and implementing next-generation intelligent platforms that enable data-driven workflows, enhanced automation, and integrated digital twins. This paper outlines key considerations for modernizing PAC design processes, including evaluation and implementation timelines, pilot project strategies, staff onboarding, template development, 3D modeling, technical support, change management, and integration with enterprise systems. Furthermore, the paper examines how modern design tools can be integrated into broader IT infrastructures such as enterprise resource planning (ERP) and product data management (PDM) systems, panel manufacturing equipment, digital substation design, and other design platforms to create a more connected and automated engineering process. This paper highlights how intelligent tools can deliver results such as single line and threeline schematics, wiring diagrams, wire numbering, error checking, bill of materials (and other report) generation, and integrated smart objects in a centralized database. Smart objects let designers reuse devices from a database across a project, keeping the device connected through a database rather than placing disjointed graphic symbols. The paper also addresses legacy data challenges and proposes strategies for phased migration into new platforms. Finally, a look into what features could be enhanced in the future with the assistance of AI will be examined.

title: Ultra-Wideband Sub-THz FMCW Radar with Chirp Linearization
authors: Kaveh Moeinimaleki, Christoph Wagner, Andreas Springer, Lothar Ratschbacher, Sandrine van Frank, Ivan Alic, Reinhard Feger
abstract: Frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar is attractive for range sensing and thickness metrology, but limited sweep bandwidth constrains range resolution. Photomixing two continuous-wave lasers on a photoconductive antenna generates an intermediate-frequency (IF) beat signal whose center frequency equals their optical frequency offset and whose sweep follows the applied laser modulation. We introduce a target-free chirp-linearization method that routes a copy of the transmit signal through an fiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer with 8.9ns delay, producing a low-IF monitor that encodes the instantaneous chirp frequency. This delay-encoded readout enables per-ramp chirp estimation and digital linearization while keeping the required IF bandwidth small. We validate the approach for fixed-stand-off thickness sensing using paper samples. With 1ms triangular ramps centered at 140 GHz and sweeping 35 GHz, we achieve a root-mean-square error of 33.18 μm over 7,000 ramps. The architecture, based on direct laser modulation without external RF, is promising for future compact chip-scale photonic implementations.

title: Secure OFDM Waveform Design for ISAC: Artificial Phase-Doppler Shifts Against Passive Sensing
authors: Umut Utku Erdem, Lucas Giroto, Tobias Chaloun, Tom Schipper, Taewon Jeong, Christian Karle, Benjamin Nuss, Thomas Zwick
abstract: This paper proposes a novel low probability of intercept (LPI) waveform design approach for orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM)-based integrated sensing and communication systems by introducing artificial phase and Doppler shifts. These controlled impairments, unknown to eavesdroppers, effectively disrupt passive radar processing and intercept attempts. At legitimate receivers, they can be fully compensated, so that standard OFDM communication and sensing performance are preserved. To support the effectiveness of the proposed LPI waveform design for OFDM-based ISAC, measurement results with 1 GHz bandwidth at 27 GHz are presented considering different impairment introduction approaches, all with no impact on cooperative system performance, and compensation capabilities at the eavesdropper.

title: Minimax Optimal Sample Complexity for Iterated CVaR Reinforcement Learning with a Generative Model
authors: Zilong Deng, Alvaro Velasquez, Shaofeng Zou
abstract: Standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms are typically designed to maximize the expected accumulative reward, which may be inadequate in scenarios where risk sensitivity is critical. In this work, the problem of risk-sensitive RL with Iterated Conditional Value at Risk is studied, where the objective is to optimize outcomes under a specified risk level τ at each step. This work provides the first minimax optimal sample complexity analysis for this problem with a generative model. Specifically, the sample complexity is firstly characterized as a function of the number of states S, actions A, and effective horizon (1−γ)−1 (resp. horizon H in the finite-horizon setting), and is further shown to be minimax optimal via a novel minimax lower bound analysis when the risk level 0 < τ ≤ 1 is treated as a constant or the risk level is above a threshold of γ (resp. 1−1/H in the finite horizon setting). For the case when the risk level is small, the limiting case of τ → 0, termed worst-path RL, is then studied, and the minimax optimal sample complexity is also theoretically characterized.

title: Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)

title: Simultaneous Measurement of the Soot Concentration and Flame Temperature Distribution through a Novel Hybrid Reconstruction Algorithm Based on Multispectral Light Field Imaging
authors: Jinjian Li, Biao Zhang, Yizhi Huang, Jian Li, Chuanlong Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Light field imaging captures multidimensional information in a single exposure and is promising for flame diagnostics in industrial systems with limited optical access. However, existing imaging systems based on a Colored Plenoptic Camera (CPC) suffer from fixed spectral channels with overly broad bandwidths. These limitations lead to inaccurate spectral information acquisition, compromising the accuracy of soot concentration and flame temperature measurements. To address these issues, this paper proposes a method for the simultaneous three-dimensional (3D) measurement of soot concentration and flame temperature based on multispectral light field imaging. Spectral information acquisition is improved by optimizing the number and bandwidth of spectral channels, and a novel algorithm—Levenberg-Marquardt with weighted least squares and weighted non-negative least squares (LMWLS-WNNLS)—is developed to support accurate reconstruction under the multispectral framework. In this algorithm, the WLS component handles intermediate variables to reduce computational complexity, while reconstruction equations are adaptively weighted by spectral radiative intensity to improve noise robustness. Algorithm performance was verified through simulations. A four-channel spectral light field measurement system was established, and experimental validation was conducted on an ethylene diffusion flame. The results show that increasing the channel number and narrowing the channel bandwidth reduce model sensitivity. Consequently, the maximum errors in soot concentration and temperature were reduced to 2.22 ppm and 64.98 K, with maximum uncertainties of 0.98 ppm and 27.98 K, respectively. Compared with two-channel case, the errors decreased by 60.80 % and 5.04 %. These results demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method for flame diagnostics in industrial combustion.

title: Validation of Integrated Microgrid Building Blocks Using At-Scale PHIL and CHIL Environments
authors: Sang Won Seo, Sanjeev Pannala, Bidur Poudel, Andy Olson, Nishcal Guruwacharya, Yashodhan Agalgaonkar, Rob Hovsapian
publish: 2026 IEEE Power and Energy Conference at Illinois (PECI)
abstract: This paper presents real-time validation of Microgrid Building Blocks (MBBs) using integrated Power Hardware-in-the-Loop (PHIL) and Controller Hardware-in-the-Loop (CHIL) testing. The MBB framework enables scalable, standardized microgrid deployment through modular control, communication, and power conversion blocks. A laboratory platform combining physical hardware with a digital real-time simulator was used to validate four key functions: dynamic decoupling during upstream grid faults, optimal power flow under variable load and generation, coordination among multiple microgrids, and decentralized restoration of a neighboring microgrid following a blackout. The test system comprises two microgrids based on modified IEEE 13-bus feeders with photovoltaics, battery energy storage systems, and diesel generators. PHIL and CHIL interfaces link real-time simulated assets with hardware controllers and field equipment to assess performance under realistic conditions.

title: Double Layer Interdigital-Pin Groove Gap D-Band Waveguide Filter
authors: Daniel Miek, Kennet Braasch, Patrick Boe, Michael Höft
abstract: This paper describes an approach to increase the operating frequency of groove gap waveguide (GGW) components while maintaining the CNC milling manufacturing requirements with respect to the cutter diameter. The approach is demonstrated by a fourth order filter. The proposed filter is implemented using the interdigital-pin approach, with a double layer structure for the upper as well as the lower part. This combination is denoted as double layer interdigital-pin and allows for smaller distances between the pins compared to single-layer interdigital pins without requiring a smaller cutter diameter. The overall approach as well as the filter design strategy are discussed. Furthermore, the manufacturing technique is described and the measurement results of a GGW filter in the D-band are presented.

title: Analysis of an Integer-N Frequency Synthesizer for Trapped Ion Quantum Computers
authors: Marvin Jaeger, Simon Schulz, Alexander Koelpin
abstract: Qubit implementations like trapped ions can be controlled by microwave signals in the gigahertz range, which require a local oscillator (LO) for upconversion. The LO superimposes a static frequency offset and phase noise, both leading to errors in qubit gate operation. Both error terms can be reduced by implementing the LO as an integer-N frequency synthesizer, where a phase-locked loop controls and stabilizes the frequency. An exemplary integer-N frequency synthesizer is realized and its performance for quantum computers is characterized in this work. The qubit fidelity calculated based on phase noise measurement results is above 99.943% for primitive π-gates.

title: Integrated Sensing-Positioning- Communication and Application in Smart Railway Systems
authors: Gang Liu, Hanli Jiang, Qianqian Zhang, Haifeng Zhang, Rui Yang, Zheng Ma, F. Richard Yu, Pingzhi Fan, George K. Karagiannidis, Ming Xiao
abstract: The goal of smart railway systems is to achieve higher speed, intelligence, and automation, which has higher requirements on intelligent sensing, precise positioning, and reliable communications. However, traditional railway sensing technologies such as Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) and video detection, positioning technologies such as balises and Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), and communication technologies such as Global System for Mobile Communications-Railway (GSM-R) and Long Term Evolution-Railway (LTE-R), are independently designed and deployed, which not only results in high construction and maintenance costs but also hinders the effective integration and cooperation among multiple technologies. To fill this gap, this article first identifies the use cases of communication, positioning, and sensing for smart railways, and then analyzes the potential techniques to support integrated sensing-positioning-communications (ISPC), including NR-V2X, Wi-Fi, 5G-Advanced (5G-A)/6G, and Secondary Surveillance Radar (SSR), and also discusses their applicability to smart railway systems. As a case study, we develop a safety-oriented SSR-based integrated communications and positioning system for the train, which supports train-to-train/ground communication and ranging by sharing the same onboard SSR. In addition, to achieve seamless and continuous location of the train, a positioning system based on a loosely coupled architecture is designed and implemented by fusing SSR ranging data and Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) data via an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF). Through the real data from extensive field tests, it is validated that reliable localization and trainto- train/ground data exchange can be achieved simultaneously. Finally, several challenges and future research directions in ISPC are highlighted, offering new insights for the development of ISPC-enabled smart railway systems.

title: Wi-Fi Perception by Using Multiple Kernel-Based Learning for Robust Positioning
authors: Xifeng Li, Hongkun Du, Yunqi Jia, Lu Xu, Shenjie Tang
abstract: The expansion of the Internet of Things has driven significant growth in wireless applications. Advancements in Wi-Fi and its evolution have raised the importance of Wi-Fi based localization. However, contemporary machine learning approaches often lack the ability to dynamically capture reliable features in real-world situations where signal fluctuations exist, resulting in reduced robustness and accuracy. To address this issue, we introduce an information-theoretic method for robust and precise Wi-Fi based localization under uncertainty. Concretely, we adopt a Laplace-Student's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t$</tex-math></inline-formula> mixture correntropy (LTMC) error model to boost accuracy and robustness, coupled with an enhanced entropy-K-means Nyström (EEK-Nyström) sparse model to limit neural network expansion. The process consists of two stages: offline training and online localization. In the offline stage, we construct a mixed-kernel correntropy model employing both Laplace and Student's <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t$</tex-math></inline-formula> kernels as the cost function and derive its sparse variant via an entropy-enhanced Nyström method. In the online stage, we use the mixed correntropy model to evaluate fingerprint similarity and apply a mixed-correntropy-driven adaptive filtering algorithm to enhance localization precision. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm delivers superior accuracy and robustness, achieving an average error of 1.15 m under real-world disturbances following impulsive and Gaussian distributions. By utilizing existing Wi-Fi systems, this approach provides a cost-efficient means of target localization and paves the way for forthcoming wireless sensing applications.

title: A Device Multiplexing DC Chopper Topology for VSC-HVDC System
authors: Wentao Li, Shunliang Wang, Hao Tu, Junpeng Ma, Ning Jiao, Tianqi Liu, Huabo Shi, Yufei Teng
abstract: The dc chopper (DCC) plays a critical role in the fault ride-through (FRT) process of voltage source converter-based high voltage direct current (VSC-HVDC) systems. To reduce the cost of DCC, this paper proposes a device multiplexing DCC topology (DM DCC). Through device multiplexing, the proposed DM DCC achieves submodule (SM) activation/bypass and SM capacitor charging/discharging with only one insulated gate bipolar transistor (IGBT), thereby reducing the number of IGBTs by 50% compared to the traditional hybrid DCC. A control strategy is proposed for DM DCC to reduce the energy absorbed by the SM capacitor in one control period. Therefore, the capacity of the water-cooling system is reduced. Economic analysis indicates that the proposed scheme achieves at least 24.16% cost reduction compared to the traditional solutions. The effectiveness of the DM DCC and its control strategy is verified through simulation, hardware-in-the-loop (HIL), and downscaled hardware experiment results.

title: Photovoltaic Power Generation Prediction Based on Statistical Characteristics of Data
authors: Wei Yang, Li Jia, Hao Zhou, Yang Zhou
abstract: As a prominent alternative for fossil energy, solar photovoltaic (PV) resource plays a key role during the migration process of energy supply structure. However, the prediction of its electric power production (EPP) data possesses randomness and instability due to environmental changes. Therefore, a novel framework is proposed which consists of a two-stage hierarchical clustering and optimal prediction model selection. According to the stability characteristics of EPP data, initial rough clusters are attained with Fuzzy C-means clustering through the first stage. The following second stage clustering based on hierarchical clustering is applied to finally generate three clusters of stable, weakly fluctuated and strongly fluctuated EPP data. Afterwards, three properly constructed prediction models have been implemented accordingly. A CNN-LSTM quantile regression model is proposed for the prediction of stable EPP data with consistent distributions. Yet for weakly fluctuated type, D-Vine copula quantile regression is applied to explore correlations between PV characteristics, which then helps to describe the specific distributions underneath. Since strongly fluctuated type of data tends to exhibit extreme variations and randomness, probability generation model combined with regression form of D-Vine copula could precisely establish correlations of different characteristics and hence weight the predictions to yield point prediction. Our framework addresses the PV power data with different random fluctuation characteristics by applying tailored prediction models to each type. Comparative experimental results show that the proposed framework has achieved higher prediction interval coverage, lower average interval width and comprehensive evaluation index in various scenarios, verifying its effectiveness and superiority.

title: Dynamic Spatiotemporal Dual-Encoder Transformer for Long-Term Traffic Prediction in LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Xingyu He, Nianci Li, Panxing Huang, Chunhua Gu, Guisong Yang, Yunhuai Liu
