title: Impact of the thermally forced accelerated aging process on lightning properties of ester liquids compared to conventional mineral oil
authors: Filip Stuchala, Pawel Rozga, Ian Hosier
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Transformers are devices built and designed for many years of failure-free operation. Since most of them have paper-oil insulation, it's important to check the condition of the insulation. This paper focuses on dielectric liquids. The purpose of this research is to examine the effects of thermally forced accelerated ageing on the lightning properties of natural (NE) – Paryol Electra 7426 and synthetic (SE) – Naturelle Transformer Fluid S4 I ester liquids in comparison to traditional inhibited mineral oil (IMO) – Nytro 10 XN. The procedure involves heating a liquid to a high temperature of 150°C for 168 hours in accordance with IEEE and CIGRE recommendations, cooling the liquid, and performing the experiments at lightning impulse voltage. These tests included determination of various of parameters characterizing lightning performance of liquids such as lightning impulse breakdown voltage (LIBV) supported by time to breakdown (t<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">b</sub>), inception voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub>) and acceleration voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</sub>). A point-to-sphere electrode system with a 25 mm inter-electrode gap and a negative polarity of lightning impulse voltage is being used for the tests. The measurements are performed using fresh liquids and then after one and two aging cycles. Liquids are also tested for additional aging-related factors such as AC breakdown voltage, dielectric loss factor and acidity. Measurements make allowance for correlation of changes in the lightning properties of liquids under study with the degree of ageing. According to observed data, for all the liquids under tests LIBV and Va decrease with aging. However, more variations are noted in case of mineral oil. This is most noticeable for the acceleration voltage, which for fresh mineral oil (IMO) is 310 kV, and after the second aging cycle (IMO14) 235 kV. For the tested esters, a decrease in this parameter is also noted, but it is not as drastic and amounts to approximately 15 kV for NE and 20 for SE.

title: SADP: Spatially-Aware DCT Priors for Low-Light Image Enhancement
authors: Hao Ke, Canrong Yao, Renyu Huang, Xiaoqi Chen
abstract: Low-light image enhancement (LLIE) remains challenging because insufficient illumination is often accompanied by color distortion, detail loss, and noise amplification. These coupled degradations further vary across spatial regions, making a uniform enhancement strategy prone to suboptimal restoration. To address this issue, we propose SADP, a spatially-aware LLIE framework that explicitly converts local frequency statistics from overlapping block discrete cosine transform (DCT) analysis into spatial priors for degradation-aware enhancement. Specifically, SADP first uses a luminance prior to construct a stable luminance base in the YCbCr space while preserving the original chrominance. It then exploits complementary color, detail, and noise priors in the RGB space to jointly guide degradation-aware RGB restoration. Experimental results on mainstream LLIE datasets demonstrate that SADP achieves high-quality enhancement with competitive efficiency.

title: Sparse Antenna Array with Metagratings for Wide-angle Beamforming and Grating-lobe Suppression
authors: Mai Anh Nguyen, Ariel Epstein, Gangil Byun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This work presents a metagrating (MG)-assisted sparse array based on a unified analytical and practical design framework. A Floquet-Bloch (F-B) modal approach is developed with practical constraints, enabling physically realizable solutions for electrically thin structures with minimum degrees of freedom. Unlike conventional optimization-based synthesis, the method directly predicts active element behavior from modal power distribution, thereby avoiding iterative full-wave optimization. The proposed unit cell is implemented using impedance-matched printed folded monopoles and validated experimentally. The fabricated finite array, with nearly one-wavelength spacing, achieves wide-angle beam scanning up to 50°, with effective grating-lobe suppression and a peak sidelobe level of -11.4 dB, in good agreement with simulations. This work demonstrates a reproducible, physics-driven design methodology for MG-assisted sparse arrays.

title: RSO-YOLO:Traffic Sign Detection Method Based on Reparameterization and Shallow Feature Enhancement
authors: Guowen Chang, Qingzhan Zhao, Xuewen Wang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Traffic sign detection is a critical task in autonomous driving and intelligent transportation systems. However, existing detection methods struggle to achieve an effective balance between accuracy and inference latency, especially in applications requiring small object detection and real-time performance. To address this challenge, this paper proposes an improved YOLO-based traffic sign detection method called RSO-YOLO. This approach enhances shallow feature extraction capabilities by optimizing network structure, deepening the downsampling process, effectively reducing feature loss and improving small object detection accuracy. To further enhance inference efficiency, this paper combines reparameterization techniques with large-kernel convolutions to expand the receptive field while reducing computational costs, significantly improving the detector's inference speed. Experimental results on the GTSDB and TT100K datasets show that the RSO-YOLO-S model achieves 98.2% and 83.6% in mAP50 metrics, representing improvements of 3.8 and 11 percentage points compared to baseline models, while maintaining inference latencies of 11.6ms and 30.3ms. Ablation studies verify the contribution of each module to model performance improvement. This research successfully balances detection accuracy and inference latency while ensuring model lightweight properties, providing a more efficient and reliable solution for visual perception tasks in intelligent transportation systems.

title: Emerging Materials and Enabling Technologies for Advancing Antenna Systems: From Design to Manufacturing
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation

title: Minimizing the Noise in Low-Current Sensing by MOSFET PN-Junction Diode Feedback
authors: Ehsan Semsar Parapari, Marco Bennati, Michele Biondi, Marco Crescentini, Federico Thei
abstract: This letter proposes a transimpedance amplifier (TIA) architecture that minimizes noise for continuous-time (CT) low-current sensing. The approach leverages a MOSFET to realize a pure PN-junction diode as the TIA feedback element, such that MOS channel conduction is completely suppressed. Therefore, channel-induced noise contributions associated with conventional MOS-based feedback TIAs are mitigated, and the input-referred noise is dominated by shot noise due to intrinsic PN-junction mechanisms, which remains ultra-low at pA-level input currents. To enable proper evaluation, the proposed concept is combined with architectural enhancements, ensuring symmetric and linear operation. The implemented TIA prototype, fabricated in a 0.35-μm CMOS process, demonstrates a minimum input-referred current noise density of 1.5 fA/ √ Hz, while providing a transimpedance gain of 100MΩ with a –3 dB bandwidth of 700 kHz.

title: A Wide-Load-Range Pseudo-Constant Frequency Buck Converter With Actual Duty-cycle Control Scheme
authors: Junying Chen, Yongyuan Li, Wei Guo, Yongbo Zhang, Yong You, Zhangming Zhu
abstract: Conventional adaptive on-time (AOT) regulators suffer from switching frequency shift caused by duty-cycle and load variations, as well as instability in different operating mode. Prior woks exhibit limited frequency stabilization capability, particularly across wide load and duty-cycle ranges, and concentrate on continuous conduction mode (CCM) stability compensation but overlook the discontinuous conduction mode (DCM) instability. Targeting on great frequency drift and instability across a wide range of load and duty cycle, an actual duty-cycle control (ADCC) scheme is proposed to achieve enhanced pseudo-constant frequency and system stability both in CCM and DCM. Besides, the ADCC scheme achieves a loop bandwidth of approximately one-third of the switching frequency with a compact implementation, as reflected by the improved FOM. A prototype converter was fabricated in a 180 nm BCD process and tested with low-ESR output capacitors. The measurement results confirm stable operation across the entire load and input range, with the frequency deviation as low as 1.74 kHz/A and 1.68 kHz/V. And the converter achieves a peak efficiency of 92.7% and a significantly improved FOM of 26.

title: SatProxy: Accelerating Web Browsing in LEO Satellite Networks via Loss-hiding
authors: Zeyi Deng, Ze Wang, Lin Wang, Jingjing Zhang, Yue Gao
abstract: Congestion control algorithms (CCAs) are critical for high-speed data transmission and fast web browsing in low Earth orbit satellite networks (LSNs). However, their performance is severely degraded by the high packet loss rates in LSNs-approximately 10× higher than in traditional WANs-caused by long-distance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula>530 km) wireless links and frequent network reconfigurations (every <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula>15 seconds). These losses are mistakenly interpreted by CCAs as signs of congestion, resulting in degraded throughput and longer page load times for web browsing. To address this, we present SatProxy, an efficient and deployable solution that constructs a lossless tunnel over the lossy LSNs via packet retransmission. Fully compatible with the TCP/IP stack, including QUIC, SatProxy enables seamless adoption by end users. Real-world experiments on Starlink demonstrate that SatProxy reduces the packet loss rate by more than 80% (from 0.39% to 0.05%) and increases throughput for Cubic, BBRv3, Vegas, and PCC by 1.86×, 1.21×, 1.20×, and 1.24×, respectively. Moreover, the throughput improvement shortens flow completion times for web applications by up to 47.1%, yielding substantial performance gains in practical LSN deployments.

title: Frequency Doubling Helical-Type Gyro-TWT for Radar Applications
authors: Alexander Marek, Max Vöhringer, John Jelonnek, Jens Klare, Katharina Mayer, Manfred Thumm
abstract: The helical-type gyro-TWT has demonstrated excellent high-power and broadband operation at frequencies up to the W-band, and is also a promising candidate for future applications in the sub-THz frequency range. Recently, an extension of the helical-type gyro-TWT has been proposed, the frequency-doubling gyrotron traveling wave amplifier (FD-GTWT). The FD-GTWT delivers over a broad bandwidth high power and gain while doubling the frequency of the input signal. It is therefore of particular interest for sub-THz frequencies. However, a modified version of the FD-GTWT at lower frequencies could also be of interest for radars that require high power and a broad bandwidth, such as ground based space observation radars. An FD-GTWT may be of particular interest if an existing radar has to be upgraded to higher frequencies, since the signal generation path does not need to be replaced. For such applications, we are investigating for the first time the possibility of FD-GTWTs with center frequencies around 70 GHz that could be operated with existing Ka-band signal generation hardware.

title: A 3-D Efficient Hybrid Magnetic Circuit Modeling Method for Transformer Interturn Short-circuit Faults
authors: Chenguang Yan, Mohan Dai, Hao Zhan, Xiao Yang, Dong Liang, Xiaoning Kang, Baohui Zhang
abstract: As the most prevalent internal transformer faults, interturn short-circuit (ITSC) faults can lead to destructive accidents if not cleared in time. To analyze and prevent ITSC faults, it is necessary to develop an accurate and efficient numerical model. In this paper, a 3-D hybrid modeling method combining lumped and distributed magnetic circuits is proposed, which enables accurate and efficient ITSC fault analysis. Lumped and distributed magnetic circuits are used to represent the main and leakage fluxes, respectively, as the main flux is nearly uniform and the leakage flux is strongly distorted during ITSC faults. The hybrid magnetic circuit is constructed by meshing the 3-D structure of the transformer using four types of elements. To solve the coupled electric-magnetic circuit system, a direct coupling solution algorithm is developed and implemented in Python, achieving ITSC fault calculation within seconds. The proposed method is validated through experiments conducted on a realistic 30 kVA/2 kV three-phase transformer. The experimental and calculated results are in close agreement, demonstrating that the proposed method can accurately reproduce the electrical transient characteristics of ITSC faults with errors less than 1%, thus providing an accurate and efficient numerical approach for the analysis and prevention of transformer ITSC faults.

title: Enhancing Wireless Networks for IoT with Large Vision Models: Foundations and Applications
authors: Yunting Xu, Jiacheng Wang, Ruichen Zhang, Dusit Niyato, Deepu Rajan, Liang Yu, Haibo Zhou, Abbas Jamalipour, Xianbin Wang
abstract: Large vision models (LVMs) have emerged as a foundational paradigm in visual intelligence, achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse visual tasks. Recent advances in LVMs have facilitated their integration into Internet of Things (IoT) scenarios, offering superior generalization and adaptability for vision-assisted network optimization. In this article, we first investigate the functionalities and core architectures of LVMs, highlighting their capabilities across classification, segmentation, generation, and multimodal visual processing. We then explore a variety of LVM applications in wireless communications, covering representative tasks across the physical layer, network layer, and application layer. Furthermore, given the substantial model size of LVMs and the challenges of model retraining in wireless domains, we propose a progressive fine-tuning framework that incrementally adapts pretrained LVMs for joint optimization of multiple IoT tasks. A case study in low-altitude economy networks (LAENets) demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed framework over conventional CNNs in joint beamforming and positioning tasks for Internet of drones, underscoring a promising direction for integrating LVMs into intelligent wireless systems.

title: Fourier Neural Operator Approach for Ionospheric Phase Compensation in L-band SAR Interferometry_supp1-3692846.docx
authors: Kazuki Yanagiya

title: Hybrid Machine Learning Model for Forest Height Estimation from TanDEM-X and Landsat Data
authors: Islam Mansour, Ronny Hänsch, Irena Hajnsek, Konstantinos Papathanassiou
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: The integration of machine learning (ML) with physical models (PM) has emerged as a promising approach for retrieving geophysical parameters from remote sensing data. Recently, a hybrid model was proposed for forest height estimation using TanDEM-X interferometric coherence measurements, where features were constrained to related PMrelated InSAR variables. The features used for training and inversion where selected to ensure the physical consistency of the solutions but appear insufficient to resolve all height / structure and baseline / terrain slope ambiguities. To improve this, the extension of the feature space with optical Landsat data is proposed able to provide complementary information on forest type or structure. This extended model is applied and validated on several TanDEM-X acquisitions over tropical site (Lope) and assessed against airborne LiDAR measurements. Results show a 13.5% reduction in RMSE and a 16.6% reduction in MAE compared to the original hybrid model, confirming the added value of multispectral inputs.

title: Source-Free Domain Adaptive Semantic Segmentation of Remote Sensing Images With Diffusion-Guided Label Enrichment
authors: Wenjie Liu, Hongmin Liu, Lixin Zhang, Bin Fan
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Source-free domain adaptation for remote sensing semantic segmentation is highly challenging due to the inaccessibility of source data and the absence of target annotations during adaptation. In SFDA for semantic segmentation, pseudo-label-based self-training has been widely adopted, but most existing methods refine the full set of pseudo-labels, which often contains substantial noise and thus severely limits their effectiveness. To address this issue, we propose a novel pseudo-label optimization framework, termed Diffusion-Guided Label Enrichment (DGLE), which starts from a small set of reliable seed pseudo-labels and transforms them into complete and high-quality pseudo-labels. Specifically, DGLE first performs confidence filtering on the predictions from the original and super-resolved target images. The retained predictions are then cross-validated and fused to produce the seed pseudo-labels. Building on these sparse seed pseudo-labels, DGLE trains an image-conditioned diffusion model to progressively generate complete and high-quality pseudo-labels with improved regional consistency and boundary integrity. In this way, DGLE avoids directly optimizing the entire noisy pseudo-label set and provides more reliable supervision for target-domain adaptation. Experiments show that DGLE achieves state-of-the-art performance on three SFDA semantic segmentation benchmarks: Vaihingen →Potsdam and Rural →Urban for remote sensing, and GTA5 →Cityscapes for urban street scenes. It surpasses the previous best methods by 2.45%, 2.18%, and 2.5% in mIoU, respectively.

title: Sampling-Free Diffusion Transformers for Low-Complexity MIMO Channel Estimation
authors: Zhixiong Chen, Hyundong Shin, Arumugam Nallanathan
abstract: Diffusion model-based channel estimators have shown impressive performance but suffer from high computational complexity because they rely on iterative reverse sampling. This paper proposes a sampling-free diffusion transformer (DiT)-based channel estimator, termed SF-DiT-CE, for low-complexity MIMO channel estimation. Exploiting angular-domain sparsity of MIMO channels, we train a lightweight DiT to directly predict the true channels from their perturbed observations and noise levels. At inference, we first obtain an initial channel estimate using the least-squares (LS) method, which can be viewed as the true channel corrupted by Gaussian noise. The DiT then takes this estimate and its corresponding noise scale as inputs to recover the channel in a single forward pass, eliminating iterative sampling. Numerical results demonstrate that our method achieves superior estimation accuracy and robustness with significantly lower complexity than state-of-the-art baselines. The code is available at: https://github.com/c-res/SF-DiT-CE.

title: An End-to-End Signal-Level Framework for Multifunction Radar Working Mode Recognition
authors: Yuming Liu, Guolong Cui, Mou Wang, Xianxiang Yu, Lingjiang Kong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Multifunction radar (MFR) systems dynamically switch among diverse working modes according to mission requirements and environmental conditions. Accurate, real-time recognition of these modes is essential for radar intelligence and electronic countermeasures. Existing pulse descriptor word (PDW)-based approaches depend on pulse-level parameter extraction, suffering from accumulated processing latency and performance degradation under low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and severe pulse-loss scenarios. To address these limitations, this article proposes an end-to-end signal-level framework for streaming MFR working mode recognition. The framework directly processes the time–frequency spectrogram of the received intermediate-frequency (IF) signal to generate temporally aligned working-mode labels at the input sampling resolution. To enable efficient online inference, a perception–knowledge–memory network (PKM-Net) was further proposed. It uniquely combines a Transformer-based perception module for local feature extraction with a GRU-based interparagraph memory module to capture long-range contextual dependencies while maintaining a constant memory cost. The experimental results demonstrate that PKM-Net achieves 99.88% accuracy, significantly outperforming recent state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines in robustness tests. Furthermore, with a compact footprint (10.43 MiB) and low latency (1.64 ms), it offers a feasible solution for real-time online processing in complex electromagnetic scenarios.

title: From Agentification to Self-Evolving Agentic AI for Wireless Networks: Concepts, Approaches, and Future Research Directions
authors: Changyuan Zhao, Ruichen Zhang, Jiacheng Wang, Dusit Niyato, Geng Sun, Xianbin Wang, Shiwen Mao, Abbas Jamalipour
abstract: Self-evolving agentic artificial intelligence (AI) offers a new paradigm for future wireless systems by enabling autonomous agents to continually adapt and improve without human intervention. Unlike static AI models, self-evolving agents embed an autonomous evolution cycle that updates models, tools, and workflows in response to environmental dynamics. This article presents a comprehensive overview of self-evolving agentic AI, highlighting its layered architecture, life cycle, and key techniques, including tool intelligence, workflow optimization, self-reflection, and evolutionary learning. We further propose a multi-agent cooperative self-evolving agentic AI framework, where multiple large language models (LLMs) are assigned role-specialized prompts under the coordination of a supervisor agent. Through structured dialogue, iterative feedback, and systematic validation, the system autonomously executes the entire life cycle without human intervention. A case study on antenna evolution in low-altitude wireless networks (LAWNs) demonstrates how the framework autonomously upgrades fixed antenna optimization into movable antenna optimization. Experimental results show that the proposed self-evolving agentic AI autonomously improves beam gain and restores degraded performance by up to 52.02%, consistently surpassing the fixed baseline with little to no human intervention and validating its adaptability and robustness for next-generation wireless intelligence.

title: HyDRA: Deadline and Reuse-Aware Cacheability for Hardware Accelerators
authors: Ayushi Agarwal, Anannya Mathur, Preeti Ranjan Panda
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: The system-level cache is a critical resource shared by processor cores and domain-specific accelerators in heterogeneous systems on chips (SoCs). The strict QoS requirements of accelerators, such as deadlines, can lead to severe performance degradation of processor cores. Thus, managing the shared cache efficiently between cores and accelerators becomes crucial. State-of-the-art cache management techniques perform reuse-aware bypassing of accesses from cores with the help of reuse predictors to improve performance. However, architectural differences between accelerators and processor cores can lead to significantly different reuse patterns at the shared cache. We propose a novel clustering-based methodology, LERN, for learning and predicting the reuse behavior of hardware accelerators at the shared cache. We then propose a deadline and reuse-aware cache management strategy, HyDRA, which explores a novel tradeoff between reuse and deadline awareness for performance efficiency. It uses LERN to dynamically predict the reuse behavior of the accelerator accesses and make bypass decisions to maximize the system throughput while meeting accelerator deadlines. We evaluate HyDRA across different workloads and varied accelerator configurations. It significantly improves the system performance and reduces the accelerator deadline miss rate.

title: FewShotMetabolic: Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning for Rapid Metabolic Risk Prediction in Data-Scarce Obesity Phenotypes
authors: Shiqi Mo, Xiumei Wu, Huijun Qiu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Creating risk prediction models based on obesity type requires large datasets, which are often not available for rare obesity phenotypes. Available technologies do not allow for consideration of the variability between different phenotypes, making it difficult to translate clinical research to underrepresented populations. We propose the FewShotMetabolic (FSM) Framework, a parameter-efficient framework to enable the creation of individualized metabolic risk models using only 10 data points per obesity phenotype. With the FSM Framework, we can connect information about the different obesity phenotypes so that knowledge can be shared between them, while keeping the unique metabolic signature associated with each obesity phenotype through selective pathway fine-tuning. The FSM model demonstrated an accuracy of 87.3% (AUC = 0.923) for metabolic syndrome risk classification among the six different obesity subtypes and achieved learnable parameters equal to 17.2% of fine-tuned models. The FSM model also produced an RMSE of 14.2 mg/dL for estimating the 2-hour post-meal glycemic response (Pearson r = 0.876). Results from external cohort testing (N=2 cohorts) indicated an AUC of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$>$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.89. Thus, the FSM Framework offers a practical way to apply precision obesity medicine and creates an opportunity to develop predictive models for populations that have historically been underrepresented.

title: Interpretable High-Pass Filter Fingerprint Model for 1000BASE-T Ethernet Authentication in IIoT
authors: Jie Zhong, Yu Jiang, Shuangyu Yang, Siwen Li, Aiqun Hu
abstract: Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) increasingly relies on Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T) as the physical back-bone for interconnecting industrial devices, while the rapid growth of IIoT nodes has intensified concerns about physical-layer identity spoofing and unauthorized access. Recently, device fingerprinting has emerged as a promising approach to achieving secure authentication at the physical layer. However, existing 1000BASE-T fingerprint extraction methods rely on randomly scrambled signals, leading to degraded authentication reliability. In addition, the absence of radio-frequency (RF) frontend modules—commonly defined in wireless systems—within 1000BASE-T transmitters prevents the direct application of conventional hardware-imperfection models. To overcome these challenges, this paper first introduces test mode (TM) signals as highly consistent and controllable reference inputs, and on this basis, proposes an interpretable high-pass filter (HPF) fingerprint model. The model characterizes the high-pass response of the transmission link using a single-pole system, establishes a monotonic relationship between filter parameters and waveform morphology, and extracts stable fingerprint features accordingly. Furthermore, a closed-loop physical-layer authentication framework is developed, integrating signal acquisition, preprocessing, feature extraction, and device identification. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves 100% identification accuracy under standard sampling conditions and preserves perfect recognition over the entire tested sampling-rate range. Moreover, the method exhibits substantially enhanced noise robustness compared with baseline methods, and retains 95.59% accuracy after a 30-day interval in temporal stability evaluations.

title: F-CP-RALS Algorithm for Channel Estimation in RIS-Aided mmWave MIMO Systems
authors: Ruiyan Du, Jiaqi Yue, Zhewen Xu, Fulai Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Accurate channel estimation remains a pivotal challenge in reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-aided millimeter wave (mmWave) multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems for next-generation wireless networks, particularly in dense urban scenarios where ultra-high data rates, massive connectivity, and seamless coverage are pursued. In this paper, a multiple Frobenius norms regularized alternating least squares (RALS) algorithm is proposed based on CANDECOMP/PARAFAC (CP) decomposition to jointly estimate all channel matrices, termed as F-CP-RALS. Firstly, the received signals from both cascaded and direct links are modeled as a third-order tensor based on a structured time-domain protocol, which inherently preserves the multi-dimensional channel structure and hidden space correlation. Subsequently, all links are jointly estimated by the proposed algorithm which can stabilize convergence and enhance noise suppression to improve estimation accuracy due to the additional regularization terms. Moreover, the algorithm utilizes an alternating iterative process to refine the channel matrices without turning off the RIS, thereby reducing the pilot overhead compared to methods that rely on separate estimations of direct and cascaded links. Additionally, a rigorous convergence analysis is conducted to guarantee algorithmic convergence, and the feasibility conditions are investigated by leveraging Kruskal’s uniqueness conditions, followed by a computational complexity analysis. Finally, experimental simulations confirm that the presented approach delivers more accurate estimation results than alternative methods.

title: Linear Convergence of Decentralized Gradient Tracking under Generalized Smoothness
authors: Yanan Bo, Yongqiang Wang
abstract: Decentralized optimization plays a central role in multi-agent systems, yet most existing convergence analyses rely on restrictive Lipschitz smoothness assumptions that fail to capture the gradient growth observed in practical problems. In this work, we study the convergence of gradient tracking under (L0, L1)-smoothness, a generalized condition that allows the Hessian norm to grow with the gradient norm. Unlike prior approaches that use gradient clipping or normalization—which slows down convergence—we show that the standard gradient tracking method, without any algorithmic modification, inherently prevents gradient explosion along the optimization trajectory and achieves linear convergence for strongly convex objectives. Our analysis provides explicit stepsize conditions based on the generalized smoothness parameters and recovers the O(1/L2) stepsize order under conventional Lipschitz smoothness as a special case. Numerical experiments on a regularized ℓp minimization problem further highlight the necessity of our analysis: stepsizes chosen according to classical L-smoothness theory lead to divergence, while the proposed theory accurately predicts stable convergence.

title: Autonomous Drone Landing Assistant System
authors: György Sebestyén, Sándor Szénási
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: An autonomous drone landing system capable of operating under variable weather conditions was developed and simulated. The core of the system is a PID controller, designed to ensure stability and precision during descent in dynamically changing environments. Weather conditions such as wind and rainfall were modeled to evaluate the performance of the system. The proposed approach was tested in multiple scenarios, demonstrating its robustness and adaptability. The results show that the system reliably manages adverse weather conditions, providing a basis for future real-world applications in challenging environments.

title: Welcome from the General Chair
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)

title: Weight Mapping Properties of a Dual Tree Single Clock Adiabatic Capacitive Neuron
authors: Mike Smart, Sachin Maheshwari, Himadri Singh Raghav, Alexander Serb
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Dual Tree Single Clock (DTSC) Adiabatic Capacitive Neuron (ACN) circuits offer the potential for highly energy-efficient Artificial Neural Network (ANN) computation in full custom analog IC designs. The efficient mapping of abstract ANN weights onto physical ACN capacitance values has, however, yet to be fully researched. In this paper, we explore the unexpected hidden complexities, challenges and properties of the mapping, as well as, the ramifications for IC designers in terms accuracy, design and implementation. We propose an optimal, ANN to ACN methodology, that promotes smaller chip sizes and improved overall classification accuracy, necessary for successful practical deployment. Using TensorFlow and Larq software frameworks, we train three different ANN networks and map their weights into the energy-efficient DTSC ACN capacitance value domain to demonstrate 100% functional equivalency. Finally, we delve into the impact of weight quantization on ACN performance using novel metrics related to practical IC considerations, such as IC floor space and comparator decision-making efficacy.

title: A Tutorial on Weight Structure of Polar Codes
authors: Mohammad Rowshan, Vlad-Florin Drăgoi
publish: IEEE BITS the Information Theory Magazine
abstract: This tutorial introduces the algebraic foundations underlying the weight structure of polar codes. Using a monomial-based polynomial formalism, we explain how polar and Reed-Muller codes can be viewed as decreasing monomial codes, enabling systematic characterization and enumeration of low-weight codewords. Its goal is to provide an accessible introduction to affine automorphisms, orbit-based descriptions of minimum and low-weight codewords, and their role in weight enumeration. Through illustrative examples and high-level overviews of recursive and coset-based techniques, the paper aims to prepare readers for deeper engagement with the recent technical literature on the weight distribution of polar codes.

title: Compact Doherty Power Amplifier–Circularly Polarized Antenna Integration for Phased Arrays
authors: Zhenzhong Chen, Min Ye, Jun Xu, Liying Sun, Yunlong Lu, Yi Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: In this communication, a compact Doherty power amplifier–circularly polarized antenna (DPA-CPA) operating at 3.5 GHz is presented for high-efficiency phased arrays. Unlike conventional designs with separate amplifiers and antennas connected via passive matching networks, the proposed DPACPA directly integrates the antenna with the amplifier, eliminating interconnect losses. The antenna provides decoupled control of input impedance and axial ratio (AR), allowing its impedance to be tuned to match the DPA’s two-port output network while maintaining stable circular polarization. Simulations and measurements show that the radiation patterns vary by less than 0.1 dB between saturation and 6-dB back-off. The integrated element achieves drain efficiencies of 67.5% and 51.5% at saturation and 6-dB back-off, respectively. With a compact size, the DPACPA is well suited for integration into planar phased arrays. The simulated beam-scanning performance of the 1×4 array shows a ±50° range with less than 2 dB gain variation and an AR consistently below 3 dB. These results demonstrate a new approach to DPA–CPA integration, offering power-invariant radiation, independent impedance–polarization control, and a compact form factor, which are highly attractive for energy-efficient multi-channel phased array transmitters.

title: Sparse Joint Inversion of Gravity and Magnetic Data Using Group L 0 -Norm Model Constraint
authors: Dan Zhu, Danping Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Owing to the nonconvexity of the L0-norm and the associated optimization difficulty, existing sparse joint inversion methods typically construct objective functions using approximating functions of the L0-norm. Although such approaches can produce focused multiphysics models, they often lose the capability of directly measuring and explicitly controlling the volume of the reconstructed models. In fact, the L0-norm provides a direct measure of the model volume. If the L0-norm is used as the sparsity constraint, focused imaging can be achieved while enabling controllable volume compression. However, in joint inversion, separately enforcing independent L0-norm constraints on the density and magnetization models leads to inconsistent sparsification, thereby disrupting the structural coupling targeted by joint inversion. To address this issue, we introduce the group L0-norm into joint inversion, treating the multiphysics parameters within each cell as a unified parameter group for L0-norm based sparsity measurement and regularization. Building on this idea, we propose a new joint inversion objective function for gravity and magnetic data that simultaneously incorporates the group L0-norm and the cross-gradient constraint. To stably solve the proposed nonconvex optimization problem, we develop an improved iterative hard-thresholding algorithm, which achieves an asymptotically linear decrease of the group L0-norm of the reconstructed model while maintaining stable convergence of the convex terms. Tests on synthetic models and field data demonstrate that the proposed method generates density and magnetization models with consistent boundaries and coherent parameter distributions. Moreover, it enables real-time sensing and control of the model volume during inversion, allowing reconstructions of multiphysics models for target bodies with expected volumes.

title: Forecasting of Meteorological Variables for Detecting Nocturnal Drainage Flows
authors: Jun Liu, Jiang Fang, Mingqiang Tu, Junming Wang, Sudheer Reddy Bhimireddy, Jielun Sun, David A. Kristovich, April L. Hiscox
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: This study applied artificial intelligence algorithms to the time-series forecasting of meteorological variables associated with nocturnal drainage flows. Using observations from the Stable Atmospheric Variability And Transport (SAVANT) field campaign as the source data, a baseline dataset was constructed by extracting a core set of variables. These variables are output varibles. the XGBoost models were subsequently developed and integrated with SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) to quantify feature importance. Based on the resulting SHAP rankings, additional variables beyond the core were selected from the original dataset to construct additional data sets. Then six deep learning models were compared with the baseline and additional datasets. Prior to the experiments, the raw data were preprocessed and resampled to a uniform 5-minute temporal resolution. In this study, the multivariate time series forecasting task is defined as follows: a continuous <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 4}$</tex>-hour observation sequence (168 time steps at 5 -minute resolution) serves as input to predict the subsequent 6 time steps (30 minutes of future meteorological evolution). This forecast horizon closely matches operational requirements for short-term atmospheric prediction. Results indicate that the optimal forecasting performance was achieved using only the baseline dataset, whereas the inclusion of non-baseline variables reduced predictive accuracy. Among the six models, MTGNN achieved the lowest MSE, while Crossformer yielded the lowest MAE. The superior performance demonstrated by MTGNN and Crossformer suggests that future research can further investigate hybrid architectures integrating graph structure modeling and attention mechanisms, and provide novel insights and approaches for improving meteorological forecasting performance through additional fusion experiments.

title: Multimodal Biosensing of Stress and Gaze Behavior for Skill Assessment in Robot-Assisted Surgery
authors: Kristóf Takács, Eszter Lukács, Tamás Haidegger
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Robot-Assisted Minimally Invasive Surgery (RAMIS) changes the perceptual and fine-motor requirements towards surgeons, but current training evaluation still relies primarily on technical performance outcomes and subjective assessment. This study investigates whether physiological stress estimation and gaze behavior can provide additional, objective insight into the surgeon's state during RAMIS and laparoscopic training. 14 novice users participated in the study and performed standardized ring-transfer and hot-wire tasks using the da Vinci Surgical System and a laparoscopic trainer for an extended time to assess stress and fatigue effects. Multimodal workload sensing was performed, Baevsky Stress Index (BSI) was calculated from heart rate variability measurements, and eye-tracking glasses were employed to derive fixation-saccade ratio, blink dynamics, and visual-motor stability. Robotic execution significantly reduced physiological strain and precision errors compared to laparoscopy, despite similar task scores. Strong correlations were observed between gaze efficiency and execution quality, while blink-based fatigue markers aligned with degraded performance. BSI and blink metrics also demonstrated meaningful relationships with self-reported SURG-TLX metrics, confirming their construct validity as workload indicators. Overall, combining biosensing with performance assessment enables deeper insights into RAMIS training and reveals latent cognitive strain not captured through task success rates alone.

title: PEMTRS: Perception-Enhanced Memory with Temporal Region Selection for Vision-Based Multirotor Navigation_supp1-3692098.mp4
authors: Xiao Liang
abstract: End-to-end deep learning methods for autonomous aerial vehicles like multirotors have been successfully applied to high-agility flight. However, existing end-to-end navigation methods for multirotors typically rely solely on the robot's current observation and state, neglecting explicit modeling of temporal sequence information. Yet trajectory prediction is inherently temporal: past observations provide cues about future feasible regions, and this temporal feasibility is still not explicitly exploited in current end-to-end navigation frameworks. To overcome these limitations, we propose the PEMTRS framework, which enables the multirotor to fly with thinking by building a perception-enhanced memory structure and employing a temporal region selector to highlight regions of interest for traversability. By jointly modeling spatial perception and temporal information, PEMTRS generalizes better within the evaluated environment class than state-of-the-art end-to-end methods. To achieve more efficient training, we adopt an implicit learning strategy that replaces expert-labeled demonstration trajectories with cost-based learning signals to guide network optimization. In addition, we design an adaptive time-allocation network to dynamically adjust the time intervals between waypoints and a residual VAE to reconstruct obstacle depth for perception enhancement. Finally, simulation and real-world experiments validate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed method.

title: E-Link: A 256-ch Mini-Pedestal Connector for High-Density Soft Neural Interfaces_supp1-3692102.mp4
authors: Hui Fang
abstract: High-density thin-film neural interfaces face persistent packaging challenges at the electrode–electronics interface, where conventional rigid connectors impose high insertion forces, strict alignment requirements, and limited reusability. We present E-Link (Elastomeric Link), a modular 256-channel mini-pedestal connector designed to facilitate simplified, user-friendly assembly through an insertion-free, alignment-tolerant interface utilizing an elastomeric conductive interposer and a compliant threaded compression mechanism. Finite element analysis and experimental validation confirm uniform contact stress across the 16 × 16 interconnect array. The system achieves stable electrical performance, exhibiting contact impedances of 0.3-0.4 kΩ, an RMS noise floor of 2.68 ± 0.46 μV, and a connection yield exceeding 97% over 100 mating cycles. The detachable architecture reduces chronic head-mounted mass from 6.6 g to 2.8 g while maintaining safe thermal operation (30.5 °C steady-state) under a full 20 kHz sampling bandwidth. Design analysis further indicates scalability toward 1024-channel integration within the same 25-mm-diameter footprint by leveraging fine-pitch elastomeric conductive pillars. This work provides a robust and scalable connection solution for high-density flexible neural interfaces with broad application for bioelectronics.

title: AI Data Centers: A Review of Power Quality Challenges and Mitigation Strategies
authors: Lakshman Maheshwari, Hang Dai, Bulent Sarlioglu, Rahul Chakraborty, Yu Xia, Mario Harper
publish: 2026 IEEE Power and Energy Conference at Illinois (PECI)
abstract: The accelerated growth of AI data centers has introduced a new class of large and highly volatile loads to the power grid. Unlike traditional data centers, which are dominated by relatively steady and predictable IT loads, AI data centers exhibit rapid power transients, leading to increased harmonic injection and a higher likelihood of power system instability. This work examines the characteristics of AI data centers’ workload and evaluates methods to mitigate them. Specifically, artificial load modulation, GPU ramp-rate tuning, power capping, and the integration of multi-level energy storage to provide supplementary power during dynamic load fluctuations are reviewed. Several energy storage methods are discussed to reduce transients due to power oscillations at AI data center interconnections and to improve the electric grid’s power quality. New power delivery architecture and system-level coordination are also identified as promising approaches to alleviating the power quality challenges.

title: Planar 2×2 WR3-Power Combiner for Integrating THz Photodiode Arrays
authors: Rihab Hamad, Sumer Makhlouf, Israa Mohammad, Vitaly Rymanov, Johanna Böhm, Andreas Stöhr
abstract: This paper presents a planar full-band rectangular waveguide power combiner for the WR3-band (220-320 GHz). The power combiner features a 2×2 planar array of standard WR3-inputs in H-plane and one standard WR3-output. In contrast to 4×1 linear array power combiners, this novel planar concept allows the integration of miniaturized planar arrays of antenna-integrated THz photodiodes. The numerically analyzed electrical performance yields average transmission coefficients of -6.5 dB for the full WR3-band, which aligns very well to the nominal transmission loss of 6 dB for a lossless 2×2 power combiner.

title: Card Counting Teaching System for the Card Game Blackjack Using Image Processing
authors: Marcell Mészöly, Ádám Pintér
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a card counting training system for the casino card game blackjack. The system consists of two loosely coupled components: a multi-player blackjack game implemented in a client-server architecture, and an independent card counting assistant that observes the game purely through its visual output using image processing. The assistant performs automatic card detection and recognition from screen captures, computes both running and true counts according to the Hi-Lo card counting system, and suggests basic-strategy actions for each hand. The goal is not to provide a production-grade casino engine, but to create an accessible environment in which players can learn and practice card counting under realistic conditions, without any real-money stakes.

title: Rules, Not Rankings: Interpretable AI for Processor Microarchitecture Design Analysis
authors: Abdoulaye Gamatié, Lincen Yang, Paulo Pimenta
abstract: Expert-driven microarchitecture analysis provides valuable insights but fails to scale to modern design spaces, while black-box AI offers scalability at the cost of interpretability. We present PTAH, a data-driven methodology that combines codified expert knowledge with an interpretable conditional-density model. PTAH derives human-readable rules that link processor mechanism configurations to the full IPC probability distribution. Clustering these rules yields design principles that highlight key interactions, trade-offs, and behavioral regimes. A case study on SPEC CPU workloads shows that PTAH uncovers design insights while preserving full interpretability.

title: FLISR Applications for the Protection of Distribution Systems with High Penetration of IBRs
authors: Alexander Apostolov
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: Fault Location, Isolation, and Service Restoration (FLISR) is a key distribution automation technology used by electric utilities to enhance grid reliability, reduce outage durations (often from hours to seconds), minimize the number of affected customers, and support self-healing grid capabilities. It automatically detects faults on distribution feeders, pinpoints or approximates their location, isolates the faulted section (typically by opening automated switches or reclosers), and restores power to unfaulted sections by reconfiguring the network (e.g., closing tie switches to transfer load to adjacent healthy feeders or sources).

title: High-accuracy Pipeline Flow Rate Monitoring via Turbulent Noise Frequency-Band Extraction in Distributed Acoustic Sensing Systems
authors: Baoqiang Yan, Zhichao Zeng, Cunzheng Fan, Keqing Zhang, Kan Liu, Lisong Yan, Zhijun Yan, Qizhen Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate and distributed flow rate measurement is essential for ensuring pipeline safety, enhancing operational efficiency, and extending service life. Conventional point-based flowmeters are intrusive, cause fluid pressure drops, and have limited scalability for real-time, multi-point sensing. This study presents a non-intrusive flow monitoring framework that integrates Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) with a Turbulent Noise Frequency-band Extraction (TNFE) algorithm. TNFE is designed to mitigate the dilution of flow sensitivity caused by flow-insensitive and interference components in conventional full-band processing. The acoustic field generated by gas flow is captured by Backscattering-enhanced optical fiber (BEOF) deployed on the pipeline surface, enabling real-time, distributed and non-intrusive flow measurements. The proposed TNFE algorithm adopts a coarse-to-fine band selection strategy to identify flow-sensitive spectral regions from full-band DAS measurements and constructs band-wise features (with wavelet packet decomposition refinement) for flow-rate regression. Field experiments on an operational natural gas pipeline demonstrate a strong quadratic correlation (R² > 0.999) between flow rate and the extracted vibration feature, with measurement accuracies of 95.66% (straight-line), 98.00% (10 cm helical pitch), and 99.14% (5 cm helical pitch) within a ±15% error margin, and consistently reduced RMSE compared with the full-band baseline. These results validate the feasibility of distributed, real-time flow measurement using DAS with TNFE and indicate its applicability to pipeline integrity management.

title: Knowledge Distillation for Collaborative Learning in Distributed Communications and Sensing
authors: Nhan Thanh Nguyen, Mengyuan Ma, Nir Shlezinger, Junil Choi, Yonina C. Eldar, A. Lee Swindlehurst, Markku Juntti
abstract: The rise of sixth generation (6G) wireless networks promises to deliver ultra-reliable, low-latency, and energy-efficient communications, sensing, and computing. However, traditional centralized artificial intelligence (AI) paradigms are ill-suited to the decentralized, resource-constrained, and dynamic nature of 6G ecosystems. This article explores knowledge distillation (KD) and collaborative learning as promising techniques that enable the efficient and scalable deployment of lightweight AI models across distributed communications and sensing (C&S) nodes. We begin by providing an overview of KD and highlight the key strengths that make it particularly effective in distributed scenarios characterized by device heterogeneity, task diversity, and constrained resources. We then examine its role in fostering collective intelligence through collaborative learning between the central and distributed nodes via various knowledge distilling and deployment strategies. Finally, we present a systematic numerical study demonstrating that KD-empowered collaborative learning can effectively support lightweight AI models for multi-modal sensing-assisted beam tracking applications with substantial performance gains and complexity reduction.

title: Accurate Rotor Position Estimation for Spherical Reluctance Motor Based on Improved Segmentation Strategy
authors: Mingfeng Shi, Qunjing Wang, Huanyu Zhao, Yongbin Liu, Siliang Lu, Shihao Gao
abstract: In this article, an accurate rotor position estimation method for spherical reluctance motor (SPRM) based on the improved segmentation strategy is proposed. This method selects the optimal segmentation points according to the variation characteristic of the flux linkage and the principle of minimizing the flux linkage calculation error. First, the reluctance, inductance and flux linkage characteristic data of SPRM are obtained based on the finite element analysis, and the flux linkage characteristic data is determined as the analysis and calculation tool. Then, according to the variation trend, the flux linkage curve family is divided into three sections, and the appropriate analytical expressions are proposed according to the characteristics of each section. Finally, based on the principle of minimizing the flux linkage calculation error, an optimization algorithm is used to obtain the best segmentation points. The effectiveness of the proposed method is verified with simulations and experiments. This method has higher estimation accuracy, lower computational complexity and higher efficiency than the traditional methods.

title: Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)

title: Threshold-Switching Memristor Neurons with Spike-Frequency Adaptation
authors: Yan Wang, Zhuangcheng Li, Yue Zhang, Sylvain Eimer, Chunyan Wu, Li Wang, Ziyu Lv, Fengxia Liang, Linbao Luo, You Wang
abstract: Threshold-switching memristors (TSMs) exhibit volatile turn-on at a threshold voltage and self-reset when the device voltage drops below the hold voltage, naturally providing the trigger-and-reset functions required for spiking neurons. This letter presents a compact SPICE-compatible phenomenological macro-model that emulates the threshold-switching window defined by Vth and Vhold and is directly compatible with CMOS co-simulation. Using this model, a minimal TSM-based leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neuron is demonstrated, showing monotonic tuning of firing frequency with input voltage and control resistance. A low-overhead adaptive module that converts spike activity into a slowly varying control voltage is further introduced to realize an adaptive LIF (ALIF) neuron with spike-frequency adaptation and recovery. Energy-per-spike scaling with membrane capacitance is investigated for C1 ranging from 0.25 to 8 nF under Vin=3.25, 3.50, and 3.75 V. Robustness against key parameter variations is further evaluated using Monte Carlo simulations to quantify the efficiency and sensitivity of the proposed circuit.

title: A Study on Rephrasing Large Earthquakes via the Least Action Principle and Evolving Machine Learning
authors: In Ho Cho, Eunseo Choi
abstract: Understanding large earthquakes (EQs) has been a critical endeavor. Recent advancements in machine learning (ML) help this endeavor, yet they face formidable performance ceilings due to our technical limitations, restricted access to seismogenic fault zones, and the lack of large datasets for ML training. This paper introduces a novel approach by conceptualizing the EQ-bearing lithosphere as a chaotic system and investigating whether the least action principle (LAP) from Hamiltonian mechanics can illuminate the potential predictability of large EQ initiation. Leveraging an abstract space defined by time and rupture derivatives, this paper establishes a potential causal pathway between novel LAP-based features and large EQs. An evolving ML framework utilizing parallel evolution strategies was developed to identify hidden rules derived from LAP. The model was trained on data from all EQs in the western U.S. over the past 40 years. Feasibility tests on historical large EQs show promising performance compared to state-of-the-art forecasting methods. While practical applicability requires deeper investigation, these findings suggest a new research direction for large EQ prediction that merges classical physics and evolving ML.

title: High Impedance Fault Detection via Distributed Sensing
authors: Pablo Paz, Shannon Strank, Souvik Chandra, Sam Talukder, Dmitry Ishchenko, Nathan Peterson
publish: 2026 79th Annual Conference for Protective Relay Engineers (CFPR)
abstract: This paper presents the development of multifunctional, non-intrusive sensors for overhead medium voltage distribution systems. The sensor can be mounted on single-phase distribution lines, is completely self-powered through energy harvesting, and supports coordinated data capture across phases. The protection function addressed in this work focuses on the detection and localization of high impedance faults (HIFs). The proposed system employs wide-bandwidth voltage and current sensors with analog signal processing applied prior to 16-bit Analog to Digital Converter (ADC) card to improve signal-to-noise ratio and enable reliable capture of fault signatures. Communication among sensors is implemented using Generic Object Oriented Substation Event (GOOSE) messaging for cross-triggering. Laboratory experiments under multiple fault scenarios, including downed conductors, vegetation contact, and insulation degradation, demonstrate that high-frequency signal components provide clear indicators of early-stage HIFs. Field data collected at 50 kHz from a rural utility feeder further highlight the system’s potential for enhanced situational awareness and predictive maintenance.

title: Deep Multi-View Clustering via Dynamic Anchors-driven Global Structure Exploration
authors: Zengbiao Yang, Yihua Tan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Deep multi-view clustering leverages the powerful representation ability of neural networks to collaboratively explore clustering structures from multiple views, and has become a research hotspot in unsupervised learning. Most current deep multi-view clustering methods aim to discover clustering structures through either mini-batch contrastive learning or the use of graphs capturing connective relationships of all multiview samples. Nevertheless, these two paradigms suffer from the following drawbacks: (1) Mini-batch contrastive learning lacks the ability to capture global clustering structures of multiview data. (2) Using fully connected graphs with graph neural network leads to high space complexity, which limits scalability to large-scale multi-view scenarios. To tackle these challenges, we introduce Deep Multi-View Clustering via Dynamic Anchorsdriven Global Structure Exploration. Our approach learns global anchors for each view and leverages them during mini-batch training to explore the sample-anchor relationships, enabling global clustering structure discovery across views. Concretely, we first extract high-confidence samples per class by measuring inter-view consistency, which serves to guide the dynamic learning of anchors. Based on the learned global anchors, we construct both sample-wise and anchor-wise adjacency matrix. The anchor-wise adjacency matrix is designed to align clustering structures among views. The sample-wise adjacency matrix is used to enhance clustering structures in embeddings via crossattention between samples and anchors. Experiments conducted on several public multi-view datasets validate the superiority of our method over existing state-of-the-art deep multi-view clustering baselines.

title: Fano-Induced Negative Group Delay Dielectric Sensor With Signal Advancement in SSPP Waveguides
authors: Guodong Lu, Jingsong Nie, Weiwen Li
abstract: This work presents a dielectric sensor based on Fano-induced negative group delay (NGD) in spoof surface plasmon polariton (SSPP) waveguides. An anomalous-dispersion SSPP unit is introduced to engineer strong dispersion near the cutoff frequency. By periodically cascading these units, the interaction between a localized resonant state and the continuous SSPP propagation mode produces a Fano resonance, leading to a pronounced NGD response with a maximum simulated group delay of −14.71 ns. By increasing the number of anomalous-dispersion units, multi-band NGD responses can also be achieved. For sensing applications, a single-band NGD SSPP waveguide is adopted to ensure a clear and stable spectral response. When a dielectric sample is placed above the waveguide, variations in relative permittivity shift the NGD resonance frequency, enabling dielectric sensing based on frequency tracking. The proposed sensor achieves a sensitivity of approximately 163 MHz/εr, a normalized sensitivity of about 5%, and a high quality factor exceeding 67. Linear regression further confirms a strong negative linear relationship between the NGD resonance frequency and dielectric permittivity (R² > 0.97). In addition, the NGD characteristic enables signal-envelope advancement, indicating promising potential for real-time sensing and microwave signal processing applications.

title: Broadband Interconnect and Assembly for Sub-THz Heterogeneous Integration
authors: Sébastien Chartier, Katarzyna Holc, Damian Stenzel, Bersant Gashi, Axel Tessmann, Arnuf Leuther, Patricie Merkert
abstract: This work presents three complementary broadband packaging and interconnect technologies for heterogeneous sub-terahertz (sub-THz) integration. First, an indium bump flip-chip process is implemented within a 35 nm InGaAs mHEMT technology, enabling low-parasitic assembly of monolithically integrated front-ends or standalone MMICs on interposer. The bumps exhibit uniform reflowed geometries, provide efficient thermal conduction, and achieve low DC chain resistances with shear forces within 3.6–5.6 mN per bump. Second, a spray-coated polymer process is developed for side-by-side hybrid integration of InGaAs mHEMT and SiGe BiCMOS chips or InGaAs mHEMT MMICs and fused silica interposer. The conformal dielectric coating supports chip-to-chip transitions with resistance well below 1 Ω up to at least 320 GHz. Finally, a sub-THz on-chip antenna combining a metastructured high-impedance ground, fused-silica dielectric resonator, and diamond anti-reflection layer achieves a measured 25% impedance bandwidth and directivity exceeding 10 dBi (27 dBi with a polypropylene lens). Together, these results demonstrate a scalable sub-THz packaging strategy supporting broadband radiation, low interconnect loss, and dense multi-channel integration for next-generation high-resolution radar and ultra-high data-rate communication front-ends.

title: Dual-radar 120 GHz Human Motion Detection for MRI Applications
authors: Fatemeh Eftekhar, Oliver Sura, Armin M. Nagel, Martin Vossiek
abstract: For state-of-the-art medical diagnostics, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) plays a crucial role. However, due to its sequential data acquisition, MRI is highly sensitive to patient motion, where even small movements can significantly degrade image quality. Reliable motion tracking during MRI is therefore essential. Existing approaches, such as optical tracking or MR-based navigators, suffer from limitations including the need for external markers or increased scan duration. This work presents a contactless, radar-based motion-tracking setup suitable for operation within an MRI environment. The setup employs two compact 120 GHz frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar systems. The concept was first implemented in a laboratory reference setup and subsequently validated during actual MRI scanning. The results demonstrate a strong correlation between the differential radial range measured by the two radars and the subject’s head-rotation angle, with no observable interference from the MRI system on the radar measurements and no radar-related artifacts in the reconstructed MR images. These findings confirm the feasibility of the presented radar-based motion tracking during MRI acquisition.

title: Free Space VSWR Method for the Uncertainty Analysis of Full-Vehicle OTA Measurements
authors: Lukas Berkelmann, Adrian Mrochen, Bert Jannsen, Zhichao Chen
abstract: This paper introduces a spatial filtering approach to the free space VSWR method for enabling the uncertainty analysis of room scattering in full-vehicle OTA measurements. As shown, the targeted spatial interference pattern due to reflections of the chamber can thus be correctly separated from further spatial path loss and antenna pattern scan effects, which occur in near-field systems with electrically large quiet zones. In addition, the automated evaluation of free space VSWR measurements is enhanced, which is also beneficial for large quiet zones where many spatial VSWR scans are required.

title: A Traceable Monocular Vision Framework for Joint Distance-Azimuth Measurement on UAV Platforms
authors: Yixuan Zhao, Shaohua Chen, Fang Gao, Yue Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate estimation of inter-target distance and azimuth is essential for safety-critical Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) applications such as rescue, surveillance, and navigation. Existing visual measurement systems typically estimate distance as an intermediate perception output and rarely treat azimuth as an explicitly defined measurand within a unified analytical framework, thereby limiting interpretability and uncertainty traceability. This paper presents UAVMD, a measurement-centric monocular projection geometry framework that enables traceable and uncertainty-aware joint distance-azimuth estimation. The method establishes closed-form analytical mappings from pixel coordinates, UAV altitude and pitch angle, and calibrated camera parameters to metric outputs, forming a transparent and traceable measurement chain. Terrestrial experiments conducted at altitudes of 639-1799 m and inter-target distances of 36-82 m achieved a distance MAE of 2.92 m and RMSE of 3.45 m. Maritime infrared experiments conducted at an altitude of 600 m with target separations of 22-45 m achieved a distance MAE of 0.481 m and azimuth MAE of 0.286°. The computational latency is 0.24 ms per measurement. Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses confirm predictable error propagation under platform disturbances. The proposed framework provides an accurate, lightweight, and real-time deployable monocular solution for UAV-based geometric measurement tasks.

title: Hand-Size-Adaptable FBG Wearable Glove for Cross-User Gesture Recognition
authors: Huiying Piao, Cheng Li, Jing Li, Xiangting Hou, Ke Du
abstract: Gesture recognition is fundamental to intuitive human-machine interaction. However, conventional glove-based systems commonly suffer from performance degradation in multi-user scenarios due to variations in hand size and the resulting sensor misalignment. To address these limitations, this paper presents a hand-size-adaptive wearable glove with five multi-node fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensors and an associated adaptive gesture recognition method. The glove integrates a distributed sensing layout with an adjustable skeletal glove structure to improve joint coverage across different hand sizes, and a PDMS-TPU composite encapsulation is used to protect the fibers while maintaining sensitivity and flexibility. An incremental update strategy based on an online linear support vector machine (SVM) trained with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is employed for cross-user adaptation, updating the classifier using a small calibration set from each new user. Experiments on a multi-user gesture dataset show that fine-tuning with only 20% of a new user’s data yields average recognition accuracies of 99.56% for static gestures and 98.83% for dynamic gestures. The combination of adaptive hardware integration and incremental classifier updating supports low-effort, high-accuracy gesture interaction in shared-use wearable applications.

title: Single Point-Supervised Object Detection With Adaptive Rotation-Aware Convolution and Full Interaction Feature Pyramid Network
authors: Zhigang Yang, Zehao Gao, Qian Miao, Wei Emma Zhang, Tao Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In the field of remote sensing object detection, rotated bounding box-based methods can achieve accurate detection, but their annotation costs are very high. Recent research involving weakly-supervised detectors, especially low-cost point-supervised detectors, has gained great attention. However, existing methods do not adequately address the absence of supervision of rotational information caused by point labeling. To address this issue, we design an adaptive rotationaware convolution (ARAC) that adaptively rotates feature maps to arbitrary angles, thereby enabling the learning of rotationsensitive representations in an annular receptive field and efficiently integrating these representations with contextual information. In addition, the feature pyramid network relies on extensive layer-by-layer transmission, which inherently leads to information loss. Therefore, we design a full interaction feature pyramid network (FIFPN) that achieves a one-step full layer interaction through cross-layer interaction channel attention (CICA) and cross-layer interaction spatial attention (CISA), thereby reducing the information loss caused by layer-by-layer interaction. FIFPN also helps preserve the rotation-related information extracted by ARAC. Extensive experiments on widely recognized datasets, DOTA and HRSC, demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance against other point-supervised models.

title: A Polarization Vision Based Weld Feature Extraction Method for Variable-Width Laser Stripes in Aluminum Alloy Laser Welding
authors: Ting Lei, Yutong Yang, Zhonghai Liu, Yuqing Fu, Xinwei Jing
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Feature extraction for aluminum alloy fillet welds in automated robotic welding is significantly challenged because of intense specular reflections and variable laser stripe widths. This paper proposes a robust feature extraction method specifically designed for variable-width laser stripes (fine, medium, and thick) in high-reflectivity environments. To reduce noise, a polarization-based pseudo-color fusion strategy is first introduced, which maps multi-angle polarization images (0°, 45°, 90°, 135°) into RGB channels to maximally eliminate noise. A decision stump denoiser using connected component pruning then removes fine-grained noise while preserving valid features. The center point of the laser stripe is then accurately located through a combination of morphological skeletonization and the Probabilistic Hough Transform. The proposed method was validated on a laser vision welding experimental platform. Experimental results demonstrate that the method maintains high precision across all investigated stripe widths, fulfilling the requirements for high-quality industrial welding. This study provides an adaptive and robust solution for autonomous seam tracking in aluminum alloy welding, ensuring high operational efficiency and reliability in complex industrial scenarios.

title: Automated Motile Marine Wildlife Detection in Coral Reefs using Airborne Fluid Lensing
authors: Noah Pikielny, Jessie Yang, Shaili Johri, Ved Chirayath
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Coral reef ecosystems are rapidly changing due to global climate change, with marine heat waves and human stressors exerting significant pressure. This has led to the decline of marine biodiversity, most famously in corals, a keystone species that supports global ocean ecosystems. While there have been advancements in assessing benthic substrate biodiversity in reefs, the evaluation of motile marine wildlife biodiversity has lagged, with potential biases from in-situ disturbances. NASA's airborne fluid lensing technology, deployed on autonomous aircraft, has enabled 3D imaging of coral reef ecosystems at the cm-scale to depths of 20m, aiding in benthic biodiversity assessment without wave distortions. However, this technology removes transient artifacts, including motile marine life, which is crucial for quantifying biodiversity in shallow marine ecosystems. We develop a new airborne fluid lensing-based marine wildlife detection algorithm to characterize moving underwater wildlife using raw fluid lensing data and solutions. The detector assesses marine animals in time series imagery, deriving animal planform area, velocity, and abundance across depths of ∼0-10m, at cm-scale resolution, in complex coral reef ecosystems over dozens of square kilometers. The algorithm's accuracy and performance were evaluated using a consensus-based technique in a single-blind experiment. The datasets span three ecologically distinct coral reefs in Guam. Results indicate the algorithm detects marine wildlife three times more efficiently than humans, with 6% less precision. Its inconsistent recall, dropping to 10% at its worst, indicates sensitivity to environmental conditions. These high quality results could greatly lower the cost of dataset curation for future machine learning methods.

title: Cloud-Edge Collaborative Zero Trust Access Control: Architecture and Scheme Design
authors: Zhuocheng Xu, Yutong Yue, Boya Di, Lingyang Song
abstract: Cloud-edge collaborative manufacturing systems have drawn considerable attention by offloading data processing tasks to edge servers to support latency-sensitive tasks and high manufacturing efficiency. In these systems, data can be accessed by external devices and the access connections are exposed on the Internet, leading to a risk of data leakage or interception. In this paper, to address such data security issues, we introduce the zero trust architecture (ZTA) where every user, whether internal or external, is required to be authorized and verified before accessing any resource, thereby ensuring data security. However, existing ZTA implementations primarily leverage security technologies that rely on either biometrics and human behavior or sparse device attributes, rendering them inapplicable in cloud-edge collaborative manufacturing systems. To deal with this challenge, we propose a cloud-edge collaborative ZTA (CE-ZTA), in which ciphertext-policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) is employed to implement ZTA principles. We propose a continuously-verifying CP-ABE scheme, in which an attribute merging algorithm is designed to avoid the exponential growth in computational latency and overhead caused by continuous verification of CE-ZTA. Theoretical analysis and simulation results show that the proposed scheme complies with ZTA principles and is superior to existing CP-ABE schemes in terms of latency, overhead, and robustness.

title: Optimal Design of Compact Ultra-Wideband Transmissive Linear-to-Circular Polarization Metasurfaces
authors: Jiao Wang, Zejing Qin, Shangfeng Cai, Yannan Jiang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: The ellipticity angle β is proposed as a circular polarization discrimination metric to overcome the inherent limitations of traditional methods involving amplitude-phase difference or axial ratio. Meanwhile, the topological structure of the high-performance linear-to-circular (LTC) polarization conversion metasurfaces (PCMs) is derived theoretically. Further, based on the topological structure and β, this study employs a physics-informed genetic algorithm for the optimal design of a compact transmissive LTC PCM featuring ultra-wideband response and easy fabrication. The simulations demonstrate that the optimized LTC PCM operates in the frequency range of 4.2- 11.3 GHz with a fractional bandwidth of 91.6%, and a mean polarization conversion efficiency of 70%, which verifies the excellent performance; the measured results are in agreement with the simulations, thus validating the proposed design method. This investigation can be applied in antenna designs, optical devices, and remote sensing systems.

title: Dot-Shaped Range-Angle Beampattern Synthesis for Frequency Diverse Array with Linear Frequency Offset
authors: Mengyuan Ru, Xiaolong Xie, Quan Xue
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Frequency Diverse Array (FDA) suffers from inherent range–angle coupling, which causes ambiguous target localization and dispersed far-field energy. Existing decoupling approaches rely on nonuniform frequency offsets that are difficult to implement in hardware. In contrast, this letter introduces a feed-to-element random remapping architecture. The method randomly routes readily available linearly incremental frequency offset signals to individual array elements. This random routing strategy shifts the key implementation challenge from signal generation to signal routing. Because the routing function can be realized with mature, low-cost switch matrix technology, the overall system complexity is substantially reduced. The architecture is validated by applying it to measured five-channel data originally acquired from a photonics-based FDA system, confirming dot-shaped range–angle beampattern synthesis under realistic hardware conditions. The approach requires only the addition of a switch matrix to existing linearly incremental frequency offset sources, offering a practical pathway toward engineering implementation of dot-shaped range–angle beampattern synthesis.

title: From the Editors: Thoughts on “ilities”
authors: Jeffrey Voas
