title: Fairness and Voltage Quality Enhancement through Active-Reactive Dynamic Operating Envelopes in Unbalanced Low-Voltage Networks
authors: Alireza Barzegar, M. Imran Azim, Ali Moradi Amani, Mahdi Jalili
abstract: This paper presents a novel fairness-aware dynamic operating envelope (DOE) framework for equitable active and reactive power allocations in unbalanced low-voltage (LV) networks with high penetration of distributed energy resources (DERs). The proposed method extends proportional fairness from conventional active power definitions to apparent power, allowing customers constrained in active export to contribute via reactive power support. A multi-phase current–voltage optimal power flow (OPF) formulation is developed, incorporating inverter capability constraints, transformer loading limits, and voltage unbalance factor (VUF) mitigation. The VUF is explicitly regularised to address phase-level voltage quality issues arising from asymmetric DER installations and single-phase connections. Time-series simulations on a real-world Australian LV feeder demonstrate that the proposed Fair (P,Q) DOE with explicit VUF regularisation consistently maintains transformer and voltage constraints, reduces VUF below industry guidelines, and improves both fairness and network utilisation compared to existing methods. Results confirm that integrating reactive power flexibility and unbalance-aware fairness metrics enables a more inclusive and power-quality-compliant operation of DER-rich LV networks.

title: An Orthogonal-Axis Spherical Parallel Mechanism With Large Workspace for Humanoid Hip Joints: Design, Optimization, and Experimental Validation_supp1-3692330.mp4
authors: Long Cui
abstract: Conventional serial-chain configurations for humanoid robot lower limbs exhibit issues such as concentrated driving torques, inertial coupling, and error accumulation, leading to inadequate joint stiffness, limited control precision, and reduced dynamic stability. To overcome these drawbacks, this study proposes a novel Orthogonal-axis Spherical Parallel Mechanism, inspired by the ball-and-socket structure of the human hip joint. The kinematic model of the mechanism is established. A multi-objective optimization framework is then constructed, considering workspace coverage, dexterity, and maximum joint torque, and optimal parameters are obtained through weighted decision-making. Simulations and experiments verify that the optimized mechanism fully covers the common range of motion of the human hip joint, demonstrates improved dexterity within common motion ranges, and supports a single-leg payload of 40 kg. Integrating high stiffness, a large workspace, and superior load capability, this design offers a comprehensive solution for humanoid robotic legs.

title:
A 60-GHz Class-F
                    <sub>2,3</sub>
                    Standing-wave Oscillator Employing Triple-line Resonator Achieving -189 dBc/Hz FoM in 65-nm CMOS
authors: Wei-Yu Lin, Nikhil Jain, Jun-Chau Chien
abstract: Implementing oscillators with harmonic engineering beyond 60 GHz poses significant challenges due to the need for small inductors resonating beyond 120 GHz. To address this issue, this work presents a 60-GHz standing-wave oscillator with both 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nd</sup> and 3<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rd</sup>-harmonic boosting for phase noise reduction. A triple-line resonator is proposed to sustain both fundamental and 2<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nd</sup>-harmonic resonance. The measurement results of the class-F<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2,3</sub> standing-wave oscillator in TSMC 65-nm LP technology achieve -101.1 dBc/Hz at 1-MHz offset while consuming 5.5 mW with a FoM of -189 dBc/Hz.

title: Real-Time Freeway Crash Occurrence and Type Prediction Using Connected Vehicle Data via a Spatiotemporal BiLSTM-Transformer
authors: Chenzhu Wang, Mohamed Abdel-Aty, Lei Han, Samgyu Yang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Real-time prediction of both crash occurrence and type is essential for timely intervention and proactive traffic control, especially under the high-speed freeway conditions. This study proposes a hybrid deep learning framework-Spatiotemporal BiLSTM-Transformer-to predict segment-level crash risk using connected vehicle (CV) data. While prior studies have explored data-driven crash prediction, few have examined how heterogeneous driving behaviors translate into distinct crash mechanisms. Using CV data with 3-second temporal resolution and spatially matched crash reports, the model integrates macro-level traffic indicators and micro-level behavioral signals to capture both spatiotemporal patterns and risk-inducing driver behaviors. A spatiotemporal attention mechanism is first applied to jointly capture the spatial (upstream, current, downstream) and temporal (5-10 min, 10-15min, 15-20 min prior to the crash) dependencies among macro-level traffic features (e.g., speed, volume) and micro-level behavioral indicators (e.g., hard braking, lane changes). The attended representations are processed by a BiLSTM–Transformer encoder to extract sequential and long-range patterns, followed by a sigmoid-based prediction head to estimate crash probabilities and types. The proposed model significantly outperforms baseline models in both crash likelihood prediction and crash type identification, achieving up to a 0.018 increase in AUC, a 0.9 percentage point gain in accuracy, and a 0.017 reduction in false alarm rate (FAR) compared to the best-performing benchmark. The findings confirm that the model effectively distinguishes rear-end and sideswipe crash-prone scenarios from normal traffic patterns, enhancing interpretability and responsiveness of CV-based safety prediction. The proposed framework lays the groundwork for proactive crash prevention and real-time safety management in connected vehicle environments.

title: Study on Single Event Leakage Current Damage in P-GaN HEMTs under Different Voltage Biases
authors: Yong-chang Sun, Ying Wang, Xing-Ji Li, Jian-qun Yang, Fei Cao
abstract: In this work, the mechanisms of single event leakage current (SELC) damage in P-type gallium nitride gate high electron mobility transistors (P-GaN HEMTs) under different voltage biases were investigated based on experiments and simulation. In the experiments, we performed irradiation work with tantalum (Ta) ions at an average energy of 1334.92 MeV and LET (Linear Energy Transfer) value of 82.06 MeV/(mg/cm²) under various drain and gate biases. Three-terminal I-V test results show that the main leakage path of SELC is between the gate and the drain. The simulation results indicate that the local electric field enhancement and heat accumulation are the main causes of device damage. Higher drain voltage bias and negative gate voltage bias will bring higher local peak electric field and normalized max temperature, which will lead to more serious SELC damage. This study focuses on the voltage bias dependence of SELC damage in P-GaN HEMTs, providing a reference for the safe operation of P-GaN HEMTs in space environments, especially under negative gate voltage.

title: A Review on Bilevel Optimization Using Evolutionary Algorithms and Machine Learning Approaches_supp1-3690256.pdf
authors: Dipti Srinivasan
abstract: Bilevel Optimization (BLO) is a hierarchical problem structure involving two interdependent levels of optimization tasks. While traditionally approached through evolutionary algorithms (EAs), recent advances in machine learning (ML) have introduced scalable and efficient methods that can address high-dimensional and data-driven BLO problems. This review provides a comprehensive survey of the evolution from EA-based to ML-based BLO approaches. We begin by outlining the mathematical foundations and challenges inherent in BLO problems. We then explore traditional EA methods, highlighting their strengths in non-convex, black-box, and simulation-driven contexts. The core focus of the paper is on ML-based bilevel solvers, including techniques from supervised learning, reinforcement learning, and differentiable programming. We compare the two paradigms across scalability, generalization, interpretability, and efficiency, and discuss emerging applications in neural architecture search, hyperparameter tuning, adversarial learning, and scientific design. The paper concludes with a discussion of open challenges and promising directions for hybrid and automated BLO frameworks. This survey lays the groundwork for future research at the intersection of optimization and learning. Additional resources are available at https://chauhandikshit.github.io/blo-website/.

title: A Semi-Supervised Framework for Remote Sensing Change Detection via Branch Complementarity and Conflict-Resolved Pseudo-Labeling
authors: Bingbing Yu, Lingxuan Zhang, Fangyu Du, Yunyu Liao, Tao Lei
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: High-resolution remote sensing change detection plays an important role in land-use monitoring, urban expansion analysis, and disaster assessment. However, semi-supervised remote sensing change detection remains challenging in low-label settings. On the one hand, severe class imbalance, sparse changed regions, and unclear boundaries often weaken feature learning and reduce the reliability of pseudo-labels. On the other hand, existing methods mainly depend on confidence filtering or prediction consistency, which are often insufficient to maintain branch diversity and fully exploit their disagreements on unlabeled data. To address these issues, we propose CoreNet-S3, a unified semi-supervised framework for remote sensing change detection. Firstly, a relational explicit discrepancy (RED) mechanism is introduced to preserve branch complementarity and alleviate representation homogenization during collaborative learning. Secondly, a conflict-resolved integration (CRI) mechanism is designed to fuse predictions from the two branches, generate more reliable pseudo-labels, and make better use of informative disagreement regions. Extensive experiments on the LEVIR-CD and SYSU-CD datasets under different labeling ratios demonstrate that CoreNet-S3 achieves consistent IoU gains across both datasets and all label ratios, especially in low-label settings.

title: Risk-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Anomaly Detection in Cyber-Medical IoMT Systems
authors: Flavio Pastore, Raja Waseem Anwar, Nafaa Hadi Jabeur, Saqib Ali
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: Modern healthcare increasingly relies on IoMTbased cyber-medical systems that continuously monitor patient vitals. However, these systems face a critical challenge: distinguishing genuine physiological anomalies from sensor faults and cyber-attacks. This paper proposes a risk-aware multimodal fusion framework that integrates network-level security analysis with physiological anomaly detection. The framework combines a supervised XGBoost classifier for IoMT traffic anomalies and an unsupervised LSTM autoencoder for vital-sign deviations. Their outputs are fused through a calibrated risk layer that classifies system states as Stable, High Risk, or Critical and provides uncertainty-aware risk scores. Experiments using IoMTTrafficData and VitalDB demonstrate robust performance under noisy conditions, achieving an overall accuracy of 89% and macroF1 of 0.89. The proposed approach offers interpretable, contextaware decisions that go beyond simple anomaly flags, supporting safer and more trustworthy IoMT deployments. This work represents a step toward integrated cyber-physical monitoring in healthcare, reducing alarm fatigue and improving clinical decision support.

title: A Millimeter-wave Reflective Polarization Conversion Metasurface with High Selectivity
authors: Hang Yuan, Kai-Ran Xiang, Fu-Chang Chen
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a millimeter-wave reflective polarization conversion metasurface (PCM) based on a novel multipath coupling topology. The periodic unit of this PCM mainly contains a slant-placed butterfly-shaped patch and a stacked ring. The butterfly-shaped patch is a dual-mode resonator with multiple design freedoms, offering a transversal coupling topology with two coupling paths. The stacked ring is not resonant within the band, acting as a non-resonant node that provides a source-load coupling path. The combination of transversal coupling and source-load coupling forms a novel topology with three coupling paths, achieving a wide bandwidth and high selectivity. The PCM has been fabricated and measured, containing a wide bandwidth of 24-30 GHz (22.2%) and two polarization conversion zeros located at 21.5 GHz and 33 GHz to improve selectivity. Compared with the existing frequency selective PCMs, the proposed design utilizes a novel multipath coupling topology to achieve a wider bandwidth and higher selectivity.

title: Federated Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Resilient Spectrum Sharing in 6G Non-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Sadia Khaf, Georges Kaddoum
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: High-altitude platform stations (HAPS) offer a pragmatic backbone for extending 6G non-terrestrial networks (NTNs) into dense urban cores, sparsely connected remote regions, and disaster-stricken areas. In these environments, cognitive radio networks (CRNs) must arbitrate spectrum access without trusting all agents or relying on intact terrestrial backhaul. Accordingly, in the present study, we propose a federated hierarchical reinforcement learning (F-HRL) framework where secondary users (SUs) cooperatively access primary users’ (PUs) spectrum, ground next-generation Node Bs (gNBs) and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) enforce trust-aware scheduling, while HAPS coordinators perform Byzantine-resilient aggregation using satellite backhaul. Area-specific reward shaping allows the same policy stack to prioritize spectral efficiency in urban deployments, coverage in remote regions, and rapid recovery after infrastructure loss. We benchmark F-HRL against non-cognitive baselines, simple heuristics, and the hierarchical deep reinforcement learning framework across the following three key scenarios over 5 000 decision steps: nominal spectrum sharing, disaster recovery with a gNB outage, and adversarial environments with malicious SUs. Experimental results demonstrate that F-HRL achieves a nearly 8-fold higher spectrum efficiency than the non-cognitive operation, maintains 86% SU quality-of-service (QoS) satisfaction with a Jain fairness index of 0.950, and sustains over 95% of nominal throughput during infrastructure failures. Under attack conditions with 25% compromised agents, trust-weighted aggregation successfully isolates malicious updates while preserving legitimate throughput. These findings demonstrate that HAPS-centric F-HRL provides adaptive, secure, and resilient spectrum management for future NTN deployments.

title: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation Publication Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation

title: Temporal Audio Narratives: Event Reasoning through Large Language Models
authors: N. Kravchenko, M. Gorodnichev, G. Mkrtchian
abstract: Large audio-language models enable scene understanding from raw audio but require substantial computational resources, limiting edge deployment. This paper proposed and evaluated a hybrid two-stage pipeline that combines sound event detection with compact chain-of-thought reasoning for efficient audio scene captioning. Using a curated subset of 204 CompA-R-Instructions samples (70 train, 134 test) with ground-truth event timestamps, a LoRA-adapted Gemma-3N model achieved superior LLM-as-judge scores (0.673) versus state-of-the-art audio LLMs (0.274–0.397), despite operating only on symbolic event inputs. The event-driven approach demonstrated greater robustness (0.11 vs. 0.18–0.21), concise outputs, and predictable hardware-bound latency, highlighting structured event conditioning and reasoning distillation as an effective architectural bias for resource-constrained audio understanding.

title: Automated Detection of Elliott Wave Patterns Using LSTM Autoencoders and Multi-Domain Feature Fusion
authors: B Swetha, Kriti Arya
abstract: This research proposes a new hybrid model for the identification of Elliott Wave patterns in financial time series, combining the strengths of LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) autoencoders with conventional technical analysis tools. The new approach has a complete processing pipeline with three different pivot identification techniques: Firstly, the combination of LSTM autoencoders with sophisticated feature engineering using an optimal preprocessing strategy involving wavelet smoothing, fractals, Fourier transforms, and the Elliott Wave Oscillator (EWO); secondly, a variety of clustering approaches aimed at pattern recognition; and thirdly, conventional wave recognition techniques (such as peak-trough identification, the Elliott Wave Oscillator, and the Zigzag indicator) – all of which are controlled by the strict rules of Elliott Wave validation. Ablation studies demonstrate the crucial role of each component, with insights provided by the aggregation of clustering approaches and the analysis of the reconstruction errors. This approach can successfully mark the impulses (1-2-3-4-5) and corrective (A-B-C) waves, aided with confidence measures. The proposed hybrid framework achieves approximately 2.4 times higher wave detection capacity compared to traditional methods, demonstrating substantial improvement in structural pattern identification thereby providing a complete platform for the automation of the ElliottWave analysis.

title: Quasi-Exponential Convergence of Higher-Order Serial Differentiators Under Bounded Input Perturbation
authors: Jang-Hyun Park, Sun-Pil Kim
abstract: This paper studies Higher-Order Serial Differentiators (HOSDs) under bounded input perturbations and develops rigorous event-based convergence bounds for their estimation errors. The analysis is formulated in terms of quasi-exponential convergence (QEC), which is used here as an event-driven characterization of transient decay for switching-based differentiators. First, for a single-stage estimator subject to bounded measurement perturbations, we prove that the derivative estimation error converges to an explicit gain-dependent steady-state tube, thereby quantifying the noise–bandwidth trade-off. Second, we establish a QEC transfer property for cascaded n-stage HOSDs: if the perturbation entering one stage satisfies a QEC-type bound, then the next stage inherits a corresponding practical QEC bound under a standard non-accumulating switching condition. Third, in the nominal perturbation-free case, we provide a refined event-based proof of convergence to zero, with the transient behavior quantified within the same QEC framework. These results provide explicit and gain-tunable bounds on both transient performance and steady-state accuracy, offering practical guidance for serial differentiator design under perturbations.

title: Event-Triggered Adaptive Tracking Control for Quarter-Car Bio-inspiration Suspension Systems with State Constraints
authors: Lei Liu, Ruonan Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper proposes an adaptive event-triggered tracking control strategy for quarter-vehicle bionic active suspension system with state constraints. Firstly, the crane-leg bionic structure is applied to the suspension design, and a model with ideal nonlinearity and damping characteristics is established, significantly improving the energy efficiency and stability of the system. Secondly, to solve the physical constraint problem of the suspension vertical displacement, an innovative double-layer barrier Lyapunov function (BLF) is adopted, combined with the backstepping method, to strictly limit the state variables within the preset safe range, converting the traditional constrained stability problem into an unconstrained control problem, avoiding the risk of mechanical structure failure. Finally, in response to the challenge of limited communication resources between the controller and the actuator, a combined relative threshold event-triggered control (ETC) strategy is proposed. This strategy significantly reduces the data transmission volume, strictly avoids the Zeno phenomenon, and overcomes the deficiency of insufficient adjustment range of fixed-threshold ETC in bionic suspension systems. Stability analysis proves the stability of the closed-loop system. Simulation results verify that the proposed scheme can effectively improve the stability and comprehensive performance of the vehicle suspension system.

title: Edge Feature Enhancement and Multi-scale Convolutional Kernel Selection Aggregation for Fine-grained Ship Detection_supp1-3693132.pdf
authors: Guirong Feng
abstract: In recent years, with the continuous advancement of high-resolution remote sensing imaging technology, the capability of perceiving maritime ship targets under complex sea conditions has been significantly improved. This holds substantial theoretical value and practical significance for enhancing maritime traffic situational awareness, strengthening the supervision of fishery activities, and safeguarding the marine environment as well as maritime security. However, due to the single imaging perspective of remote sensing images, fine-grained ship detection still faces challenges. such as large intra-class scale variations caused by varying imaging heights and ambiguous inter-class boundaries for visually similar ship types.To address this issue, we propose the Edge-Aware Feature Enhancement Module (EFEM) to strengthen the fusion of edge details and spatial contextual information of ships in remote sensing images, and design the Multi-Scale Kernel Aggregation Module (MSKAM) to improve the model’s ability to perceive ships of varying sizes. These modules effectively solve the problem of extracting discriminative features of ships. Finally, experiments conducted on the third hierarchy of the ShipRSImageNet dataset and the FAIR1M dataset demonstrate that, compared with the benchmark model, the proposed fine-grained ship detection model achieves improvements of 1.87 % and 1.72 % in the mAP50 metric, respectively. Moreover, comparative validations on the two datasets further confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in enhancing the accuracy of fine-grained ship detection.

title: Decentralized Variational Bayesian UKF with Maximum Generalized Student’s t-kernel Correntropy for Wide-Area Power System state estimation
authors: Jinhui Hu, Haiquan Zhao, Yi Peng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: A Conventional centralized state estimators exhibit limited robustness in large-scale grids and face practical deployment hurdles. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a decentralized maximum generalized Student's t-kernel correntropy Variational Bayesian unscented Kalman filter (D-MGST-VBUKF). The algorithm optimizes the estimation performance at three levels for the regionalized state estimation needs: first, to address non-Gaussian measurement noise in practical systems, we propose the cost function using MGST, retaining Student's t robustness while improving adaptability to complex noise by expanding the degree-of-freedom parameter; secondly, the VB inference framework is constructed to model the unknown noise distribution online, and the joint optimization of the noise statistical characteristics and state estimation is realized by constructing the conjugate prior distribution; finally, the regional state fusion mechanism is established based on the topological correlation characteristics of the power grid, and the global consistency correction of the local estimation results is realized by constructing the state coordination equation of the boundary nodes. Simulation experiments in IEEE 14-bus and IEEE 39-bus system show that the method has stronger robustness compared with the traditional algorithm under non-Gaussian noise environment and unknown noise environment.

title: D-NDT: Distributed Network Digital Twin Architecture - Use Case on 5G Network Slice Management
authors: Lal Verda Cakir, Aryan Kaushik, Berk Canberk
abstract: Network Digital Twins (NDTs) are set to play a crucial role in next-generation networks. However, their deployment in the real world is constrained by high synchronisation overhead and scalability limitations. To address this, while distributed computing is a promising paradigm, it cannot be implemented directly in the NDTs due to their tightly coupled architectures. Therefore, we present Distributed NDT (D-NDT) and decouple synchronisation from model execution. In this, dedicated components handle bidirectional telemetry flows, preprocessing, and aggregation near the physical network, while separate execution components perform model inference using the abstracted, synchronised representation. This separation enables independent scaling, eliminates redundant data collection across domains, and supports distributed deployment. Consequently, we present a use case for 5G network slice management and compare the performance of D-NDT with centralised and partially distributed architectures. The results show that D-NDT reduces synchronisation latency and maintains scalability as NDT complexity increases.

title: Mixture Distribution Modeling Pseudo-Label Propagation of Domain Adaptation for Low-Data Resource Cross-Scene Classification
authors: Zhihao Che, Tong Zhang, Yin Zhuang, Xinyi Zhang, He Chen, Lianlin Li, Jun Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Low-Data Resource Cross-Scene Classification (LDR-CSC) aims to transfer learnable knowledge from a limited labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain. However, different imaging conditions, diverse spatial layouts, and complex land covers can lead to significant domain discrepancies between the source and target domains. Moreover, the low-data resource condition of the source domain would further aggravate domain discrepancies that hinder knowledge transfer, resulting in inferior cross-scene classification performance. Therefore, in this article, a novel Mixture Distribution modeling Pseudo-label Propagation of Domain Adaptation called MDP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>-DA is proposed for LDR-CSC. First, to improve learnable knowledge transferability under low-data resource conditions, a domain-invariant and domain specific collaborative learning is designed to jointly capture inter-domain spatial cues of consistency from dual-domain complicated remote sensing scenes and in-domain specific local regions. It can bridge domain discrepancies and ensure a more complete description of unlabeled data for high-quality pseudo-label generation. Second, to improve the quality of cross-domain pseudo-label propagation, a mixture distribution modeling strategy is designed for cross-domain optimization learning of pseudo-label propagation. This strategy can build reliable and unreliable sets using a Gaussian-uniform model and form a self-calibration mechanism to mine the effective information latent in unreliable sets, facilitating cross-scene classification ability in the unlabeled target domain. Finally, extensive experiments are conducted on various LDR-CSC scenarios. The results demonstrate that the proposed MDP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>-DA achieves state-of-the-art performance for low-data resource cross-scene classification.

title: A physically constrained Energy Spectrum Superposition Method-Machine Learning coupling algorithm for obtaining the neutron spectrum of BNCT rapidly
authors: JunKai Yang, HaoPeng Deng, ZhiMeng Hu, FangCong Zhang, LiKai Guo, Min Peng, DengJie Xiao, PingQuan Wang, Hui Zhang, BoWen Zhou, Chungming Paul Chu, Lin Xiao, Giuseppe Gorini
abstract: Accurately and rapidly acquiring the neutron energy spectrum online is one of the critical challenges for Image-Guided Radiotherapy of BNCT (IGRT-BNCT). To address the limitations of the Energy Spectrum Superposition Method (ESSM), this study integrates machine learning techniques, deep neural networks (DNN) and random forest (RF), into the framework, resulting in a novel algorithm termed ESSM-ML. First, the algorithm achieves high predictive performance. The predictions of neutron energy spectra of two physical processes by both DNN and RF show high R² values and extremely low RMSE. In the three energy regions, the predicted spectrum curves are in good agreement with the true ones, with a maximum deviation of less than 2.4% in the epithermal neutron energy region in particular. For the total neutron energy spectrum at the beam shaping assembly (BSA) emission window, the ratio of the total neutron fluence rate obtained by ESSM-ML to that by the traditional simulation-based method reaches 95.3%. Second, the algorithm achieves high efficiency in prediction and calculation of energy spectrum. The time required to obtain the therapeutic neutron energy spectrum via ESSM-ML is only (63.4±2.4) s, with a 4900-fold improvement in computational efficiency. The ML modules take merely (0.0027±0.0001) s on average to predict a single energy spectrum. By enabling rapid and accurate prediction of spectra not present in the database, ESSM-ML not only enhances clinical applicability and reduces database construction time, but also provides a theoretical and algorithmic foundation for the realization of IGRT-BNCT.

title: Complementary Representations of Invariant in Domain Generalization for Industrial Data Drift
authors: He Ding, Kuangrong Hao, Lei Chen, Yaozhong Zhuang, Ruimin Xie, Xiaoxue Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: It is well known that data drift may occur in complex industrial processes, which can cause changes in the distribution of data sampled by sensors. Therefore, the ability to generalize across unseen domains is essential for monitoring systems deployed in industrial processes. The invariant-complement domain generalization (ICDG) is proposed to alleviate data drift in industrial processes. This study reveals how the proposed covariant representation complements the invariant representation. Additionally, it derives novel theoretical error bounds characterizing the relationship between seen and unseen domains. Intuitively, the proposed invariant-complement representation method mitigates the influence of variant factors and encourages the learning of invariant and covariant representations. From the perspective of information theory, the boundaries for invariant and covariant representations are established and integrated as a joint learning objective with multiple information constraints. Theoretically, we elucidate that optimizing the ICDG objective function is equivalent to minimizing the upper bound of the empirical risk associated with unseen domains. This result helps ensure accurate prediction of quality variables under data drift. Case studies on the gas turbine (GT) dataset and the actual polyester esterification dataset validate the effectiveness of the proposed ICDG. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/heheding/ICDG</uri>

title: An Adaptive Analog PI-Based Active Gate Driver Method for Transient Current Sharing in Parallel-Connected SiC MOSFETs
authors: Tao Tang, Wensheng Song, Jian Chen, Tingwen Hu, Hao Yue, Haitao Liu, Guoyou Liu
abstract: Transient current imbalance induced by parasitic mismatches severely limits the high-speed switching performance of parallel-connected SiC MOSFETs. This paper proposes an adaptive active gate driving strategy to suppress transient current imbalance. Based on a time-domain analysis revealing the intrinsic coupling between power-source parasitic inductance mismatch and gate–source voltage deviation, the proposed scheme dynamically regulates gate behavior to achieve equalization within a single switching event. The 400-MHz PCB Rogowski coil is employed for high-fidelity transient current feedback, and an analog PI-based loop ensures self-adaptive regulation. Experimental results validate that the proposed driver maintains an average current imbalance Δζave below 5% across diverse operating voltages, currents, mismatch parameters, and temperatures, offering a rapid, scalable, and practical solution for high-power parallel SiC converters.

title: IoT-Enabled Greenhouse Automation: A Machine Learning Approach to Precision Farming
authors: Kumar Dorthi, Ravi Kanth Kotha, Kesoju Sairam, Kuna Spandana, Anil Kumar, Mahadevi S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: Greenhouse farming provides a controlled environment that prevents crops from weather fluctuations and climate risks and enhances year-round food production. However, enhancement of crop growth conditions lies in providing suitable environmental conditions for a variety of crops, selecting suitable crops with respect to the greenhouse conditions, and yield prediction. To achieve this, an Internet of Things (IoT) and machine learning-based greenhouse monitoring system is developed to identify suitable crops, climate zones, and yield prediction. The system monitors temperature and humidity, soil temperature, soil moisture, soil conductivity, and soil pH. The LoRaWAN communication protocol is used for transmitting the data to the cloud from the greenhouse. Machine learning models such as Random Forest (RF), Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), and K-Means clustering provided an accuracy of 92.5% in crop classification, yield prediction attained an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 0.89 and RMSE of 0.8 tons/hectare, and classified greenhouse climates into four zones, respectively, by analyzing the sensor data. This study explores the potential of IoT and machine learning to improve greenhouse farming.

title: Modeling Augmented Visual Servoing Dynamics of Over-Actuated Multirotors Using Persistently-Excited Physics-Informed Neural Networks
authors: Michael Shi, Archit Krishna Kamath, Bing Yan
publish: 2026 IEEE 13th International Joint Conference on Cybernetics and Computational Cybernetics, Cyber-Medical Systems (ICCC)
abstract: This paper presents a persistently excited physicsinformed neural network framework for modeling the augmented visual servoing dynamics of an over-actuated multirotor equipped with a stereo camera pair. Unlike existing dynamics driven visual servoing methods that are restricted to underactuated platforms, the proposed approach captures the full nonlinear coupling between thrust vectoring inputs, the dynamics of the over-actuated quadrotor, and the evolution of stereo visual features. The continuous time dynamics driven visual servoing model is discretised and embedded into a physics-informed neural network that learns deviations from the nominal model using a combined real data loss and physics residual loss. Training is carried out under a persistency of excitation constrained stochastic gradient descent scheme, where the Lipschitz constant that determines the learning rate bound is estimated from recorded gradients and parameter updates to ensure stable convergence. The learned neural model is integrated within a monotonically weighted nonlinear model predictive controller to provide accurate state prediction and real time computation. Simulation studies across four representative motion profiles show that the proposed controller achieves substantially lower tracking error and reduced computational latency when compared with Tube MPC and Adaptive MPC. On average, tracking accuracy improves by more than 50% and computation time reduces by over 40%, demonstrating that the proposed physics-informed neural network based modeling framework is an effective strategy for real time dynamics driven visual servoing of over-actuated aerial robots.

title: Inverted U-Shaped Counter Electrode Enables Uniform Current Distribution in Planar PCB Electrochemical Sensors
authors: Khi Khim Beh, Asrulnizam Abd Manaf, Khi Poay Beh, Chuan Wei Oo, Kazuaki Sawada
abstract: This article presents systematic optimization of electrode geometry for electrochemical sensors fabricated on flame retardant 4 (FR-4) substrate using conventional printed circuit board (PCB) technology. Through finite element modeling and experimental validation, we demonstrate that counter-to-working electrode (CE/WE) surface area ratio critically determines sensor performance in planar geometries. Twelve configurations with CE/WE ratios from 1:1 to 12:1 were systematically evaluated using COMSOL Multiphysics simulations and cyclic voltammetry (CV) characterization. Results reveal that the optimal 12:1 ratio achieves the best overall electrochemical performance across all discriminating metrics: peak current magnitude (Ipa <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$= 27.70~\pm ~0.57~\mu $ </tex-math></inline-formula>A, mean across three independently fabricated devices, relative standard deviation (RSD) = 2.05%), minimum peak separation (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta $ </tex-math></inline-formula>Ep = 63 mV approaching the theoretical 59 mV for reversible one-electron transfer), excellent field uniformity, and near-unity peak current ratio (ipa/ipc = 1.02). While ipa/ipc approaches 1.02 at ratios as low as 4:1, the 12:1 configuration uniquely satisfies all performance criteria simultaneously. CV comparison with a commercial DropSens gold screen-printed electrode (SPE) confirms that the optimized PCB geometry produces electrochemically comparable behavior to an established commercial benchmark.

title: Folding Convolutional Neural Network for Rotating Machinery Fault Diagnosis
authors: Tiantian Lu, Hongbin Zhu, Jisheng Dai, Huadong Lai, Weichao Xu
abstract: One-dimensional convolutional neural network (1D-CNN)-based methods are widely used in fault diagnosis for rotating machinery. However, traditional 1D-CNN-based approaches are limited by the fixed receptive field of their convolutional kernels, which restricts their ability to capture long-range dependencies in signal data. To address the issue, a folding convolutional neural network (FCNN) is proposed. By folding the input signals before convolution, the FCNN enables a relative shift between distant information and the CNN's receptive field, allowing the model to effectively utilize both local and long-range features. Building on the FCNN, a multiview fault diagnosis framework is constructed through the integration of a transformer encoder and a long short-term memory (LSTM) network. Transfer learning is employed to optimize the initial parameters of the model. The proposed method first uses a three-layer FCNN to extract features from each channel individually, followed by feature fusion. The fused features are then analyzed by a transformer encoder, which captures global contextual relationships through self-attention, and further processed by an LSTM to model temporal dynamics. Experiments conducted on one private and three public datasets demonstrate the effectiveness, robustness, noise tolerance, and advanced performance of the proposed model.

title: Partially-Coupled Staircase LDPC Codes for High Speed Inter-Satellite Communications
authors: Yaosheng Zhang, Jian Jiao, Ke Zhang, Jiayin Xue, Ye Wang, Qinyu Zhang
abstract: In this paper, we propose a rate-compatible partially-coupled staircase low density parity check (PS-LDPC) coding scheme for high speed inter-satellite communications. First, we introduce the encoding process and sliding window decoding (SWD) algorithm of PS-LDPC codes, and we investigate the error floor of component codes, which validate that the PS-LDPC codes with short block-length component code can maintain the reliability, and significantly reduce the decoding latency. Then, we analyze the density evolution (DE) of PS-LDPC codes based on the multi-edge type (MET)- LDPC framework under the Gaussian approximation, and derive its decoding thresholds of SWD. Further, we propose an optimized coupling pattern (OCP) encoding algorithm that achieves the optimal coupling patterns with the minimized threshold by introducing two-stage column permutations, and modify the message exchanges in SWD algorithm according to this encoding algorithm. Moreover, we design a new decoding algorithm, named cascaded SWD (C-SWD) algorithm, which reduces the error floor and enhances decoding performance by pre-decoding, reliability enhancement, and cascading belief propagation (BP) decoder or ordered likelihood decoder (OLD) due to the error floor. Simulation results demonstrate that our PS-LDPC coding scheme outperforms the existing rate-compatible spatially coupled LDPC (SC-LDPC) coding schemes in terms of bit error rate and complexity.

title: Detuning-Controlled Evolution of Kerr Frequency Combs in a Silicon Nitride Microring Resonator
authors: Weizhong Zhang, Abdou E. Shetewy, Kambiz Jamshidi
abstract: We experimentally investigate the dynamics of the Kerr comb generation in a high-Q silicon nitride (Si<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>N<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>) microring resonator pumped with a continuous-wave (CW) laser in the C-band, operating in the fundamental transverse electric mode (TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">00</inf>). As the pump wavelength is gradually tuned toward the cavity resonance, we observe the transition from a single pump line to a broadband comb spanning over 8 THz (more than 36 lines). The observed comb evolution provides insight into the detuning-dependent dynamics for chip-scale frequency comb generation.

title: Dual-Axis Directional Attention and Selective Information Gating for Trimap-Free Video Matting
authors: Huayu Zhang
publish: 2026 5th Asia Conference on Algorithms, Computing and Machine Learning (CACML)
abstract: Video matting is a pivotal yet ill-posed task aimed at estimating per-frame alpha mattes, essential for high-quality foreground extraction in virtual reality and cinematic production. While recent research has shifted toward trimap-free methods to avoid labor-intensive manual labeling, existing architectures often struggle to preserve fine-grained details and suppress background noise. Specifically, conventional channel attention mechanisms frequently lose critical spatial coordinate information through global pooling, while indiscriminate skip connections introduce redundant background clutter into the decoder. In this paper, we propose DSGNet (Dual-axis Selective Gating Network), a robust framework designed to enhance both spatial precision and feature purity. Our method introduces the Dual-Axis Directional Attention (DADA) module, which decouples spatial encoding into independent horizontal and vertical 1D processes to provide precise coordinate awareness while maintaining a global receptive field. Furthermore, we design the Selective Information Gating (SIG) module, which leverages high-level semantic guidance to adaptively filter shallow features and suppress background interference at a pixel-wise level. Experimental evaluations on the RVM benchmark demonstrate that DSGNet achieves superior performance in terms of both accuracy and temporal stability. Qualitative results further confirm that our approach produces sharper boundaries and cleaner backgrounds, offering a practical and efficient solution for complex video matting scenarios.

title: An Air-Jet Continuum Robot for Industrial Integrated Fluidic Circuit Inspection
authors: Lechen Sun, Yuanzhuo Li, Haosen Wang, Yongzhao Chen, Hongmei Zhang, Jie Han, Zhiguang Xing, Yongchang Zhang, Jianwen Zhao
abstract: Industrial fluidic circuits operating under long-term extreme conditions are prone to defects such as cavitation, corrosion, and cracks, raising a critical demand for in-situ robotic inspection. Traditional manual disassembly-based methods are inefficient, while existing robots lack the ability to simultaneously adapt to complex geometries and maintain stable adsorption on surfaces of varying curvature. This study proposes an air-jet continuum robot (AJCR) that integrates three core modules: air-jet modules, a unit flexible continuum skeleton (UFCS), and stiffness-adjustable flexible suction cups (FSCs), enabling effective crawling and inspection inside integrated industrial fluid circuits. The UFCS provides omnidirectional bending capability of up to 196° enabling navigation through complex channels including abrupt turns of 120° inside stop valves. Under an input pressure of 0.8 MPa, the AJCR generates a maximum air-jet counter-thrust of 4.72 N for adsorption and 4.16 N for driving, ensuring reliable locomotion on unstructured, slippery, and discontinuous surfaces. Based on the sliding suction strategy, the FSC offers a maximum static adsorption force of 35.1 N, reduces sliding friction to 2.25 N on stainless steel, and adapts to curved surfaces with radii as small as 125 mm, facilitating stable sliding adsorption even across welded seams. Experimental results demonstrate that the AJCR smoothly navigates pipeline systems, enters stop valves, mixed-flow pumps and pressure vessels, and operates in liquid-filled environments, significantly improving inspection coverage and operational safety in fluidic delivery systems.

title: Novel Dynamic Amplitude Normalization Method Based on Tracking Differentiator for SOGI-FLL
authors: Hanlin Wang, Rende Zhao, Juntao Xu, Jinkui He, Qingzeng Yan, Qingxiao Du
abstract: The second-order generalized integrator-frequency locked loop (SOGI-FLL) is widely applied in power electronic converter control to extract grid voltage amplitude, frequency, and phase. Amplitude normalization is usually incorporated to mitigate errors induced by voltage deviation; however, the increasing integration of new energy sources has reduced grid inertia, leading to more frequent voltage amplitude fluctuations. Traditional SOGI-based normalization suffers from inadequate dynamic performance due to the phase lag of orthogonal signals, degrading SOGI-FLL extraction accuracy. To address this, a dynamic amplitude normalization method based on a tracking differentiator (TD) is proposed. It leverages the phase-leading characteristic of TD to generate strictly orthogonal signals and employs an independent SOGI for input prefiltering to suppress noise interference on TD, thereby achieving accurate amplitude extraction and dynamic normalization. Theoretical analysis and experimental verification show that the proposed method can accurately extract amplitude and frequency information even under voltage amplitude fluctuations and sudden changes, and its performance is superior to the traditional method.
